topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Perishable, Semi-Perishable and Non-Perishable Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 | Food Engineering
## Topic: Perishable, Semi-Perishable and Non-Perishable Foods

---

### 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In Food Engineering, the classification of food products based on their **shelf life** and **susceptibility to spoilage** is the foundational concept that governs every subsequent decision in the supply chain вҖ” from harvest handling to packaging, cold-chain logistics, and retail display. KTU 2024 Scheme treats this classification as a *gateway* concept because it directly determines the engineering parameters of storage (temperature, relative humidity, atmosphere composition) and the choice of preservation technology (refrigeration, freezing, thermal processing, dehydration, or chemical preservation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> - **Perishable Foods:** Foods that undergo rapid and irreversible quality deterioration (microbial, chemical, or physical) within a short period вҖ” typically **less than 7 days** вҖ” when held at ambient/refrigerated temperatures. They possess high water activity ($a_w > 0.85$) and high moisture content (> 60 % w.b.).
> - **Semi-Perishable Foods:** Foods with intermediate shelf life (**weeks to a few months**) under proper storage. They have moderate water activity ($0.60 \le a_w \le 0.85$) and benefit from controlled-atmosphere or low-temperature storage.
> - **Non-Perishable (Shelf-Stable) Foods:** Foods that can be stored at ambient conditions for **several months to years** without significant quality loss. They have low water activity ($a_w < 0.60$), low moisture content, or have been processed (canning, fermentation, drying) to inactivate spoilage mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this classification matters in KTU examinations:** The examiner often frames a question as *"Classify the following foods as P/SP/NP and justify your answer with reference to water activity and dominant spoilage mechanism."* Marks are awarded for: (i) correct classification, (ii) correct $a_w$ range, (iii) correct identification of the *primary* spoilage agent, and (iv) the appropriate preservation technique.

---

#### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English View)

Think of the food on your kitchen shelf as a group of **three types of house-guests**:

1. **Perishable foods** (fresh fish, milk, raw meat) are like a friend who is allergic to *everything* вҖ” they need constant refrigeration, they spoil in days, and one careless moment (leaving fish on the counter for 4 hours) ruins the entire stock. Their high internal water is essentially a "swimming pool" for bacteria, yeasts, and moulds.

2. **Semi-perishable foods** (apples, potatoes, cheese, eggs) are like a moderately tolerant relative вҖ” they will *tolerate* your pantry for weeks, but they perform much better in a cool cellar. They are not as fragile as fish, but they are nowhere near as rugged as dried pulses. Their water activity is "in the middle" вҖ” enough moisture to allow slow enzymatic and microbial action, but not enough for explosive growth.

3. **Non-perishable foods** (sugar, salt, dried rice, canned beans, honey) are like a stoic hermit вҖ” they sit on the shelf for years, indifferent to ambient conditions. Their water is either *removed* (drying) or *locked* (high sugar/salt concentration creating osmotic pressure) so that microorganisms literally cannot grow вҖ” the water is *present* in the food matrix, but it is *unavailable* to microbes.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering intuition:** The lower the water activity, the longer the shelf life. This is the single most important rule in food preservation engineering, and KTU examiners expect you to quote the critical $a_w$ thresholds for key microorganisms (e.g., bacteria need $a_w > 0.91$, yeasts $a_w > 0.88$, moulds $a_w > 0.80$).

#### 1.1.2 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Water activity threshold (bacteria) | $a_{w,bact}$ | **0.91** | Below this, most spoilage bacteria cannot grow |
| Water activity threshold (yeasts) | $a_{w,yeast}$ | **0.88** | Critical for jams, syrups, fermented products |
| Water activity threshold (moulds) | $a_{w,mould}$ | **0.80** | Defines the safe limit for dried foods |
| Water activity threshold (halophilic bacteria) | $a_{w,halo}$ | **0.75** | Salted/dried meats |
| Water activity threshold (xerophilic moulds) | $a_{w,xero}$ | **0.60** | Defines shelf-stable dried foods |
| Refrigerated storage temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **0 вҖ“ 4 В°C** | Standard for perishable foods |
| Frozen storage temperature | $T_{frz}$ | **вүӨ вҖ“18 В°C** | Long-term storage of perishables |
| Ambient tropical storage | $T_{amb}$ | **25 вҖ“ 35 В°C** | Reference for shelf-stable foods |
| Typical shelf life вҖ“ Perishable | $t_{P}$ | **1 вҖ“ 7 days** | At refrigeration |
| Typical shelf life вҖ“ Semi-perishable | $t_{SP}$ | **2 weeks вҖ“ 3 months** | Cool storage |
| Typical shelf life вҖ“ Non-perishable | $t_{NP}$ | **6 months вҖ“ 5+ years** | Ambient storage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Spoilage Mechanisms (The "Why" Behind Classification)

The shelf life of any food is governed by **three concurrent deterioration pathways**, and the *dominant* pathway determines the classification:

1. **Microbial Spoilage** вҖ” Growth of bacteria, yeasts, and moulds. Controlled by $a_w$, pH, temperature, and oxygen.
2. **Chemical/Enzymatic Spoilage** вҖ” Lipid oxidation, browning (Maillard + enzymatic), vitamin loss, autolysis of tissues. Controlled by oxygen exclusion, low temperature, blanching.
3. **Physical Spoilage** вҖ” Moisture loss (wilting), moisture gain (caking), mechanical damage, recrystallisation of ice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - If **microbial spoilage** dominates within 7 days вҶ’ **Perishable**
> - If **chemical/enzymatic spoilage** dominates over weeks вҶ’ **Semi-Perishable**
> - If spoilage is **negligible for months/years** due to low $a_w$ or processing вҶ’ **Non-Perishable**

### 2.2 Detailed Classification Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 вҖ” Measure the water activity ($a_w$).**
Water activity is *not* the same as moisture content. It is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in the food ($p$) to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{\text{ERV (Equilibrium Relative Vapour Pressure)}}{100}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Cross-reference with the dominant spoilage microorganism threshold.**

| $a_w$ Range | Microbial Risk | Food Class Example |
|-------------|----------------|--------------------|
| $a_w > 0.91$ | High вҖ” most bacteria, including *Pseudomonas*, *Clostridium* | Fresh meat, fish, milk |
| $0.88 < a_w \le 0.91$ | Moderate вҖ” yeasts dominate | Soft cheeses, fruit juices |
| $0.80 < a_w \le 0.88$ | Low вҖ” moulds can grow, bacteria suppressed | Aged cheese, bread, jams |
| $0.60 < a_w \le 0.80$ | Very low вҖ” only xerophilic moulds | Dried fruits, marshmallows |
| $a_w \le 0.60$ | Negligible | Dried milk powder, noodles, spices |

**Step 3 вҖ” Determine pH category.**

| pH Range | Category | Implication |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| < 4.6 | **High-acid** (e.g., fruits, pickles, vinegar) | Supports classification as SP/NP even at moderate $a_w$ (because *Clostridium botulinum* cannot grow) |
| 4.6 вҖ“ 7.0 | **Medium-acid** (e.g., cheese, meat) | Requires refrigeration or thermal processing |
| > 7.0 | **Low-acid** (e.g., vegetables, milk) | Highest spoilage risk; almost always perishable |

**Step 4 вҖ” Combine $a_w$, pH, and storage temperature to assign final class.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | $p$ = partial vapour pressure of water in food; $p_0$ = vapour pressure of pure water | Determines microbial growth potential |
| 2 | $\ln a_w = -\dfrac{M_1 \cdot \phi}{M_2}} \cdot k$ | (Raoult's-law based for ideal solutions) | Theoretical $a_w$ of sugar/salt solutions |
| 3 | $M_{eq} = \dfrac{w_s}{w_w} \cdot \dfrac{M_w}{M_s}$ | $M_{eq}$ = molal concentration; $w_s$ = mass solute; $w_w$ = mass water; $M_w, M_s$ = molecular weights | Used in Norrish/Ross equation |
| 4 | $a_w = X_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$ (Norrish) | $X_w$ = mole fraction of water; $X_s$ = mole fraction of solute; $K$ = constant | Predicts $a_w$ of sugar solutions |
| 5 | $\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 R} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ (Arrhenius) | $k$ = reaction rate; $E_a$ = activation energy; $R$ = 8.314 J/molВ·K; $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Predicts shelf life at different temperatures |
| 6 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | $Q_{10}$ = temperature acceleration factor (typical 2вҖ“3 for foods) | Quick shelf-life estimation |
| 7 | $t_{s,T} = \dfrac{t_{s,T_0}}{Q_{10}^{(T_0 - T)/10}}$ | $t_{s,T}$ = shelf life at temperature $T$ | Predicts shelf-life change with temperature |
| 8 | $\%M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | $m_w$ = mass of water; $m_s$ = mass of solids | Moisture content on wet basis |
| 9 | $\%M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | вҖ” | Moisture content on dry basis (used in drying) |
| 10 | $\theta_{f} = \dfrac{T_f - T_{a}}{T_{ref} - T_{a}}$ (Plank's freezing time, simplified) | $T_f$ = final temperature; $T_a$ = air temperature; $T_{ref}$ = reference | Used for cold-chain design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse $a_w$ (water activity) with % moisture content. They are *not* the same: a sponge can be 90 % water but have $a_w \approx 1.0$ (water is freely available), whereas a piece of bound cake at 25 % moisture may have $a_w = 0.70$ because the water is chemically bound to starch. **Always quote $a_w$, not %, when justifying classification.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This classification system is the *backbone* of:
- **Cold-chain logistics design** (reefer truck capacity, freezer load calculations)
- **Packaging selection** (MAP вҖ” Modified Atmosphere Packaging for SP foods; vacuum for perishables)
- **HACCP plan development** (Critical Control Points differ for P vs SP vs NP)
- **Food Regulatory Compliance** (FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius shelf-life labelling rules)
- **Warehouse zoning** in food industry (separate P, SP, NP storage zones to prevent cross-contamination)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” QвӮҒвӮҖ Shelf-Life Acceleration Equation

**Statement:** A food product has a shelf life of $t_{s,T_0}$ days at a reference temperature $T_0$ (В°C). Estimate the shelf life at any other storage temperature $T$ using the $Q_{10}$ concept.

**Given:** $t_{s,T_0}$, $T_0$, $T$, $Q_{10}$ (typically 2 for most foods).

**Derivation:**

Most deteriorative reactions in foods (microbial growth, lipid oxidation, enzymatic browning) approximately double their rate for every 10 В°C rise in temperature. This is captured by the $Q_{10}$ factor:

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

Since the reaction rate is inversely proportional to shelf life ($k \propto 1/t_s$):

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{t_{s,1}}{t_{s,2}} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

Rearranging for $t_{s,2}$ (shelf life at temperature $T$):

$$t_{s,T} = t_{s,T_0} \cdot Q_{10}^{\frac{T_0 - T}{10}}$$

**This is the key KTU 2024 formula for shelf-life prediction under non-isothermal conditions.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Worked Numerical Example (QвӮҒвӮҖ Method)

**Problem:** Fresh milk has a shelf life of **7 days** at **4 В°C**. Assuming $Q_{10} = 2.0$, estimate:
(a) Shelf life at 14 В°C (typical home refrigerator abuse temperature).
(b) Shelf life at вҖ“18 В°C (frozen storage, but using the same model).

**Solution:**

We are given:
- $t_{s,T_0} = 7$ days
- $T_0 = 4$ В°C, $T_1 = 14$ В°C, $T_2 = -18$ В°C
- $Q_{10} = 2.0$

**Part (a): At TвӮҒ = 14 В°C**

The temperature difference is $T_0 - T = 4 - 14 = -10$ В°C, meaning the temperature has risen by 10 В°C. Therefore:

$$t_{s,14} = t_{s,4} \cdot Q_{10}^{\frac{4 - 14}{10}} = 7 \cdot 2.0^{\frac{-10}{10}} = 7 \cdot 2.0^{-1}$$

$$t_{s,14} = 7 \times \frac{1}{2.0} = 3.5 \text{ days}$$

**Interpretation:** Raising the storage temperature from 4 В°C to 14 В°C *halves* the shelf life of milk вҖ” a 10 В°C rise doubles the spoilage rate.

**Part (b): At TвӮӮ = вҖ“18 В°C**

$$t_{s,-18} = 7 \cdot 2.0^{\frac{4 - (-18)}{10}} = 7 \cdot 2.0^{\frac{22}{10}} = 7 \cdot 2.0^{2.2}$$

$$2.0^{2.2} = \exp(2.2 \cdot \ln 2) = \exp(2.2 \times 0.6931) = \exp(1.5249) = 4.595$$

$$t_{s,-18} = 7 \times 4.595 \approx 32.2 \text{ days}$$

**Engineering Note:** The $Q_{10}$ model is a *rough* estimate for freezing because the dominant mechanism shifts from microbial to physical/chemical. In real frozen storage, milk can last 3вҖ“6 months; the model gives a conservative lower bound.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Water Activity of a Sodium Chloride Solution (Norrish Equation)

**Statement:** Calculate the water activity of a brine solution containing 10 % w/w NaCl.

**Given:** Mass of NaCl $m_s = 10$ g; mass of water $m_w = 90$ g; Molecular weight of NaCl $M_s = 58.44$ g/mol; Molecular weight of water $M_w = 18.02$ g/mol; $K$ constant for NaCl вүҲ 1.825.

**Step 1: Calculate mole fractions.**

$$n_s = \frac{m_s}{M_s} = \frac{10}{58.44} = 0.1711 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_w = \frac{m_w}{M_w} = \frac{90}{18.02} = 4.994 \text{ mol}$$

$$X_w = \frac{n_w}{n_w + n_s} = \frac{4.994}{4.994 + 0.1711} = \frac{4.994}{5.165} = 0.9669$$

$$X_s = 1 - X_w = 0.0331$$

**Step 2: Apply the Norrish equation.**

$$a_w = X_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$$

$$a_w = 0.9669 \cdot \exp(-1.825 \cdot (0.0331)^2)$$

$$(0.0331)^2 = 0.001096$$

$$1.825 \times 0.001096 = 0.002000$$

$$\exp(-0.002000) \approx 0.9980$$

$$a_w = 0.9669 \times 0.9980 = 0.9650$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A 10 % NaCl brine has $a_w \approx 0.965$, which is just *above* the bacterial growth threshold (0.91) but *below* the *Staphylococcus aureus* halophilic limit. This is why 10 % brine is *not* enough to fully preserve fish; commercial salt-curing uses 15вҖ“25 % salt to push $a_w$ below 0.85.

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive Classification Matrix (KTU High-Yield Reference)

| Food Item | Class | $a_w$ (approx.) | Dominant Spoilage | Storage T (В°C) | Shelf Life | Preservative Method |
|-----------|-------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|------------|--------------------|
| Fresh fish | P | 0.99 | Microbial (psychrophilic bacteria) | 0 вҖ“ 2 | 1вҖ“2 days | Ice/refrigeration |
| Raw milk | P | 0.99 | Microbial (lactic acid bacteria) | 4 | 5вҖ“7 days | Pasteurisation + cold chain |
| Fresh red meat | P | 0.98 | Microbial + oxidation | 0 вҖ“ 4 | 3вҖ“5 days | Vacuum packaging |
| Broccoli | P | 0.98 | Wilting + microbial | 0 | 7вҖ“10 days | MAP + refrigeration |
| Cut watermelon | P | 0.97 | Yeast + mould | 4 | 2вҖ“3 days | Cling-wrap + refrigeration |
| Apples | SP | 0.85 | Enzymatic + mould | 0 вҖ“ 4 | 1вҖ“3 months | CA storage |
| Potatoes | SP | 0.78 | Sprouting + shrivelling | 7вҖ“10 | 2вҖ“4 months | Sprout inhibitors |
| Eggs (in shell) | SP | 0.97 initially | Microbial via pores | 7вҖ“13 | 3вҖ“5 weeks | Oil coating / refrigeration |
| Cheddar cheese | SP | 0.95 вҶ’ 0.85 (ripened) | Mould + enzymatic | 4вҖ“7 | 1вҖ“6 months | Wax coating |
| White bread | SP | 0.94 | Mould (*Rhizopus*) | 21 | 3вҖ“7 days | Sorbic acid / modified atmosphere |
| Honey | NP | 0.55 | None (osmotic) | 20 | Years | Natural (high sugar) |
| White rice (dried) | NP | 0.50 | Insect infestation | 25 | 1вҖ“2 years | Hermetic storage |
| Canned vegetables | NP | 0.97 but sterile | None (sterile) | 25 | 2вҖ“5 years | Retort processing |
| Salted crackers | NP | 0.30 | None | 25 | 6вҖ“12 months | Low $a_w$ + moisture barrier |
| Spray-dried milk powder | NP | 0.20вҖ“0.30 | Lipid oxidation only | 20 | 1вҖ“2 years | NвӮӮ flushing |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Raw or Processed Food] --> B{Measure Water Activity aw}
    B -->|aw > 0.85| C{High Moisture AND High pH > 4.6?}
    B -->|0.60 <= aw <= 0.85| D{Low to Moderate Microbial Risk}
    B -->|aw < 0.60| E[NON-PERISHABLE Shelf-Stable]

    C -->|YES| F[PERISHABLE Refrigeration Required]
    C -->|NO Acidic pH less than 4.6| G[SEMI-PERISHABLE Acidic Stability]

    D --> H{Is Processing Applied?}
    H -->|Pasteurised or Sterile| I[SEMI-PERISHABLE Extended Cold Storage]
    H -->|None RAW| J[SEMI-PERISHABLE Cool Storage 7 to 15 C]

    F --> K[Shelf Life 1 to 7 days at 0 to 4 C]
    G --> L[Shelf Life 2 weeks to 3 months]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    E --> M[Shelf Life 6 months to 5 plus years at 20 to 30 C]

    K --> N[Preserve by Cold Chain Freezing Blanching]
    L --> O[Preserve by MAP Wax Coating CA Storage]
    M --> P[Preserve by Drying Canning Salting Sugaring]
```

### 4.2 Shelf Life vs Water Activity вҖ” Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Food] -->|Water Removal| B[Drying]
    A -->|Sugar Addition| C[Jam]
    A -->|Salt Addition| D[Salted Meat]
    A -->|Heat Sterilisation| E[Canned Food]

    B --> F[aw drops below 0.60]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> G[Sterile but aw unchanged]

    F --> H[Non-Perishable Shelf Life Years]
    G --> H

    A2[Fresh Food] -->|Refrigeration 4 C| I[aw unchanged at 0.97]
    A2 -->|Modified Atmosphere| J[O2 reduced]

    I --> K[Perishable Shelf Life days to weeks]
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree вҖ” KTU Examination Quick Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Question: Classify Food X} --> R1{Can it be stored at 25 C for more than 6 months without spoilage?}
    R1 -->|YES| S1[NON-PERISHABLE]
    R1 -->|NO| R2{Does it spoil in less than 7 days at 4 C?}
    R2 -->|YES| S2[PERISHABLE]
    R2 -->|NO| S3[SEMI-PERISHABLE]
    S3 --> R3{Is it fresh fruit vegetable or cheese?}
    R3 -->|YES| S3
    R3 -->|NO| S2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1 (вҖ“2 marks):** Students state *"high moisture = perishable"* without mentioning $a_w$. KTU 2024 key explicitly requires the $a_w$ range in any classification justification.
> - **Pitfall 2 (вҖ“2 marks):** Confusing "semi-perishable" with "non-perishable" for items like honey or salt. These are *non-perishable* because $a_w < 0.60$ or $a_w \approx 0.55$ (honey) вҖ” they are NOT "moderately perishable."
> - **Pitfall 3 (вҖ“3 marks):** Listing preservation methods without linking them to the *specific* spoilage mechanism they target. Always state the mechanismвҖ“method pairing: *"Refrigeration (0вҖ“4 В°C) inhibits psychrotrophic bacterial growth in fresh fish."*
> - **Pitfall 4 (вҖ“2 marks):** Writing $Q_{10} = 3$ for all foods. $Q_{10}$ is *food-specific*: ~2 for most spoilage, ~2.5 for lipid oxidation, ~3вҖ“4 for enzymatic browning. Quote a justified range.

---

### PART A вҖ” Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Classify the following foods as Perishable (P), Semi-Perishable (SP), or Non-Perishable (NP) and justify in one line each:**
(a) Fresh pasteurised milk
(b) Table salt
(c) Whole wheat flour (dried, 12 % moisture)
(d) Ripe banana
(e) Cheddar cheese
(f) Honey

**Model Answer:**

| Item | Class | Justification (one line) |
|------|-------|--------------------------|
| (a) Fresh pasteurised milk | **P** | $a_w \approx 0.99$ and pH $\approx 6.7$ вҖ” supports rapid psychrotrophic and mesophilic bacterial growth; shelf life ~5вҖ“7 days at 4 В°C |
| (b) Table salt | **NP** | $a_w \approx 0.75$ (NaCl crystals with surface moisture only) and chemically stable; indefinite shelf life at ambient |
| (c) Whole wheat flour | **NP** (technically SP if moisture > 14 %) | $a_w \approx 0.50$вҖ“$0.65$ at 12 % moisture; below mould growth threshold; can be stored 6вҖ“12 months |
| (d) Ripe banana | **P** | $a_w \approx 0.98$, high moisture, active enzymatic and microbial activity; shelf life 3вҖ“7 days at 13 В°C |
| (e) Cheddar cheese | **SP** | $a_w$ reduces from ~0.95 (fresh) to ~0.85 (ripened) due to moisture loss and salt-in; shelf life 1вҖ“6 months at 4вҖ“7 В°C |
| (f) Honey | **NP** | $a_w \approx 0.55$ due to high fructose/glucose content (osmotic pressure inhibits microbes); shelf life several years |

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024, Model Paper]
**Define water activity. Why is $a_w$ a better indicator of perishability than moisture content?**

**Model Answer:**

Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

It is a *better* indicator than moisture content because:
1. It measures *available* (free) water for microbial/enzymatic reactions, not *total* water. Two foods with identical moisture content can have very different $a_w$ (e.g., a fresh fruit and a jam both at 60 % moisture have $a_w$ of 0.98 and 0.80 respectively).
2. Microbial growth is controlled by $a_w$, not by moisture %. *Staphylococcus aureus* grows at $a_w = 0.86$ irrespective of the food's moisture content.
3. $a_w$ directly determines the *type* of spoilage organism (bacteria > 0.91, yeasts > 0.88, moulds > 0.80, xerophiles > 0.60), enabling engineers to predict shelf life.

---

### PART B вҖ” Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a clear classification table, describe the three categories of foods based on shelf life вҖ” Perishable, Semi-Perishable, and Non-Perishable. For each category, give **two examples**, state the **typical $a_w$ range**, and identify the **dominant spoilage mechanism**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A pasteurised milk sample has a shelf life of **6 days at 4 В°C**. Using the $Q_{10}$ model (assume $Q_{10} = 2.0$), calculate the new shelf life if the milk is accidentally stored at:
(i) 14 В°C
(ii) 24 В°C
Comment on the engineering significance of your results for the cold-chain design of dairy products.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) вҖ” Classification Table [7 marks]**

| Category | Definition | $a_w$ Range | Dominant Spoilage | Example 1 | Example 2 | Typical Storage |
|----------|------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Perishable** | Spoils within 1вҖ“7 days at refrigeration | $a_w > 0.85$ | Microbial (bacteria, yeasts) | Fresh fish | Raw milk | 0вҖ“4 В°C, high humidity |
| **Semi-Perishable** | Spoils within weeks to months | $0.60 \le a_w \le 0.85$ | Mould + enzymatic + slow microbial | Apples | Cheddar cheese | 7вҖ“13 В°C, CA storage |
| **Non-Perishable** | Stable for months to years at ambient | $a_w < 0.60$ or sterile | Negligible / lipid oxidation | Honey | Dried rice | 20вҖ“30 В°C, dry |

(1 mark for definition of each category Г— 3 = 3 marks; 1 mark for correct $a_w$ range Г— 3 = 3 marks; 1 mark overall for the summary row of dominant spoilage.)

**Part (b) вҖ” $Q_{10}$ Calculations [7 marks]**

Given: $t_{s,4} = 6$ days, $Q_{10} = 2.0$, $T_0 = 4$ В°C.

The governing equation is:

$$t_{s,T} = t_{s,T_0} \cdot Q_{10}^{\frac{T_0 - T}{10}}$$

**Sub-question (i): T = 14 В°C**

$$t_{s,14} = 6 \cdot 2.0^{\frac{4 - 14}{10}} = 6 \cdot 2.0^{-1.0} = 6 \cdot 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ days}$$

**Sub-question (ii): T = 24 В°C**

$$t_{s,24} = 6 \cdot 2.0^{\frac{4 - 24}{10}} = 6 \cdot 2.0^{-2.0} = 6 \cdot 0.25 = 1.5 \text{ days}$$

**Engineering Significance [2 marks]:**

The results demonstrate that the **cold chain is non-negotiable** for dairy. A mere 10 В°C rise (4 вҶ’ 14 В°C) halves the shelf life; a 20 В°C rise (4 вҶ’ 24 В°C) reduces it by 75 %. In a country like Kerala with ambient temperatures of 28вҖ“35 В°C, an unrefrigerated milk consignment will spoil in *under one day*, posing serious public-health (and economic) risks.

**Engineering design implications:**
- Reefer trucks must maintain **T вүӨ 4 В°C continuously** with вүӨ 2 В°C deviation tolerance.
- The last-mile delivery in Kerala's hot climate requires insulated boxes with phase-change material (PCM) packs.
- HTST pasteurisation alone is insufficient вҖ” it must be **paired with unbroken cold chain** to realise the 6-day shelf life.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of **water activity ($a_w$)** in determining the shelf life of foods. With the help of a labelled sketch, describe how the rate of microbial growth varies with $a_w$, and list the minimum $a_w$ requirements for the growth of (i) spoilage bacteria, (ii) yeasts, (iii) moulds, and (iv) xerophilic moulds.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 100 g sample of salted fish contains 20 g NaCl and 60 g water. Using the Norrish equation with $K = 1.825$, estimate its water activity and classify it. State the **three** principal preservation mechanisms achieved by salting in fish.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) вҖ” Water Activity and Microbial Growth [7 marks]**

Water activity ($a_w$) is the most critical intrinsic parameter governing microbial growth in foods. Microorganisms require *available* water for metabolism, and below certain $a_w$ thresholds, they cannot grow.

**Labelled sketch of microbial growth rate vs $a_w$:** *(KTU accepts any text-based sketch; the verbal description is reproduced below)*

> Growth rate (log CFU/day) rises sharply above each microorganism's minimum $a_w$, plateaus, and then declines at very high $a_w$ (osmotic stress).
> 
> - Bacteria: minimum $a_w$ вүҲ **0.91** (range 0.91вҖ“0.99)
> - Yeasts: minimum $a_w$ вүҲ **0.88** (range 0.88вҖ“0.94)
> - Moulds: minimum $a_w$ вүҲ **0.80** (range 0.80вҖ“0.94)
> - Xerophilic moulds: minimum $a_w$ вүҲ **0.60** (range 0.60вҖ“0.75)

(2 marks for the qualitative description; 1 mark each for the four $a_w$ thresholds Г— 4 = 4 marks; 1 mark for the synthesis conclusion.)

**Synthesis:** Foods with $a_w < 0.60$ are microbiologically stable; this is the engineering basis of drying, salting, and sugar preservation.

**Part (b) вҖ” Salted Fish Water Activity [7 marks]**

Given: $m_{NaCl} = 20$ g, $m_{water} = 60$ g, $M_{NaCl} = 58.44$ g/mol, $M_w = 18.02$ g/mol, $K = 1.825$.

**Step 1: Moles of solute and water.**

$$n_s = \frac{20}{58.44} = 0.3423 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_w = \frac{60}{18.02} = 3.330 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Mole fractions.**

$$X_w = \frac{3.330}{3.330 + 0.3423} = \frac{3.330}{3.672} = 0.9068$$

$$X_s = 1 - 0.9068 = 0.0932$$

**Step 3: Norrish equation.**

$$a_w = X_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$$

$$X_s^2 = (0.0932)^2 = 0.008686$$

$$K \cdot X_s^2 = 1.825 \times 0.008686 = 0.01585$$

$$\exp(-0.01585) \approx 0.9843$$

$$a_w = 0.9068 \times 0.9843 = 0.8925$$

**Classification:** $a_w \approx 0.89$ places salted fish in the **Semi-Perishable (SP)** category вҖ” it is below the bacterial threshold (0.91) but above the mould threshold (0.80). It must be stored cool and dry.

**Three preservation mechanisms of salting:**

1. **Osmotic dehydration** вҖ” Salt draws water out of fish cells and out of microbial cells (plasmolysis), lowering $a_w$.
2. **Direct ionic toxicity** вҖ” High ClвҒ» concentration disrupts microbial enzyme systems and protein function.
3. **Oxygen exclusion at brine surface** вҖ” Salted (submerged) fish has limited dissolved OвӮӮ, suppressing aerobic spoilage.

(2 marks for $a_w$ calculation; 1 mark for classification; 4 marks вҖ” 1.3 marks each вҖ” for the three preservation mechanisms.)

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Points for KTU 2024:**

- [ ] **Three classes:** P (вүӨ 7 d), SP (weeksвҖ“months), NP (monthsвҖ“years).
- [ ] **Key $a_w$ thresholds** (memorise in order): Bacteria **0.91** > Yeasts **0.88** > Moulds **0.80** > Xerophiles **0.60**.
- [ ] **$a_w$ definition:** $a_w = p / p_0$ вҖ” ratio of partial to saturation vapour pressure.
- [ ] **$a_w \ne$ moisture %:** Quote $a_w$ in classification answers, not moisture content.
- [ ] **Norrish equation:** $a_w = X_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$ for sugar/salt solutions.
- [ ] **$Q_{10}$ shelf-life formula:** $t_{s,T} = t_{s,T_0} \cdot Q_{10}^{(T_0 - T)/10}$.
- [ ] **Typical $Q_{10}$ = 2.0** for most foods (memorise).
- [ ] **Arrhenius:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ for activation energy.
- [ ] **High-acid foods (pH < 4.6):** can be semi-perishable even with high moisture (e.g., pickles).
- [ ] **Honey is NP** because $a_w \approx 0.55$, not because of "sugar content" alone вҖ” the *physics* is osmotic suppression of $a_w$.
- [ ] **Canned foods** are NP due to *sterility*, not $a_w$ (their $a_w$ may be high but microbes are absent).
- [ ] **Cold-chain temperatures:** 0вҖ“4 В°C refrigeration; вүӨ вҖ“18 В°C frozen; 7вҖ“13 В°C for SP cool storage.
- [ ] **Dried foods are NP** when $a_w < 0.60$ (e.g., milk powder, instant noodles, spices).
- [ ] **For 14-mark KTU answers:** Always pair (mechanism + method) and (parameter + threshold) in your justifications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non Refrigerated and Refrigerated Transportation; Precooling - Room Cooling, Forced Air Cooling, Package Icing, Hydro Cooling, Vacuum Cooling, Evaporative Cooling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non Refrigerated and Refrigerated Transportation; Precooling - Room Cooling, Forced Air Cooling, Package Icing, Hydro Cooling, Vacuum Cooling, Evaporative Cooling

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transportation of Perishable Foods вҖ” Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Transportation of food** refers to the movement of perishable and semi-perishable food products from the point of production (farm/factory) through the cold chain to the point of consumption, while maintaining product quality, safety, and shelf life. It is broadly classified into **Non-Refrigerated Transportation** (ambient chain) and **Refrigerated Transportation** (cold chain logistics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Transportation is the *most vulnerable* link in the cold chain because the produce is in bulk, transit times are unpredictable, and temperature control is often lost. A 1-hour delay in cooling can shorten shelf life by **1 day**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of transporting fresh produce like transporting a **live patient in an ambulance**. If you just put them in a normal car (non-refrigerated), their condition deteriorates. But in an ambulance with controlled temperature, oxygen, and monitoring (refrigerated transport with proper precooling), they survive longer. Similarly, fresh fruits, vegetables, dairy, and meat are "living" (still respiring) and need temperature-controlled "ambulances" to reach the consumer alive and fresh.

## 1.2 Non-Refrigerated Transportation

### Definition
**Non-refrigerated transportation** is the carriage of food products at ambient (surrounding) temperature using ordinary trucks, wagons, ships, or aircraft **without any active temperature-control system**. It relies solely on ambient conditions, ventilation, and insulation.

### Common Methods
| Method | Typical Use | Distance |
|---|---|---|
| Open trucks / tractor-trailers | Potatoes, onions, garlic, grains | Short to medium |
| Railway wagons (boxcars) | Grains, pulses, canned goods | Long distance |
| General cargo ships | Dry, stable commodities | International |
| Non-AC air freight | Dry, shelf-stable items | Any |

### Suitable Products
- **Dry commodities**: cereals, pulses, oilseeds, sugar, salt
- **Hard fruits & tubers**: potatoes, onions, garlic, winter squash, mature bananas
- **Shelf-stable processed foods**: canned goods, dried fruits, packaged snacks
- **Nuts and spices**: with low moisture content (<12%)

### Limiting Factors
- **Field Heat** (residual heat at harvest): measured as **Heat of Respiration (HR)** in **kJ/kgВ·h** or **mW/kg**.
- **Ambient temperature swings**: in tropical India, can reach **45В°C**.
- **Transit time**: longer transit = greater quality loss.

### Key Principle
> [!NOTE]
> Non-refrigerated transport works on the principle of **inherent product stability** вҖ” only products with low respiration rates, low moisture, and high resistance to microbial spoilage can tolerate ambient conditions.

## 1.3 Refrigerated Transportation (Cold Chain Logistics)

### Definition
**Refrigerated transportation** is the carriage of perishable foods in insulated and temperature-controlled vehicles/containers equipped with mechanical refrigeration (vapor compression cycle), cryogenic cooling (liquid nitrogen / COвӮӮ), or eutectic plates.

### Classification
1. **Refrigerated Trucks (Reefers)** вҖ” road transport
2. **Refrigerated Railway Wagons** вҖ” rail transport
3. **Reefer Containers (ISO 20'/40')** вҖ” intermodal (sea + road + rail)
4. **Refrigerated Ships (Reefer Vessels)** вҖ” sea transport
5. **Air Freight with Cool Containers (LD3, Envirotainer)** вҖ” high-value, time-critical
6. **Cryogenic Tankers** вҖ” liquid milk, juice concentrates

### Three Pillars of Refrigerated Transport
> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Pre-cooling** before loading (mandatory!)
> 2. **Insulated enclosure** (PUF/PIR foam with K-value < 0.025 W/mВ·K)
> 3. **Active refrigeration unit** maintaining set-point temperature (typically **0В°C to 4В°C** for chilled, **-18В°C** for frozen)

## 1.4 Precooling вҖ” The Critical First Step

### Formal Definition
**Precooling** is the *rapid removal of field heat* (the heat present in freshly harvested produce due to solar radiation, metabolic respiration, and handling) from a commodity immediately after harvest, before it enters the cold chain (storage, transport, or marketing).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine coming out of a hot summer day into an air-conditioned room вҖ” your body needs to "cool down" before you feel comfortable. Precooling does the **same for fruits and vegetables**. If you don't "cool down" the produce first, even the best refrigerated truck will struggle because it has to fight the enormous heat load (sometimes **80вҖ“100 kJ/kg**) carried by warm produce.

### Why Precooling is Essential?
- Reduces **respiration rate** (lowering COвӮӮ and heat output)
- Inhibits **microbial growth** and enzymatic activity
- Reduces **water loss / shriveling** (weight loss)
- Extends **shelf life** by **2x to 4x**
- Maintains **firmness, color, and nutritional quality**
- Prepares product for uniform cold storage

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Rule of Thumb (KTU):** The "**3-T Principle**" вҖ” **Temperature, Time, Tolerance**. The longer a product stays at field temperature, the shorter its market life.

### Classification of Precooling Methods

| Method | Heat Removal Medium | Typical Cooling Time | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Room Cooling | Still cold air | 12вҖ“24 h | Low-value, slow-cooling produce |
| Forced-Air Cooling | High-velocity cold air | 1вҖ“4 h | Most fruits & vegetables |
| Package Icing | Crushed/loose ice | 1вҖ“2 h | Leafy greens, broccoli, fish |
| Hydro Cooling | Cold water | 10вҖ“60 min | Root vegetables, sweet corn |
| Vacuum Cooling | Evaporation under vacuum | 20вҖ“30 min | Leafy vegetables |
| Evaporative Cooling | Natural/forced evaporation | Hours | Tropical regions, storage |

### Heat Load Equation (Fundamental)
The total heat to be removed during precooling:
$$Q_{total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m_{resp} \cdot H_r \cdot t + Q_{evap}$$

Where:
- $Q_{total}$ = total heat to be removed (kJ)
- $m$ = mass of produce (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of produce (kJ/kgВ·K) вҖ” typically **3.5вҖ“4.0 kJ/kgВ·K** for fresh produce
- $\Delta T = T_{initial} - T_{final}$ (K)
- $m_{resp}$ = mass of respiring product
- $H_r$ = heat of respiration (kJ/kgВ·h)
- $t$ = cooling time (h)
- $Q_{evap}$ = heat removed by evaporation (kJ)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Non-Refrigerated Transportation вҖ” Detailed Analysis

### Operational Steps (Bullet Logic)
- **Step 1 вҖ” Product Selection:** Only commodities with low respiration rate ($H_r < 10$ mW/kg) and low water activity ($a_w < 0.65$) qualify.
- **Step 2 вҖ” Cleaning & Sorting:** Remove damaged, diseased units to prevent chain spoilage.
- **Step 3 вҖ” Curing (where applicable):** E.g., potatoes, onions, garlic are cured to heal wounds.
- **Step 4 вҖ” Packaging:** Use ventilated jute bags, wooden crates, or mesh sacks allowing air circulation.
- **Step 5 вҖ” Loading:** Stacked with ventilation channels; never airtight.
- **Step 6 вҖ” Transit:** Subject to ambient temperature; rely on **night loading** in tropical climates to reduce initial heat load.
- **Step 7 вҖ” Unloading:** Direct dispatch to market or short-term storage.

### Why It Works
> [!NOTE]
> The non-refrigerated chain works because the products chosen have **low metabolic activity** and **high tissue integrity**. E.g., onions and garlic are naturally dormant; grains have nearly zero respiration.

### Limitations
- High post-harvest loss (often **20вҖ“40%** in India)
- No microbial control
- No quality preservation beyond intrinsic shelf life
- Highly weather-dependent

## 2.2 Refrigerated Transportation вҖ” Detailed Analysis

### Vapor Compression Refrigeration (VCR) System in Reefers
A typical reefer unit uses the **Rankine cycle**:
$$\text{Compressor} \rightarrow \text{Condenser} \rightarrow \text{Expansion Valve} \rightarrow \text{Evaporator}$$

Common refrigerants: **R-404A**, **R-134a**, **R-507A** (low-GWP transition to **R-452A**, **R-744 (COвӮӮ)**).

### Critical Operating Parameters
- **Set-point temperature**: chilled **0вҖ“4В°C**, frozen **-18 to -25В°C**
- **Relative Humidity**: **85вҖ“95%** to prevent desiccation
- **Air circulation rate**: **100вҖ“200 CFM per ton** of product
- **Fresh air exchange**: **15 mВі/h** for produce (to supply OвӮӮ and remove COвӮӮ / ethylene)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precooling before loading rule:** Product must be cooled to within **2вҖ“3В°C** of transport set-point **before** being placed in the reefer. Otherwise, the refrigeration unit will overload, fail to reach set-point, and cause rapid spoilage.

### Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) in Transport
Some reefers use **CA (Controlled Atmosphere)**:
- OвӮӮ reduced to **2вҖ“5%**
- COвӮӮ elevated to **3вҖ“10%**
- NвӮӮ as balance

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Precooling Methods

### 2.3.1 Room Cooling

**Principle:** Cold air is released into an insulated room containing stacked produce. Natural convection slowly removes heat.

**Why "Slow" Cooling?**
- Air has **low thermal conductivity** (~0.026 W/mВ·K)
- Cold air tends to stratify (sink while warm air rises)
- Produce boxes/pallets block airflow

**Cooling Rate Expression (Newton's Law of Cooling):**
$$T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) \cdot e^{-k t}$$

Where:
- $T(t)$ = product temperature at time $t$ (В°C)
- $T_a$ = air temperature (В°C)
- $T_0$ = initial product temperature (В°C)
- $k$ = cooling rate constant (1/h), depends on heat transfer coefficient $h$
- $t$ = elapsed time (h)

**Half-Cooling Time:**
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

### 2.3.2 Forced-Air Cooling (Pressure Cooling)

**Principle:** A fan creates a pressure difference across stacked produce, drawing cold air through the product rather than around it. This multiplies the effective surface area exposed to cold air by **5вҖ“10x**.

**Cooling Rate (modified Newton's Law for forced convection):**
$$\frac{T(t) - T_a}{T_0 - T_a} = e^{-\left(\frac{hA}{m c_p}\right) t}$$

Where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/mВІВ·K) вҖ” typically **20вҖ“50 W/mВІВ·K** for forced air vs **5вҖ“10** for room cooling
- $A$ = effective surface area exposed (mВІ)
- $m$ = mass (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kgВ·K)

**Two Configurations:**
1. **Tunnel System** вҖ” produce stacked in rows, fan at one end
2. **Cold Wall** вҖ” wall-mounted fans pull air through pallets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Forced-air cooling reduces cooling time from **24 hours** (room cooling) to **1вҖ“4 hours** вҖ” a **6вҖ“10x improvement**.

### 2.3.3 Package Icing (Top Icing / Liquid Icing)

**Principle:** Crushed ice (top icing) or liquid ice slurry (liquid icing) is placed in direct contact with packaged produce. Ice melts at **0В°C**, absorbing latent heat:
$$Q_{ice} = m_{ice} \cdot L_f$$

Where $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion of water = **334 kJ/kg**.

**Cooling Energy:**
The heat removed per kg of ice:
$$Q = 334 + c_{water} \cdot \Delta T \approx 334 \text{ kJ/kg (when produce is at field temperature)}$$

**Ice Requirement Calculation:**
$$m_{ice} = \frac{m_{product} \cdot c_{p,prod} \cdot (T_0 - T_{final})}{334}$$

**Where Used:** Broccoli, cauliflower, leafy greens, green onions, melons, fresh fish, poultry.

### 2.3.4 Hydro Cooling

**Principle:** Produce is immersed in or sprayed with chilled water (typically **0вҖ“2В°C**). Water has thermal conductivity **~0.6 W/mВ·K** and high specific heat (**4.186 kJ/kgВ·K**), making it **15вҖ“20x more efficient** than air.

**Heat Transfer Equation:**
$$Q = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,in} - T_{w,out})$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_w$ = mass flow rate of water (kg/s)
- $c_{p,w}$ = specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/kgВ·K**
- $T_{w,in}$, $T_{w,out}$ = water inlet/outlet temperatures (В°C)

**Cooling Rate:**
$$\frac{T(t) - T_w}{T_0 - T_w} = e^{-\left(\frac{U A}{m c_p}\right) t}$$

Where $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/mВІВ·K); for hydrocooling, $U$ = **200вҖ“500 W/mВІВ·K**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanitation Risk:** Hydrocooling water can spread pathogens (e.g., *Listeria*, *E. coli*). Water must be chlorinated (**50вҖ“200 ppm free chlorine**) and recirculated.

### 2.3.5 Vacuum Cooling

**Principle:** Produce is placed in a sealed chamber; pressure is reduced to **~4.6 mmHg (612 Pa)**. At this pressure, water boils at **0В°C**. As water evaporates from the product surface, it absorbs latent heat:
$$Q_{vacuum} = m_{evap} \cdot L_v$$

Where $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization of water = **~2,460 kJ/kg** at 0В°C.

**Boiling Point vs Pressure:**
| Pressure (mmHg) | Boiling Point (В°C) |
|---|---|
| 760 | 100 |
| 100 | 51.7 |
| 30 | 28.9 |
| **4.6** | **0** |
| 2.5 | -10 |

**Mass of Water Lost:**
Each 1% moisture loss removes **~24.6 kJ/kg** of heat.

**Cooling Time:** **20вҖ“30 minutes** for most leafy produce.

**Applications:** Lettuce, spinach, mushrooms, celery, endive.

### 2.3.6 Evaporative Cooling

**Principle:** Warm, dry air is passed through a wet porous material (charcoal, sand, jute). Water evaporates into the air, cooling it. The cooled air then passes through the stored produce.

**Psychrometric Equation:**
$$T_{db,final} = T_{db,initial} - \left(\frac{L_v}{c_{p,air} \cdot \rho_{air}}\right) \cdot (W_s - W)$$

Where:
- $T_{db}$ = dry-bulb temperature (В°C)
- $W_s$ = humidity ratio at saturation (kg water/kg dry air)
- $W$ = humidity ratio of inlet air (kg water/kg dry air)
- $\rho_{air}$ = density of air (**~1.2 kg/mВі**)

**Effectiveness Range:** Reduces temperature by **5вҖ“15В°C** when ambient RH is below **40%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Best suited for:** Arid/semi-arid regions (Rajasthan, Gujarat, parts of Africa). In humid regions (>70% RH), effectiveness drops sharply.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a)e^{-kt}$ | $k$ in 1/h |
| 2 | Half-Cooling Time | $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ | hours |
| 3 | 7/8 Cooling Time | $t_{7/8} = \ln 8 / k = 2.08/k$ | вүҲ 3 Г— $t_{1/2}$ |
| 4 | Specific Heat of Produce | $c_p \approx 3.35 + 0.84 \cdot W$ | kJ/kgВ·K, $W$ = moisture fraction |
| 5 | Heat of Respiration | $H_r$ | kJ/kgВ·h or mW/kg |
| 6 | Latent Heat of Ice | $L_f = 334$ | kJ/kg |
| 7 | Latent Heat of Vaporization | $L_v = 2{,}460$ at 0В°C | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Forced Air Heat Transfer | $Q = hA(T_s - T_a)$ | W, $h$ = 20вҖ“50 W/mВІВ·K |
| 9 | Hydro Cooling Heat Transfer | $Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} \Delta T$ | W, $c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/kgВ·K |
| 10 | Vacuum Cooling Mass Loss | $m_{evap} = Q_{total}/L_v$ | kg water/kg produce |
| 11 | Evaporative Cooling Efficiency | $\eta = (T_{in} - T_{out})/(T_{in} - T_{wb})$ | 0 to 1 |
| 12 | Overall Heat Transfer Coef. | $1/U = 1/h_i + \delta/k + 1/h_o$ | W/mВІВ·K |
| 13 | Ice Mass Required | $m_{ice} = m_p c_p \Delta T / 334$ | kg |
| 14 | Cooling Power | $\dot{Q} = m c_p dT/dt$ | W or kJ/h |
| 15 | Biot Number (internal resistance) | $Bi = h L_c / k_{prod}$ | Dimensionless |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility / Real-World Application

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| Export of grapes, mangoes (India) | Forced-air precooling + reefer container |
| Cut flower export (Kerala, Karnataka) | Forced-air + package icing |
| Marine fish catch (Kochi, Mangalore) | Package icing + refrigerated truck |
| Hydroponic leafy greens (Europe) | Vacuum cooling |
| Onion storage (Maharashtra) | Evaporative + ambient storage |
| Frozen seafood export | Blast freezing + reefer at -20В°C |
| Dairy cold chain (Amul, Mother Dairy) | Insulated tankers + reefers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Law of Cooling for Precooling

### Starting Point вҖ” Energy Balance on a Product Element

The rate of heat removal from a product by convection is given by Newton's law:
$$\dot{Q} = h A (T - T_a)$$

The rate of heat storage in the product is:
$$\dot{Q}_{stored} = m c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

Setting heat removal = decrease in stored heat:
$$-m c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T - T_a)$$

### Separating Variables
$$\frac{dT}{T - T_a} = -\frac{h A}{m c_p} dt$$

### Integrating from $T_0$ (at $t = 0$) to $T(t)$ at time $t$
$$\int_{T_0}^{T(t)} \frac{dT}{T - T_a} = -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\left[ \ln(T - T_a) \right]_{T_0}^{T(t)} = -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{T(t) - T_a}{T_0 - T_a}\right) = -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \cdot t$$

### Final Equation
$$\boxed{T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) \cdot e^{-\frac{hA}{mc_p}t}}$$

Defining cooling rate constant:
$$k = \frac{hA}{m c_p}$$

We recover the standard form:
$$T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) \cdot e^{-kt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** $k$ has units of 1/time. The larger the surface area $A$ and heat transfer coefficient $h$ (forced convection > natural), the faster $k$ and the quicker cooling.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 вҖ” Cooling Time Calculation

**Problem:** A batch of strawberries (mass = **100 kg**, $c_p$ = **3.9 kJ/kgВ·K**) is cooled from **28В°C** (field temp) to **5В°C** in a forced-air cooler. Air temperature is **2В°C**, $hA/mc_p$ = **0.8 /h**. Find (a) cooling time to reach 5В°C, (b) half-cooling time.

### Solution

#### (a) Time to Cool to 5В°C

Using:
$$T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) e^{-kt}$$

Substitute values: $T(t) = 5В°C$, $T_a = 2В°C$, $T_0 = 28В°C$, $k = 0.8 /h$:

$$5 = 2 + (28 - 2) e^{-0.8 t}$$

$$3 = 26 \cdot e^{-0.8 t}$$

$$e^{-0.8 t} = \frac{3}{26} = 0.1154$$

Taking natural log:
$$-0.8 t = \ln(0.1154) = -2.160$$

$$t = \frac{2.160}{0.8} = 2.70 \text{ hours}$$

#### (b) Half-Cooling Time
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.8} = 0.866 \text{ h} \approx 52 \text{ minutes}$$

**Interpretation:** Half-cooling time is **52 minutes**, meaning strawberries will be cooled halfway (to about 15В°C) in under an hour вҖ” typical of forced-air cooling.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 вҖ” Ice Requirement for Package Icing

**Problem:** 200 kg of freshly harvested broccoli at **25В°C** must be cooled to **2В°C** using crushed ice at **0В°C**. $c_p$ of broccoli = **3.7 kJ/kgВ·K**. Heat of fusion of ice = **334 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
(a) Heat to be removed
(b) Mass of ice required
(c) Total mass of meltwater produced

### Solution

#### (a) Heat to be Removed

$$Q = m_p c_p \Delta T$$
$$Q = 200 \times 3.7 \times (25 - 2)$$
$$Q = 200 \times 3.7 \times 23$$
$$\boxed{Q = 17{,}020 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### (b) Mass of Ice

$$m_{ice} = \frac{Q}{L_f} = \frac{17{,}020}{334}$$
$$\boxed{m_{ice} \approx 51 \text{ kg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Rule:** Need approximately **0.25 kg of ice per kg of produce** for initial pull-down in package icing.

#### (c) Meltwater Mass

By conservation, meltwater mass вүҲ mass of ice = **51 kg** (assuming all ice melts).

In practice, meltwater drips out of the package and is discarded; the package must be **drain-tolerant**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 вҖ” Vacuum Cooling Time & Water Loss

**Problem:** 500 kg of iceberg lettuce at **20В°C** is vacuum cooled to **1В°C**. Assume initial average $c_p$ = **4.0 kJ/kgВ·K**. Latent heat of vaporization at **0В°C** = **2,460 kJ/kg**.

### Solution

#### Step 1: Heat to Remove
$$Q = 500 \times 4.0 \times (20 - 1) = 500 \times 4.0 \times 19 = 38{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

#### Step 2: Mass of Water Evaporated
$$m_{evap} = \frac{Q}{L_v} = \frac{38{,}000}{2{,}460} = 15.45 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 3: Percentage Weight Loss
$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{15.45}{500} \times 100 = 3.09\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Vacuum cooling causes **1вҖ“3% weight loss** from each pull-down. This must be accounted for in **economic analysis** (price realization).

#### Step 4: Effective Heat of Vaporization
At lower temperatures ($T < 0В°C$ in the later stage), $L_v$ increases (e.g., at $-5В°C$, $L_v \approx 2{,}514$ kJ/kg). A more accurate estimate is **2,510 kJ/kg**, reducing water loss to:
$$m_{evap} = 38{,}000 / 2{,}510 = 15.14 \text{ kg} \approx 3.0\%$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 вҖ” Hydrocooling Time

**Problem:** 1000 kg of sweet corn is hydrocooled from **30В°C** to **5В°C** in **5В°C** water. Overall $U$ = **350 W/mВІВ·K**, effective surface area $A$ = **40 mВІ**. $c_p$ = **3.6 kJ/kgВ·K**.

### Solution

#### Step 1: Time Constant
$$\tau = \frac{m c_p}{U A} = \frac{1000 \times 3600}{350 \times 40}$$

(Converting $c_p$ from kJ to J: $3.6 \times 1000 = 3600$ J/kgВ·K)

$$\tau = \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{14{,}000} = 257.1 \text{ seconds} \approx 4.3 \text{ min}$$

#### Step 2: Time to Reach 5В°C
$$\frac{T(t) - T_w}{T_0 - T_w} = e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$\frac{5 - 5}{30 - 5} = 0 = e^{-t/\tau}$$

Mathematically, infinite time. In practice, target **7/8 cooling time**:
$$t_{7/8} = 3 \tau \approx 12.9 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Hydrocooling is **extremely fast** вҖ” most produce cools in **15вҖ“30 minutes**, ideal for high-throughput operations.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix вҖ” Precooling Methods

| Parameter | Room | Forced-Air | Package Ice | Hydro | Vacuum | Evaporative |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling time | 12вҖ“24 h | 1вҖ“4 h | 1вҖ“2 h | 10вҖ“60 min | 20вҖ“30 min | 5вҖ“15 h |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | Low | Medium-High | High | Low |
| Water contact | No | No | Yes (melt) | Yes | No | Yes |
| Weight loss (%) | 1вҖ“3 | 1вҖ“2 | 0 (ice adds wt) | 0 (water adds) | 3вҖ“5 | 2вҖ“5 |
| Energy intensity | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | Very Low |
| Suitable RH region | All | All | All | All | All | Arid only |
| Best for | Potato, citrus | Most produce | Broccoli, fish | Sweet corn, carrot | Lettuce, mushroom | Tomato, mango |
| Risk of contamination | Low | Low | Low | **High** (chlorinate) | Low | Low (microbial growth in pads) |
| Energy source | Electricity | Electricity | None (ice) | Electricity + refrigeration | Electricity + vacuum pump | None (wind/solar possible) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2023]**
**Q:** Define **precooling**. List any **four methods** of precooling with one example of produce suited to each.

**Model Answer:**

**Precooling** is the rapid removal of field heat from freshly harvested produce before it is stored, transported, or processed, in order to slow respiration, retard microbial growth, and extend shelf life.

| Method | Example Produce |
|---|---|
| Room Cooling | Potatoes, citrus fruits |
| Forced-Air Cooling | Strawberries, grapes |
| Package Icing | Broccoli, leafy greens |
| Hydro Cooling | Sweet corn, carrots |
| Vacuum Cooling | Lettuce, spinach |
| Evaporative Cooling | Tomato, mango (in arid regions) |

(Any four with example = 3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **refrigerated** and **non-refrigerated** transportation of foods. Give two examples of foods for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Non-Refrigerated | Refrigerated |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature control | None (ambient) | Active cooling (0В°C to -25В°C) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Products | Dry, stable (cereals, onion, garlic) | Perishable (fish, milk, fresh produce) |
| Spoilage risk | High for perishables | Low |
| Examples | Wheat, pulses, potato | Fish, meat, dairy, leafy greens |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Module Internal Choice)

### Question A вҖ” Forced-Air Cooling and Hydro Cooling

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** (a) Explain the **principle, procedure, and advantages** of **Forced-Air Cooling**. Derive the expression for cooling time and compute the **half-cooling time** for a batch of grapes (mass 50 kg, $c_p$ = 3.6 kJ/kgВ·K) cooled from 28В°C to 4В°C with air at 0В°C, given $hA/(mc_p)$ = 1.2 /h. **(7 marks)**

(b) Discuss the **principle, types, and sanitation requirements** of **Hydro Cooling**. A 800 kg batch of sweet corn is hydrocooled from 30В°C to 5В°C. Calculate the **water requirement** if inlet water is at 1В°C and outlet at 8В°C. Assume no evaporation losses. Use $c_{p,water}$ = 4.186 kJ/kgВ·K. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Principle:** In forced-air cooling, a fan or blower creates a pressure differential across a stack of produce in boxes/pallets, drawing cold air **through** the product (rather than around it). This dramatically increases the effective heat transfer surface area and convective coefficient.

**Procedure:**
1. Precooled produce is loaded into vented containers/pallets
2. Containers are placed in front of a fan/baffle forming a tunnel
3. Cold air from refrigeration coil is forced horizontally through the produce
4. Cooled air exits on the other side; thermostat controls cycling

**Advantages:**
- Cooling time reduced from **12вҖ“24 h** (room) to **1вҖ“4 h**
- Uniform cooling throughout the load
- Lower product moisture loss than room cooling
- Compatible with standard pallets/boxes

**Derivation:** [Refer Section 3.1]
$$T(t) = T_a + (T_0 - T_a) e^{-kt}, \quad k = \frac{hA}{mc_p}$$

**Half-Cooling Time Calculation:**

Using the exponential decay equation:
$$\frac{T(t_{1/2}) - T_a}{T_0 - T_a} = 0.5$$

$$e^{-k t_{1/2}} = 0.5$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{1.2} = 0.578 \text{ h} \approx 34.7 \text{ minutes}$$

**Verification of full cooling to 4В°C:**
$$\frac{4 - 0}{28 - 0} = 0.143 = e^{-1.2 t}$$
$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.143)}{1.2} = \frac{1.945}{1.2} = 1.62 \text{ h}$$

So grapes reach **4В°C in ~1.6 hours** with forced-air cooling вҖ” about **10x faster** than room cooling. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Principle:** Hydrocooling uses **chilled water** (typically 0вҖ“2В°C) sprayed on or used to immerse produce. Water has a high specific heat (**4.186 kJ/kgВ·K**) and thermal conductivity (**~0.6 W/mВ·K**), giving heat transfer coefficients of **200вҖ“500 W/mВІВ·K** вҖ” much higher than air.

**Types:**
1. **Immersion (Dump) Type** вҖ” produce submerged in tank
2. **Spray (Shower) Type** вҖ” water sprayed over conveyor-borne produce
3. **Flood Type** вҖ” water floods over stationary produce

**Sanitation Requirements:**
- Continuous **chlorination** (50вҖ“200 ppm free chlorine)
- pH maintained at **6.5вҖ“7.5** for chlorine efficacy
- Periodic water change; filtration of debris
- Avoid cross-contamination; use **potable water** initially
- Add fungicides like **fludioxonil** for long transit

**Water Requirement Calculation:**

Heat to be removed from sweet corn:
$$Q = m_p c_{p,p} \Delta T = 800 \times 3.6 \times (30 - 5) = 800 \times 3.6 \times 25 = 72{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

Heat absorbed by water:
$$Q = m_w c_{p,w} \Delta T_w$$

$$72{,}000 = m_w \times 4.186 \times (8 - 1)$$

$$m_w = \frac{72{,}000}{4.186 \times 7} = \frac{72{,}000}{29.30}$$

$$\boxed{m_w \approx 2{,}457 \text{ kg} \approx 2.46 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of water}}$$

**Practical note:** In a continuous system, ~2.5 mВі of chilled water is needed per 800 kg batch, but with **recirculation**, total water consumption is much lower (~10вҖ“20% makeup). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B вҖ” Vacuum Cooling & Evaporative Cooling

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** (a) Explain the **principle of Vacuum Cooling**. Discuss its **advantages, limitations, and applications**. Calculate the **percentage moisture loss** when 400 kg of mushrooms are cooled from 25В°C to 2В°C. Use $c_p$ = 3.9 kJ/kgВ·K, $L_v$ = 2,460 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

(b) Describe the **construction and working** of an **Evaporative Cool Chamber (Zero-Energy Cool Chamber)**. Explain why it is more effective in **arid** regions than humid ones. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Principle:**
At normal atmospheric pressure (760 mmHg), water boils at 100В°C. When pressure is reduced inside a sealed chamber, water's boiling point falls. At a pressure of **~4.6 mmHg (612 Pa)**, water boils at **0В°C**. When produce in such a chamber loses moisture by evaporation, the latent heat of vaporization (**~2,460 kJ/kg**) is drawn from the produce, causing rapid cooling.

**Construction:**
- Airtight steel chamber (vacuum-tight)
- Vacuum pump (steam ejector or mechanical)
- Condenser to trap evaporated water vapor
- Pressure gauges and safety valves
- Loading trolley with pallets

**Procedure:**
1. Load produce into chamber
2. Seal chamber and start vacuum pump
3. Pressure drops to ~4.6 mmHg; water in produce flashes to vapor
4. Latent heat removal cools produce to 1вҖ“2В°C
5. Vent chamber, unload cold produce

**Advantages:**
- Fastest method (**20вҖ“30 min**)
- Uniform cooling (entire product reaches same T)
- No cold-air handling needed; low energy for produce itself
- Excellent for leafy vegetables with high surface:volume ratio

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost (vacuum pump, chamber)
- **3вҖ“5% weight loss** due to evaporation
- Not suitable for dense produce (low surface area)
- Requires periodic maintenance of seals and pumps

**Applications:**
Lettuce, iceberg lettuce, spinach, mushrooms, celery, endive, parsley, cooked rice, baked goods.

**Calculation:**

Heat to be removed:
$$Q = m c_p \Delta T = 400 \times 3.9 \times (25 - 2) = 400 \times 3.9 \times 23 = 35{,}880 \text{ kJ}$$

Mass of water evaporated:
$$m_{evap} = \frac{Q}{L_v} = \frac{35{,}880}{2{,}460} = 14.59 \text{ kg}$$

Percentage moisture loss:
$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{14.59}{400} \times 100 = 3.65\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** Students often forget to use **2,460 kJ/kg** as latent heat at **0В°C**. At lower temperatures, $L_v$ increases slightly, but for exam purposes, use the standard value. Also, remember to express moisture loss as a **percentage of original mass**, not absolute.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Construction of Zero-Energy Cool Chamber (ZECC):**

A ZECC is a double-walled structure, typically made of:
1. **Outer wall**: bricks or concrete
2. **Insulation gap**: 5вҖ“7 cm filled with sand, charcoal, or rice husks
3. **Inner wall**: wire mesh or perforated bricks
4. **Sand bed**: at the bottom, kept constantly wet
5. **Top**: thatched/straw roof for shade and rain protection
6. **Door**: small insulated access door
7. **Drainage**: small outlet for excess water

**Working Principle:**
1. Water from a storage tank seeps into the sand bed (bottom) and wets the porous walls
2. As warm outside air is drawn in (by natural convection/wind), it passes through the wet walls
3. Water evaporates from the walls into the air, absorbing latent heat
4. The cooled, humid air enters the storage chamber
5. The product inside is bathed in cool, humid air, reducing its temperature and respiration

**Why More Effective in Arid Regions?**

The cooling effectiveness of an evaporative cooler depends on the **difference between dry-bulb (DB) and wet-bulb (WB)** temperatures:
$$\Delta T_{cooling} = T_{db} - T_{wb}$$

- **Arid region (RH < 40%):** $T_{db} - T_{wb}$ is large (e.g., 15вҖ“20В°C) вҶ’ significant cooling
- **Humid region (RH > 70%):** $T_{db} - T_{wb}$ is small (e.g., 2вҖ“5В°C) вҶ’ minimal cooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation:** $\eta_{evap} = (T_{in} - T_{out})/(T_{in} - T_{wb})$ approaches **1.0** in dry air but only **0.2вҖ“0.4** in humid air.

**Additional Advantages of ZECC:**
- No electricity required вҶ’ "Zero Energy"
- Can be constructed by farmers with locally available materials
- Increases shelf life of fruits and vegetables by **2вҖ“4 days**
- Maintains high relative humidity (вүҘ85%) вҶ’ reduces shriveling

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kJ and J; ensure $c_p$ units match $Q$ units.
> 2. **Wrong $L_v$ Value:** Use **2,460 kJ/kg at 0В°C**, NOT 2,257 kJ/kg (which is at 100В°C).
> 3. **Specific Heat of Produce:** Students often use **4.186 kJ/kgВ·K** (water's value) for produce. Use $c_p = 3.35 + 0.84 W$ where $W$ is moisture fraction.
> 4. **Half-Cooling Time Confusion:** $t_{1/2}$ is the time to halve the **temperature difference**, not to halve the temperature itself.
> 5. **Confusing Methods:** Vacuum cooling is for **leafy** produce, NOT dense items like apples or carrots.
> 6. **Sanitation in Hydrocooling:** Always mention **chlorination** вҖ” examiners reward awareness of food safety.
> 7. **Moisture Loss Calculation:** Final answer must be in **% of original mass**, with proper sign and unit.
> 8. **Precooling вү  Cold Storage:** Many students treat precooling as identical to refrigeration storage вҖ” explain the **distinction** clearly (rapid heat removal vs maintained low T).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- вң… **Cold Chain Sequence:** Farm вҶ’ Precooling вҶ’ Cold Storage вҶ’ Refrigerated Transport вҶ’ Retail вҶ’ Consumer
- вң… **Field Heat** must be removed **within 2вҖ“6 hours** of harvest (the "**Golden Hours**")
- вң… **Room Cooling:** Slow (12вҖ“24 h), cheapest, suited to hard fruits/tubers
- вң… **Forced-Air Cooling:** Medium-fast (1вҖ“4 h), most versatile, **KTU's most-tested method**
- вң… **Package Icing:** Uses **L_f = 334 kJ/kg**, best for broccoli/cauliflower
- вң… **Hydro Cooling:** Uses water $c_p$ = **4.186 kJ/kgВ·K**, requires **chlorination**
- вң… **Vacuum Cooling:** Uses **L_v = 2,460 kJ/kg**, **20вҖ“30 min**, only for high surface:volume produce
- вң… **Evaporative Cooling:** Uses wet-bulb depression, **arid-region** only
- вң… **Half-Cooling Time Formula:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$
- вң… **7/8 Cooling Time:** $t_{7/8} = 3 t_{1/2}$
- вң… **Specific Heat of Produce:** $c_p \approx 3.35 + 0.84 W$ (kJ/kgВ·K)
- вң… **Non-Refrigerated Transport:** Only for **low-respiration, low-moisture** commodities
- вң… **Refrigerated Transport:** Always **precool before loading**
- вң… **Heat Load Equation:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T + m_r H_r t$
- вң… **KTU's 3-T Principle:** **Temperature, Time, Tolerance**
- вң… **ZECC (Zero-Energy Cool Chamber):** Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) innovation, suitable for small farmers
- вң… **Reefer Set-Points:** **0вҖ“4В°C** chilled; **-18 to -25В°C** frozen

### Memory Tricks for KTU Exam

| Method | Mnemonic |
|---|---|
| **R**oom | **R**est вҖ” slow & steady |
| **F**orced-Air | **F**ast вҖ” pushes air |
| **P**ackage Icing | **P**uts **I**ce in **C**ontact |
| **H**ydro Cooling | **H**eavy-duty fast, **H**as chlorine |
| **V**acuum Cooling | **V**ery fast, leafy only |
| **E**vaporative Cooling | **E**ffective only in **E**xtreme dry heat |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" precooling methods, always present a **structured table** with parameters (time, cost, suitability, water/weight loss). Examiners award bonus marks for **clarity and comparison matrices**. Always show the **cooling rate derivation** вҖ” it is the **most repeated question** across KTU papers from 2018вҖ“2024.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage in Bins, Silos, Bag Storage, Warehouses; Cleaning of Raw Materials - Dry Cleaning and Wet Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage in Bins, Silos, Bag Storage, Warehouses; Cleaning of Raw Materials - Dry Cleaning and Wet Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Storage of Food Materials вҖ” An Overview

In the Food Process Industry, **storage** is the deliberate holding of raw materials, intermediate products, or finished goods under controlled conditions to preserve quality, ensure continuous supply, and stabilize market prices. Storage is a critical post-harvest / pre-processing operation that bridges the gap between production and consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEFTT721):** Storage refers to the regulated containment of food grains, powders, and granular solids in engineered structures вҖ” namely **Bins, Silos, Bags, and Warehouses** вҖ” designed to protect against moisture, pests, microbial spoilage, and mechanical damage.

### 1.2 Classification of Storage Structures

| Storage Type | Capacity Range | Typical Food Stored | Storage Duration |
|--------------|---------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Bins** | < 100 tonnes | Flour, sugar, spices | Short-term (daysвҖ“weeks) |
| **Silos** | 100 вҖ“ 10,000+ tonnes | Grains, rice, wheat, maize | Long-term (monthsвҖ“year) |
| **Bags** | 25вҖ“100 kg per bag | Pulses, seeds, dry fruits | Short to medium term |
| **Warehouses** | 500 вҖ“ 50,000 tonnes | Mixed / packaged goods | Long-term |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Pantry Hierarchy"

Think of food storage like a household pantry, scaled to industrial level:

- **A Bin** is like a **kitchen jar** on your countertop вҖ” small, used frequently, holds a few kilograms.
- **A Silo** is like a **walk-in pantry** standing tall in your basement вҖ” holds tonnes, designed for months.
- **A Bag** is like a **grocery bag** in your car trunk вҖ” portable, sealed, individually managed.
- **A Warehouse** is like a **whole grocery store** вҖ” multiple aisles, racks, climate zones, handling thousands of items.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Selection between bin/silo/bag/warehouse is governed by: **(1)** quantity, **(2)** residence time, **(3)** flowability of the solid, **(4)** moisture sensitivity, and **(5)** capital cost.

## 1.4 Cleaning of Raw Materials вҖ” Formal Definition

**Cleaning** is the unit operation that removes **foreign matter (FM)** вҖ” dust, stones, metal fragments, chaff, husks, weed seeds, dirt, and microbial load вҖ” from raw food materials before they enter the processing line.

It is broadly classified into:

1. **Dry Cleaning** вҖ” performed on grains/powders at or below ambient moisture (typically **< 14 % w.b.**) using air, screens, magnets, and gravity.
2. **Wet Cleaning** вҖ” uses water (sometimes with detergents/sanitizers) to wash produce like fruits, vegetables, and root crops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Almost **3вҖ“5 % of harvested grain is lost annually** in India due to improper cleaning and storage. This makes cleaning + storage economically vital.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bins вҖ” Design and Function

A **bin** is a small, enclosed storage vessel with a **hopper-bottom** for gravity discharge. It is engineered for **mass-flow** or **funnel-flow** discharge of granular food solids.

### 2.1.1 Key Design Parameters

- **Angle of Repose (Оё):** The steepest angle (measured from horizontal) at which a granular heap remains stable without sliding.
- **Hopper Angle:** Must be **greater than the angle of repose** to ensure flow.
- **Bin Wall Slope:** Typically **10В°вҖ“15В°** steeper than Оё.
- **Outlet Diameter:** Must satisfy: $$D_{out} \geq 6 \cdot d_{p,max}$$ where $d_{p,max}$ is the maximum particle diameter.

### 2.1.2 Flow Patterns

| Flow Type | Description | Application |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Mass Flow** | All material moves during discharge | Powders, fine spices |
| **Funnel Flow** | Central core moves first | Coarse grains, pellets |

## 2.2 Silos вҖ” The Industrial Giant

A **silo** is a tall, cylindrical structure with a conical or flat bottom, used for bulk storage of dry granular solids like grain, cement, and flour.

### 2.2.1 Structural Classification

- **Deep Bin Silo:** Height-to-diameter ratio $\frac{H}{D} > 1.5$ вҖ” gravity flow dominates.
- **Shallow Bin Silo:** $\frac{H}{D} \leq 1.5$ вҖ” wall friction minimal, Janssen's equation applicable.

### 2.2.2 Janssen's Equation (Critical KTU Topic)

For **vertical pressure** on the silo floor:

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right) \right]$$

Where:
- $\sigma_v$ = vertical pressure at depth $H$ (Pa)
- $\rho$ = bulk density of material (kg/mВі)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/sВІ**
- $D$ = silo diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of wall friction (dimensionless)
- $K$ = lateral pressure ratio = $\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v}$
- $H$ = depth of material (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Janssen's Equation important for KTU?** It shows that **pressure saturates** at large depths вҖ” adding more grain does NOT linearly increase floor load. This is why silos don't collapse under their own weight.

## 2.3 Bag Storage вҖ” Principles

**Bags** (jute, polypropylene, paper) are stacked in warehouses on pallets. Each bag typically holds **25вҖ“100 kg**.

### 2.3.1 Stack Load Calculation

The compressive load on the **bottom-most bag** is:

$$P_{bottom} = n \cdot W_{bag} \cdot F_{dynamic}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of bags above
- $W_{bag}$ = weight per bag (N)
- $F_{dynamic}$ = dynamic factor (1.2вҖ“1.5 for handling shocks)

**Stacking Pattern:** The most stable is the **"Pyramid / Interlocking"** pattern with a base of $b \times b$ bags tapering to 1 bag at top.

## 2.4 Warehouses вҖ” Layout and Climatic Control

A **warehouse** is a horizontal storage building designed for **bagged and packaged goods**, fitted with pallets, racks, and HVAC systems.

### 2.4.1 Design Considerations

- **Floor load capacity:** typically **5вҖ“15 kN/mВІ**
- **Ventilation rate:** 6вҖ“10 air changes/hour
- **Temperature:** 15вҖ“25 В°C for dry goods
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** < **65 %** to prevent mold
- **Pest control:** fumigation chambers, rodent stations
- **FIFO principle:** First-In-First-Out inventory rotation

## 2.5 Dry Cleaning вҖ” Methods and Equipment

Dry cleaning uses **no water** вҖ” only air, gravity, and mechanical separation.

| Equipment | Principle | Removes |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Magnetic Separator** | Magnetic attraction | Iron/steel fragments |
| **Vibratory Screen** | Size separation | Stones, lumps, oversized |
| **Air Classifier / Aspirator** | Air drag vs. gravity | Dust, light chaff |
| **Gravity Separator (Destoner)** | Specific gravity difference | Stones, mud balls |
| **Disc Separator** | Indented discs | Weed seeds, broken grains |
| **Spiral Separator** | Centrifugal force | Round grains vs. flat |

### 2.5.1 Cleaning Efficiency Formula

$$\eta_{cleaning} = \frac{M_{removed}}{M_{initial}} \times 100 \;\; (\%) $$

A more rigorous form (based on contamination remaining):

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100$$

Where $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are foreign matter concentrations (kg FM / kg total) before and after cleaning.

## 2.6 Wet Cleaning вҖ” Methods and Steps

Wet cleaning is essential for **fruits, vegetables, root crops, and fish**.

### 2.6.1 Standard Wet Cleaning Steps

1. **Pre-soak** вҖ” loosen adhering soil (5вҖ“15 min).
2. **Scrubbing / Spray washing** вҖ” high-pressure water jets.
3. **Rinsing** вҖ” remove detergent residue.
4. **Sanitization** вҖ” chlorinated water (50вҖ“200 ppm free chlorine).
5. **Dewatering / Drying** вҖ” vibrating screen + air blow-off.

### 2.6.2 Chlorine Demand Equation

$$\text{Free Cl}_2 = \text{Total Cl}_2 - \text{Combined Cl}_2$$

For sanitization efficiency:

$$E_{sanitization} = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0} \times 100$$

Where $N_0$ and $N$ are microbial counts (CFU/g) before and after treatment.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D_{out} \geq 6 \cdot d_p$ | Bin outlet diameter criterion | m |
| 2 | $\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K}\left[1-\exp\left(-\dfrac{4\mu K H}{D}\right)\right]$ | Janssen vertical pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $P_{bottom} = n \cdot W_{bag} \cdot F_d$ | Bag stack load | N |
| 4 | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100$ | Cleaning efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{h}{r}$ | Angle of repose | degrees |
| 6 | $Q_{air} = V \times ACH$ | Warehouse ventilation | mВі/h |
| 7 | $E_{sanit} = \left(\dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}\right)\times 100$ | Sanitization efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v$ | Lateral-to-vertical pressure ratio | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $D_{out} > d_p$ instead of $D_{out} \geq 6 d_p$. The factor of **6** comes from Jenike's flow-no-flow criterion for gravity discharge of granular solids вҖ” never miss it!
