# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Janssen's Equation (Detailed)

The Janssen equation is derived by considering a **differential slice** of granular material at depth $z$ inside a vertical cylindrical silo of diameter $D$.

### Step 1 вҖ” Free Body Diagram of Slice

Consider a horizontal slice of thickness $dz$ at depth $z$ below the free surface.

Forces acting on the slice:

1. Weight of the slice (downward): $dW = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot dz$, where $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.
2. Vertical pressure from material above (downward): $(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v) \cdot A$.
3. Vertical pressure support from below (upward): $\sigma_v \cdot A$.
4. Frictional force on the side wall (upward, resisting settlement): $dF_f = \mu \cdot \sigma_h \cdot \pi D \cdot dz$.

### Step 2 вҖ” Vertical Equilibrium

Summing forces in the vertical direction and setting equal to zero:

$$\sigma_v A + \rho g A \, dz = (\sigma_v + d\sigma_v)A + \mu \sigma_h \pi D \, dz$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Substitute Lateral Pressure Ratio

Using $\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v$ (where $K$ = Rankine's lateral pressure ratio):

$$\sigma_v A + \rho g A \, dz = \sigma_v A + A \, d\sigma_v + \mu K \sigma_v \pi D \, dz$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Simplify and Substitute Area

Substituting $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$\rho g \frac{\pi D^2}{4} dz = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} d\sigma_v + \mu K \sigma_v \pi D \, dz$$

Dividing through by $\dfrac{\pi D}{4}$:

$$\rho g D \, dz = D \, d\sigma_v + 4 \mu K \sigma_v \, dz$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Rearrange into Standard ODE Form

$$\frac{d\sigma_v}{dz} + \frac{4 \mu K}{D} \sigma_v = \rho g$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + Py = Q$ with constant coefficients.

### Step 6 вҖ” Solve the ODE

Integrating factor: $IF = \exp\left(\dfrac{4 \mu K z}{D}\right)$.

Multiplying both sides and integrating from $z = 0$ to $z = H$ with $\sigma_v(0) = 0$ (free surface):

$$\sigma_v \cdot \exp\left(\frac{4\mu K H}{D}\right) = \frac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K} \left[\exp\left(\frac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right) - 1\right]$$

### Step 7 вҖ” Final Form

$$\boxed{\sigma_v = \frac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K} \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As $H \to \infty$, $\sigma_v \to \dfrac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K}$ вҖ” a **finite maximum pressure**. Adding infinite grain does not produce infinite floor load вҖ” confirming Janssen's saturation behavior.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Janssen's Equation

**Problem:** A silo of diameter $D = 6$ m stores wheat with bulk density $\rho = 750$ kg/mВі, wall friction coefficient $\mu = 0.4$, and lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.5$. Find the vertical pressure at the floor when filled to $H = 20$ m. Take $g = 9.81$ m/sВІ.

### Step 1 вҖ” Substitute Numerical Values

$$\sigma_v = \frac{750 \times 9.81 \times 6}{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5} \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 20}{6}\right)\right]$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Evaluate the Exponent

$$\frac{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 20}{6} = \frac{16}{6} = 2.667$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Evaluate the Pre-factor

$$\frac{750 \times 9.81 \times 6}{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5} = \frac{44{,}145}{0.8} = 55{,}181.25 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Evaluate the Exponential

$$\exp(-2.667) = 0.0697$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Final Pressure

$$\sigma_v = 55{,}181.25 \times (1 - 0.0697)$$

$$\sigma_v = 55{,}181.25 \times 0.9303$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_v \approx 51{,}335 \;\; \text{Pa} \approx 51.3 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Comparison Without Janssen (Hydrostatic-like)

If we ignored Janssen's friction model:

$$\sigma_{v,naive} = \rho g H = 750 \times 9.81 \times 20 = 147{,}150 \;\; \text{Pa} \approx 147 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Janssen predicts **only ~35 %** of the naive hydrostatic value. This is why silos can be built with thinner floors вҖ” a critical safety factor in design.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Cleaning Efficiency

**Problem:** 1000 kg of wheat contains 12 kg of foreign matter (stones, chaff). After passing through a vibratory screen + aspirator, the cleaned wheat contains 1.8 kg of FM. Calculate cleaning efficiency.

### Step 1 вҖ” Initial Concentration

$$C_{in} = \frac{12}{1000} = 0.012 \;\; \text{kg FM / kg wheat}$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Final Concentration

Assume final wheat mass вүҲ $1000 - 12 = 988$ kg.

$$C_{out} = \frac{1.8}{988} = 0.00182 \;\; \text{kg FM / kg wheat}$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Efficiency

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{0.00182}{0.012}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.1517) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 84.83 \%}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Bag Stack Load

**Problem:** 100 kg wheat bags are stacked in a 5 Г— 5 pyramid (5 layers including top). Find the load on the bottom-centre bag. Take $F_d = 1.3$.

### Step 1 вҖ” Bags Above the Bottom-Centre Bag

Pyramid: layer 1 = 25, layer 2 = 16, layer 3 = 9, layer 4 = 4, layer 5 = 1 (top). The bottom-centre bag has the central bag of layer 2 directly above + 9 from layer 3 + 4 from layer 4 + 1 from layer 5 = **15 bags above**, but accounting for partial loads from corners:

For conservative KTU design, take **all bags above** stacked centrally:

$$n = 16 + 9 + 4 + 1 = 30 \;\; \text{bags}$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Compute Load

$$W_{bag} = 100 \times 9.81 = 981 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$P_{bottom} = 30 \times 981 \times 1.3$$

$$\boxed{P_{bottom} \approx 38{,}259 \;\; \text{N} \approx 38.3 \;\; \text{kN}}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define angle of repose. Why is it critical in bin design?**

**Model Answer:**

Angle of repose (Оё) is the maximum angle (measured from the horizontal) that the surface of a granular heap makes with the horizontal plane under gravity, without sliding.

$$\tan\theta = \frac{h}{r}$$

where $h$ = heap height, $r$ = radius of heap base.

**Criticality in bin design:**
- The hopper wall angle must be **steeper than Оё** (by 5В°вҖ“10В°) to ensure **mass flow discharge**.
- If the hopper angle < Оё, material forms a stable arch or rat-hole вҖ” **bridging** or **arching** failures occur.
- It governs **outlet diameter**, **flow pattern (mass vs. funnel)**, and **internal wall friction**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often quote the definition but fail to mention the **5В°вҖ“10В° safety margin** above Оё. This costs 1 mark in KTU valuation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between dry cleaning and wet cleaning of food raw materials. Give two examples of each method.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Dry Cleaning | Wet Cleaning |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Medium used | Air, gravity, screens, magnets | Water + sanitizers |
| Material moisture | < 14 % w.b. | Any (usually > 14 % w.b.) |
| Examples of equipment | Magnetic separator, aspirator, gravity destoner | Spray washer, soak tank, flotation washer |
| Typical materials | Wheat, rice, pulses, spices | Fruits, vegetables, fish, root crops |
| Water consumption | None | 2вҖ“10 L/kg of product |
| Microbial reduction | Minimal | Significant (esp. with chlorine) |

**Examples:**
- **Dry Cleaning** вҶ’ Aspirator for chaff removal in wheat; Magnetic separator for iron filings in flour.
- **Wet Cleaning** вҶ’ Spray washing of apples; Chlorine rinsing of leafy vegetables.

## PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the various types of storage structures used in the food industry with neat sketches. Discuss their relative advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Storage Structures in Food Industry:**

1. **Bins:**
   - Small enclosed vessels with hopper bottom.
   - Capacity: < 100 tonnes.
   - Used for: spices, flour, sugar, intermediate powders.
   - **Advantage:** Easy to clean, fast discharge, modular.
   - **Limitation:** Limited capacity, not for long-term storage.

2. **Silos:**
   - Tall cylindrical vertical storage, typically 6вҖ“30 m height.
   - Capacity: 100вҖ“10,000+ tonnes.
   - Used for: wheat, rice, maize, soybean, flour.
   - **Advantage:** Compact footprint, gravity discharge, low labour.
   - **Limitation:** High capex, hard to inspect/clean internally, risk of silo "funnel flow" or bridging.

3. **Bag Storage:**
   - Jute/PP/paper bags (25вҖ“100 kg) stacked on pallets.
   - **Advantage:** Portable, low capex, easy FIFO tracking, no specialized structure.
   - **Limitation:** Rodent/pest risk, manual handling labour-intensive, stack instability.

4. **Warehouses:**
   - Single-storey horizontal buildings with racks, pallets, HVAC.
   - Capacity: 500вҖ“50,000 tonnes.
   - **Advantage:** Versatile for mixed goods, climate-controlled, easy access.
   - **Limitation:** Large footprint, requires pest & fire management, expensive in urban areas.

**Conclusion:** Storage selection is based on **material type, capacity, residence time, and economics**.

---

**(b) Derive Janssen's equation for vertical pressure in a silo. A wheat silo of 8 m diameter contains grain up to 25 m height. Given: $\rho$ = 780 kg/mВі, $\mu$ = 0.4, $K$ = 0.5, $g$ = 9.81 m/sВІ. Compute the floor pressure. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (refer Section 3.1 of these notes):**

Starting from vertical equilibrium of a differential slice of thickness $dz$ at depth $z$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_v}{dz} + \frac{4 \mu K}{D}\sigma_v = \rho g$$

Solving with boundary condition $\sigma_v(0) = 0$:

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right]$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Substituting $D = 8$ m, $H = 25$ m, $\rho = 780$ kg/mВі, $\mu = 0.4$, $K = 0.5$, $g = 9.81$ m/sВІ:

**Step 1 вҖ” Pre-factor:**

$$\frac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K} = \frac{780 \times 9.81 \times 8}{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5} = \frac{61{,}214.4}{0.8} = 76{,}518 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Exponent:**

$$-\frac{4 \mu K H}{D} = -\frac{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 25}{8} = -\frac{20}{8} = -2.5$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Exponential:**

$$\exp(-2.5) = 0.0821$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Final Pressure:**

$$\sigma_v = 76{,}518 \times (1 - 0.0821) = 76{,}518 \times 0.9179$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_v \approx 70{,}235 \;\; \text{Pa} \approx 70.2 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

**Interpretation:** The floor pressure is only **~37 %** of the naive hydrostatic value ($\rho g H = 191{,}295$ Pa), confirming Janssen's friction-load-sharing mechanism.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 вҖ” Internal Alternative) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) With the help of a flow diagram, describe the various methods of dry cleaning of grains. Mention the equipment used at each stage. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Dry Cleaning Train for Grains:**

```
Raw Grain вҶ’ Reception вҶ’ Scalper (Coarse Screen) вҶ’ Magnetic Separator вҶ’ 
Vibratory Screen (Fine) вҶ’ Aspirator (Air Stream) вҶ’ Gravity Destoner вҶ’ 
Disc Separator (Optional) вҶ’ Cleaned Grain вҶ’ Storage
```

**Stage-wise Equipment:**

1. **Scalper (Coarse Vibratory Screen):**
   - Removes oversized foreign matter вҖ” straw, stones, lumps, large debris.
   - Aperture: 10вҖ“25 mm.

2. **Magnetic Separator:**
   - Permanent or electromagnetic drum removes ferrous fragments.
   - Field strength: 0.1вҖ“0.3 Tesla typical.

3. **Vibratory Sieve (Finishing Screen):**
   - Removes sand, small stones, broken kernels.
   - Aperture: 1вҖ“5 mm.

4. **Aspirator (Air Classifier):**
   - Vertical air column lifts low-density material (chaff, dust, husk).
   - Based on **terminal velocity** difference: $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2mg}{C_d \rho_a A}}$.

5. **Gravity Separator (Destoner):**
   - Vibrating inclined deck with air-through-fluidization.
   - Separates by **specific gravity** вҖ” heavy stones settle, grain floats on air cushion.

6. **Disc Separator (Indented Cylinder):**
   - Indented pockets pick short/long grains differently; removes weed seeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Drawing a **flow diagram** is mandatory вҖ” students who write only bullet points lose 2 marks. Also mention the **physical principle** (size, density, magnetism, terminal velocity) at each stage.

---

**(b) What are the objectives of grain storage? Discuss the design and constructional features of a modern grain silo. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Objectives of Grain Storage:**

1. **Preservation of quality** вҖ” protect against moisture, pests, microbes, and rodents.
2. **Buffer supply** вҖ” bridge seasonal gap between harvest and consumption.
3. **Price stabilization** вҖ” release grain strategically to dampen market volatility.
4. **Quality improvement** вҖ” some grains (e.g., paddy for rice) need aging.
5. **National food security** вҖ” strategic reserve for emergencies.

**Modern Grain Silo вҖ” Design & Constructional Features:**

1. **Foundation:** Reinforced concrete raft or pile foundation; must withstand $\sigma_v$ from Janssen.
2. **Cylindrical Shell:** Galvanized corrugated steel (0.5вҖ“4 mm thickness) or reinforced concrete.
3. **Hopper Bottom:** Steel cone with angle 35В°вҖ“45В° for gravity discharge.
4. **Aeration System:** Perforated floor + centrifugal fans (0.05вҖ“0.15 mВі/min per tonne) to control temperature & moisture.
5. **Temperature & Moisture Sensors:** Cables with thermocouples at multiple depths.
6. **Fumigation System:** Recirculating system injecting **phosphine (PHвӮғ)** or **methyl bromide**.
7. **Level Indicators:** Rotary, capacitance, or ultrasonic.
8. **Roof:** Conical or dome, ventilated with exhaust fans.
9. **Discharge Auger / Conveyor:** At base for evacuation.
10. **Safety:** Ladders with safety cage, gas detectors for fumigation, lightning arrestors.

**Capacity Calculation Example:**

Volume of silo: $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(8)^2(25) = 1256.6$ mВі.

Mass of wheat stored: $m = \rho V = 780 \times 1256.6 = 980{,}000$ kg вүҲ **980 tonnes**.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings вҖ” Compilation

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Module 1 вҖ” Storage & Cleaning):**
> 1. **Confusing "bin" and "silo"** вҖ” bins are small (< 100 t), silos are large. Mixing them loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 6** in $D_{out} \geq 6 d_p$ вҖ” students write $D_{out} > d_p$.
> 3. **Missing $\sigma_v(0) = 0$** as boundary condition in Janssen derivation вҖ” full mark deduction.
> 4. **Not mentioning "mass flow vs. funnel flow"** in silo/bin design answers.
> 5. **Writing "chlorine removes all bacteria"** in wet cleaning вҖ” must say "reduces microbial load by X %".
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers вҖ” KTU strict on **Pa or kPa** for pressure, **kg/mВі** for density.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap вҖ” Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Bin:** small hopper-bottomed vessel; **hopper angle > Оё**; $D_{out} \geq 6 d_p$.
- **Silo:** large vertical storage; deep bin $\frac{H}{D}>1.5$; shallow $\leq 1.5$.
- **Janssen equation:** $\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho g D}{4\mu K}\left[1-\exp\left(-\dfrac{4\mu K H}{D}\right)\right]$; pressure **saturates** at $\dfrac{\rho g D}{4\mu K}$.
- **Lateral pressure:** $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$ вҖ” Rankine's relation.
- **Bag stack:** pyramid pattern; bottom bag bears $P = n \cdot W \cdot F_d$.
- **Warehouse:** horizontal storage; RH < 65 %, 15вҖ“25 В°C, FIFO, pallet-based.
- **Dry cleaning:** uses air, screens, magnets, gravity вҖ” for grains, pulses, spices at < 14 % moisture.
- **Equipment chain:** Scalper вҶ’ Magnet вҶ’ Vibratory Screen вҶ’ Aspirator вҶ’ Destoner вҶ’ Disc Separator.
- **Wet cleaning:** uses water + sanitizer (50вҖ“200 ppm free ClвӮӮ); for fruits, vegetables, fish.
- **Steps:** Pre-soak вҶ’ Spray Wash вҶ’ Chlorine Rinse вҶ’ Dewater вҶ’ Dry.
- **Cleaning efficiency:** $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100$ %.
- **Sanitization efficiency:** $E = \left(\dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}\right) \times 100$ %.
- **Angle of repose:** $\tan\theta = h/r$; critical for hopper design and flow assurance.
- **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** Module 1 maps to **CO1** вҖ” Understand storage & cleaning unit operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic вҖ” "BSBW-DryWet":** **B**ins, **S**ilos, **B**ags, **W**arehouses for storage; **Dry** (screens+magnets+air) and **Wet** (water+chlorine) for cleaning. Memorize this flow on exam eve!

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Treatment - Blanching, Sulphur Dioxide Dipping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pre-Treatment - Blanching, Sulphur Dioxide Dipping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Blanching вҖ” Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Blanching** is a thermal pre-treatment operation in food processing in which fruits or vegetables are subjected to a short-duration exposure to hot water, steam, or hot air (typically **80 В°C вҖ“ 100 В°C** for **1 вҖ“ 10 minutes**, depending on product geometry and enzyme load), followed immediately by rapid cooling, with the primary engineering objective of inactivating deteriorative native enzymes such as **peroxidase (POD)**, **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)**, **catalase**, and **lipoxygenase (LOX)** that would otherwise compromise colour, flavour, texture, and nutritional integrity during subsequent storage.

The secondary objectives of blanching include:
- Removal of intercellular gases вҶ’ facilitating can-filling density.
- Softening of tissue вҶ’ reducing packaging strain.
- Reduction of surface microbial load by **1вҖ“2 log cycles** (90 % вҖ“ 99 % reduction).
- Fixation of natural pigments (chlorophyll вҶ’ chlorophyllide conversion in some vegetables).

### 1.2 Sulphur Dioxide (SOвӮӮ) Dipping вҖ” Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphur Dioxide Dipping** is a chemical pre-treatment in which cut, peeled, or comminuted fruit/vegetable tissue is immersed in an aqueous solution of **sulphurous acid (HвӮӮSOвӮғ)** or its salts (sodium/potassium metabisulphite, NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… / KвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ…) at concentrations ranging from **0.1 % to 0.5 % (w/v)** for a contact time of **30 s to 30 min**, in order to inhibit enzymatic browning, suppress microbial proliferation, and retard non-enzymatic oxidative reactions.

The active species in solution are the equilibrium mixture:

$$SO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2SO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HSO_3^- \rightleftharpoons 2H^+ + SO_3^{2-}$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> **Analogy 1 вҖ” Blanching = "Resetting the Biological Clock"**
> Imagine a freshly cut apple slice gradually turning brown because of enzymes still "alive" inside. Blanching is like placing the apple in a hot bath for a few minutes вҖ” not long enough to cook it, but long enough to put those enzymes to sleep (denature them). The rapid cold-water dip that follows is the "alarm clock" that stops the heating process before the apple itself becomes overcooked.

> **Analogy 2 вҖ” SOвӮӮ Dipping = "Applying Invisible Sunscreen"**
> When you apply sunscreen on your skin, it absorbs harmful UV rays before they damage your cells. Similarly, sulphite ions (HSOвӮғвҒ» / SOвӮғВІвҒ») bind to the active sites of the browning enzyme (PPO) and to quinone intermediates, effectively "absorbing" the oxidative damage before it discolours the fruit. The fruit looks fresher for longer, just like protected skin.

> **Analogy 3 вҖ” The Lock-and-Key Blockade**
> PPO enzyme is shaped like a lock that accepts phenols (the key) and oxygen to produce brown melanin pigments. Sulphite acts like chewing gum jammed into the keyhole вҖ” the key can no longer fit, so the browning reaction is mechanically blocked.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Metric | Typical Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Blanching Temperature (Water) | **85 вҖ“ 100 В°C** | Vegetables |
| Blanching Temperature (Steam) | **100 вҖ“ 105 В°C** | Saturated steam |
| Blanching Time | **1 вҖ“ 10 min** | Depends on cube size |
| Peroxidase inactivation endpoint | **Negative guaiacol test** | Quality control marker |
| SOвӮӮ concentration in dip | **0.1 вҖ“ 0.5 % (w/v)** | Fruits, mushrooms |
| Residual SOвӮӮ in finished product | **вүӨ 350 ppm** | Codex Alimentarius limit (dried fruits) |
| SOвӮӮ as food additive (E-number) | **E220** | EU classification |
| pH of metabisulphite dip | **3.0 вҖ“ 4.0** | Optimal antimicrobial range |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Blanching Methods

Blanching is fundamentally categorised by the heat-transfer medium employed. Each method has distinct engineering trade-offs:

1. **Hot-Water Blanching (HW blanching)**
   - Most common; high heat-transfer coefficient (h вүҲ **500 вҖ“ 5000 W/mВІВ·K**).
   - Causes significant **leaching losses** of water-soluble nutrients (sugars, vitamins B & C, minerals) вҖ” up to **20 вҖ“ 30 %** of dry matter.
   - Uniform heating; suitable for diced/cubed products.

2. **Steam Blanching**
   - Latent-heat transfer (h вүҲ **6000 вҖ“ 15000 W/mВІВ·K** during condensation).
   - Minimal nutrient leaching (only **5 вҖ“ 10 %** loss).
   - Non-uniform heating at product surface вҖ” interior may remain under-blanched.

3. **Hot-Air / Convective Blanching**
   - Low heat-transfer coefficient (h вүҲ **50 вҖ“ 300 W/mВІВ·K**); longer time required.
   - Used for products with high water activity and high thermal sensitivity.

4. **Microwave Blanching (MW blanching)**
   - Volumetric heating; rapid and uniform; minimal leaching.
   - High capital cost; risk of runaway heating and textural damage.

5. **Infrared (IR) / Ohmic Blanching**
   - Emerging technologies for high-quality retention.

> **Engineering Selection Logic:** A food engineer selects the blanching medium based on (i) product thermal sensitivity, (ii) acceptable nutrient loss, (iii) throughput requirements, and (iv) downstream packaging (canning vs. freezing).

### 2.2 Why Blanching Works вҖ” The "Why" Behind Enzyme Inactivation

Enzymes are globular proteins whose 3-D tertiary/quaternary structure is stabilised by hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and disulphide bridges. When temperature rises above a critical threshold (typically **60 вҖ“ 80 В°C** for most plant enzymes):

- The kinetic energy of molecules overcomes the weak non-covalent bonds.
- The protein unfolds (denaturation) вҶ’ loss of catalytic active-site geometry.
- The MichaelisвҖ“Menten binding pocket can no longer recognise its substrate.
- Enzyme activity drops irreversibly to **< 5 %** of initial value.

**Peroxidase (POD)** is universally used as the **over-processing index** because:
- It has the **highest thermal resistance** among common plant enzymes.
- If POD is inactivated, all other enzymes (PPO, catalase, LOX) are also inactivated.
- It can be detected with a simple colourimetric **guaiacol test** (brown/orange colour if active).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** "Just-peroxidase-inactivation" (1-second negative guaiacol test) is the industry gold standard for blanching endpoint. Over-blanching causes texture loss and nutrient destruction; under-blanching causes residual enzymatic deterioration.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Heat-Transfer Logic During Hot-Water Blanching

Step 1 вҶ’ The product (e.g., a carrot cube) at initial temperature **Tбөў вүҲ 25 В°C** is immersed in water at **TвҲһ = 95 В°C**.

Step 2 вҶ’ Convective heat transfer occurs at the cube surface (Newton's Law of Cooling):

$$q'' = h \cdot (T_\infty - T_{surface})$$

Step 3 вҶ’ Heat conducts into the cube interior by Fourier conduction, with thermal diffusivity **Оұ = k / (ПҒВ·cвӮҡ)** вүҲ **1.4 Г— 10вҒ»вҒ· mВІ/s** for most plant tissues.

Step 4 вҶ’ Internal temperature rises following a transient conduction solution (lumped vs. distributed). For cubes with characteristic length **L < 15 mm** and **Bi = hL/k < 0.1**, the **lumped capacitance method** is valid.

Step 5 вҶ’ The cube centre reaches **80 В°C** within 1 вҖ“ 3 min, sufficient for POD denaturation.

Step 6 вҶ’ The product is plunged into cold water (**5 вҖ“ 10 В°C**) to halt over-processing and re-establish firmness.

### 2.4 Sulphur Dioxide вҖ” Chemistry of Preservation

Sulphur dioxide acts via **four principal mechanisms**:

1. **Enzyme Inhibition (Anti-browning):** SOвӮӮ reduces the CuВІвҒә cofactor of PPO to CuвҒә, rendering the enzyme catalytically inactive. It also combines with quinone intermediates to form colourless sulphone adducts.
2. **Antimicrobial Action:** Undissociated HвӮӮSOвӮғ penetrates microbial cell membranes, acidifying the cytoplasm and disrupting enzymatic activity. Effective against yeasts, moulds, and aerobic bacteria.
3. **Antioxidant Action:** SOвӮӮ scavenges OвӮӮ in the sealed headspace and reacts with peroxides, preventing lipid oxidation and ascorbic acid degradation.
4. **Vitamin Preservation:** Particularly effective at preserving **Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)** and **Vitamin BвӮҒ (thiamine)** by suppressing oxidative degradation.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the table below, the absolute value bars and "such that" pipes are replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NewtonвҖҷs Law of Cooling (convective heat flux) | $q'' = h \cdot (T_\infty - T_s)$ | W/mВІ | Surface heat transfer rate |
| 2 | Lumped Capacitance (Biot вүӨ 0.1) | $\dfrac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{h A t}{\rho V c_p}\right)$ | dimensionless | Centre-temperature prediction |
| 3 | Fourier Number | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L^2}$ | dimensionless | Penetration of heat |
| 4 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$ | mВІ/s | Material property |
| 5 | First-order enzyme inactivation | $\dfrac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D}$ | dimensionless | Decimal reduction time (D-value) |
| 6 | z-value (thermal death time) | $z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10} D_1 - \log_{10} D_2}$ | В°C | Temp change for 10Г— D-value change |
| 7 | Arrhenius inactivation rate | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | sвҒ»В№ | Rate constant vs. T |
| 8 | Sensible heat absorbed by product | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | J | Energy demand of blancher |
| 9 | Total mass of SOвӮӮ required | $m_{SO_2} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{m_{product}}{10^6}$ | kg SOвӮӮ | Dipping formulation |
| 10 | Sodium metabisulphite вҶ’ SOвӮӮ yield | 1 g NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… вҶ’ **0.5 g SOвӮӮ** (theoretical) | g/g | Reagent dosing |
| 11 | HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch for SOвӮӮ speciation | $pH = pK_{a1} + \log\!\left(\dfrac{\vert HSO_3^- \vert}{\vert H_2SO_3 \vert}\right)$ | вҖ” | pH-control of dip (pKвӮҗвӮҒ вүҲ 1.81) |
| 12 | Decimal reduction ratio (Z-value form) | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$ | dimensionless | Cross-temperature D-value |
| 13 | Heat-transfer coefficient, condensing steam | $h \approx 0.943 \left(\dfrac{k_l^3 \rho_l^2 g \, h_{fg}}{N \mu_l \Delta T \, D}\right)^{1/4}$ | W/mВІВ·K | Nusselt-type correlation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Kinetics of Enzyme Inactivation

Enzyme inactivation at a constant blanching temperature proceeds as a **pseudo-first-order reaction**. Let **N** be the residual enzyme activity (or viable enzyme concentration) and **k** the inactivation rate constant.

**Step 1 вҖ”** Write the rate of loss of active enzyme:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \, N$$

**Step 2 вҖ”** Separate variables and integrate from initial activity **NвӮҖ** at **t = 0** to activity **N** at time **t**:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 вҖ”** Evaluate both integrals:

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -k \, t$$

**Step 4 вҖ”** Exponentiate to obtain the standard form:

$$N = N_0 \, e^{-k t}$$

**Step 5 вҖ”** Convert from natural to base-10 logarithm by introducing the **D-value** (decimal reduction time вҖ” the time required to reduce activity by 90 %, i.e., a 1-log reduction):

$$D = \frac{2.303}{k}$$

**Step 6 вҖ”** Substituting **k = 2.303/D**:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D}$$

This is the **canonical thermal inactivation equation** that KTU examiners love to test.

### 3.2 Derivation: The z-value Relation

The D-value itself is a function of temperature. Empirical observation shows that log(D) decreases linearly with increasing temperature.

**Step 1 вҖ”** Assume a linear relationship:

$$\log_{10}(D) = a - b \, T$$

**Step 2 вҖ”** The slope **b** is, by definition, the reciprocal of the **z-value**, where z is the temperature change required for a 10-fold change in D:

$$b = \frac{1}{z}$$

**Step 3 вҖ”** Evaluate at two temperatures **TвӮҒ** and **TвӮӮ**:

$$\log_{10}(D_1) = a - \frac{T_1}{z} \quad ; \quad \log_{10}(D_2) = a - \frac{T_2}{z}$$

**Step 4 вҖ”** Subtract the two equations (the intercept **a** cancels):

$$\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

**Step 5 вҖ”** Rearrange to the standard form taught in KTU:

$$z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Lumped Capacitance Temperature Response

For a small food particle where internal conduction resistance is negligible relative to surface convection, energy balance gives:

**Step 1 вҖ”** Rate of energy accumulation in the particle = rate of convective heat inflow:

$$\rho V c_p \, \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T_\infty - T)$$

**Step 2 вҖ”** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dT}{T_\infty - T} = \frac{h A}{\rho V c_p} \, dt$$

**Step 3 вҖ”** Integrate from **T = Tбөў** at **t = 0** to **T** at time **t**:

$$-\ln\!\left(\frac{T_\infty - T}{T_\infty - T_i}\right) = \frac{h A}{\rho V c_p} \, t$$

**Step 4 вҖ”** Exponentiate and rearrange:

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A t}{\rho V c_p}\right)$$

**Step 5 вҖ”** This is the working form used to compute the time required to reach a target centre temperature.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Time Required to Blanch a Pea

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Style):** A batch of green peas (initially at **25 В°C**, density **ПҒ = 1050 kg/mВі**, specific heat **cвӮҡ = 3600 J/kgВ·K**, characteristic radius **r = 4 mm = 0.004 m**) is immersed in water at **95 В°C**. The surface heat-transfer coefficient is **h = 1200 W/mВІВ·K**. Determine the time needed for the pea centre to reach **80 В°C** (peroxidase inactivation point).

**Step 1 вҖ”** Verify the **Biot number** assumption. Assume thermal conductivity **k вүҲ 0.5 W/mВ·K**:

$$Bi = \frac{h \cdot L_c}{k} = \frac{1200 \times 0.004}{0.5} = 9.6$$

> The Biot number exceeds 0.1 вҖ” the **lumped capacitance method is NOT strictly valid** for this geometry, but for an order-of-magnitude KTU calculation, we proceed with a **conservative estimate** (actual heating will take longer than predicted, providing a safety margin).

**Step 2 вҖ”** Set up the exponential form with the target centre temperature:

$$\frac{80 - 95}{25 - 95} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A t}{\rho V c_p}\right)$$

**Step 3 вҖ”** Compute the left-hand side:

$$\frac{-15}{-70} = 0.2143$$

**Step 4 вҖ”** For a sphere, **A/V = 3/r** = 3 / 0.004 = **750 mвҒ»В№**. Compute the time constant:

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A} = \frac{\rho c_p r}{3 h} = \frac{1050 \times 3600 \times 0.004}{3 \times 1200}$$

$$\tau = \frac{15120}{3600} = 4.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 вҖ”** Solve for **t**:

$$t = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.2143) = -4.2 \times (-1.540) = 6.47 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 вҖ”** Add a safety factor (industry practice uses 2вҖ“3Г— the calculated minimum):

$$t_{design} \approx 2 \times 6.47 \approx 13 \text{ s}$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** In practice, peas are blanched for **60 вҖ“ 90 s** in industry to ensure uniform heating throughout the batch and account for size distribution вҖ” a much more conservative design.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix вҖ” When to Use Which Pre-Treatment

| Product | Recommended Pre-Treatment | Time / Concentration | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apple slices (drying) | **SOвӮӮ dipping** | 0.2 % KMS, 30 min | Strong browning tendency; cannot be blanched (texture loss) |
| Green peas (freezing) | **Water blanching** | 90 В°C, 90 s | Mild product; inactivates POD; preserves colour |
| Cauliflower (freezing) | **Steam blanching** | 100 В°C, 4 min | Sensitive to nutrient leaching |
| Mango pulp | **SOвӮӮ addition** | 350 ppm | pH < 4.5; inhibits moulds during storage |
| Potato cubes (drying) | **Water blanching** | 85 В°C, 4 min | Inactivates PPO without SOвӮӮ chemical residue |
| Dried apricots | **SOвӮӮ + sun drying** | 2000 вҖ“ 3000 ppm initial | Classic preservation for golden colour retention |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram вҖ” Industrial Blanching Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Reception] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Washing with Chlorinated Water]
    C --> D[Size Reduction Optional]
    D --> E[Loading Conveyor]
    E --> F[BLANCHING UNIT]
    F --> F1[Hot Water Tank 90 to 95 C]
    F --> F2[Steam Belt 100 C]
    F --> F3[Hot Air Tunnel]
    F --> F[Selected medium contacts product for 1 to 10 min]
    F1 --> G[Cooling Section Cold Water 5 to 10 C]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Dewatering and Surface Drying]
    H --> I[Peroxidase Guaiacol Test QC]
    I --> J{QC Pass}
    J -->|Yes| K[Packaging or Freezing or Drying]
    J -->|No| L[Return to Blancher for Re-blanching]
    L --> F
    K --> M[Finished Product to Cold Storage]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram вҖ” Sulphur Dioxide Dipping Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Fruits or Vegetables] --> B[Sorting and Trimming]
    B --> C[Peeling and Slicing]
    C --> D[Immediate Transfer to Dip Tank]
    D --> E{Dip Type Selection}
    E -->|KMS Powder Solution| F1[0.1 to 0.5 percent Sodium Metabisulphite]
    E -->|Liquid SO2 Solution| F2[0.05 to 0.2 percent SO2 in Water]
    E -->|Sulphurous Acid| F3[0.1 percent H2SO3]
    F1 --> G[Immersion for 30 s to 30 min]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Draining Excess Solution]
    H --> I[Surface Drying by Forced Air]
    I --> J[Residual SO2 Analysis Iodometric Titration]
    J --> K{SO2 within Codex Limits}
    K -->|Yes| L[Packaging in Sealed Containers]
    K -->|No| M[Re-dipping or Desorption Step]
    M --> G
    L --> N[Storage at Low RH and Cool Temperature]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture вҖ” Coupled Heat and Mass Transfer During Blanching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Heat Source Boiler or Steam Line] -->|Heat Energy Q| HTU[Heat Transfer Unit Blancher]
    M1[Product Surface Water Activity] -->|Moisture Loss| HTU
    HTU -->|Conduction| P1[Product Interior Temperature Rises]
    HTU -->|Convection| P2[Product Surface Temperature Rises]
    P1 --> P3[Enzyme Denaturation when T exceeds 80 C]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Loss of Catalytic Activity POD PPO LOX]
    P4 --> P5[Inactivation Confirmed via Guaiacol Test]
    P5 --> O1[Cooling Stage Stops Over-processing]
    P5 --> O2[Microbial Load Reduced by 90 to 99 percent]
    P5 --> O3[Intercellular Gases Expelled]
    P5 --> O4[Tissue Softened for Can-filling]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram вҖ” SOвӮӮ Action Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fruit Cellular System] --> B[PPO Enzyme Cu2+ Cofactor]
    A --> C[Phenolic Substrates]
    A --> D[Oxygen Dissolved in Tissue]
    B --> E[PPO Catalyses Oxidation]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Quinone Intermediates]
    F --> G[Polymerisation to Brown Melanins]
    G --> H[Visible Browning of Product]
    I[SO2 Dip Applied] --> J[HSO3- Ions Enter Tissue]
    J --> K1[Reduces Cu2+ to Cu+ in PPO]
    J --> K2[Binds Quinones to Form Colourless Sulphones]
    K1 --> L[Enzyme Inhibited]
    K2 --> M[Browning Intermediates Neutralised]
    L --> N[Enzymatic Browning Blocked]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Product Colour Retained]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]`

Define **blanching**. List any **four objectives** of blanching in food processing.

**Model Answer:**

Blanching is a short-duration heat pre-treatment of fruits or vegetables (typically at **80 вҖ“ 100 В°C** for 1 вҖ“ 10 minutes) followed by rapid cooling, primarily intended to inactivate deteriorative enzymes such as peroxidase and polyphenol oxidase.

**Four objectives:**

1. Inactivation of native enzymes (peroxidase, polyphenol oxidase, lipoxygenase).
2. Reduction of surface microbial load by 1 вҖ“ 2 log cycles.
3. Expulsion of intercellular gases, improving can-filling density.
4. Softening of tissue for ease of further processing (peeling, packing).

> *[Examiner Note: Award full 3 marks if definition is precise and 4 objectives are clearly listed. Partial marks for incomplete lists.]*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]`

What is **sulphur dioxide dipping**? State its **two principal functions** in fruit preservation.

**Model Answer:**

Sulphur dioxide dipping is a chemical pre-treatment in which cut/peeled fruits are immersed in an aqueous solution of sulphurous acid or its salts (sodium/potassium metabisulphite) at concentrations of **0.1 вҖ“ 0.5 % (w/v)** for a contact time of **30 s to 30 min**.

**Two principal functions:**

1. **Inhibition of enzymatic browning** вҖ” sulphite ions reduce the CuВІвҒә cofactor of polyphenol oxidase and bind quinone intermediates to form colourless sulphone adducts.
2. **Antimicrobial preservation** вҖ” undissociated HвӮӮSOвӮғ penetrates microbial cells, lowers internal pH, and inhibits mould, yeast, and bacterial proliferation.

---

### 5.2 PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle and mechanism** of enzyme inactivation by blanching. Derive the **first-order thermal inactivation equation** and define the **D-value** and **z-value** clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A peroxidase enzyme solution exhibits a D-value of **DвӮҒ = 240 s at 80 В°C** and **DвӮӮ = 24 s at 90 В°C**. Calculate:
- (i) The **z-value** for this enzyme. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The **time required to achieve a 6-log reduction** in enzyme activity at **85 В°C**. **(4 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Principle:** Enzymes are heat-labile proteins. When subjected to temperatures above their denaturation threshold (typically 60 вҖ“ 80 В°C), the non-covalent bonds maintaining tertiary structure break, leading to unfolding and irreversible loss of catalytic activity.

**Mechanism:**
- Heat absorption increases molecular kinetic energy.
- Hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and Van der Waals forces weaken.
- The active site loses its geometric specificity.
- The substrate can no longer bind вҶ’ enzyme activity drops to negligible values.

**Derivation of First-Order Inactivation:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k N \quad \xrightarrow{\text{integrate}} \quad N = N_0 e^{-kt}$$

In base-10 form, with **D = 2.303/k**:

$$\boxed{\frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D}}$$

**D-value definition:** The time (at a given temperature) required to reduce the active enzyme population by **90 %** (i.e., a 1-log reduction).

**z-value definition:** The temperature increase (В°C) required to reduce the D-value by a factor of **10** (i.e., a 1-log reduction in D).

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Calculation of z-value:**

Using the defining equation:

$$z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2)}$$

$$z = \frac{90 - 80}{\log_{10}(240) - \log_{10}(24)}$$

Compute the logarithms:

$$\log_{10}(240) = 2.3802 \quad ; \quad \log_{10}(24) = 1.3802$$

$$z = \frac{10}{2.3802 - 1.3802} = \frac{10}{1.0000}$$

$$\boxed{z = 10 \text{ В°C}}$$

**(ii) Time for 6-log reduction at 85 В°C:**

Step 1 вҖ” Find DвӮғ at **TвӮғ = 85 В°C** using z-value:

$$\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_3) = \frac{T_3 - T_1}{z}$$

$$2.3802 - \log_{10}(D_3) = \frac{85 - 80}{10} = 0.5$$

$$\log_{10}(D_3) = 2.3802 - 0.5 = 1.8802$$

$$D_3 = 10^{1.8802} = 75.86 \text{ s}$$

Step 2 вҖ” Time for **6-log reduction** (i.e., **N/NвӮҖ = 10вҒ»вҒ¶**):

$$t = 6 \times D_3 = 6 \times 75.86$$

$$\boxed{t = 455.2 \text{ s} \approx 7.59 \text{ min}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** вҖ” *Internal Choice Alternative*

> `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Describe the **chemistry and mechanism** by which sulphur dioxide prevents enzymatic browning in cut fruits. Include the relevant **chemical equations** and discuss the **role of pH** in the equilibrium of SOвӮӮ species. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food processor wishes to prepare **1000 kg of dried apricots** with a target residual SOвӮӮ of **2000 ppm** (mg SOвӮӮ per kg fruit). Using **sodium metabisulphite (NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ…)**, calculate:
- (i) The **mass of SOвӮӮ** required in the product. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The **mass of NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… powder** to be dissolved in the dipping solution, given the theoretical conversion factor of **1 g NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… вҶ’ 0.5 g SOвӮӮ**. **(4 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Chemistry of SOвӮӮ Dipping:**

Sulphur dioxide dissolves in water to form sulphurous acid, which dissociates in two steps:

$$SO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2SO_3 \quad (pK_{a1} = 1.81)$$

$$H_2SO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HSO_3^- \quad (pK_{a2} = 6.91)$$

**Mechanism against browning:**

1. **Direct enzyme inhibition:** Sulphite ions (HSOвӮғвҒ») reduce the **CuВІвҒә cofactor** of polyphenol oxidase (PPO) to **CuвҒә**, inactivating the enzyme.
2. **Quinone trapping:** HSOвӮғвҒ» adds across the carbonyl group of o-quinones (the product of PPO action on phenols), forming colourless sulphone adducts:

$$Quinone + HSO_3^- \longrightarrow Quinone\text{-}HSO_3 \text{ adduct (colourless)}$$

3. **Oxygen scavenging:** SOвӮӮ consumes dissolved OвӮӮ in the tissue:

$$2 SO_2 + O_2 + 2 H_2O \rightarrow 2 H_2SO_4$$

**Role of pH:**
At lower pH (3.0 вҖ“ 4.0), a greater fraction of the total sulphite exists as the **undissociated HвӮӮSOвӮғ** form, which more readily penetrates microbial cell membranes and tissues, enhancing antimicrobial and anti-browning efficacy.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**(i) Mass of SOвӮӮ required:**

Total product mass = **1000 kg**
Target concentration = **2000 ppm = 2000 mg/kg**

$$m_{SO_2} = 2000 \frac{mg}{kg} \times 1000 \text{ kg} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg} = 2 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{SO_2} = 2.0 \text{ kg}}$$

**(ii) Mass of NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… required:**

Given the stoichiometric conversion: **1 g NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… вҶ’ 0.5 g SOвӮӮ**

Rearrange to find the reagent mass:

$$m_{Na_2S_2O_5} = \frac{m_{SO_2}}{0.5} = 2 \times 2.0 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{Na_2S_2O_5} = 4.0 \text{ kg}}$$

> **Practical Adjustment:** In industry, due to inefficiencies (volatilisation losses, incomplete reaction, oxidation to sulphate), the actual KMS dose is **1.5 вҖ“ 2Г—** the theoretical value. Hence, in practice, **6 вҖ“ 8 kg** of NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… would be recommended for 1000 kg of apricots.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing blanching with pasteurisation.** Blanching targets **enzyme inactivation**; pasteurisation targets **microbial destruction**. They are NOT the same process, even though both use heat.
> 2. **Forgetting the cooling step.** A blanching answer that omits the rapid post-heating cooling is incomplete. Cooling halts over-processing and prevents textural degradation. **(вҖ“2 marks typical deduction)**
> 3. **Mixing up D-value and z-value units.** D-value is in **time units (s, min)**; z-value is in **temperature units (В°C)**. Confusing these is a frequent error.
> 4. **Stating SOвӮӮ concentration in incorrect units.** Students often write "0.1 g" without specifying per 100 mL (w/v) or per 100 g (w/w). Always specify.
> 5. **Ignoring the Codex residual limit.** Final-product SOвӮӮ must be **вүӨ 350 ppm** (dried fruits per Codex) вҖ” failing to mention regulatory compliance loses marks.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" of peroxidase as the marker.** Examiners expect students to explain **why POD is the indicator enzyme** (highest thermal resistance, simple guaiacol test). Omitting this forfeits 1 вҖ“ 2 marks.
> 7. **Writing "sulphur" instead of "sulphur dioxide" or "sulphite".** These are chemically distinct; precision of language matters in technical exams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Blanching Essentials**
> - Blanching = short heat treatment (**80 вҖ“ 100 В°C, 1 вҖ“ 10 min**) followed by rapid cooling.
> - Primary purpose: **enzyme inactivation** (POD is the gold-standard indicator enzyme).
> - Methods: hot water, steam, hot air, microwave, IR, ohmic.
> - Trade-off: severity of treatment вҶ” nutrient/quality loss.
> - **Newton's Law of Cooling** governs surface heat transfer; **lumped capacitance** works when Bi вүӨ 0.1.
> - First-order kinetics: **N/NвӮҖ = 10^(вҖ“t/D)**; D-value = 90 % inactivation time.
> - z-value: temperature rise for 10Г— decrease in D-value.
> - Cooling is **mandatory** to stop residual heat damage.

> **SOвӮӮ Dipping Essentials**
> - SOвӮӮ = the most widely used chemical preservative in fruit processing.
> - Active species: HвӮӮSOвӮғ / HSOвӮғвҒ» / SOвӮғВІвҒ» (pH-dependent equilibrium; pKвӮҗвӮҒ = 1.81, pKвӮҗвӮӮ = 6.91).
> - Four mechanisms: enzyme inhibition, quinone trapping, OвӮӮ scavenging, antimicrobial acidification.
> - Forms used: **sodium metabisulphite (NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ…)**, **potassium metabisulphite (KвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ…)**, **liquid SOвӮӮ**, **sulphurous acid (HвӮӮSOвӮғ)**.
> - Typical dip: **0.1 вҖ“ 0.5 % (w/v)** for **30 s вҖ“ 30 min** depending on product.
> - **1 g NaвӮӮSвӮӮOвӮ… releases 0.5 g SOвӮӮ** (theoretical).
> - Regulatory limit: **вүӨ 350 ppm residual SOвӮӮ** in dried fruits (Codex Alimentarius); E-number **E220**.
> - Critical for: dried fruits (apricots, raisins, figs, mango), wine, fruit pulps, dehydrated vegetables.
> - **Drawback:** triggers asthma in sensitive individuals; declining consumer acceptance.

> **Cross-Connections (for KTU 2024 Scheme integrated questions)**
> - Blanching + SOвӮӮ dipping are **often complementary**: blanching fixes colour via enzyme kill; SOвӮӮ protects against residual/browning during storage.
> - Both are **pre-treatments** вҖ” they precede the main preservation step (canning, freezing, drying, fermentation).
> - Heat transfer in blanching вҶ” the thermal engineering module; SOвӮӮ chemistry вҶ” the food chemistry module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sorting and Grading вҖ” The Foundation of Quality Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sorting** is the separation of a heterogeneous food lot into distinct quality-based categories, while **Grading** is the classification of an already homogeneous food lot into uniform quality classes based on predefined standards.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you bring home a big sack of mangoes from the market. **Sorting** is when you separate the ripe from the unripe, the rotten from the fresh вҖ” separating *qualitatively different* units. **Grading** is when you then arrange only the good mangoes by size into a "premium large" basket, a "medium" basket, and a "small" basket вҖ” classifying *quantitatively similar* units into defined grades.

### 1.1.1 Sorting and Grading According to Size
Size-based sorting uses **aperture-based equipment**. Materials pass over screens or rollers with calibrated gaps:
- **Vibratory screens** with square or round apertures
- **Drum screens** (trommels) for round foods like potatoes
- **Roller sorters** for elongated products like carrots and cucumbers

> [!NOTE]
> Size grading is the most economical and widely used method in food industries because it can be mechanized with high throughput (typically **5вҖ“50 tonnes/hour**).

### 1.1.2 Sorting and Grading According to Shape
Shape sorting exploits **geometrical irregularities**:
- **Spiral separators** вҖ” round objects roll faster down the spiral than flat ones
- **Disk sorters** with indented pockets sized for specific shapes (e.g., separating broken rice from whole rice)
- **Inclined belt separators** for separating spherical peas from flat weed seeds

### 1.1.3 Sorting and Grading According to Weight
Weight sorting uses **mass-based differentiation**:
- **Specific gravity separators** (gravity tables) вҖ” heavier grains settle, lighter chaff floats
- **Winnowing** вҖ” air streams separate lighter chaff from heavier grain
- **Floatation tanks** вҖ” damaged/diseased produce (lower density) float while sound produce sinks

### 1.1.4 Sorting and Grading According to Colour
Colour sorting is the **most modern, non-destructive method**:
- **Optical sorters** use high-resolution CCD cameras and RGB sensors
- Defective, discolored, or foreign items are ejected by precision air jets (typically within **50 milliseconds**)
- Used in rice, pulses, coffee, nuts, and frozen vegetables

## 1.2 Peeling вҖ” The Unit Operation Before Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peeling** is the removal of the outer skin (epidermis, pericarp, or seed coat) of fruits, vegetables, and tubers to improve palatability, remove microbial contamination, reduce pesticide residues, and prepare for downstream operations.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of peeling like removing the *jacket of a book* before reading it. The "jacket" (skin/peel) often contains bitter compounds, dirt, or contaminants that you don't want to consume. Just like there are multiple ways to remove a jacket вҖ” *pulling, cutting, tearing, burning* вҖ” there are multiple peeling technologies, each suited to different products.

### 1.2.1 Flash Steam Peeling
- **Principle**: High-pressure saturated steam (**0.7вҖ“2.0 MPa**) is applied for **15вҖ“60 seconds**
- The sudden depressurization causes **instantaneous flash evaporation** within the peel tissue, rupturing the cell walls
- Peel is removed by **high-pressure water sprays** or rubber rollers
- **Applications**: Potatoes, tomatoes, peaches, sweet corn
- **Advantages**: Minimal product loss (3вҖ“8%), uniform peeling

### 1.2.2 Knife Peeling
- **Principle**: Rotating sharp blades (static or moving) scrape off the peel mechanically
- **Types**: Lathe peeling (tomatoes, apples), hand-knife peeling
- **Applications**: Apples (Spartan, Golden Delicious), peaches, pears
- **Advantages**: Precise control over peel thickness
- **Disadvantages**: High product loss (**10вҖ“25%**), labour-intensive

### 1.2.3 Abrasion Peeling
- **Principle**: Product is tumbled against an **abrasive carborundum (silicon carbide) surface** rotating at **600вҖ“1200 rpm**
- **Applications**: Potatoes (especially for chips and French fries), carrots, beetroot
- **Advantages**: Continuous, high-throughput operation
- **Disadvantages**: Surface becomes rough, may absorb oil during frying

### 1.2.4 Caustic Peeling (Lye Peeling)
- **Principle**: Food is immersed in hot **sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution** (typically **2вҖ“10% w/w**) at **80вҖ“110В°C** for **30 seconds to 5 minutes**
- NaOH dissolves the pectin layer beneath the cuticle, loosening the peel
- **Critical Step**: Thorough **water wash** to remove residual alkali
- **Applications**: Peaches, apricots, tomatoes, carrots
- **Advantages**: Minimal product loss (5вҖ“10%), ideal for delicate fruits

### 1.2.5 Flame Peeling
- **Principle**: Product passes through a **gas-fired furnace at 1000вҖ“1200В°C** for a brief exposure (**5вҖ“15 seconds**)
- The peel chars and burns off; the inner flesh remains protected by water content
- **Applications**: Onions, garlic, shallots
- **Advantages**: Extremely fast, removes only the outermost papery skin

## 1.3 Size Reduction вҖ” The Macro-to-Micro Transformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Size Reduction** is the mechanical process of breaking larger food particles into smaller ones by applying compressive, shear, impact, or attrition forces. It is essential for increasing surface area, improving extraction efficiency, and enabling uniform mixing.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of size reduction like **crushing a sugar cube into fine powder**. To break the cube, you must put in energy (your hand pressure, then grinding). Three scientists (Rittinger, Kick, and Bond) studied how this energy relates to the change in particle size вҖ” and they came up with *three different mathematical laws* for three different size ranges!

### The Three Laws of Size Reduction Energy

| Law | Scientist | Year | Assumption | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rittinger's Law | P.R. von Rittinger | 1867 | Energy вҲқ New Surface Area | Fine grinding |
| Kick's Law | F. Kick | 1885 | Energy вҲҘ Volume Ratio | Coarse crushing |
| Bond's Law | F.C. Bond | 1952 | Energy вҲқ Crack Length | Intermediate grinding |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory вҖ” Sorting and Grading

### 2.1.1 Size Grading вҖ” Operational Logic Steps
1. **Feed presentation**: Material is fed onto the screen via vibratory feeders for uniform distribution
2. **Aperture selection**: Screens chosen based on **Sieve Aperture Size (mm)** with openings ranging from 5 mm (small seeds) to 100 mm (large fruits)
3. **Vibration mechanics**: Eccentric motor produces vibrations of amplitude **2вҖ“10 mm** at frequency **800вҖ“1500 rpm**
4. **Stratification**: Smaller particles "percolate" downward while larger ones "ride" the top layer (Brazilian nut effect)
5. **Discharge**: Oversize, midsize, and undersize fractions exit at separate chutes

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Sieve Series**: ISO 565 (preferred in KTU syllabus), ASTM E11, Tyler series. Common sizes: **500 Вөm, 1 mm, 2 mm, 4 mm, 8 mm**.

### 2.1.2 Shape Grading вҖ” Physics Behind Separation
- The **drag coefficient (C_D)** differs by shape: spheres вүҲ 0.44, flat disks вүҲ 1.1
- Round objects gain higher **terminal velocity** in air streams
- On inclined surfaces, spherical items experience **rolling friction** while flat items experience **static friction** (Ој_s > Ој_r)

### 2.1.3 Weight/Weight Grading вҖ” Density-Based Logic
- **Specific Gravity (SG)** = ПҒ_material / ПҒ_water
- Sound grains (wheat SG вүҲ 1.35) sink in water; damaged grains (SG < 1.0) float
- **Floatation velocity** depends on Stoke's Law: $v = \frac{d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu}$

Where:
- $d$ = particle diameter
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/sВІ**)
- $\rho_s$ = particle density
- $\rho_f$ = fluid density
- $\mu$ = fluid viscosity

### 2.1.4 Colour Grading вҖ” Optoelectronic Workflow
1. **Illumination**: LED lights of specific wavelengths (**620 nm** red, **520 nm** green) reflect off product
2. **Capture**: CCD line-scan camera captures RGB signature at 4000+ pixels/line
3. **Comparison**: Software compares each pixel to reference color space (e.g., Hunter L*a*b*)
4. **Decision**: Pixels outside threshold trigger **ejection pulse**
5. **Ejection**: Compressed air jet (at **2.5 bar**) ejects defect within **50 ms**
6. **Throughput**: Modern optical sorters achieve **5вҖ“15 tonnes/hour**

## 2.2 Detailed Theory вҖ” Peeling Methods

### 2.2.1 Flash Steam Peeling вҖ” Thermo-Mechanism
- Steam condenses on the cooler peel surface, releasing **latent heat (вүҲ 2257 kJ/kg)**
- Rapid pressure release causes water inside peel cells to vaporize
- **Steam penetration depth** вүҲ 1вҖ“3 mm
- Peel loosened but not cooked вҖ” preserves color and texture

### 2.2.2 Knife Peeling вҖ” Mechanical Geometry
- **Lathe Peeler Setup**: Product rotates on driven rolls at **5вҖ“10 rpm**; stationary blade advances parallel to surface
- **Peel thickness control** via micrometer adjustment (**0.5вҖ“3 mm**)
- Critical for "peeled tomato" canned products requiring intact shape

### 2.2.3 Abrasion Peeling вҖ” Tribological Concept
- **Carborundum (SiC) grit size**: **20вҖ“60 mesh**
- **Contact pressure**: 5вҖ“20 kPa on product surface
- **Frictional shear** removes peel layer cell by cell
- Continuously sprayed with water to wash away peel fragments

### 2.2.4 Caustic Peeling вҖ” Chemical Mechanism
$$[\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_6(\text{COOCH}_3)]_n + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Soluble Pectates}$$

- NaOH **saponifies waxes** in the cuticle
- NaOH **hydrolyzes pectin** in middle lamella between peel cells
- **Critical control**: Lye concentration Г— time Г— temperature = **constant yield**
- Residual lye neutralized with dilute citric acid (0.5вҖ“1%) wash

### 2.2.5 Flame Peeling вҖ” Combustion Kinetics
- Combustion of cellulose: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Only the **dry, papery outer skin** burns вҖ” high water content (~80%) protects inner layers
- Speed: ~10 m/min through the furnace

## 2.3 Detailed Theory вҖ” Energy Consumption Laws

### 2.3.1 Rittinger's Law (1867) вҖ” Surface Area Hypothesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Postulate**: The energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the **newly created surface area**.

**Reasoning (Why)**: When a solid is broken, energy is needed to create new surfaces against which the molecular forces of the solid must act. The greater the new surface, the more the energy consumed.

**Derivation Logic (How)**:
- Consider a cube of side $L$, density $\rho_p$
- Original surface area: $A_1 = 6L^2$, Volume: $V = L^3$
- After crushing to $N$ cubes of side $L_c$:
  - New surface area: $A_2 = 6N \cdot L_c^2 = 6L^3/L_c$
  - New surface created: $\Delta A = 6L^3(1/L_c - 1/L)$

**Result**: $E \propto \Delta A \propto (1/D_P - 1/D_F)$

### 2.3.2 Kick's Law (1885) вҖ” Volume Ratio Hypothesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Postulate**: The energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **logarithmic ratio of the volumes (or masses) before and after crushing**.

**Reasoning (Why)**: Kick assumed that particles are homogeneous and that the work done is to deform them plastically before fracture. He equated the strain energy stored in a stressed body to the breaking stress, treating it as a volume phenomenon.

**Derivation Logic (How)**:
- Strain energy in elastic body: $E_{strain} = \frac{\sigma^2 \cdot V}{2E}$
- Ratio of strain energies before and after crushing: $E_1/E_2 = V_1/V_2 = D_F^3/D_P^3$ (for similar shapes)

**Result**: $E \propto \ln(V_F/V_P) = \ln(D_F/D_P)$

### 2.3.3 Bond's Law (1952) вҖ” Crack Length Hypothesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Postulate**: The energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **length of the crack formed** during fracture.

**Reasoning (Why)**: Bond postulated that fracture occurs via crack propagation. The energy per unit crack length is constant. For an isotropic cube, the crack length scales as $\sqrt{\text{volume}}$, i.e., with $D$.

**Result**: $E \propto (\sqrt{D_F} - \sqrt{D_P})$ or equivalently $(1/\sqrt{D_P} - 1/\sqrt{D_F})$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Law | Equation | Constants/Units | Applicable Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rittinger's Law** | $E = K_R \left(\dfrac{1}{D_P} - \dfrac{1}{D_F}\right)$ | $E$ = kWh/ton, $D$ in mm, $K_R$ in kWhВ·mm/ton | Fine grinding (D < 1 mm) |
| **Kick's Law** | $E = K_K \ln\left(\dfrac{D_F}{D_P}\right)$ | $E$ = kWh/ton, $D$ in mm, $K_K$ in kWh/ton | Coarse crushing (D > 50 mm) |
| **Bond's Law** | $E = K_B \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)$ | $E$ = kWh/ton, $D$ in $\mu$m, $K_B = 10\,W_i$ | Intermediate (1 mm < D < 50 mm) |
| **Work Index Relation** | $W = W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$ | $W$ = kWh/ton, $W_i$ in kWh/ton | Bond's standard form |
| **Rittinger Number** | $N_R = \dfrac{E}{K_R \cdot \Delta(1/D)}$ | Dimensionless | Material comparison |
| **Kick's Constant** | $K_K = E/\ln(D_F/D_P)$ | kWh/ton | Material comparison |
| **Bond Work Index** | $W_i = W / [(1/\sqrt{P}) - (1/\sqrt{F})]$ | kWh/ton | Standard metric |

### Typical Bond Work Index Values (for KTU reference)

| Material | $W_i$ (kWh/ton) |
|---|---|
| Wheat (ground) | 8.0 |
| Corn (shelled) | 7.5 |
| Sugar (crystal) | 11.0 |
| Limestone | 11.6 |
| Barite | 6.0 |
| Coffee beans | 9.0 |

### Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Rittinger's Law** is preferred for **fine grinding** (ball mills, jet mills) where new surface area creation is the dominant cost
> - **Kick's Law** is preferred for **coarse crushing** (jaw crushers, gyratory crushers) where elastic deformation dominates
> - **Bond's Law** is the **industry standard** for sizing crushers and mills using the **Work Index ($W_i$)** concept вҖ” universally used in design of size reduction equipment

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation вҖ” Rittinger's Law

**Starting Assumption**: Energy required вҲқ New surface area created.

Consider a single cube of side $D_F$ (feed) being broken into $N$ cubes of side $D_P$ (product).

**Step 1 вҖ” Original Surface Area**:
$$A_{\text{original}} = 6 \cdot D_F^2$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Volume Conservation** (mass and volume are conserved):
$$V_{\text{original}} = V_{\text{new}} \implies D_F^3 = N \cdot D_P^3$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Solve for N**:
$$N = \left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 вҖ” New Surface Area of All Smaller Cubes**:
$$A_{\text{new}} = N \cdot 6 \cdot D_P^2 = \left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)^3 \cdot 6 \cdot D_P^2 = \frac{6 \cdot D_F^3}{D_P}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Newly Created Surface Area**:
$$\Delta A = A_{\text{new}} - A_{\text{original}} = \frac{6 D_F^3}{D_P} - 6 D_F^2 = 6 D_F^2 \left(\frac{D_F}{D_P} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Express in terms of $1/D$ ratio**:
$$\Delta A = 6 D_F^2 \cdot D_F \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right) = 6 D_F^3 \left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right)$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Energy per unit mass** (divide by mass $m = \rho_p D_F^3$):
$$\frac{E}{\text{unit mass}} \propto \Delta A / m = \frac{6 D_F^3}{\rho_p D_F^3}\left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right) = \frac{6}{\rho_p}\left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right)$$

**Step 8 вҖ” Combine constant $6/\rho_p$ with proportionality into $K_R$**:
$$\boxed{E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right)}$$

Where $E$ is energy per unit mass (kWh/ton) and $K_R$ is **Rittinger's constant** (kWhВ·mm/ton).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation вҖ” Kick's Law

**Starting Assumption**: Energy required to produce a proportional reduction in volume is constant.

**Step 1 вҖ” Strain Energy in a Cube**:
Under uniform compressive stress $\sigma$, the strain energy stored in an elastic body of volume $V$ is:
$$E_{\text{strain}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E_{\text{modulus}}} \cdot V$$

Where $E_{\text{modulus}}$ is Young's modulus.

**Step 2 вҖ” For Cubes of Different Sizes but Same Stress**:
If the crushing stress $\sigma$ is the same (assumed for similar material):
$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Express Volumes in terms of characteristic dimension**:
$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Take natural logarithm** (work done per unit mass):
$$E = K_K \ln\left(\frac{V_F}{V_P}\right) = K_K \ln\left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)^3 = 3 K_K \ln\left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Absorb factor 3 into redefined constant**:
$$\boxed{E = K_K \ln\left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)}$$

Where $K_K$ is **Kick's constant** (kWh/ton).

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation вҖ” Bond's Law

**Starting Assumption**: Energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **length of the crack** formed during fracture.

**Step 1 вҖ” Geometry of a Cube**:
For a cube of side $D$, the longest internal crack that splits it has a length $L_c \propto D$.
Specifically, if the cube is viewed as an octahedron of equivalent diameter $D$, the longest diagonal crack length is:
$$L_c = D \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Energy per unit crack length is constant**:
$$E \propto L_c \propto D$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Bond considered the energy for a *differential* size reduction**:
$$\frac{dE}{dD} = -\frac{K_B}{2 \sqrt{D}}$$

(Energy decreases as particle size decreases during reduction.)

**Step 4 вҖ” Integrate from $D_F$ to $D_P$**:
$$\int_0^E dE = -\frac{K_B}{2} \int_{D_F}^{D_P} \frac{dD}{\sqrt{D}}$$

$$E = -\frac{K_B}{2} \cdot 2\left[\sqrt{D}\right]_{D_F}^{D_P} = -K_B \left[\sqrt{D_P} - \sqrt{D_F}\right]$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Rearrange to standard form**:
$$E = K_B \left(\sqrt{D_F} - \sqrt{D_P}\right)$$

But the more common form uses $1/\sqrt{D}$ by rationalizing:

Let $D_F$ and $D_P$ be in micrometers. Express the difference:
$$\sqrt{D_F} - \sqrt{D_P} = \sqrt{D_F \cdot D_P} \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)$$

Since for many size reduction operations $\sqrt{D_F \cdot D_P} \approx$ constant (or absorbed into $W_i$):

$$\boxed{E = K_B \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)}$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Industry-Standard Work Index Form**:
Bond defined $K_B = 10\,W_i$ for $D$ in micrometers:
$$W = 10\,W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$

Equivalently:
$$\boxed{W = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)}$$

Where:
- $W$ = specific energy consumption (kWh/ton)
- $W_i$ = **Bond Work Index** (kWh/ton) вҖ” measured by standard test
- $P$ = 80% passing size of product ($\mu$m)
- $F$ = 80% passing size of feed ($\mu$m)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Bond's Law

**Problem**: A ball mill grinds limestone from feed size $F = 2000\,\mu\text{m}$ (80% passing) to product size $P = 75\,\mu\text{m}$ (80% passing). The Bond Work Index of limestone is $W_i = 11.6\,\text{kWh/ton}$. Calculate the specific energy consumption.

**Solution**:

$$W = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$

$$W = 11.6 \times \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{75}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2000}}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{75} = 8.660, \quad \sqrt{2000} = 44.721$$

$$\frac{1}{8.660} = 0.1155, \quad \frac{1}{44.721} = 0.02236$$

$$W = 11.6 \times (0.1155 - 0.02236) = 11.6 \times 0.09314$$

$$\boxed{W = 1.08\,\text{kWh/ton}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix вҖ” The Three Laws

| Aspect | Kick (1885) | Bond (1952) | Rittinger (1867) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reduction Ratio** $R = D_F/D_P$ | Energy вҲқ $\ln R$ | Energy вҲқ $\sqrt{R} - 1$ | Energy вҲқ $R - 1$ |
| **At $R \to 1$ (small reduction)** | $E \to 0$ (valid) | $E \to 0$ (valid) | $E \to 0$ (valid) |
| **At $R \to \infty$ (large reduction)** | $E \to \infty$ (slowly) | $E \to \infty$ (medium) | $E \to \infty$ (rapidly) |
| **Order of Magnitude (R=10)** | $K \cdot 2.303$ | $K \cdot 2.162$ | $K \cdot 9.0$ |
| **Particle Size Range** | $>50$ mm | 1вҖ“50 mm | $<1$ mm |
| **Dominant Mechanism** | Elastic deformation | Crack propagation | Surface creation |
| **Use in Design** | Rare | **Standard in industry** | Specialty mills |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip**: For KTU problems, identify the **size range** first:
> - If $D > 50$ mm вҶ’ **Kick's Law**
> - If $1 < D < 50$ mm вҶ’ **Bond's Law** (most likely!)
> - If $D < 1$ mm вҶ’ **Rittinger's Law**

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Rittinger's Law

**Problem**: Sugar crystals are ground from 2 mm to 0.2 mm average size. Rittinger's constant for sugar is $K_R = 0.015\,\text{kWhВ·mm/ton}$. Calculate the energy required.

**Solution**:

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right)$$

$$E = 0.015 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.2} - \frac{1}{2}\right) = 0.015 \times (5 - 0.5)$$

$$E = 0.015 \times 4.5$$

$$\boxed{E = 0.0675\,\text{kWh/ton}}$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Kick's Law

**Problem**: A jaw crusher crushes limestone rocks from 100 mm to 25 mm. Kick's constant is $K_K = 1.5\,\text{kWh/ton}$. Calculate the energy required per ton.

**Solution**:

$$E = K_K \ln\left(\frac{D_F}{D_P}\right)$$

$$E = 1.5 \times \ln\left(\frac{100}{25}\right) = 1.5 \times \ln(4)$$

$$\ln(4) = 1.3863$$

$$\boxed{E = 2.08\,\text{kWh/ton}}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sorting and Grading. Give any two differences between them.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer**:

**Sorting** is the process of separating a heterogeneous food lot into distinct categories based on **quality** attributes (e.g., defective vs. sound, ripe vs. unripe). It removes **foreign matter, damaged units, and off-grade items** before further processing.

**Grading** is the process of classifying an already homogeneous (sorted) food lot into **uniform quality classes** (Grade A, B, C) based on **quantitative attributes** like size, weight, colour, or shape.

**Two Differences**:

| Aspect | Sorting | Grading |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Qualitative (yes/no decision) | Quantitative (measured parameter) |
| **Objective** | Remove defects and unwanted material | Categorize acceptable material into uniform classes |
| **Equipment** | Optical sorters, manual belts, gravity separators | Screens, roller sorters, weight graders |
| **Number of Outputs** | Usually 2 (accept/reject) | Usually multiple grades (A, B, C) |

---

### Question 2: State Rittinger's Law of Size Reduction. Mention the range of particle size for which it is most applicable.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer**:

**Statement**: Rittinger's Law (1867) states that the **energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area created** during the reduction process.

**Mathematical Form**:
$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_P} - \frac{1}{D_F}\right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = specific energy consumption (kWh/ton)
- $K_R$ = Rittinger's constant
- $D_F$ = feed size, $D_P$ = product size

**Applicable Range**: **Fine grinding** where particle size is **less than 1 mm** (e.g., ball mills, jet mills producing fine powders).

**Assumption**: All energy consumed is used in creating new surface area; no energy is lost as heat or sound.

---

## PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Question on Sorting, Grading, and Peeling

**(a)** Explain in detail the **optical colour sorting method** used in food industries. List any **three advantages** of optical sorters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the principle, working, and applications of **caustic peeling**. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages** compared to **abrasion peeling**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) вҖ” Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Optical Colour Sorting вҖ” Detailed Working Principle**:

**Step 1 вҖ” Feed Presentation**:
The food material (e.g., rice, pulses, nuts) is fed through a vibratory feeder onto a chute. The chute is designed to accelerate the product and present individual items in **single file** to the inspection zone, ensuring each piece is viewed separately.

**Step 2 вҖ” Illumination**:
High-intensity **LED arrays** illuminate the product with specific wavelengths:
- **Red LEDs (620 nm)** for detecting yellowish/brown defects
- **Green LEDs (520 nm)** for detecting chlorophyll-based defects
- **Infrared LEDs (NIR, 850вҖ“1450 nm)** for detecting foreign materials and internal defects invisible to the eye

**Step 3 вҖ” Image Capture**:
A **CCD line-scan camera** with **4000+ pixels** captures the reflected light as the product falls past. Each pixel records an RGB value, building a high-resolution colour signature of every item.

**Step 4 вҖ” Image Processing and Comparison**:
The digital signal processor converts RGB values to the **Hunter L*a*b* colour space** and compares each pixel against the **pre-programmed reference colour**. Items falling outside the acceptable range are flagged as defects.

**Step 5 вҖ” Ejection**:
A **precision air jet valve** (operating at **2.5 bar**, response time **<5 ms**) fires a focused air pulse within **50 milliseconds** of detection, deflecting the defective item into a reject chute. Sound product continues into the accept stream.

**Three Advantages of Optical Sorters**:
1. **Non-destructive** вҖ” does not physically touch the product
2. **High accuracy** вҖ” can detect defects as small as **0.5 mmВІ**
3. **High throughput** вҖ” modern machines process **5вҖ“15 tonnes/hour**
4. Labour cost reduction and **consistent, repeatable** quality
5. Can detect **internal defects** using NIR (e.g., insect-damaged grains, hollow hearts)

---

#### Part (b) вҖ” Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Caustic Peeling вҖ” Detailed Description**:

**Principle**:
Caustic (lye) peeling uses a hot solution of **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)**, typically **2вҖ“10% w/w concentration**, at temperatures of **80вҖ“110В°C**. The lye dissolves the waxy cuticle and hydrolyzes the pectin in the middle lamella вҖ” the "glue" that holds peel cells together. The peel loosens and can be washed off easily.

**Chemical Reaction**:
$$[\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_6(\text{COOCH}_3)]_n + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Sodium Pectate (soluble)}$$

**Working Procedure**:
1. **Pre-heating**: Product is warmed to reduce thermal shock
2. **Lye treatment**: Product immersed in hot NaOH for **30 seconds to 5 minutes**
3. **Reaction dwell**: A hold period (typically 1вҖ“2 minutes) allows penetration
4. **Water spray wash**: High-pressure water removes loosened peel
5. **Acid neutralization**: Dilute citric acid (0.5вҖ“1%) neutralizes residual alkali
6. **Final rinse**: Clean water wash completes the process

**Applications**: Peaches, apricots, tomatoes, carrots, sweet potatoes, plums

**Advantages of Caustic Peeling**:
- Low product loss (**5вҖ“10%** vs. 10вҖ“25% for knife peeling)
- Suitable for irregular shapes that cannot be peeled mechanically
- Continuous, high-throughput operation possible
- Uniform peeling with no surface bruising

**Disadvantages of Caustic Peeling**:
- Requires careful **wastewater treatment** (high pH effluent)
- Residual alkali must be thoroughly neutralized (food safety concern)
- Higher capital cost than manual peeling
- Skilled operators required for concentration and temperature control

**Comparison with Abrasion Peeling**:

| Parameter | Caustic Peeling | Abrasion Peeling |
|---|---|---|
| Product loss | 5вҖ“10% | 10вҖ“15% |
| Suitable products | Soft fruits (peach, apricot) | Root vegetables (potato, carrot) |
| Surface finish | Smooth | Rough (affects oil absorption in frying) |
| Capital cost | High | Moderate |
| Wastewater concern | High (alkaline) | Low (mostly organic) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Comprehensive Question on Energy Consumption Laws

**(a)** State and derive **Bond's Law** of size reduction. Explain its significance with reference to **Work Index ($W_i$)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **hammer mill** grinds wheat from feed size $F = 1500\,\mu\text{m}$ (80% passing) to product size $P = 250\,\mu\text{m}$ (80% passing). The Bond Work Index for wheat is $W_i = 8.0\,\text{kWh/ton}$. Calculate the **specific energy consumption** using Bond's Law. If the mill processes **5 tonnes per hour**, what is the **power requirement** of the motor (assuming 90% mechanical efficiency)? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) вҖ” Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Statement of Bond's Law**:
> *The energy required for size reduction is proportional to the length of the crack formed during fracture, which in turn is proportional to the square root of the diameter of the particle.*

**Mathematical Form**:
$$W = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$

**Derivation**:

Consider a cubic particle of side $D$. When the particle fractures along its longest internal crack, the crack length $L_c$ is proportional to the diagonal of the cube:
$$L_c = D\sqrt{2}$$

Hence, the energy per unit crack length being constant:
$$E \propto L_c \propto D$$

Differentiating with respect to $D$ (energy decreases as size decreases):
$$\frac{dE}{dD} = -\frac{K_B}{2\sqrt{D}}$$

Integrating from feed size $F$ to product size $P$:
$$W = \int_P^F \frac{K_B}{2\sqrt{D}}\,dD = K_B \left(\sqrt{F} - \sqrt{P}\right)$$

Rewriting in standard form by rationalizing and absorbing constants, and defining $K_B = 10\,W_i$ for $D$ in $\mu$m:
$$\boxed{W = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)}$$

**Significance of Work Index ($W_i$)**:
- $W_i$ is the **energy required to reduce one ton** of material from infinite size to 80% passing **100 $\mu$m**
- It is a **fundamental material property**, measured by a standard **Bond Ball Mill Test**
- **Higher $W_i$** вҶ’ material is harder to grind вҶ’ more energy needed
- Used universally in **mill sizing and power calculation**
- Typical $W_i$ values: Limestone = 11.6, Wheat = 8.0, Sugar = 11.0, Barite = 6.0 kWh/ton

---

#### Part (b) вҖ” Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data**:
- Feed size $F = 1500\,\mu\text{m}$
- Product size $P = 250\,\mu\text{m}$
- Bond Work Index $W_i = 8.0\,\text{kWh/ton}$
- Throughput = $5\,\text{tonnes/hour}$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta = 90\% = 0.90$

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply Bond's Law**:
$$W = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Calculate the square roots**:
$$\sqrt{P} = \sqrt{250} = 15.811\,\mu\text{m}^{1/2}$$
$$\sqrt{F} = \sqrt{1500} = 38.730\,\mu\text{m}^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Calculate the reciprocals**:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} = \frac{1}{15.811} = 0.06325$$
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} = \frac{1}{38.730} = 0.02582$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Compute the difference**:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} = 0.06325 - 0.02582 = 0.03743$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Compute the specific energy**:
$$W = 8.0 \times 0.03743$$
$$\boxed{W = 0.2994\,\text{kWh/ton}}$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Compute the power required (input energy)**:
$$\text{Energy per hour} = 0.2994 \times 5 = 1.497\,\text{kWh/hour}$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Convert to kW and account for efficiency**:
$$\text{Useful Power} = 1.497\,\text{kW}$$
$$\text{Actual Motor Power} = \frac{\text{Useful Power}}{\eta} = \frac{1.497}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{motor}} = 1.66\,\text{kW}}$$

A **2 kW motor** (standard next size) should be selected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls
>
> **1. Wrong Law for Wrong Size Range** (2вҖ“3 marks lost per problem!)
> Students frequently apply Bond's Law when feed size is in cm (use Kick's) or in nm (use Rittinger's). **Always check the size range first.**
>
> **2. Units Inconsistency** (1вҖ“2 marks lost)
> Bond's Law strictly requires $F$ and $P$ in **micrometers** (Ојm). Converting to mm gives answers off by **a factor of ~31.6**. Always state units clearly.
>
> **3. Forgetting the Efficiency Factor**
> When asked for motor power, students often quote useful power without dividing by efficiency (О·). KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
>
> **4. Peeling Question вҖ” Confusing the Methods**
> Caustic peeling uses **NaOH** (alkali), NOT acid. Flame peeling is for **bulbs only** (onion, garlic). Flash steam uses **steam + sudden depressurization**, not direct flame. Misnaming reagents/processes loses 2 marks.
>
> **5. Sorting vs. Grading Mix-up**
> **Sorting** = qualitative separation (defect removal). **Grading** = quantitative classification (Grade A, B, C). Many students interchange these in definitions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Unit 1: Sorting and Grading
> - **Sorting** = qualitative separation (defect removal); **Grading** = quantitative classification (uniform quality classes)
> - **Size grading**: Vibratory screens, drum screens, roller sorters; uses aperture-based separation
> - **Shape grading**: Spiral separators (round vs. flat), disk sorters (pocket-sized for shape)
> - **Weight grading**: Gravity tables, floatation tanks, winnowing; uses SG differences
> - **Colour grading**: CCD cameras, RGB to L*a*b* conversion, air-jet ejection in **<50 ms**
