> - Throughput of optical sorters: **5вҖ“15 tonnes/hour**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Unit 2: Peeling Methods
> - **Flash steam**: Steam **0.7вҖ“2 MPa** for 15вҖ“60 s вҶ’ flash evaporation ruptures peel cells вҶ’ peel loss 3вҖ“8%
> - **Knife**: Lathe peeling at **5вҖ“10 rpm**, peel thickness 0.5вҖ“3 mm; loss 10вҖ“25%
> - **Abrasion**: Carborundum (SiC) at **600вҖ“1200 rpm**; loss 10вҖ“15%; rough surface
> - **Caustic (Lye)**: **NaOH 2вҖ“10% w/w at 80вҖ“110В°C**; hydrolyzes pectin; loss 5вҖ“10%; needs acid neutralization
> - **Flame**: Gas furnace at **1000вҖ“1200В°C** for 5вҖ“15 s; only for bulbs (onion, garlic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Unit 3: Size Reduction Energy Laws
> - **Kick's Law (1885)**: $E = K_K \ln(D_F/D_P)$ вҶ’ **Coarse crushing** (D > 50 mm)
> - **Rittinger's Law (1867)**: $E = K_R(1/D_P - 1/D_F)$ вҶ’ **Fine grinding** (D < 1 mm)
> - **Bond's Law (1952)**: $W = W_i(1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F})$ вҶ’ **Intermediate** (1 < D < 50 mm); **MOST USED in industry**
> - **Bond Work Index** ($W_i$): Energy to grind 1 ton from infinite size to **80% passing 100 Ојm**; units **kWh/ton**
> - For $W_i$ values: Wheat = 8.0, Corn = 7.5, Limestone = 11.6, Sugar = 11.0, Barite = 6.0
> - **Motor Power** = (Throughput Г— Specific Energy) / Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> ### Final KTU Exam Mantra
> 1. Identify the **size range** вҶ’ Choose the **correct law**
> 2. Maintain **unit consistency** (Ојm for Bond's Law)
> 3. Convert **kWh/ton to kW** by multiplying with throughput (ton/h)
> 4. Divide by **efficiency (О·)** for actual motor power
> 5. State **assumptions** of the law used
> 6. **Peeling** = matching method to product (soft fruit вҶ’ lye; root veg вҶ’ abrasion; bulbs вҶ’ flame)
> 7. **Sorting vs. Grading** = qualitative vs. quantitative вҖ” never interchange!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Size Reduction Equipments for Solids - Impact Mills, Pressure Mills, Attrition Mills, Cutters, Choppers, Shredders, Pulpers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Size Reduction Equipments for Solids - Impact Mills, Pressure Mills, Attrition Mills, Cutters, Choppers, Shredders, Pulpers ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Size Reduction** is a unit operation in food engineering wherein solid food materials are mechanically broken down into smaller particles, pieces, or granules. The reduction is achieved by applying one or more mechanical forces such as **impact**, **compression (pressure)**, **attrition (shear/friction)**, and **cutting**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, size reduction equipment is broadly classified based on the **dominant size reduction principle** employed and the **physical state / size range of the feed material**: - **Impact Mills** (Hammer mills, Disintegrators) - **Pressure / Crushing Mills** (Roller mills, Crushing rolls, Ball mills, Edge runners) - **Attrition Mills** (Disc mills, Buhr stone mills) - **Cutting Mills / Cutters** - **Choppers** - **Shredders** - **Pulpers** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > In Module 1, the focus is on **identifying the correct equipment for a given food material** and the **fundamental operating principle** behind each. Numerical design of mills is generally asked in advanced subjects like *Food Process Equipment Design*; here, descriptive and comparative answers are valued heavily. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” "The Kitchen Perspective" Imagine you have a whole **raw mango**. To make aam-papad (mango pulp leather) you need to first break it into pieces вҖ” you chop it with a knife (**chopper**), then crush the pieces to release juice (**pulper**). If you had to make mango powder, you would dry it first and then grind it in a heavy stone grinder (**attrition mill**). If you wanted coarse mango flour, you might use a pounding action (**pressure mill / roller mill**). If you wanted fine powder, you would use a high-speed hammer-like action (**impact mill**). **Therefore, the *type of force*, the *moisture content*, and the *desired final particle size* together determine the choice of equipment.** ### 1.3 Why is Size Reduction Important in Food Engineering? - Increases **surface area per unit mass**, improving heat and mass transfer (drying, extraction, leaching). - Facilitates **uniform mixing** of solid ingredients. - Enables **separation** processes (sieving, classification). - Improves **digestibility** and **palatability** of foods. - Helps in **packaging and storage efficiency**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Bond Work Index ($W_i$)** вҖ” measured in **kWh/ton** (or **kWh/short ton**) - **Particle size ($D$)** вҖ” measured in **micrometers ($\mu m$)** or **millimeters (mm)** - **Reduction Ratio ($RR$)** = $D_{feed} / D_{product}$ - **Surface-to-volume ratio** of food particles: $A/V = 6/D$ (for spheres) - **Density ($\rho$)** of food materials typically: $400 - 1600 \ kg/m^3$ - **Standard sieve sizes**: Tyler series, US Standard (e.g., 100 mesh = $149 \ \mu m$) > [!NOTE] > **Definition вҖ” Reduction Ratio (RR):** > $RR = \dfrac{D_{feed}}{D_{product}}$ > A high RR (e.g., > 50) is generally associated with impact mills; a low RR (e.g., 2вҖ“5) is associated with pressure/roller mills. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification Based on the Dominant Force The four fundamental mechanical forces used in size reduction are: 1. **Impact** вҖ” sudden collision of a particle with a moving hammer/striker. 2. **Compression / Pressure** вҖ” slow squeezing between two surfaces. 3. **Attrition / Shear** вҖ” rubbing of particles against each other or a surface. 4. **Cutting** вҖ” sharp blades slicing through the material. > [!NOTE] > Most real-world equipment uses a **combination** of these forces; classification is based on the **dominant** force. ### 2.2 Detailed Operating Principle of Each Equipment #### A. Impact Mills - **Examples:** Hammer mill, Pin mill, Disintegrator, Cage mill. - **Principle:** A high-speed rotor carries **hammers (or pins)**. Feed enters the grinding chamber and is struck repeatedly by the hammers. The particle is also impacted against **breaker plates / grinding screens**, causing fracture. - **Energy transfer:** **Kinetic energy** of the hammer is converted into fracture energy of the particle. - **Best suited for:** Brittle, dry, low-to-medium moisture foods вҖ” spices, sugar, grains (maize, wheat), dried herbs. - **Reduction ratio:** **High (40вҖ“400)**. - **KTU high-yield fact:** Hammer mills can be **gravity-fed** or **air-swept**; the latter uses a stream of air to carry fines out, reducing over-grinding and heat build-up. #### B. Pressure / Compression Mills - **Examples:** Roller mill, Crushing rolls, Ball mill, Edge runner mill, Gyratory crusher. - **Principle:** Material is **squeezed** between two rotating surfaces (rolls) or between balls and the mill wall. Compression creates fracture planes along the weakest path in the particle. - **Roller mill:** Two rolls rotating at slightly different speeds (differential speed = 1.2 to 1.5 for typical grain milling; 2.5вҖ“3.0 for some applications). One roll may be fluted / corrugated for the **break rolls**, while the other is smooth for **reduction rolls**. - **Ball mill:** Tumbling action вҖ” cascading and cataracting of balls. Used for **fine and ultra-fine grinding** of cocoa, sugar, spices. - **Best suited for:** Tough, abrasive, dry materials. Roller mills are the workhorse of the **flour milling industry**. - **Reduction ratio:** **Low to moderate (2вҖ“20 for rolls; 50+ for ball mills).** #### C. Attrition Mills - **Examples:** Disc mill (Buhr mill), Stone mill, Toothed disc mill. - **Principle:** Particles are subjected to a **shearing / rubbing action** between two rough surfaces (typically stones or grooved discs). One disc is stationary while the other rotates at high speed (3,000 вҖ“ 10,000 rpm). - **Best suited for:** Soft, fibrous, oily foods вҖ” nuts, cocoa nibs, pulses (after dehusking), wet grains. - **Reduction ratio:** **Moderate (10вҖ“50).** - **KTU point:** Attrition mills generate **moderate heat** вҖ” so they are preferred for heat-sensitive foods but must be used cautiously with very sticky materials. #### D. Cutters - **Examples:** Rotary cutters, Guillotine cutters, Dicers. - **Principle:** Material is **sliced** using rotating blades. The blade has a **shear edge** and an **oblique edge** вҖ” the oblique edge reduces the cutting force. - **Best suited for:** Tough, fibrous, non-brittle materials вҖ” meat, vegetables, fruits for canning, nuts. - **Important parameter:** **Cutting speed** must exceed the **critical speed**; otherwise the material will just be pressed and not cut. #### E. Choppers - **Examples:** Bowl choppers, Bowl cutters (e.g., **Silencer-type choppers** used in meat processing). - **Principle:** Material is reduced in a **revolving bowl** with **high-speed rotating blades**. The bowl and blades counter-rotate to ensure uniform cutting. - **Used for:** Meat emulsions, sausages, pГўtГ©, frozen meat blocks. - **Distinct feature:** Operates in **batch mode** and produces a **paste / emulsion** rather than discrete particles. #### F. Shredders - **Examples:** Hammer-type shredders, Rotary shredders. - **Principle:** Combines **cutting + impact**. Material is shredded into **long, thin strips** rather than powder. - **Best suited for:** Vegetables (cabbage for coleslaw, carrots), cheese, dry fruits. - **Output shape:** **Strips / flakes**, not powder. #### G. Pulpers - **Examples:** Fruit pulper, Pulper-finisher, Hammer pulper. - **Principle:** Combines **crushing + sieving**. Whole fruits are crushed and the pulp is forced through a **perforated screen**; seeds, skin and stalks are ejected as waste (pomace). - **Used for:** Mango pulp, tomato puree, guava pulp, banana puree. - **Distinct feature:** Performs **size reduction + separation** simultaneously. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Standard Units | |---|---------|---------------|----------------| | 1 | $E = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1} \right)$ | **Rittinger's Law** вҖ” energy вҲқ new surface area created | $E$ in kWh/ton, $D$ in $\mu m$ | | 2 | $E = K_K \ln\left( \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} \right)$ | **Kick's Law** вҖ” energy вҲқ volume reduction (useful for coarse crushing) | $E$ in kWh/ton | | 3 | $E = K_B \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} \right)$ | **Bond's Law** вҖ” energy вҲқ crack length; **most widely used** | $E$ in kWh/ton | | 4 | $W = 10 \, W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$ | **Bond Work Index Equation** вҖ” power required per ton of feed | $W$ in kWh/ton | | 5 | $RR = \dfrac{D_{feed}}{D_{product}}$ | **Reduction Ratio** | dimensionless | | 6 | $\dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{6}{D}$ | **Surface-to-volume ratio** of a sphere (used in Rittinger's law) | $m^{-1}$ | | 7 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ | **Critical speed of ball mill** | $N_c$ in rev/s, $R$ in m | | 8 | $D_{optimum} = 6.3 \, d^{0.5}$ | **Optimum ball size in ball mill** (Bond's empirical rule) | mm, where $d$ = feed size in mm | | 9 | $K_R = 0.1 \, W_i$ | Approximate relation between Rittinger constant and Bond index | kWh/ton | | 10 | $v_{critical} = \sqrt{gr}$ | **Critical cutting speed** for rotary cutters | m/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In Module 1 questions, you will be asked to **identify which law applies** to a given situation: > - **Rittinger's Law** вҶ’ fine grinding, surface-area-dominated work. > - **Kick's Law** вҶ’ coarse crushing, volume-dominated work. > - **Bond's Law** вҶ’ general-purpose, intermediate, the most common in board exam numericals. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Flour industry:** Uses a **gradual reduction system** вҖ” break rolls (fluted) вҶ’ reduction rolls (smooth) вҶ’ attrition mills. - **Spice industry:** **Hammer mills** with air-swept classification for fine spice powders. - **Meat industry:** **Bowl choppers** for sausage emulsions. - **Fruit processing:** **Pulpers** for juices, purees, nectars. - **Vegetable processing:** **Shredders and cutters** for coleslaw, salads, frozen vegetables. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Three Fundamental Laws of Size Reduction #### 3.1.1 Rittinger's Law (1867) **Hypothesis:** The energy required for size reduction is **directly proportional to the new surface area created**. Let: - $D_1$ = initial mean particle diameter - $D_2$ = final mean particle diameter - $n_1$ = number of feed particles (per unit mass) - $n_2$ = number of product particles (per unit mass) - $A$ = surface area of a single particle (assumed spherical, $A = \pi D^2$) **Step 1:** The new surface area created per unit mass is: $$\Delta A = n_2 A_2 - n_1 A_1$$ **Step 2:** Since the mass of each particle is $\rho \cdot (\pi/6) D^3$ (where $\rho$ = density), the number of particles per unit mass $m$ is: $$n = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot (\pi/6) D^3} = \dfrac{6 m}{\pi \rho D^3}$$ **Step 3:** Total surface area of particles per unit mass is: $$S = n \cdot A = \dfrac{6 m}{\pi \rho D^3} \cdot \pi D^2 = \dfrac{6m}{D \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 4:** New surface area created per unit mass becomes (letting $m = 1$ for unit mass and assuming constant $\rho$): $$\Delta S = \dfrac{6}{\rho} \left( \dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1} \right)$$ **Step 5:** Since $E \propto \Delta S$ and $\rho$ is constant, we obtain: $$E = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1} \right)$$ This is **Rittinger's Law**, where $K_R$ is the **Rittinger constant** with units of energy per unit area. > [!NOTE] > **Assumption violated in practice:** Particles are not perfectly spherical. Hence a **shape factor** is sometimes included, but for KTU Module 1 the spherical assumption is sufficient. #### 3.1.2 Kick's Law (1885) **Hypothesis:** The energy required is **proportional to the volumetric strain**, i.e., to the logarithm of the size reduction ratio. **Step 1:** For an infinitesimal reduction in size $D \rightarrow D - dD$, the work done is: $$dE = -K_K \, \dfrac{dD}{D}$$ **Step 2:** Integrating from $D_1$ (initial) to $D_2$ (final): $$E = -K_K \int_{D_1}^{D_2} \dfrac{dD}{D}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluating the integral: $$E = -K_K \left[ \ln D \right]_{D_1}^{D_2} = -K_K (\ln D_2 - \ln D_1)$$ **Step 4:** Rearranging: $$E = K_K \ln \left( \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} \right)$$ This is **Kick's Law**. The dimensionless ratio $D_1 / D_2$ is the **reduction ratio**. #### 3.1.3 Bond's Law (1952) **Hypothesis:** The energy required is **proportional to the new crack length created**, which scales as $1/\sqrt{D}$. **Step 1:** Assume each particle contains a **square network of cracks** of length $L$ per unit volume, with $L \propto 1/\sqrt{D}$ (the smaller the particle, the denser the crack network). **Step 2:** New crack length per unit mass: $$\Delta L \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}$$ **Step 3:** Since $E \propto \Delta L$: $$E = K_B \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} \right)$$ **Step 4:** Bond expressed the constants in terms of the **Work Index $W_i$**, defined as the energy (in kWh/ton) required to **reduce a material from infinite size to 100 $\mu m$**: $$W_i = \dfrac{K_B \cdot 10}{\sqrt{100}}$$ Solving for $K_B$ and substituting yields the **practical Bond equation**: $$W = 10 \, W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ where $W$ = work input (kWh/ton), $P$ = product size, $F$ = feed size (both in $\mu m$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU tip:** Bond's law is **the most frequently tested** because it uses the work index вҖ” a real engineering parameter found in standard tables. Typical $W_i$ values: > - Limestone: 11вҖ“13 kWh/ton > - Coal: 11вҖ“15 kWh/ton > - Wheat (corn): ~7 kWh/ton > - Spices (dry): 5вҖ“10 kWh/ton ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Selecting the Right Mill | Food Material (Typical) | Moisture Content | Desired Output | Dominant Force | Recommended Equipment | |------------------------|------------------|----------------|----------------|------------------------| | Wheat (grain) | 12вҖ“14% | Flour (fine) | Compression + Attrition | Roller mill + Disc mill | | Spices (turmeric, coriander) | 8вҖ“12% | Fine powder | Impact | Hammer mill (air-swept) | | Sugar (crystal) | <1% | Icing sugar | Impact / Attrition | Pin mill / Hammer mill | | Cocoa nibs | 2вҖ“5% | Cocoa liquor | Attrition + Pressure | Disc mill / Ball mill | | Meat (chilled) | 60вҖ“75% | Emulsion / mince | Cutting | Bowl chopper / Rotary cutter | | Mango (whole) | 80% | Pulp | Crushing + Sieving | Pulper-finisher | | Cabbage | 90% | Strips | Cutting | Shredder / Rotary cutter | | Wet grains (mash) | 30вҖ“50% | Coarse meal | Attrition | Disc mill | | Salt (rock) | <1% | Granular | Impact | Hammer mill / Cage mill | | Frozen meat block | 70% (frozen) | Minced frozen | Cutting + Impact | Flaker / Shredder | ### 3.3 Illustrative Numerical (KTU Style) **Problem:** A hammer mill is to be used to grind wheat from a mean feed size of **3 mm** to a product size of **200 $\mu m$**. The Bond work index of wheat is **$W_i = 7 \ kWh/ton$**. Estimate the power required per ton of feed. **Solution:** Feed size: $F = 3 \ mm = 3000 \ \mu m$ Product size: $P = 200 \ \mu m$ Work index: $W_i = 7 \ kWh/ton$ Applying Bond's equation: $$W = 10 \, W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ $$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{200}} = \dfrac{1}{14.142} = 0.07071$$ $$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3000}} = \dfrac{1}{54.772} = 0.01826$$ $$W = 10 \times 7 \times (0.07071 - 0.01826)$$ $$W = 70 \times 0.05245$$ $$W = 3.67 \ kWh/ton$$ **Interpretation:** Approximately **3.67 kWh** of energy is required to grind **1 ton** of wheat from 3 mm to 200 $\mu m$ using a hammer mill. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Size Reduction System The following Mermaid block describes how a generic size reduction process flows, from raw material input to finished particulate product, with classification and quality control loops. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Food Material
Whole or Lumpy] --> B[Pre-conditioning
Cleaning Drying Tempering] B --> C[Feed Hopper
Gravity or Forced Feed] C --> D[Size Reduction Unit
Hammer Mill or Roller Mill] D --> E[Particle Air Stream
Mixed with Carrier Air] E --> F[Classification Unit
Sieve or Air Classifier] F --> G{Coarse Particles
Larger than Cut Size} F --> H[Fine Product
Smaller than Cut Size] G --> I[Recycle Loop
Return to Mill Inlet] I --> D H --> J[Collection System
Bag Filter or Cyclone] J --> K[Finished Powder Product
Packaging] D --> L[Energy Input
Electric Motor or Diesel] L --> D ``` > [!NOTE] > This **closed-circuit grinding** layout is a hallmark of modern food industry milling plants (e.g., flour mills, spice plants). The recycle loop prevents **over-grinding** and ensures **uniform particle size distribution**. ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Hammer Mill (Impact Mill) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Hopper] --> B[Rotating Shaft] B --> C[Hammers
Swing or Fixed] C --> D[Grinding Chamber] D --> E[Breaker Plates] D --> F[Perforated Screen] F --> G[Product Outlet] H[Electric Motor] --> B D --> I[Air Stream for Cooling] I --> G ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Roller Mill (Pressure Mill) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Hopper] --> B[Feed Roll
Slow Roll] B --> C[Upper Break Roll
Fluted Large Diameter] C --> D[Lower Break Roll
Fluted Smaller Diameter] D --> E[Sifter
Plansifter] E --> F{Coarse Fraction} E --> G{Fine Fraction
Flour} F --> H[Reduction Rolls
Smooth Rolls] H --> E G --> I[Packaging] J[Roll Differential
1.2 to 1.5 Ratio] --> C J --> D ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Disc Mill (Attrition Mill) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Inlet] --> B[Stationary Disc] B --> C[Rotating Disc
3000 to 10000 rpm] C --> D[Grinding Gap
Adjustable] D --> E[Product Outlet] F[Feed Adjustment Screw] --> D G[Drive Motor] --> C ``` ### 4.5 Functional Architecture of a Pulper (Size Reduction + Separation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Whole Fruits
Washed] --> B[Crushing Blades
High Speed] B --> C[Pulp Chamber] C --> D[Perforated Screen
0.5 to 2 mm holes] D --> E[Pulp Outlet
Smooth Puree] D --> F[Pomace Outlet
Seeds Skin and Stalks] G[Drive Shaft] --> B ``` ### 4.6 Comparison Matrix of Equipment (Visual Block Format) ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Size Reduction Equipments] --> A[Impact Mills] Root --> B[Pressure Mills] Root --> C[Attrition Mills] Root --> D[Cutters] Root --> E[Choppers] Root --> F[Shredders] Root --> G[Pulpers] A --> A1[Hammer Mill] A --> A2[Pin Mill] A --> A3[Disintegrator] B --> B1[Roller Mill] B --> B2[Crushing Rolls] B --> B3[Ball Mill] B --> B4[Edge Runner] C --> C1[Disc Mill] C --> C2[Buhr Stone Mill] C --> C3[Toothed Disc Mill] D --> D1[Rotary Cutter] D --> D2[Guillotine Cutter] D --> D3[Dicer] E --> E1[Bowl Chopper] E --> E2[Silent Cutter] F --> F1[Rotary Shredder] F --> F2[Hammer Shredder] G --> G1[Fruit Pulper] G --> G2[Pulper Finisher] G --> G3[Hammer Pulper] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Impact Mills and Pressure Mills with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Impact Mills | Pressure Mills | |-----------|--------------|-----------------| | Dominant force | Sudden impact | Slow compression | | Mechanism | High-speed rotating hammers strike the particle | Rolls or balls slowly compress the particle | | Energy transfer | Kinetic energy of hammers | Pressure applied by rolls | | Particle size produced | Fine to very fine powder | Coarse to medium particles | | Reduction ratio | High (40вҖ“400) | Low to moderate (2вҖ“20) | | Examples | Hammer mill, pin mill, disintegrator | Roller mill, crushing rolls, ball mill | | Best suited for | Brittle, dry foods (spices, sugar, grains) | Tough, abrasive foods (cereals, ores) | | Heat generation | High (due to friction at high speed) | Moderate | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2023]** **What is a pulper? Explain its working principle and list two food applications.** **Model Answer:** A **pulper** is a size reduction equipment that simultaneously **crushes** and **separates** food materials. It combines mechanical crushing with a **sieving operation** in a single unit. **Working principle:** Whole fruits (or vegetables) are fed into a chamber where **high-speed rotating blades** crush the material against a stationary surface. The resulting pulp is then forced through a **perforated screen** of specified hole size. Seeds, skin, fibre, and stalks вҖ” being larger than the screen holes вҖ” are ejected as **pomace** through a separate outlet. **Food applications:** 1. **Mango pulping** for aam-papad, nectar, and pulp concentrate. 2. **Tomato pulping** for ketchup, puree, and sauce production. > [!NOTE] > **Other valid applications:** guava pulp, banana puree, papaya pulp, apple sauce, and tamarind pulp. --- ### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. You must attempt **either Question A OR Question B.** Each question is divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) вҖ” [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2022] **Q.A. (a)** Classify size reduction equipments used in the food industry based on the **dominant force** employed. With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Hammer Mill**. List its applications and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Classification of Size Reduction Equipments (2 marks):** Size reduction equipments in the food industry are classified based on the dominant force: 1. **Impact mills** вҖ” Hammer mill, Pin mill, Disintegrator. 2. **Pressure mills** вҖ” Roller mill, Crushing rolls, Ball mill, Edge runner. 3. **Attrition mills** вҖ” Disc mill, Buhr stone mill. 4. **Cutting equipment** вҖ” Rotary cutter, Guillotine cutter, Dicer. 5. **Choppers** вҖ” Bowl chopper. 6. **Shredders** вҖ” Rotary shredder. 7. **Pulpers** вҖ” Fruit pulper, Pulper-finisher. **Construction of Hammer Mill (2 marks):** A hammer mill consists of: - A **rotating shaft** (horizontal or vertical) carrying a series of **hammers** (swing or fixed) at its periphery. - A **grinding chamber** fitted with **perforated screens** at the bottom and sides. - **Breaker plates** inside the chamber to provide additional impact surfaces. - A **feed hopper** at the top. - A **discharge outlet** at the bottom. - A **drive system** (electric motor + pulley/belt drive). **Working Principle (2 marks):** Feed material enters the grinding chamber via the hopper. The hammers rotate at high speed (3,000вҖ“10,000 rpm) and strike the particles with high kinetic energy. The particles are also thrown against the breaker plates and perforated screens. When the particles become small enough to pass through the screen openings, they exit through the discharge outlet. In **air-swept hammer mills**, a stream of air carries the fines out, preventing over-grinding. **Applications (0.5 mark):** - Spice grinding (turmeric, coriander, cumin). - Grain milling. - Sugar grinding to icing sugar. - Dry food waste size reduction. **Limitations (0.5 mark):** - High heat generation вҖ” unsuitable for heat-sensitive foods. - High power consumption for fine grinding. - Not suitable for very wet or sticky materials. - Hammer wear due to abrasion. --- **Q.A. (b)** State and explain the **three laws of size reduction**. Derive the **Bond's Law equation** and explain the significance of the **Work Index**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **The three laws of size reduction (3 marks):** 1. **Rittinger's Law (1867):** $E = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1} \right)$ вҖ” Energy is proportional to the **new surface area created**. Best suited for **fine grinding**. 2. **Kick's Law (1885):** $E = K_K \ln \left( \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} \right)$ вҖ” Energy is proportional to the **logarithm of the reduction ratio** (i.e., volumetric strain). Best suited for **coarse crushing**. 3. **Bond's Law (1952):** $E = K_B \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} \right)$ вҖ” Energy is proportional to the **new crack length created**, which scales as $1/\sqrt{D}$. Best suited for **intermediate grinding** and is the most widely used law. **Derivation of Bond's Law (3 marks):** Assume a single particle of size $D$ contains a square network of internal cracks of length $L$. The smaller the particle, the denser the crack network, so: $$L \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D}}$$ The total crack length per unit mass for a system of particles of size $D$ is: $$L_{total} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D}}$$ The new crack length created per unit mass during size reduction from $D_1$ to $D_2$ is: $$\Delta L \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}$$ Since the energy required is proportional to the new crack length: $$E = K_B \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} \right)$$ Bond defined the **Work Index ($W_i$)** as the energy in kWh/ton required to reduce a material from an infinite size to 100 $\mu m$: $$W_i = K_B \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100}} \cdot 10 = \dfrac{K_B \cdot 10}{10} = K_B$$ (when $P = 100 \ \mu m$ and $F \to \infty$, the equation reduces to $E = K_B / \sqrt{P}$). Hence, the practical Bond equation is: $$W = 10 \, W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ where $W$ is in kWh/ton, $P$ and $F$ are in $\mu m$. **Significance of Work Index (1 mark):** - The **Work Index ($W_i$)** is a **material property** representing the resistance of a material to size reduction. - A **high $W_i$** indicates a **hard, tough** material requiring more energy (e.g., quartz $W_i \approx 14$). - A **low $W_i$** indicates a **soft, friable** material requiring less energy (e.g., wheat $W_i \approx 7$). - It enables **scale-up** from laboratory to industrial mills and is essential for **mill sizing and power estimation**. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) вҖ” [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2022] **Q.B. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Roller Mill** used in the flour milling industry. Discuss the role of **fluted and smooth rolls** and the concept of **roll differential**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction of Roller Mill (2 marks):** A roller mill consists of: - A pair of **horizontal rolls** mounted in a heavy cast-iron frame. - The rolls rotate in **opposite directions** (counter-rotating). - One roll is driven by a motor (the **fast roll**); the other is coupled via gears (the **slow roll**). - A **feed roll** (smaller) at the inlet controls the rate and uniform distribution of feed into the roll gap (the **nip**). - Each roll is housed in a separate **bearing assembly** that allows precise **hydraulic or spring-loaded pressure adjustment** of the roll gap. - A **scraper blade** removes material sticking to the slow roll. **Working Principle (2 marks):** The feed material (e.g., wheat kernels) enters the **nip** вҖ” the wedge-shaped gap between the two rolls. The rolls rotate inward, gripping the material by **friction** and pulling it into the nip. As the particle passes through the nip, it is subjected to intense **compressive and shear forces**, causing it to fracture and flatten into smaller pieces. The product is discharged below the rolls and conveyed to a **sifter (plansifter)** for classification. **Fluted Rolls vs. Smooth Rolls (2 marks):** - **Fluted rolls (break rolls):** The roll surface has spiral grooves (flutes). They are used in the **break system** to **open the grain** by cutting through the bran and releasing the endosperm as large pieces called **semolina** or **middlings**. The flutes are typically **sharp-to-sharp** or **sharp-to-dull** configurations. - **Smooth rolls (reduction rolls):** The roll surface is highly polished. They are used in the **reduction system** to **gradually grind** the semolina into fine flour by **compression and shearing** without further cutting the bran. **Roll Differential (1 mark):** The ratio of the **surface speeds** of the two rolls is called the **differential**: $$Differential = \dfrac{V_{fast}}{V_{slow}}$$ - For **break rolls:** Differential = 1.2 to 1.5 (shearing effect to cut the bran). - For **reduction rolls:** Differential = 1.0 to 1.25 (compressive effect to flatten endosperm). - A higher differential produces a **greater shearing action**, useful for break rolls; a lower differential is more **compressive**, suitable for reduction. --- **Q.B. (b)** Compare **Cutters, Choppers, Shredders, and Pulpers** with respect to their working principle, output characteristics, and food applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | Cutters | Choppers | Shredders | Pulpers | |-----------|---------|----------|-----------|---------| | **Dominant force** | Slicing by sharp blades | High-speed cutting + impact | Cutting + impact (combination) | Crushing + sieving | | **Working principle** | Rotating blades slice the material | Counter-rotating bowl and blades reduce material | Rotating hammers/teeth tear into strips | Crushing blades force pulp through a screen | | **Output form** | Uniform slices/cubes | Paste, emulsion, mince | Strips, flakes, shreds | Smooth puree / pulp | | **Particle size produced** | 1 mm to 50 mm | < 1 mm (emulsion) | 2вҖ“10 mm width strips | 0.1вҖ“2 mm (fines) | | **Material state** | Fresh, firm | Soft, semi-solid | Fibrous, firm | Whole fruits/vegetables | | **Moisture tolerance** | Low to high | High (up to 80%) | Medium to high | Very high (80вҖ“95%) | | **Heat generation** | Low | High (due to friction) | Moderate | Moderate | | **Batch / Continuous** | Both | Mostly batch | Mostly continuous | Mostly continuous | | **Examples** | Rotary cutter, dicer | Bowl chopper, silent cutter | Rotary shredder, hammer shredder | Fruit pulper, pulper-finisher | | **Food applications** | Vegetables, fruits for canning, nuts | Meat for sausage, pГўtГ©, frozen meat | Cabbage (coleslaw), carrots, cheese | Mango pulp, tomato puree, guava pulp | **Brief Notes (2 marks):** - **Cutters** are used when the food must retain its **structural identity** (e.g., diced carrots, sliced potatoes). - **Choppers** are used in the **meat industry** to produce emulsions and fine pastes; they are the workhorse of sausage manufacture. - **Shredders** are used in **vegetable processing** for products that require thin strips (coleslaw, salad mixes, cheese shreds). - **Pulpers** uniquely combine **size reduction and separation** in a single unit; they are indispensable in the **juice and concentrate industry** for de-seeding and de-skinning whole fruits. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Chopper with Shredder:** A chopper produces a **paste/emulsion**; a shredder produces **strips/flakes**. Examiners will **deduct 1вҖ“2 marks** if you interchange them. > > 2. **Mixing up Rittinger's and Bond's laws:** Always remember: > - Rittinger вҶ’ $1/D$ (surface area) > - Kick вҶ’ $\ln(D_1/D_2)$ (volume) > - Bond вҶ’ $1/\sqrt{D}$ (crack length) > Writing the wrong exponent is a **classic 1-mark error**. > > 3. **Forgetting units in Bond's equation:** $P$ and $F$ must be in the **same units** (preferably $\mu m$); $W_i$ is in **kWh/ton**; $W$ is in **kWh/ton**. Unit mismatch costs full marks in numericals. > > 4. **Not mentioning "sieving" in pulper working:** A pulper is **not** just a crusher; the **screen separation step is essential**. Omitting it loses at least 1 mark. > > 5. **Neglecting to mention the appropriate food application:** Always tie the equipment to a **specific food material** (e.g., "hammer mill for turmeric"). A generic statement like "used in food industry" attracts only **partial marks**. > > 6. **In Roller Mill answers, omitting the role of fluted vs. smooth rolls:** This is a **favourite KTU question** вҖ” students who write only "two rolls rotate" lose **2вҖ“3 marks**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Size reduction** is a mechanical unit operation in food engineering that increases **surface area per unit mass**, improving heat and mass transfer. - **Four fundamental forces:** Impact, Compression, Attrition, Cutting. - **Impact mills** (hammer mill, pin mill) вҶ’ **high R.R., fine powder**, dry brittle foods. - **Pressure mills** (roller mill, ball mill) вҶ’ **moderate R.R.**, used in **flour industry** and **fine grinding**. - **Attrition mills** (disc mill, stone mill) вҶ’ **soft, oily foods** (cocoa, nuts); moderate heat. - **Cutters** вҶ’ **uniform slices**; retain structural identity. - **Choppers** вҶ’ **emulsion / paste**; meat industry workhorse. - **Shredders** вҶ’ **strips and flakes**; vegetable processing. - **Pulpers** вҶ’ **pulp + separation**; juice and concentrate industry. - **Rittinger's Law** вҶ’ fine grinding; $\propto (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$. - **Kick's Law** вҶ’ coarse crushing; $\propto \ln(D_1/D_2)$. - **Bond's Law** вҶ’ general; $\propto (1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$; uses **Work Index $W_i$** in **kWh/ton**. - **Reduction Ratio (R.R.)** = $D_{feed}/D_{product}$; typical ranges: - Hammer mill: 40вҖ“400 - Roller mill: 2вҖ“20 - Ball mill: 50+ - **Roll differential** for roller mills: **1.2вҖ“1.5 (break)**, **1.0вҖ“1.25 (reduction)**. - **Closed-circuit grinding** with sifter/airswept classifier prevents **over-grinding** and **heat build-up**. - For **KTU 2024** Module 1, focus on **classification, working principles, applications, and selection criteria** for each equipment. Numerical application of Bond's law is the most common calculation question. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Mantra for this Topic:** > "*Match the equipment to the food вҖ” by force type, by moisture, and by desired particle size.*"

topics.title: Size Reduction of Liquid Foods - Homogenization, Homogenizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Size Reduction of Liquid Foods - Homogenization, Homogenizers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Homogenization):** > Homogenization is a **size reduction process for liquid foods** in which coarse droplets/particles of the dispersed phase (typically fat globules) are mechanically broken down into **uniformly small droplets** (typically **< 2 Ојm**) and simultaneously stabilized against re-coalescence. It is a form of *emulsification under extreme shear, turbulence, and cavitation*. **Sub-classification within "Size Reduction of Liquid Foods":** | Process | Typical Particle Size | Product Examples | |---|---|---| | Homogenization | **0.1 вҖ“ 2 Ојm** | Milk, cream, ice cream, yogurt | | Ultra-Sonication | 0.01 вҖ“ 0.1 Ојm | Nano-emulsions, functional beverages | | High-Shear Mixing | 1 вҖ“ 10 Ојm | Salad dressings, sauces | | Colloid Milling | 1 вҖ“ 5 Ојm | Peanut butter, fruit purees | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** > Think of unhomogenized milk left in a glass overnight вҖ” a thick **cream layer rises to the top** because the large fat droplets (4вҖ“8 Ојm) coalesce and float. Now imagine forcing that milk at high pressure through a tiny nozzle against a hard surface: the fat droplets are literally **torn apart by shear forces and shock waves** into trillions of microscopic droplets. The result is a permanently uniform, smooth, creamy liquid вҖ” this is homogenization. > - **Big fat drops** = unstable, separable, dull appearance > - **Tiny fat drops (< 2 Ојm)** = stable, smooth, whiter, richer mouthfeel **Key Terminology for KTU Examinations:** - **Dispersed Phase:** The phase present in the form of droplets/particles (e.g., fat in milk). - **Continuous Phase:** The liquid in which the dispersed phase floats (e.g., skim milk serum). - **Emulsion:** A two-phase liquid system where one liquid is dispersed as droplets in another. - **Laplace Pressure (О”P):** The pressure difference across a curved liquid-liquid interface. - **Cavitation:** Formation and violent collapse of vapor bubbles in a low-pressure zone. - **Shear Stress (П„):** Tangential force per unit area that deforms the droplet. - **Weber Number (We):** Ratio of inertial forces to surface tension forces вҖ” the **key dimensionless number** governing droplet breakup. > [!TIP] > **Why is homogenization studied under "Size Reduction"?** > Although classical size reduction (crushing, grinding) deals with solids, the *fundamental objective* вҖ” **reducing the mean particle/droplet diameter and creating a uniform distribution** вҖ” is identical. KTU classifies homogenization here because it obeys the same energy-input-vs-new-surface-area relationship: *Rittinger's Law adapted for liquids*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Mechanisms of Droplet Disruption in a Homogenizer When liquid food passes through a homogenizing valve, droplet breakup occurs by **three simultaneous mechanisms** (HIGH-YIELD for KTU theory questions): 1. **Shear (Viscous) Breakup** вҖ” The droplet is deformed by velocity gradients in the narrow gap. 2. **Turbulent (Inertial) Breakup** вҖ” Local turbulent eddies exert fluctuating pressures exceeding the Laplace pressure of the droplet. 3. **Cavitation** вҖ” Local pressure drops below the vapor pressure of the liquid; vapor bubbles form and collapse, sending micro-jets that shatter droplets. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability Criterion (Why droplets do NOT re-coalesce immediately):** > Immediately downstream of the homogenizing valve, fresh surfactant proteins (e.g., **casein micelles in milk**) rapidly adsorb onto the newly created droplet surface, creating an **electrostatic + steric barrier** that prevents re-coalescence. This is why the *order of addition* and *temperature* (affecting protein unfolding) are critical process parameters. ### 2.2 Process Flow in a High-Pressure Homogenizer (Step Logic) - **Step 1 вҖ” Suction:** The liquid feed is drawn into a piston pump at low pressure (вүҲ 0.5 вҖ“ 2 bar). - **Step 2 вҖ” Pressurization:** A positive-displacement pump (typically 2- or 3-piston) compresses the liquid to **100 вҖ“ 400 bar**. - **Step 3 вҖ” Acceleration:** The high-pressure liquid is forced through a narrow annular gap (вүҲ 100 вҖ“ 300 Ојm) between the valve seat and the valve rod, reaching velocities of **100 вҖ“ 300 m/s**. - **Step 4 вҖ” Disruption:** Droplets are broken up by combined shear + turbulence + cavitation in the gap. - **Step 5 вҖ” Pressure Recovery:** Sudden deceleration against an impact ring creates additional shock disruption. - **Step 6 вҖ” Cooling:** Outlet temperature rises by **15 вҖ“ 25 В°C per 100 bar**; an aseptic cooler follows. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Name / Significance | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | **Laplace Pressure** вҖ” pressure resisting droplet deformation | Pa | | 2 | $We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, L}{\gamma}$ | **Weber Number** вҖ” inertial vs surface tension forces | dimensionless | | 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds Number** in homogenizing valve | dimensionless | | 4 | $\dfrac{d_{m}}{D} = C \cdot We^{-0.6}$ | **Critical Weber condition** for droplet breakup | dimensionless ratio | | 5 | $\log_{10}(d_{m}) = K - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ | **Phipps Correlation** (empirical) вҖ” mean droplet size vs pressure | Ојm vs bar | | 6 | $d_{m} = \dfrac{0.014 \, \gamma^{0.65}}{\rho^{0.05} \cdot P^{0.85}}$ | **Walstra's Equation** (mean droplet diameter) | m | | 7 | $\dot{W} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | **Power input to homogenizer** | W (J/s) | | 8 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho \, c_{p}}$ | **Adiabatic temperature rise** across valve | K | | 9 | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho \, d_{m}}$ | **Specific Surface Area** of new droplets | mВІ/kg | | 10 | $\Delta A = N \cdot \pi \cdot (d_{1}^{2} - d_{2}^{2})$ | **New interfacial area** created (Rittinger adapted) | mВІ | Where: - $\gamma$ = interfacial tension (N/m) - $r$ = droplet radius (m) - $\rho$ = continuous-phase density (kg/mВі) - $v$ = fluid velocity (m/s) - $L$ = characteristic length (droplet diameter, m) - $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter of valve gap (m) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (PaВ·s) - $P$ = homogenization pressure (Pa) - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (mВі/s) - $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kgВ·K) - $N$ = number of droplets - $d_1, d_2$ = initial and final droplet diameters (m) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > These equations are used in **plant design** (selecting pump capacity, motor rating), **process optimization** (predicting final droplet size for a given pressure), and **scale-up** (translating lab results to industrial homogenizers). The Phipps and Walstra equations are the *most frequently asked formulas* in KTU 2018-2023 previous-year papers. ### 2.4 Types of Homogenizers (KTU Classification) | Type | Operating Principle | Pressure / Energy | Typical Products | |---|---|---|---| | **High-Pressure (Piston) Homogenizer** | Forced through narrow valve gap | 100 вҖ“ 400 bar | Milk, cream, ice-cream mix | | **Two-Stage Homogenizer** | First stage for breakup, second for stability | 150 + 30 bar | Market milk | | **Microfluidizer** | Liquid split into two micro-channels colliding | 600 вҖ“ 1500 bar | Nano-emulsions, liposomes | | **Ultrasonic Homogenizer** | High-frequency sound waves create cavitation | 20 kHz, 100вҖ“1000 W | Lab-scale, essential oils | | **Membrane Homogenizer** | Pressed through uniform pores | 1 вҖ“ 10 bar | Premium juices, dairy | | **High-Shear Rotor-Stator** | Mechanical shear in stator-rotor gap | Centrifugal | Sauces, dressings | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Laplace Pressure Equation (Mechanical Force Balance on a Curved Interface) > **Starting Premise:** Consider a spherical droplet of radius $r$ of the dispersed phase suspended in a continuous phase. The interfacial tension $\gamma$ acts tangentially along the droplet surface, trying to minimize the surface area. Any attempt to deform the droplet must overcome the internal pressure that is higher than the surrounding pressure by $\Delta P$. **Step 1 вҖ” Conceptual Setup:** Imagine cutting the spherical droplet into two equal hemispheres. The two halves are held together by: - The interfacial tension acting along the circular cut (circumference $= 2\pi r$). - The internal excess pressure acting over the circular cross-section (area $= \pi r^{2}$). **Step 2 вҖ” Force due to Interfacial Tension (pulling the hemispheres together):** The surface tension acts along the perimeter of the cut, in the plane of the cut, trying to pull the two halves together. The vertical component of this force is: $$F_{\gamma} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot \cos(0) = 2\pi r \gamma$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Force due to Internal Excess Pressure (pushing hemispheres apart):** The pressure difference $\Delta P$ inside the droplet pushes the two hemispheres apart over the cross-sectional area: $$F_{P} = \Delta P \cdot (\pi r^{2})$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Mechanical Equilibrium (Force Balance):** For a stable droplet, the two forces must be equal in magnitude: $$F_{P} = F_{\gamma}$$ $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = 2\pi r \gamma$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Solving for $\Delta P$:** $$\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$$ > **Result (Laplace Equation):** The internal pressure excess of a spherical droplet is inversely proportional to its radius. **Smaller droplets = higher internal pressure = greater resistance to further deformation.** > [!TIP] > **Physical Interpretation for KTU Exam:** > This is the *fundamental reason* why a homogenizer must apply enormous pressure. To break a 4 Ојm fat globule into a 0.5 Ојm droplet, $\Delta P$ increases **eight-fold**, demanding more energy and a stronger valve. The homogenizer must continuously supply a pressure that exceeds the *new* Laplace pressure of the *final* droplet size. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Power Input to a Homogenizer **Step 1 вҖ” Definition of Power:** Power is the rate of energy transfer per unit time. For a fluid flowing through a control volume with a pressure drop $\Delta P$ at a volumetric flow rate $Q$: $$\dot{W} = \dfrac{dE}{dt} = \dfrac{(\Delta P) \cdot dV}{dt}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Substituting Volumetric Flow Rate:** $$\dot{W} = \Delta P \cdot Q$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Linking to Mean Droplet Size (Combining with Phipps/Walstra):** Since $\Delta P$ is the driving pressure, and using Phipps' correlation in differential form, the **energy per unit mass** required to reduce droplet size from $d_1$ to $d_2$ becomes: $$\dfrac{\dot{W}}{m} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot Q} = \dfrac{\dot{W}}{\dot{m}}$$ > **Result:** A 4-fold increase in homogenization pressure (e.g., 100 bar вҶ’ 400 bar) requires 4Г— the motor power but, per the Phipps slope of $-0.31$, reduces $d_m$ by a factor of $10^{0.31 \times \log_{10}(4)} \approx 1.55$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Sub-Question Style) > **Problem:** > A milk homogenizer processes **5,000 L/h** of milk at a pressure of **200 bar**. The fat globules are reduced from an initial mean diameter of **4.0 Ојm** to a final mean diameter of **0.8 Ојm**. Milk density $\rho = 1030 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, interfacial tension $\gamma = 0.012 \, \text{N/m}$. Calculate: > (a) The new specific surface area created. > (b) The power input to the homogenizer in kW. > (c) The Laplace pressure of the final droplet. **Part (a) вҖ” Specific Surface Area Created:** The *additional* interfacial area generated per kg of milk is: $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho} \left( \dfrac{1}{d_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{d_{1}} \right)$$ Substituting values (with $d_1 = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $d_2 = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$): $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{1030} \left( \dfrac{1}{0.8 \times 10^{-6}} - \dfrac{1}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}} \right)$$ $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{1030} \left( 1.25 \times 10^{6} - 0.25 \times 10^{6} \right)$$ $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{1030} \times 1.0 \times 10^{6}$$ $$\Delta SSA \approx 5825 \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{kg}$$ > **Result:** Each kg of homogenized milk has about **5,825 mВІ of new fat-serum interface** вҖ” larger than a football field! **Part (b) вҖ” Power Input:** Volumetric flow rate: $$Q = \dfrac{5000 \, \text{L/h}}{3600} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ Pressure drop: $$\Delta P = 200 \, \text{bar} = 200 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa}$$ Power: $$\dot{W} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \times 1.389 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\dot{W} \approx 27,778 \, \text{W} \approx 27.8 \, \text{kW}$$ > **Result:** The homogenizer consumes roughly **27.8 kW** of useful hydraulic power (excluding motor + pump mechanical losses of typically 60вҖ“70%). **Part (c) вҖ” Laplace Pressure of Final Droplet:** $$\Delta P_{Laplace} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.012}{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta P_{Laplace} = \dfrac{0.024}{4 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.0 \times 10^{4} \, \text{Pa} = 0.6 \, \text{bar}$$ > **Result:** The final droplet experiences an internal pressure excess of only **0.6 bar** вҖ” easily stable in the milk matrix. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Effect of Process Variables on Droplet Size) | Process Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on Mean Droplet $d_m$ | Effect on Power | Effect on Stability | |---|---|---|---|---| | Homogenization Pressure $P$ | вҶ‘ Increase | вҶ“ Decrease (per Phipps slope $-0.31$) | вҶ‘ Increases linearly | вҶ‘ Increases | | Temperature $T$ | вҶ‘ Increase | вҶ“ Decreases (lower $\gamma$) | вҖ” | вҶ‘ Increases | | Interfacial Tension $\gamma$ | вҶ“ Decrease (with surfactant) | вҶ“ Decreases | вҖ” | вҶ‘ Increases | | Viscosity $\mu$ | вҶ‘ Increase | вҶ‘ Increases (poorer shear transfer) | вҶ‘ Increases | вҶ” Neutral | | Two-Stage (vs Single-Stage) | Adoption | Marginal вҶ“ | вҶ‘ Slightly | вҶ‘ Greatly (avoids re-coalescence) | | Surfactant Concentration | вҶ‘ Increase | вҶ“ Decreases | вҖ” | вҶ‘ Increases | | Throughput $Q$ | вҶ‘ Increase | вҶ‘ Increases (less residence time) | вҶ‘ Increases | вҶ“ Decreases | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Convention:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside quotes), per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards. ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a High-Pressure Two-Stage Homogenizer ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[FEED TANK
RAW MILK OR LIQUID FOOD] --> B1[LOW PRESSURE
SUCTION LINE 0.5 TO 2 BAR] B1 --> C1[HIGH PRESSURE
PISTON PUMP 100 TO 400 BAR] C1 --> D1[FIRST STAGE
HOMOGENIZING VALVE
SHEAR AND CAVITATION ZONE] D1 --> E1[IMPACT RING
SHOCK DISRUPTION SURFACE] E1 --> F1[SECOND STAGE
STABILITY VALVE 30 BAR] F1 --> G1[ASEPIC COOLER
HEAT EXCHANGER] G1 --> H1[FINAL HOMOGENIZED
LIQUID PRODUCT OUTLET] subgraph P1[POWER AND CONTROL] I1[ELECTRIC MOTOR
30 TO 75 kW] --> C1 J1[PRESSURE GAUGE
AND CONTROL LOOP] -.-> D1 end ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Liquid moves **left to right** through the process train. - The **Piston Pump** (Step 2 of theory) provides the driving pressure. - The **First Stage Valve** is the *primary* disruption site вҖ” droplets broken by shear, turbulence, cavitation. - The **Second Stage Valve** operates at low pressure (20вҖ“40 bar) and serves *only* to break up re-coalescing clusters formed immediately after stage 1. - The **Aseptic Cooler** removes the adiabatic heat gained (вүҲ 16 В°C per 100 bar of pressure drop). --- ### 4.2 Process Logic Map: From Coarse Emulsion to Homogenized Product ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[START COARSE EMULSION
FAT GLOBULES 4 TO 8 MICRON] --> P1{PROTEIN ADSORPTION
ONTO GLOBULES} P1 --> V1[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP
200 BAR TYPICAL] V1 --> V2[ANNULAR VALVE GAP
100 TO 300 MICROMETER] V2 --> M1[MECHANISM 1
VISCOUS SHEAR STRESS] V2 --> M2[MECHANISM 2
TURBULENT EDDY DISRUPTION] V2 --> M3[MECHANISM 3
CAVITATION BUBBLE COLLAPSE] M1 --> R1[REDUCED DROPLET
LESS THAN 1 MICRON] M2 --> R1 M3 --> R1 R1 --> ST1{RE COALESCENCE
RISK EVALUATION} ST1 -->|YES| ST2[SECOND STAGE VALVE
30 BAR STABILIZATION] ST1 -->|NO| EX1[DIRECT EXPORT TO
COOLING AND PACKAGING] ST2 --> EX1 EX1 --> END1[FINAL STABLE
HOMOGENIZED PRODUCT] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The three mechanism nodes (M1, M2, M3) act **in parallel** on the fluid вҖ” they are not sequential; all three occur *simultaneously* in the valve gap. - The decision diamond `ST1` represents the engineering judgement: most industrial milk lines use the second stage to ensure cream does not form a layer during the 7вҖ“14 day shelf life. --- ### 4.3 Effect-of-Parameter Cause-Effect Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[HOMOGENIZATION
QUALITY OUTCOME] --> BR1[DROPLET SIZE
REDUCTION] ROOT --> BR2[ENERGY
CONSUMPTION] ROOT --> BR3[PRODUCT
STABILITY] BR1 --> C1A[INCREASE
PRESSURE P] BR1 --> C1B[INCREASE
TEMPERATURE T] BR1 --> C1C[ADD SURFACTANT
REDUCE INTERFACIAL TENSION] BR2 --> C2A[HIGHER P
INCREASES POWER] BR2 --> C2B[HIGHER Q
INCREASES POWER] BR2 --> C2C[HIGHER MU
INCREASES POWER] BR3 --> C3A[TWO STAGE
CONFIGURATION] BR3 --> C3B[ADEQUATE PROTEIN
ADSORPTION] BR3 --> C3C[PROPER COOLING
POST VALVE] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, OEFTT721]** > *Define the term "homogenization index" and state its significance in evaluating the efficiency of milk homogenization.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **homogenization index** is a numerical measure of the *degree of disruption* of fat globules, defined as: $$\text{Homogenization Index} \, (\%) = \dfrac{N_{1\mu}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$ where $N_{1\mu}$ is the number fraction of fat globules with diameter **< 1 Ојm** and $N_{\text{total}}$ is the total number of globules in a representative sample. It is determined using microscopy or laser light scattering (e.g., Malvern Mastersizer). - **Significance:** A higher index implies more efficient breakup, better creaming resistance, and longer shelf life. - A value **> 80%** indicates efficient homogenization of market milk. - (Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for significance.) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, OEFTT721]** > *State and explain the principle of operation of an ultrasonic homogenizer. Mention any two advantages over high-pressure homogenizers.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Principle:** Ultrasonic homogenizers use a **piezoelectric transducer** (typically operating at **20вҖ“24 kHz**) that converts electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical vibrations. These vibrations are coupled into the liquid via a titanium probe (sonotrode), creating alternating **high- and low-pressure cycles**. During the low-pressure phase, **cavitation bubbles** form; during the high-pressure phase, they collapse violently, generating **localized shear, micro-jets, and shock waves** that break up droplets/particles to sub-micron sizes. **Two advantages:** 1. No high-pressure moving parts вҖ” *safer* and *lower maintenance*. 2. Suitable for **heat-sensitive** bioactives (enzymes, probiotics) since bulk temperature rise is minimal. 3. (Optional third point) Easy to integrate in lab-scale and continuous flow reactors. --- ### Part B вҖ” Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice) > **Question A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, OEFTT721, Module 1]** > > **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **two-stage high-pressure homogenizer**. Discuss the role of each stage in determining the *final droplet size distribution* and *product stability*. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A dairy plant homogenizes **8,000 L/h of milk** at **250 bar**. If the fat content is **3.5% w/w** and the mean fat globule diameter is reduced from **3.5 Ојm to 0.7 Ојm**, calculate: > (i) The mass flow rate of fat. > (ii) The total new interfacial area created per hour. > (iii) The motor power required (assume pump efficiency 70%). > Given: $\rho_{\text{milk}} = 1032 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\gamma = 0.011 \, \text{N/m}$, $c_p = 3900 \, \text{J/kgВ·K}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):** A two-stage high-pressure homogenizer consists of: 1. **Feed Inlet + Suction Valve:** Raw milk enters at low pressure through a suction check-valve. 2. **Positive-Displacement Piston Pump (typically 3-piston):** Compresses milk to 150вҖ“350 bar. The 3-piston design ensures smoother flow with low pulsation. 3. **First-Stage Homogenizing Valve (Primary Valve):** A tungsten carbide or ceramic valve with a narrow **annular gap of 100вҖ“300 Ојm** between the valve seat and valve rod. Pressure is set typically at 200вҖ“250 bar. Velocity of milk reaches 200вҖ“300 m/s. Droplet breakup occurs by **viscous shear + turbulent eddy disruption + cavitation**. 4. **Impact Ring:** Located ~1 mm downstream of the first valve, this stationary surface causes a sudden deceleration of the high-velocity jet, generating additional shock disruption. 5. **Second-Stage Valve (Stability Valve):** Operates at a *much lower pressure* (20вҖ“50 bar) with a *wider gap*. Its role is to break up **re-coalescing clusters** formed by droplet collisions immediately after stage 1, *not* to create new surface area. 6. **Pressure Gauge & Spring-Loaded Actuator:** A hydraulic or pneumatic feedback system maintains constant pressure by adjusting the spring tension on the valve rod. 7. **Outlet + Aseptic Cooler:** Adiabatic temperature rise is $\Delta T = \Delta P / (\rho c_p) = 250 \times 10^{5} / (1032 \times 3900) \approx 6.2 \, \text{K}$, which is removed by a plate heat exchanger. *Role in droplet size:* Stage 1 controls the *mean diameter*; Stage 2 controls the *width of the distribution* and prevents re-coalescence. Without stage 2, fat clusters re-form and the product creams over 3вҖ“5 days. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Mass flow rate of fat (1 mark):** $$\dot{m}_{\text{milk}} = \dfrac{8000}{3600} \times 1032 = 2293.3 \, \text{kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{\text{fat}} = 0.035 \times 2293.3 = 80.27 \, \text{kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{80.27}{3600} = 0.0223 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **(ii) New interfacial area created per hour (3 marks):** $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho} \left( \dfrac{1}{d_2} - \dfrac{1}{d_1} \right) = \dfrac{6}{920} \left( \dfrac{1}{0.7 \times 10^{-6}} - \dfrac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}} \right)$$ (Note: use $\rho_{\text{fat}} \approx 920 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$ for interfacial area calculation; 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for the final answer.) $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{920} \times (1.4286 \times 10^{6} - 0.2857 \times 10^{6})$$ $$\Delta SSA = \dfrac{6}{920} \times 1.1429 \times 10^{6} = 7453 \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{kg}$$ Total area per hour: $$A_{\text{total}} = \Delta SSA \times \dot{m}_{\text{fat}} = 7453 \times 80.27 \approx 5.98 \times 10^{5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{h}$$ **(iii) Motor power (3 marks):** Volumetric flow rate: $$Q = \dfrac{8000}{3600} = 2.222 \, \text{L/s} = 2.222 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ Hydraulic power: $$\dot{W}_{\text{hydraulic}} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 250 \times 10^{5} \times 2.222 \times 10^{-3} = 55,556 \, \text{W} \approx 55.56 \, \text{kW}$$ Motor power (with 70% efficiency): $$\dot{W}_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{55.56}{0.70} = 79.37 \, \text{kW}$$ --- > **Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEFTT721, Module 1 - Alternative Choice]** > > **(a)** Compare the working principles, advantages, and limitations of **high-pressure homogenizers, ultrasonic homogenizers, and microfluidizers** in the size reduction of liquid foods. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the **Laplace pressure equation** for a spherical droplet. Using the Phipps correlation $\log_{10}(d_m) = 4.20 - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ (with $d_m$ in Ојm and $P$ in bar), determine the increase in homogenization pressure required to reduce mean droplet diameter from **2.0 Ојm to 0.8 Ојm**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | High-Pressure Homogenizer | Ultrasonic Homogenizer | Microfluidizer | |---|---|---|---| | Driving Energy | Pressure (100вҖ“400 bar) | Acoustic cavitation (20 kHz) | Pressure (600вҖ“1500 bar) + jet collision | | Mechanism | Shear + Turbulence + Cavitation | Cavitation micro-jets | Shear + Jet impact | | Typical Final Size | 0.5 вҖ“ 2 Ојm | 0.05 вҖ“ 0.5 Ојm | 0.01 вҖ“ 0.2 Ојm | | Capacity | High (industrial) | Low (lab/pilot) | Medium | | Capital Cost | Moderate | Low | High | | Maintenance | Moderate (valve wear) | Low (no moving parts in liquid) | High (special Zr or diamond chambers) | | Heat Sensitivity Risk | Moderate (О”T ~16 В°C/100 bar) | Low (bulk liquid) | Moderate | | Best Application | Market milk, cream, ice-cream | Nano-emulsions, liposomes | Pharma, nutraceuticals | | Limitation | Valve clogging with particulates | Limited throughput | Energy-intensive | (Allocate ~1 mark per row of comparison and 2 marks for the concluding summary statement.) --- **(b) Derivation of Laplace Pressure (4 marks) + Phipps Calculation (3 marks):** The derivation follows exactly the five-step force balance from Section 3.1 of these notes: **Final result:** $$\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$$ **Significance:** Shows that smaller droplets require higher internal pressure to deform вҖ” the homogenizer must supply this excess energy. --- **Phipps Correlation Calculation:** Given: $$\log_{10}(d_m) = 4.20 - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$$ For $d_{m,1} = 2.0 \, \mu m$: $$\log_{10}(2.0) = 4.20 - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P_1)$$ $$0.3010 = 4.20 - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P_1)$$ $$\log_{10}(P_1) = \dfrac{4.20 - 0.3010}{0.31} = \dfrac{3.899}{0.31} = 12.577$$ $$P_1 = 10^{12.577} \approx 3.78 \times 10^{12} \, \text{bar}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Correction вҖ” Unit Inconsistency in Phipps Original Equation:** > The Phipps equation as published uses $d_m$ in Ојm but $P$ in **psi (lbf/inВІ)**, *not* bar. The numerical constants above assume psi. To use bar, replace 4.20 with the **bar-equivalent constant 4.20 + 0.31В·logвӮҒвӮҖ(14.504) = 4.20 + 0.358 вүҲ 4.558**. For KTU exams, students should clearly state the unit system assumed. For $d_{m,2} = 0.8 \, \mu m$: $$\log_{10}(0.8) = 4.558 - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P_2)$$ $$-0.0969 = 4.558 - 0.31 \cdot \log_{10}(P_2)$$ $$\log_{10}(P_2) = \dfrac{4.558 + 0.0969}{0.31} = \dfrac{4.655}{0.31} = 15.016$$ $$P_2 = 10^{15.016} \approx 1.04 \times 10^{15} \, \text{bar (in psi units)}$$ Ratio: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 10^{(15.016 - 12.577)} = 10^{2.439} \approx 275$$ > **Result:** To reduce $d_m$ from 2.0 Ојm to 0.8 Ојm, the homogenization pressure must be increased by a factor of **~275**. This huge pressure sensitivity is why **second-stage valves are designed for stability, not further size reduction.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to state units of $P$ in the Phipps equation** вҖ” leads to wrong numerical answer; -2 marks. > 2. **Confusing Phipps with Walstra** вҖ” Walstra uses an explicit $\gamma$ and $\rho$ dependence, Phipps is purely empirical. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion. > 3. **Drawing the homogenizer with single-stage valve and labelling it "two-stage"** вҖ” this is the most common diagram error. -2 marks. > 4. **Not mentioning the role of the impact ring** вҖ” examiners expect it as a distinct component. -1 mark. > 5. **Using $\rho_{\text{milk}}$ instead of $\rho_{\text{fat}}$ for interfacial area calculation** вҖ” minor but commonly penalized. > 6. **Omitting cavitation from the mechanism list** вҖ” KTU specifically tests whether students know that cavitation is one of the *three* disruption mechanisms. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Homogenization** is a liquid size-reduction process that reduces fat/droplet diameter from **3вҖ“8 Ојm to < 1 Ојm** by combining high pressure, shear, turbulence, and cavitation. - The **Laplace pressure** $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$ defines the *resistance* of a droplet to deformation; smaller droplets = higher internal pressure = more energy required to break. - The **Weber number** ($We$) governs droplet breakup: $We > We_{critical}$ is required for fragmentation. - The **Phipps correlation** is the most-used empirical equation relating mean droplet diameter to homogenization pressure. - **Three mechanisms** of disruption must always be mentioned: viscous shear, turbulent inertial breakup, and cavitation. - **Two-stage homogenization**: Stage 1 reduces size; Stage 2 stabilizes by breaking re-coalescing clusters. - **Power input** $\dot{W} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ scales linearly with pressure; motor rating is 1.4вҖ“1.5Г— the hydraulic power. - **Specific surface area** of new droplets $\Delta SSA = 6/(\rho d_m)$ explains why homogenized milk appears whiter (more light scattering) and creamier (more fat-protein interface). - The **adiabatic temperature rise** of ~16 В°C per 100 bar must be removed by an aseptic cooler to prevent thermal damage. - **Industrial pressure range**: 150вҖ“250 bar for market milk; 400+ bar for UHT and cream products. - **Homogenization index** (globules < 1 Ојm fraction) is the standard quality metric; > 80% is considered efficient. - **Material of construction** for valves: tungsten carbide or stellite, due to extreme wear from cavitation erosion. - The **order of addition** of ingredients and **pre-heating** to 60вҖ“70 В°C reduce milk viscosity and lower $\gamma$, improving efficiency. - **High-pressure homogenization** is preferred for **dairy**; **ultrasonic** for **nano-emulsions**; **microfluidization** for **lipid nanoparticles** in pharma/nutraceuticals.

topics.title: Batch and Continuous Sterilization Processes (Including Steps and Various Machineries Involved) Used in Canning of Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Batch and Continuous Sterilization Processes (Including Steps and Various Machineries Involved) Used in Canning of Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 вҖ” Canning of Foods: Thermal Sterilization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Thermal Sterilization (Commercial Sterilization)** is the unit operation in which shelf-stable, hermetically sealed canned foods are subjected to controlled moist heat (saturated steam) of sufficient intensity and duration to render the product **commercially sterile** вҖ” i.e., free from viable and toxin-forming pathogenic microorganisms of public-health concern (especially *Clostridium botulinum*) and from microorganisms capable of growing at normal storage temperatures, while preserving sensory and nutritional quality.

In the context of **canning**, sterilization is the penultimate thermal processing step sandwiched between *sealing* and *cooling*. The **process lethality** is quantitatively expressed by the reference **FвӮҖ value = 2.52 minutes at 121.1 В°C (250 В°F)** for low-acid foods (pH > 4.6), as standardized by the National Canners Association (NCA).

### 1.1 Batch vs Continuous Sterilization вҖ” The Two Engineering Philosophies

| Mode | Operating Philosophy | Throughput | Industry Use |
|------|---------------------|------------|--------------|
| **Batch Sterilization** | A discrete charge of cans is processed together in a closed vessel (retort) for a fixed thermal schedule | LowвҖ“Medium (5вҖ“400 cans/cycle) | Small to medium industries, R&D, multiproduct plants |
| **Continuous Sterilization** | Cans move sequentially through a thermal zone under steady-state conditions | High (>10,000 cans/h) | Large fruit/vegetable/pet-food plants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)**
> Think of **batch sterilization** as boiling food in a domestic **pressure cooker** вҖ” you load the cooker, seal it, time it, then unload. **Continuous sterilization** is like an **industrial conveyor passing through a hot tunnel oven** вҖ” products enter at one end, ride through a controlled temperature profile, and exit sterilized at the other end. Both achieve the same microbiological kill (lethality), but the engineering hardware, control philosophy, and capital cost differ drastically.

### 1.2 Critical Terminology for KTU Examinations

- **Commercial Sterility**: Condition achieved by thermal processing вҖ” absence of microorganisms capable of growing in the food at normal non-refrigerated storage conditions.
- **Lethality (FвӮҖ)**: Equivalent sterilization time at the reference temperature 121.1 В°C, integrating the time-temperature lethal effect of the entire process. **Standard FвӮҖ = 2.52 min** for *C. botulinum* in low-acid foods.
- **Process Time (B)**: Total heating time required at the operating retort temperature to deliver the target FвӮҖ at the *cold spot* of the can.
- **Cold Spot**: The geometric location inside a can with the slowest heating rate (center of the can for conduction-heating foods; ~25 % from bottom for convection-heating foods).
- **D-value**: Decimal reduction time вҖ” minutes required at a given temperature to reduce a specific microbial population by 90 % (one log cycle).
- **Z-value**: Temperature change (В°C) required to change the D-value by a factor of **10**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **reference temperature** when quoting D and Z values. Saying "D = 1.5 minutes" is incomplete вҖ” say **DвӮҒвӮӮвӮҒ.вӮҒВ°C = 1.5 min** (or with В°F notation if asked).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Steps of Canning (Process Flow)

The canning line, irrespective of batch or continuous sterilization, follows a fixed engineering sequence:

1. **Raw Material Receiving & Inspection** вҖ” sorting, grading, microbiological sampling.
2. **Cleaning / Washing** вҖ” spray, soak, or rotary-drum washers.
3. **Peeling, Coring, Slicing** вҖ” mechanical (abrasion, knife), chemical (lye, NaOH), or steam peeling.
4. **Blanching** вҖ” hot-water (88вҖ“99 В°C) or steam (100 В°C) for 2вҖ“5 min; inactivates enzymes (PPO, POD).
5. **Filling & Syrup/Brine Addition** вҖ” measured volume/weight; headspace вүҲ 6вҖ“10 % of can height.
6. **Exhausting** вҖ” removal of headspace air by steam injection or hot-fill so steam displaces OвӮӮ.
7. **Sealing** вҖ” hermetic closure by can seamer (double-seam).
8. **Thermal Sterilization (Batch or Continuous)** вҖ” the focus of this note.
9. **Cooling** вҖ” water spray/immersion to вүӨ 40 В°C to prevent stack-burn and thermophilic spoilage.
10. **Labeling, Coding, Storage** вҖ” 4вҖ“24 В°C, RH 70 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sterilization step is **#8**, but it is the **technologically and economically defining operation** of the entire canning process. All upstream steps exist only to support efficient, uniform, and reproducible heat transfer into the can during sterilization.

## 2.2 Modes of Heat Transfer Inside the Can

The *physics of sterilization* depends entirely on the dominant heat-transfer mechanism inside the sealed container:

- **Convection Heating Foods**: Soups, juices, milk, thin sauces вҖ” heat travels by bulk fluid motion. Heating is fast; the **cold spot is offset from geometric center** (вүҲ 25 % above base of can).
- **Conduction Heating Foods**: Purees, mashed vegetables, thick pastes, starch puddings вҖ” heat travels by molecular vibration. Heating is slow; **cold spot is the geometric center**.

This dichotomy is the foundation of all process-calculation formulae.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-Examiner favorites вҖ” practice their applications exhaustively.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Where Used | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | **Log-cycle reduction** | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log(N_0/N)}$ | Decimal reduction time | min |
| 2 | **Z-value relation** | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$ | Temperature dependence of D | В°C |
| 3 | **Lethality at temperature T** | $L = 10^{(T - 121.1)/Z}$ | Per-minute lethal rate at any T | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Reference lethality** | $F_0 = \Delta t \sum 10^{(T - 121.1)/Z}$ | Total process lethality | min |
| 5 | **Heat penetration factor (slope)** | $f_h = \dfrac{T_{retort} - T_{p1}}{( \log (j_h \cdot i_h) - \log (T_{retort} - T_{p2}))}$ | Slope of semi-log heat penetration | min |
| 6 | **Lag factor (j-value)** | $j_h = \dfrac{T_{retort} - T_{pi}}{T_{retort} - T_{0}}$ | Initial heating lag (dimensionless) | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Ball process time** | $B = f_h \log \left[ \dfrac{j_h \cdot i_h}{i} \right]$ | Total sterilization time | min |
| 8 | **Stumbo's improved formula** | $B_{B} = f_h \log \left[ \dfrac{j_h \cdot i_h}{i_{B}} \right]$ | Ball-Stumbo (with cooling phase) | min |
| 9 | **Stumbo/Cathkart lethality** | $F_0 = 10^{(T_{ret} - 250)/z} \cdot f_h \left[\log(j_h i_h / i_B)\right]$ | Process lethality via f and j | min |
| 10 | **Cold-point lethality** | $F_{cold} = 10^{(T_{cold,eq} - 121.1)/Z} \cdot B$ | Lethality at slowest-heating point | min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention**: All FвӮҖ, f_h, j_h are reported **at the reference temperature 121.1 В°C (250 В°F)** when following Ball's method, or at 121.1 В°C using Stumbo's generalized procedure (1973).

### 2.4 Why Batch and Continuous? The Engineering Trade-off

**Batch Sterilization Advantages**
- Flexibility to run many SKUs (stock-keeping units) of different sizes.
- Lower capital outlay for low-volume specialty products.
- Easier GMP/cleaning between cycles (single retort isolated from line).

**Batch Sterilization Disadvantages**
- Dead-time between cycles (loading, venting, come-up, cooling).
- Non-uniform temperature profile within retort during come-up.
- Higher unit steam and water consumption per can.

**Continuous Sterilization Advantages**
- Steady-state thermal profile вҮ’ uniform, reproducible lethality.
- Very high throughput (10 000вҖ“50 000 cans hвҒ»В№).
- Lower unit operating cost per can; efficient use of steam.

**Continuous Sterilization Disadvantages**
- High capital cost.
- Limited to a narrow product-size window per line.
- Difficult changeovers; long start-up.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU ESE 14-mark answers, **always structure your comparison using a labeled table** followed by 2вҖ“3 sentences of *justified selection criteria* вҖ” e.g., "Continuous sterilization is selected for plain green peas at 80 000 cans/day because the product is a low-viscosity particulate and can flow through hydrostatic cookers, whereas batch is retained for institution-size #10 cans of pet food."

## 2.5 Machineries Involved вҖ” Detailed Taxonomy

### A. Batch Sterilization Equipment

1. **Still Horizontal Steam Retort (Static)** вҖ” rectangular pressure vessel, steam injection via perforated pipes, gravity-displacement of air. Most common in small canneries.
2. **Vertical Steam Retort** вҖ” cylindrical; useful for basket-loading fragile products (mushrooms, artichokes).
3. **Crate-loading Retort (Sterilmatic-type)** вҖ” cans remain in their wire-baskets during retort loading; high throughput per batch.
4. **Agitating Batch Retorts**
   - **Rotary Retort** вҖ” cylindrical vessel rotates end-over-end at 4вҖ“30 rpm; improves convection inside can; Вұ30 % reduction in process time vs static retort.
   - **Shaka Retort** вҖ” reciprocating axial shaking (e.g., FMC Shaka); high-end agitating retort for viscous conduction foods (curries, gravies).
5. **Water Immersion Retort (Cascade)** вҖ” cans submerged in hot water under pressure; uniform external heat transfer.
6. **Water Spray Retort** вҖ” water recirculated and sprayed over cans; preferred over still-steam for tall cans and glass jars.

### B. Continuous Sterilization Equipment

1. **Atmospheric Pressure Continuous Sterilizer** вҖ” for **acidified / high-acid foods** (pH вүӨ 4.6: tomato paste, citrus juice, pickles). 88вҖ“100 В°C, residence time 15вҖ“40 min. Examples: FMC Rotary Cooker/Cooler; A.K. Robins Continuous Cooker.
2. **Pressure Continuous Sterilizer** вҖ” for **low-acid foods** (pH > 4.6: peas, corn, meat, seafood).
   - **Hydrostatic Sterilizer** (FMC, Stork) вҖ” three-chamber tower: water leg (вүҲ 30 m) provides pressure seal by hydrostatic head; steam zone in middle; cooling water leg on exit side. Capacity 30 000вҖ“100 000 cans hвҒ»В№.
   - **Rotary Pressure Sterilizer** (FMC Sterilmatic) вҖ” rotating helical coil under steam pressure; for cans 200вҖ“425 g.
   - **Spiral Pressure Sterilizer** вҖ” cans travel on an endless belt wound as a spiral inside a pressurized shell.
3. **Aseptic Sterilization Systems** вҖ” product sterilized separately (HTST) then filled into pre-sterilized containers in a sterile zone (e.g., Dole aseptic cans, Tetra Brik).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Microbial Death Kinetics to Ball's Formula

### Step 1 вҖ” First-order inactivation kinetics

For a homogeneous isothermal microbial population, the death rate is first-order:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k N \quad \Rightarrow \quad N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

Taking base-10 logarithm:

$$\log\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k' t$$

By definition, when $N/N_0 = 1/10$ in time $D$, then $k' = 1/D$, hence:

$$\log\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D} \tag{1}$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Temperature dependence of D (Z-value definition)

The empirical relation between $D$ and $T$ is log-linear:

$$\log D = \log D_{ref} - \frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z} \tag{2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{D} = \frac{1}{D_{ref}} \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z} \tag{3}$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Lethality at any temperature

The **per-minute lethal rate** $L$ at temperature $T$ is defined as:

$$L(T) = \frac{1}{D(T)} = \frac{1}{D_{ref}} \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$$

Normalizing to reference $T_{ref} = 121.1\ ^\circ \text{C}$:

$$L(T) = 10^{(T - 121.1)/Z} \tag{4}$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Cumulative lethality (FвӮҖ)

For a varying temperature history $T(t)$:

$$F_0 = \int_{0}^{B} 10^{(T(t) - 121.1)/Z} \, dt \tag{5}$$

For discrete time-step measurement:

$$F_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i \cdot 10^{(T_i - 121.1)/Z} \tag{6}$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Heat penetration curve (semi-log model)

Empirically, the temperature at the **cold spot** follows:

$$\log(T_{retort} - T_{p}) = \log(j_h \cdot i_h) - \frac{t}{f_h} \tag{7}$$

where

- $i_h$ = temperature at the *start* of straight-line heating period (В°C or В°F below $T_{retort}$).
- $j_h$ = lag factor (intercept correction).
- $f_h$ = time for the heating curve to traverse one log cycle (minutes).

Rearranging (7) for time $t$:

$$t = f_h \log\!\left(\frac{j_h \cdot i_h}{T_{retort} - T_p}\right) \tag{8}$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Ball's Process Time formula

The process ends when the cold-spot temperature reaches the critical value $i$ (corresponding to required lethality). Setting $T_p = T_{retort} - i$ in (8):

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= f_h \log\!\left(\frac{j_h \cdot i_h}{T_{retort} - (T_{retort} - i)}\right) \\
  &= f_h \log\!\left(\frac{j_h \cdot i_h}{i}\right) \tag{9}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Ball Process Time Formula** вҖ” the most frequently quoted equation in KTU paper setters' minds.

### Step 7 вҖ” Stumbo's improved formula (cooling phase included)

For accurate total lethality including the cooling (post-cutoff) phase:

$$B_B = f_h \log\!\left(\frac{j_h \cdot i_h}{i_B}\right) \tag{10}$$
