# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`
**Define the term "Steam Economy" of an evaporator. What is its value for a single effect evaporator operating with water as the feed?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Steam Economy (E)** is defined as the **mass of water (or solvent) vaporized per unit mass of steam supplied** to the evaporator.
>
> $$
> E = \frac{V}{S} \quad \text{(kg water / kg steam)}
> $$
>
> For a single effect evaporator operating with pure water as the feed, the energy balance gives $S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v$. If $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_v$ (which holds near atmospheric pressure), then $V \approx S$ and the theoretical maximum steam economy is **E = 1.0** (i.e., 1 kg of water evaporated per kg of steam). In practice, due to sensible heat, BPE, and heat losses, the value is typically **0.7 вҖ“ 0.95**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`
**Explain the concept of Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) and its significance in the design of food evaporators.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the increase in the boiling point of a solution relative to that of pure water at the same pressure. It arises from the **colligative effect** of dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, proteins, acids in food systems).
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$
> T_{boil, solution} = T_{sat, water}(P) + BPE
> $$
>
> **Significance in food evaporator design:**
> 1. **Reduces effective О”T** available for heat transfer, since boiling temperature of solution is higher than pure water at the same pressure.
> 2. **Increases required heat transfer area** $A$ (since $A = Q/(U \cdot \Delta T_{eff})$).
> 3. **For highly concentrated foods** (e.g., 65 % orange juice concentrate, sugar syrups), BPE can be 4вҖ“8 В°C, reducing evaporator capacity by 15вҖ“25 %.
> 4. **BPE must be considered** in vacuum operation to correctly predict boiling temperature and to design the condenser.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A вҖ” `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A single effect evaporator is to be used to concentrate a fruit juice from 12 % to 50 % total solids. The feed rate is 20,000 kg/h at 30 В°C. Saturated steam at 2.0 bar (gauge) is supplied. The evaporator operates at an absolute pressure of 20 kPa. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 2500 W/mВІВ·K. The BPE at the operating conditions is 3.0 В°C. Latent heat of steam = 2200 kJ/kg, latent heat of vapor at 20 kPa = 2350 kJ/kg. Specific heat of dilute juice вүҲ 3.9 kJ/kgВ·K. The condensate leaves at 100 В°C.**

**(a)** Calculate the **mass of water evaporated (V)** and the **concentrated product flow rate (L)**.
**(b)** Calculate the **steam consumption**, **steam economy**, and **heat transfer area** required.

#### Solution A(a): Mass Balance [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $F = 20{,}000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.12$, $x_L = 0.50$.

**Water to be evaporated:**

$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 20{,}000 \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{0.50}\right)
$$

$$
V = 20{,}000 \times (1 - 0.24) = 20{,}000 \times 0.76
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 15{,}200 \text{ kg/h}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}
$$

**Product flow rate:**

$$
L = F - V = 20{,}000 - 15{,}200
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 4800 \text{ kg/h}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Verification (solids balance):**
- Solids in: $20{,}000 \times 0.12 = 2400$ kg/h
- Solids out: $4800 \times 0.50 = 2400$ kg/h вң… **[2 marks for verification]**

#### Solution A(b): Energy Balance & Area [7 marks]

**Operating temperatures (from steam tables):**
- Steam at 2.0 bar(g) вҶ’ $T_s = 134$ В°C, $\lambda_s = 2200$ kJ/kg.
- Water at 20 kPa вҶ’ $T_{sat} = 60$ В°C, $\lambda_v = 2350$ kJ/kg.
- Boiling point of solution = $60 + 3.0 = 63$ В°C.
- $\Delta T_{eff} = 134 - 63 = 71$ K.

**Step 1: Heat required for evaporation:**

$$
Q_{vap} = V \cdot \lambda_v = 15{,}200 \times 2350 = 35{,}720{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

$$
Q_{vap} = \frac{35{,}720{,}000}{3600} = 9922.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Heat for sensible heating (feed from 30 В°C to 63 В°C):**

$$
Q_{sens} = F \cdot c_{p,F} \cdot \Delta T = 20{,}000 \times 3.9 \times (63 - 30)
$$

$$
Q_{sens} = 20{,}000 \times 3.9 \times 33 = 2{,}574{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 715.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Total heat load:**

$$
Q_{total} = 9922.2 + 715.0 = 10{,}637.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Steam consumption (full balance including condensate at 100 В°C):**

$$
S \cdot \lambda_s = Q_{total} + S \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot T_{cond}
$$

Working in kJ/h:

$$
S \times 2200 = 38{,}294{,}000 + S \times 4.18 \times 100
$$

$$
S \times 2200 = 38{,}294{,}000 + 418 \cdot S
$$

$$
S \times (2200 - 418) = 38{,}294{,}000
$$

$$
S \times 1782 = 38{,}294{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 21{,}488.2 \text{ kg/h}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}
$$

**Step 5: Steam economy:**

$$
E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{15{,}200}{21{,}488.2} = 0.707
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 0.71 \text{ kg water/kg steam}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Step 6: Heat transfer area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q_{total}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{eff}} = \frac{10{,}637.2 \times 1000}{2500 \times 71} = \frac{10{,}637{,}200}{177{,}500}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 59.93 \text{ m}^2} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

---

### Question B вҖ” Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**A single effect evaporator concentrates milk from 10 % to 50 % total solids. Feed rate = 5000 kg/h, feed enters at 50 В°C. Saturated steam at 1.5 bar(g) (T = 127 В°C, О»вӮӣ = 2225 kJ/kg) is used. Vacuum is maintained at 30 kPa (TвӮӣвӮҗвӮң = 69 В°C, О»бөҘ = 2280 kJ/kg). BPE = 2.0 В°C, U = 2200 W/mВІВ·K, cвӮҡ of milk вүҲ 3.85 kJ/kgВ·K. The feed is pre-heated by a vapor bleed to its boiling point.**

**(a)** Determine the **rate of water evaporation (V)** and the **concentrate flow rate (L)**.
**(b)** Calculate the **steam consumption, steam economy, and the required heat transfer area** when the feed enters at its boiling point.

#### Solution B(a): Mass Balance [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $F = 5000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.10$, $x_L = 0.50$.

$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 5000 \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right)
$$

$$
V = 5000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 5000 \times 0.80
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 4000 \text{ kg/h}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}
$$

$$
L = F - V = 5000 - 4000
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 1000 \text{ kg/h}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Verification:**
- Solids in: $5000 \times 0.10 = 500$ kg/h.
- Solids out: $1000 \times 0.50 = 500$ kg/h вң… **[2 marks]**

#### Solution B(b): Steam Economy & Area [7 marks]

**Operating temperatures:**
- $T_s = 127$ В°C (steam).
- Boiling temperature of milk = $69 + 2.0 = 71$ В°C.
- $\Delta T_{eff} = 127 - 71 = 56$ K.

**Since feed enters at boiling point, $Q_{sens} = 0$.**

$$
Q = V \cdot \lambda_v = 4000 \times 2280 = 9{,}120{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{9{,}120{,}000}{3600} = 2533.3 \text{ kW}
$$

**Steam consumption (ignoring condensate):**

$$
S = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{9{,}120{,}000}{2225} = 4098.9 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 4099 \text{ kg/h}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Steam economy:**

$$
E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{4000}{4099} = 0.976
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 0.98} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Heat transfer area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{eff}} = \frac{2533.3 \times 1000}{2200 \times 56} = \frac{2{,}533{,}300}{123{,}200}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 20.56 \text{ m}^2} \quad \text{[3 marks]}
$$

---

## 5.3 вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting BPE** вҖ” Many students compute О”T using only the saturation temperature of water at the operating pressure, ignoring the Boiling Point Elevation. This **overestimates** О”T and **underestimates** the heat transfer area. Loss: **2вҖ“3 marks** consistently.
>
> 2. **Mixing up latent heats** вҖ” Using $\lambda_s$ (steam-side) where $\lambda_v$ (vapor-side) is required, or vice versa. Steam-side $\lambda_s$ is used to compute **S from Q**, while $\lambda_v$ is used to compute **Q from V**. Loss: **1вҖ“2 marks** per error.
>
> 3. **Ignoring sensible heat** вҖ” When the feed temperature is given (e.g., 30 В°C) but boiling point is higher, students must include $F \cdot c_{p,F} \cdot \Delta T$ in the energy balance. Omission loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Unit conversion errors** вҖ” Mixing kJ/h and kW. Always state both: $Q$ in kW for area calculation (using W/mВІВ·K for U) and $Q$ in kJ/h for steam calculation. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **No verification of solids balance** вҖ” Always plug $L \times x_L$ to confirm it equals $F \times x_F$. This simple step shows examiner that you understand conservation principles and **earks a free half mark** for completeness.
>
> 6. **Steam economy formula confusion** вҖ” Steam economy $E = V/S$, **NOT** $E = S/V$. Some students invert it.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to subtract condensate enthalpy** вҖ” When the problem states "condensate leaves at X В°C", the effective latent heat available becomes $(\lambda_s - c_w \cdot T_{cond})$. Missing this yields an incorrect $S$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember рҹ“қ

| # | Key Concept | Quick Recall |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Evaporation** | Removal of volatile solvent (water) by boiling |
| 2 | **Single Effect** | Vapor from boiling is **condensed and discarded** after one stage |
| 3 | **Steam Economy (E)** | $E = V/S$; theoretical max = 1.0 for single effect |
| 4 | **Capacity** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$ (in kW or kJ/h) |
| 5 | **Mass Balance** | $F = V + L$; $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ |
| 6 | **Vapor formula** | $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ |
| 7 | **Energy Balance** | $S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + \text{(sensible + losses)}$ |
| 8 | **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** | $\Delta T_{BPE}$ = solution BP вҲ’ water BP at same pressure |
| 9 | **Effective О”T** | $\Delta T_{eff} = T_{steam} - T_{boil, solution}$ |
| 10 | **Area formula** | $A = S \cdot \lambda_s / (U \cdot \Delta T_{eff})$ |
| 11 | **Vacuum** | Lowers boiling temperature вҶ’ protects heat-sensitive foods |
| 12 | **Pre-heating** | Increases economy (e.g., 0.89 вҶ’ 0.97 in worked example) |
| 13 | **Multiple Effect** | Splits О”T across stages вҶ’ higher E, lower capacity per stage |
| 14 | **Calandria** | Steam chest containing tube bundle |
| 15 | **Demister** | Prevents liquid carry-over into vapor line |
| 16 | **Condensate** | Hot water; can be recovered for pre-heating |
| 17 | **Steam tables** | Always needed for $T_s$, $T_{sat}$, $\lambda_s$, $\lambda_v$ |
| 18 | **Hydrostatic head** | Tall calandrias add 1вҖ“3 В°C to BPE |
| 19 | **Fouling** | Reduces U over time; requires CIP |
| 20 | **U value** | Decreases with viscosity; 1500вҖ“3500 W/mВІВ·K typical |

### рҹ”‘ Top 5 Most-Asked KTU Topics from this Module

1. **Derivation of V and L** from mass balance (asked in 80 % of papers).
2. **Steam economy calculation** with and without feed pre-heating.
3. **Heat transfer area calculation** including BPE.
4. **Constructional features** of single effect evaporator (labeled diagram).
5. **Comparison of atmospheric vs vacuum** evaporation for food products.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says "with and without pre-heating", always compute **two** values of $E$ and $A$ and **comment on the difference** to grab the full 14 marks. The examiners love a comparative discussion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Features of Cold Storage and Basic Design Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructional Features of Cold Storage and Basic Design Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cold Storage?

A **Cold Storage** (also called a *refrigerated warehouse* or *cold room*) is a specially engineered, insulated structure designed to store perishable food products (fruits, vegetables, meat, fish, dairy, and frozen foods) at controlled low temperatures for extended durations. It integrates **building construction**, **thermal insulation**, **refrigeration machinery**, and **air-handling systems** into a single functional unit to preserve the quality, safety, and shelf-life of food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A cold storage is an *insulated, temperature-controlled enclosed space* in which temperature (and sometimes humidity and gas composition) is artificially maintained at a level suitable for the preservation and storage of food commodities, using a vapour-compression refrigeration system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Giant Thermos + Refrigerator" Model

Imagine your household refrigerator. Now scale it up to the size of a **single-storey godown** that can hold 5,000 tonnes of apples. The cold storage is essentially a **giant, super-insulated thermos flask** combined with a **central air-conditioning system** that never stops running.

- The **insulated walls, floor, and ceiling** behave like the thermos vacuum gap вҖ” they *resist heat inflow* from the hot outside world.
- The **refrigeration unit** is the *compressor/condenser* that pumps heat out continuously.
- The **air circulation fans** spread the cold air uniformly, like the small fan inside your fridge.
- The **humidity control** keeps produce from shrivelling (too dry) or rotting (too damp).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold Chain Concept:** A cold storage is just **one node** in a continuous temperature-controlled supply chain (cold chain) вҖ” from the farm gate вҶ’ transport вҶ’ cold storage вҶ’ retail display вҶ’ consumer refrigerator. Breaking the chain anywhere causes spoilage.

## 1.3 Classification of Cold Storage (KTU-Syllabus Standard)

| Type | Temperature Range | Typical Products |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Chilled Storage** | **+2 В°C to +8 В°C** | Fruits, vegetables, dairy, fresh meat, eggs |
| **Frozen Storage** | **вҲ’18 В°C to вҲ’25 В°C** | Frozen meat, fish, ice cream, processed foods |
| **Deep Freeze / Sharp Freezer** | **вҲ’25 В°C to вҲ’40 В°C** | Long-term frozen preservation, industrial freezing |
| **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage** | **0 В°C to +5 В°C** with modified OвӮӮ/COвӮӮ | Apples, pears (long-term, 6вҖ“12 months) |
| **Modular Cold Room** | Variable | Small-scale, plug-and-play units |

## 1.4 Primary Constructional Features вҖ” Overview

A cold storage is NOT just a "room with a chiller." It has **eight major engineering systems** working in unison:

1. **Structural Shell** (walls, floor, roof)
2. **Thermal Insulation** (PUF, EPS, mineral wool, cork)
3. **Vapour Barrier** (polyethylene / aluminium foil)
4. **Refrigeration Plant** (compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator)
5. **Air Distribution System** (fans, ducts, air coolers)
6. **Doors & Air Curtains** (insulated sliding / hinged doors)
7. **Lighting & Electrical** (low-wattage, vapour-proof)
8. **Monitoring & Control** (temperature/humidity sensors, alarms, PLCs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants for Design (BOLD per protocol):**
> - Thermal conductivity of **PUF (Polyurethane Foam)**: **0.020 вҖ“ 0.024 W/mВ·K**
> - Thermal conductivity of **EPS (Expanded Polystyrene)**: **0.032 вҖ“ 0.038 W/mВ·K**
> - Thermal conductivity of **Cork board**: **0.040 вҖ“ 0.045 W/mВ·K**
> - Standard design ambient (outside) for India: **+43 В°C DB / +27 В°C WB** (as per ISHRAE)
> - Standard floor temperature for chilled rooms: **+1 В°C to +4 В°C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Load Components вҖ” The "Seven Heat Infiltrators"

Any cold storage design begins with a **Heat Load Calculation (HLC)**. The total cooling load is the sum of seven individual heat sources. Think of them as seven different "leaks" through which heat tries to enter your refrigerated box.

### 2.1.1 Transmission Heat Load (QвӮҒ) вҖ” Heat through walls, roof, floor

This is heat conducted from the warm outside to the cold inside through the insulated envelope.

$$
Q_1 = U \times A \times \Delta T \quad [W]
$$

Where:
- $U$ = Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient of wall/floor/roof **(W/mВІВ·K)**
- $A$ = Surface area of that component **(mВІ)**
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between outside and inside **(K or В°C)**

The **U-value** for a composite wall (insulation + cladding + air film) is:

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + R_{insulation} + R_{cladding} + R_{se}}
$$

Where for a single insulation layer of thickness $t$ and conductivity $k$:

$$
R_{insulation} = \frac{t}{k} \quad [m^2 \cdot K / W]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The thicker and lower-$k$ the insulation, the bigger the $R$ (thermal resistance), and the smaller the $U$. The $U$-value is essentially "how easily heat passes through" вҖ” low $U$ = good insulation.

### 2.1.2 Product Heat Load (QвӮӮ) вҖ” Heat that must be removed from food itself

Food entering the cold storage is at a higher temperature. It must be cooled to storage temperature. This is often the **largest single heat load** in practice.

$$
Q_2 = m \times c_p \times (T_{in} - T_{out}) \quad [W]
$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of product entering per unit time **(kg/s)**
- $c_p$ = specific heat of product **(kJ/kgВ·K)**
- $T_{in}$ = product entry temperature **(В°C)**
- $T_{out}$ = final storage temperature **(В°C)**

For **freezing applications**, latent heat of fusion must be added:

$$
Q_{2,freeze} = m \times \left[ c_{p,unfrozen} (T_{in} - T_{f}) + L_f + c_{p,frozen} (T_{f} - T_{out}) \right]
$$

Where $L_f$ = latent heat of freezing (water: **334 kJ/kg**).

### 2.1.3 Respiration Heat Load (QвӮғ) вҖ” Live produce "breathes"

Fruits and vegetables are *living tissues* that respire вҖ” consuming OвӮӮ, releasing COвӮӮ, and **generating heat**. This is unique to fresh produce cold stores.

$$
Q_3 = m \times r \quad [W]
$$

Where $r$ = respiration rate of the commodity **(W/kg or mW/kg)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Apples have $r \approx$ 10вҖ“20 mW/kg at 0 В°C, while strawberries have $r \approx$ 40вҖ“60 mW/kg. Higher temperature = drastically higher respiration (roughly doubles every 10 В°C вҖ” *QвӮҒвӮҖ effect*).

### 2.1.4 Internal Load вҖ” Equipment, Lights, Personnel (QвӮ„, QвӮ…, QвӮҶ)

- **Equipment heat (QвӮ„):** Forklifts (~2вҖ“4 kW each), conveyor motors
- **Lighting (QвӮ…):** Vapour-proof fixtures, ~10вҖ“15 W/mВІ floor area
- **Personnel (QвӮҶ):** ~270 W per active worker, ~110 W per seated

### 2.1.5 Infiltration / Door Opening / Air Curtain Loss (QвӮҮ)

Every time the door opens, warm humid air rushes in. This is modelled as:

$$
Q_7 = V_{air} \times \rho_{air} \times c_{p,air} \times \Delta T + V_{air} \times \rho_{air} \times h_{fg} \times \Delta W
$$

Where $\Delta W$ = humidity ratio difference, $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporisation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Air curtains** at doors can reduce infiltration losses by 60вҖ“80%.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Heat Load / Parameter | Formula | Unit | Typical Value (India) |
|---|----------------------|---------|------|----------------------|
| 1 | Transmission load | $Q_1 = U A \Delta T$ | W | 30вҖ“40% of total |
| 2 | Product sensible cooling | $Q_2 = m c_p \Delta T$ | W | 40вҖ“55% of total |
| 3 | Product freezing | $m[c_{pu}(T_i - T_f) + L_f + c_{pf}(T_f - T_o)]$ | W | for frozen stores |
| 4 | Respiration load | $Q_3 = m r$ | W | 2вҖ“8% of total |
| 5 | Air-change / infiltration | $Q_7 = \dot{V} \rho c_p \Delta T$ | W | 5вҖ“10% of total |
| 6 | Insulation R-value | $R = t / k$ | mВІВ·K/W | PUF 50 mm вҶ’ 2.27 |
| 7 | Total cooling load | $Q_{total} = (1.1 \text{ to } 1.2) \times \Sigma Q_i$ | W | safety factor 10вҖ“20% |
| 8 | Refrigeration tonnage (TR) | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW | 1 TR = 3024 kcal/h |
| 9 | Insulation thickness | $t = k \left(\frac{1}{U_{target}} - R_{films} - R_{cladding}\right)$ | m | 50вҖ“150 mm typical |
| 10 | Dew-point check | $T_{surface} > T_{dew,outside}$ | В°C | avoid condensation |

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Food Engineering

The **basic design approach** integrates these loads to size the compressor, refrigerant piping, evaporator coils, and insulation. Errors in load estimation cause:
- **Undersizing** вҶ’ product spoilage, compressor trip, food safety risk
- **Oversizing** вҶ’ capital waste, compressor short-cycling, high humidity/icing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability Angle:** Modern cold stores use **screw compressors with VFD**, **ammonia (R-717)** or **COвӮӮ (R-744)** as refrigerants (low GWP), **PUF panels with zero-ODP blowing agents**, and **heat-recovery condensers** вҖ” all aligned to KTU 2024's "Sustainable Food Engineering" outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wall U-Value and Insulation Thickness

A cold storage wall typically consists of (outside вҶ’ inside):
1. Outer cladding (GI sheet or RCC)
2. Vapour barrier (PE sheet / aluminium foil)
3. Insulation (PUF / EPS)
4. Inner cladding (GI sheet / aluminium)

### Step 1 вҖ” List thermal resistances

The thermal circuit is a series of four resistances:

$$
R_{total} = R_{se} + R_{clad,out} + R_{ins} + R_{vapour} + R_{clad,in} + R_{si}
$$

For a simplified single-insulation case (ignoring thin claddings & vapour barrier):

$$
R_{total} \approx \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_i}
$$

Where:
- $h_o$ = outside air film coefficient вүҲ **10 W/mВІВ·K**
- $h_i$ = inside air film coefficient вүҲ **8 W/mВІВ·K** (forced convection, internal fans ON)

### Step 2 вҖ” Express U

By definition:

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_i}}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Solve for required insulation thickness

Rearranging for $t_{ins}$ when target $U$ is given:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_i}
$$

$$
\frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} = \frac{1}{U} - \frac{1}{h_o} - \frac{1}{h_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, t_{ins} = k_{ins} \left( \frac{1}{U} - \frac{1}{h_o} - \frac{1}{h_i} \right) \,}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Worked Numerical Example (Chilled Room, PUF)

**Problem:** A chilled room is to be maintained at **+2 В°C** when outside design temperature is **+43 В°C DB**. Use PUF insulation with $k = 0.022$ W/mВ·K. Target $U$-value = 0.25 W/mВІВ·K. Find required insulation thickness.

**Solution:**

Step A вҖ” Compute $\Delta T$:

$$
\Delta T = 43 - 2 = 41 \text{ K}
$$

Step B вҖ” Substitute into the thickness formula:

$$
t_{ins} = 0.022 \left( \frac{1}{0.25} - \frac{1}{10} - \frac{1}{8} \right)
$$

$$
t_{ins} = 0.022 \left( 4.00 - 0.10 - 0.125 \right)
$$

$$
t_{ins} = 0.022 \times 3.775
$$

$$
\boxed{\, t_{ins} \approx 0.083 \text{ m} = 83 \text{ mm} \,}
$$

Hence, **use standard 80 mm or 100 mm PUF panels** in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Heat Load Estimation (Step-by-Step)

Consider a small cold storage: **10 m Г— 8 m Г— 4 m (height)** storing **20 tonnes of apples** entering daily at **+25 В°C** to be cooled to **0 В°C**, located in a region with **+40 В°C** ambient.

### Step A вҖ” Transmission Load (QвӮҒ)

Walls (two 10Г—4 + two 8Г—4) = $2(40 + 32) = 144$ mВІ  
Ceiling: $10 \times 8 = 80$ mВІ  
Floor: $10 \times 8 = 80$ mВІ  
Total $A \approx 304$ mВІ, average $U \approx 0.30$ W/mВІВ·K

$$
Q_1 = 0.30 \times 304 \times (40 - 0) = 3648 \text{ W} \approx 3.65 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step B вҖ” Product Sensible Load (QвӮӮ)

Daily inflow: $m = 20{,}000 \text{ kg} / 86400 \text{ s} = 0.231 \text{ kg/s}$  
For apples, $c_p \approx 3.80$ kJ/kgВ·K (above freezing)

$$
Q_2 = 0.231 \times 3.80 \times (25 - 0) = 21.97 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step C вҖ” Respiration Load (QвӮғ)

For apples at 0 В°C, $r \approx 15$ mW/kg = 0.015 W/kg. Stock вүҲ 50 tonnes (assuming 2.5 day residence) вҶ’ use 20 t/day turnover for design conservatism:

$$
Q_3 = 20{,}000 \times 0.015 = 300 \text{ W} = 0.30 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step D вҖ” Internal Loads (QвӮ„ + QвӮ… + QвӮҶ)

- 1 Forklift: ~2.5 kW
- Lighting: 80 mВІ Г— 12 W/mВІ = 0.96 kW
- 2 workers: 2 Г— 0.27 = 0.54 kW

$$
Q_{4+5+6} = 2.5 + 0.96 + 0.54 = 4.0 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step E вҖ” Infiltration / Door Losses (QвӮҮ)

Assume 15 door openings/hr, each exchanging 5 mВі of air:

$$
\dot{V} = 15 \times 5 = 75 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 0.0208 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_7 = 0.0208 \times 1.2 \times 1005 \times 40 \approx 1003 \text{ W} \approx 1.0 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step F вҖ” Total Cooling Load with Safety Factor

$$
Q_{total} = 1.15 \times (3.65 + 21.97 + 0.30 + 4.0 + 1.0)
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 1.15 \times 30.92 \approx 35.56 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\text{Refrigeration capacity in TR} = \frac{35.56}{3.517} \approx 10.1 \text{ TR}
$$

So a **10 TR refrigeration plant** is specified.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Choosing Insulation Material

| Property | PUF | EPS | Mineral Wool | Cork |
|----------|-----|-----|--------------|------|
| Thermal conductivity $k$ (W/mВ·K) | **0.020вҖ“0.024** | 0.032вҖ“0.038 | 0.040 | 0.040вҖ“0.045 |
| Density (kg/mВі) | 30вҖ“45 | 15вҖ“30 | 30вҖ“200 | 100вҖ“160 |
| Water absorption | Very low | Low | High | Medium |
| Fire rating | Self-extinguishing | Flammable | Non-combustible | Good |
| Cost (вӮ№/mВІ for 50 mm) | High | Low | Medium | High |
| Best use | **Cold rooms, freezers** | Floor insulation | Roof (non-cold side) | Heritage / pharma |
| KTU recommended | **Yes вҖ” preferred** | Yes вҖ” economy | Limited | Specialised |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vapour Barrier Placement Rule:** The vapour barrier must be on the **WARM (outside) side** of the insulation. If placed on the cold side, water vapour from the room migrates *into* the insulation, condenses, freezes, and destroys thermal performance (a common KTU exam trap).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram вҖ” Cold Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Outside Ambient Air +43C] --> B[Structural Shell RCC GI Cladding]
    B --> C[Vapour Barrier PE Al Foil]
    C --> D[Insulation Layer PUF 80mm]
    D --> E[Inner Cladding GI Sheet Food Grade]
    E --> F[Cold Room Interior 0C to 4C]
    F --> G[Stored Food Product]
    F --> H[Air Cooler Evaporator with Fans]
    H --> I[Refrigeration Plant Compressor Condenser]
    I --> J[Expansion Valve]
    J --> H
    F --> K[Insulated Sliding Door with Air Curtain]
    F --> L[Temperature and Humidity Sensors]
    L --> M[PLC Controller and Alarm]
    F --> N[Lighting Vapour Proof LED]
    F --> O[Floor Insulation Load Bearing]
```

## 4.2 Heat Load Aggregation Flow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Q1 Transmission Load U A dT] --> Sum
    Q2[Q2 Product Sensible Load m cp dT] --> Sum
    Q3[Q3 Respiration Load m r] --> Sum
    Q4[Q4 Equipment Forklift Motors] --> Sum
    Q5[Q5 Lighting Heat] --> Sum
    Q6[Q6 Personnel Heat] --> Sum
    Q7[Q7 Infiltration Door Opening] --> Sum
    Sum[Total Heat Load Sum of Qi] --> SF[Apply Safety Factor 1.10 to 1.20]
    SF --> TR[Convert to Tons of Refrigeration TR kW]
    TR --> Comp[Screw or Reciprocating Compressor Sizing]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Section of Insulated Wall (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Outer[Outer Cladding GI 0.63mm] --> VB[Vapour Barrier Polyethylene 200 micron]
    VB --> INS[Insulation PUF 80 to 150mm]
    INS --> Inner[Inner Cladding GI 0.63mm PVC Coated]
    INS -.-> Floor[Sub Floor: EPS 100mm Load Bearing]
    INS -.-> Roof[Roof: PUF 100mm plus Weatherproof Bitumen]
```

## 4.4 Constructional Feature Checklist (Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Cold Storage Design Starts] --> Site[Site Selection and Civil Works]
    Site --> Struct[Structural Shell RCC PUF Panels]
    Struct --> Ins[Insulation and Vapour Barrier]
    Ins --> Floor[Insulated Floor with Heating Cable if Freezer]
    Floor --> Door[Insulated Doors and Air Curtains]
    Door --> RefPlant[Refrigeration Plant Installation]
    RefPlant --> AirDist[Air Distribution Ducts and Coolers]
    AirDist --> Elec[Electrical and Lighting]
    Elec --> Inst[Instrumentation PLC SCADA]
    Inst --> Test[Commissioning Leak Test and Performance Test]
    Test --> End[Operational Handover]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For Freezer Floors (-25 В°C):** A **heater cable / glycol loop** is embedded below the floor insulation to prevent **frost heave** (ground freezing and heaving the floor upwards). This is a *favourite KTU question* вҖ” students often forget it.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A вҖ” 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024]**
**List the seven major sources of heat load in a cold storage. State which is usually the largest.**

**Model Answer:**

The seven sources of heat load are:
1. Transmission load through walls, floor, and roof ($Q_1 = UA\Delta T$)
2. Product sensible cooling load ($Q_2 = m c_p \Delta T$)
3. Respiration heat from fresh produce ($Q_3 = m \cdot r$)
4. Heat from internal equipment such as forklifts and motors ($Q_4$)
5. Heat from lighting fixtures ($Q_5$)
6. Heat from personnel working inside ($Q_6$)
7. Infiltration / door-opening / air-curtain loss ($Q_7$)

**Largest contributor:** In a typical cold store, the **product cooling load (QвӮӮ)** accounts for **40вҖ“55 %** of the total, followed by transmission load QвӮҒ (30вҖ“40 %).

> [!NOTE]
> Marks split: List 7 Г— 0.5 = 3.5 вҶ’ round; but KTU gives 3 marks for a concise answer. Use bullet form.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]**
**Define U-value. Why is PUF preferred over EPS for cold storage insulation?**

**Model Answer:**

**U-value (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient):** It is the rate of heat flow in watts through one square metre of a building element for each kelvin of temperature difference across it. Unit: **W/mВІВ·K**. A lower U-value indicates better insulation.

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_{si} + R_{insulation} + R_{cladding} + R_{se}}
$$

**Why PUF is preferred over EPS:**

| Property | PUF | EPS |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Thermal conductivity | 0.020вҖ“0.024 W/mВ·K | 0.032вҖ“0.038 W/mВ·K |
| Closed-cell structure (water ingress) | Excellent | Moderate |
| Strength and rigidity | High | Lower |
| Fire performance | Self-extinguishing | Flammable |
| Long-term R-value retention | Excellent | Degrades over time |

PUF gives **~40 % lower k-value**, better moisture resistance, and superior structural strength, making it the **KTU-recommended insulation** for cold rooms and freezers.

---

## PART B вҖ” 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A вҖ” 14 Marks [KTU University Exam вҖ” Model Paper Pattern]**

**A chilled storage room of internal dimensions 12 m Г— 10 m Г— 5 m is to be maintained at +2 В°C. The outside design temperature is +42 В°C DB and +28 В°C WB. The room is constructed with 100 mm PUF panels (k = 0.022 W/mВ·K) and a 100 mm EPS floor (k = 0.036 W/mВ·K). The room stores 30 tonnes of fresh fruits entering daily at +25 В°C (cp = 3.8 kJ/kgВ·K). Respiration rate = 18 mW/kg. One electric forklift (3 kW) operates 4 hours/day. Lighting load = 12 W/mВІ. Assume 2 workers inside (270 W each). Door openings = 20/hr, 6 mВі air exchange per opening. Apply safety factor of 15 %.**

#### (a) **Calculate the transmission heat load for walls, roof, and floor.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Compute surface areas:**

Walls: $2(12 \times 5) + 2(10 \times 5) = 2(60 + 50) = 220$ mВІ  
Roof: $12 \times 10 = 120$ mВІ  
Floor: $12 \times 10 = 120$ mВІ

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute U-values:**

For PUF walls/roof, with air films $h_o = 10$, $h_i = 8$:

$$
U_{wall} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.100}{0.022} + \frac{1}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 4.545 + 0.125} = \frac{1}{4.770} \approx 0.210 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
$$

For EPS floor:

$$
U_{floor} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.100}{0.036} + \frac{1}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 2.778 + 0.125} = \frac{1}{3.003} \approx 0.333 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Transmission loads:**

Walls: $Q_{1,wall} = 0.210 \times 220 \times (42 - 2) = 1848$ W  
Roof: $Q_{1,roof} = 0.210 \times 120 \times 40 = 1008$ W  
Floor: $Q_{1,floor} = 0.333 \times 120 \times 40 = 1598$ W

$$
Q_{1,total} = 1848 + 1008 + 1598 = 4454 \text{ W} \approx 4.45 \text{ kW}
$$

#### (b) **Calculate the total refrigeration load and recommend the plant capacity in TR.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Product sensible heat (QвӮӮ):**

Mass flow = $30{,}000 / 86400 = 0.347$ kg/s

$$
Q_2 = 0.347 \times 3.8 \times (25 - 2) = 30.27 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Respiration load (QвӮғ):** For 30,000 kg/day turnover, take 30,000 kg of stock for conservative design:

$$
Q_3 = 30{,}000 \times 0.018 = 540 \text{ W} = 0.54 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Internal loads (QвӮ„ + QвӮ… + QвӮҶ):**

Forklift (averaged): $3 \text{ kW} \times (4/24) = 0.50$ kW  
Lighting: $120 \text{ m}^2 \times 12 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1.44$ kW  
Personnel: $2 \times 0.27 = 0.54$ kW  

$$
Q_{456} = 0.50 + 1.44 + 0.54 = 2.48 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Infiltration load (QвӮҮ):**

$\dot{V} = 20 \times 6 = 120$ mВі/hr = 0.0333 mВі/s

$$
Q_7 = 0.0333 \times 1.2 \times 1005 \times 40 \approx 1606 \text{ W} \approx 1.61 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Total raw load:**

$$
Q_{raw} = 4.45 + 30.27 + 0.54 + 2.48 + 1.61 = 39.35 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Apply safety factor 15 %:**

$$
Q_{total} = 1.15 \times 39.35 = 45.25 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Convert to TR:**

$$
\text{TR} = \frac{45.25}{3.517} \approx 12.87 \text{ TR}
$$

**Recommendation:** Specify a **15 TR refrigeration plant** (next standard size up, with 15вҖ“20 % margin for pull-down, defrost, and future expansion).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall вҖ” KTU 2024:** Students commonly:
> - Forget to convert hours of forklift operation to average kW (loss: 1 mark)
> - Apply safety factor to the wrong term (must multiply **total**, not individual)
> - Use $c_p$ in **kJ** but leave flow rate in **kg/s** without unit conversion (loss: 1 mark)
> - Specify plant *equal* to calculated TR вҖ” must round **up** to next standard capacity.

---

### **QUESTION B вҖ” 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Design a frozen food storage for storage of 50 tonnes of frozen fish at вҲ’20 В°C. The outside ambient is +40 В°C. Fish enters at вҲ’15 В°C (already partially frozen). Use cp of frozen fish = 1.7 kJ/kgВ·K, cp of unfrozen fish = 3.5 kJ/kgВ·K, latent heat of freezing Lf = 240 kJ/kg, freezing point = вҲ’2 В°C. Wall construction: 150 mm PUF (k = 0.022 W/mВ·K).**

#### (a) **Calculate the U-value of the wall and the transmission load for a wall area of 250 mВІ.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” U-value calculation:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_i}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.150}{0.022} + \frac{1}{8}}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{0.10 + 6.818 + 0.125} = \frac{1}{7.043} = 0.142 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Transmission load:**

$\Delta T = 40 - (-20) = 60$ K

$$
Q_1 = U \times A \times \Delta T = 0.142 \times 250 \times 60 = 2130 \text{ W} \approx 2.13 \text{ kW}
$$

#### (b) **Estimate the daily product cooling/freezing load.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The fish enters at вҲ’15 В°C. It must reach вҲ’20 В°C. Since it is *already frozen* (below its freezing point of вҲ’2 В°C), only sensible cooling is needed вҖ” no latent heat component for this product. However, since fish was previously only "partially frozen" per problem statement, we conservatively treat the unfrozen fraction.

**Approach вҖ” Conservative Estimate:**

Assume 50 % of the mass crosses the freezing point. Total daily mass = 50,000 kg.

**Step 1 вҖ” Sensible cooling of frozen fraction (50 %):**

$$
Q_{frozen} = 25{,}000 \times 1.7 \times [(-15) - (-20)] = 25{,}000 \times 1.7 \times 5 = 212{,}500 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Unfrozen fraction (50 %) вҖ” full freezing:**

$$
Q_{unfrozen} = 25{,}000 \times \left[ 3.5 \times (0 - (-2)) + 240 + 1.7 \times ((-2) - (-20)) \right]
$$

$$
= 25{,}000 \times [3.5 \times 2 + 240 + 1.7 \times 18]
$$

$$
= 25{,}000 \times [7.0 + 240 + 30.6] = 25{,}000 \times 277.6 = 6{,}940{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Total daily product load:**

$$
Q_{2,total} = 212{,}500 + 6{,}940{,}000 = 7{,}152{,}500 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to kW (per second basis):**

$$
Q_2 = \frac{7{,}152{,}500}{86400} \approx 82.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Conclusion:** The product load of **~82.8 kW (вүҲ 23.5 TR)** dominates the design, justifying selection of a **30 TR or higher** refrigeration plant for the frozen store.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall вҖ” Freezing vs. Cooling:** Many students omit the **latent heat of fusion** term, treating frozen products as only needing sensible cooling. The problem deliberately uses a partially frozen inlet state to test whether you include $L_f$ (loss: 3 marks if missed). Always check whether the product crosses its freezing point.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember for KTU 2024 вҖ” Cold Storage Module 3:**

- **Cold storage = insulated structure + refrigeration plant + air distribution + control** вҖ” all four are mandatory.
- **Seven heat loads** to be summed: Transmission, Product, Respiration, Equipment, Lighting, Personnel, Infiltration.
- **Transmission load formula:** $Q_1 = U A \Delta T$ вҖ” compute $U$ from thermal resistance series network.
- **Product load formula:** $Q_2 = m c_p \Delta T$; add **latent heat $L_f$** if product is being frozen.
- **Respiration load applies only to fresh produce** (fruits, vegetables) вҖ” not to meat, fish, or frozen goods.
- **Insulation preference (KTU): PUF > EPS > Mineral Wool > Cork** вҖ” driven by $k$-value and moisture resistance.
- **Vapour barrier MUST be on the warm side** of insulation вҖ” placing it on the cold side causes condensation and ice damage inside the insulation.
- **For frozen store floors:** provide **heater cable** below insulation to prevent frost heave.
- **Standard design ambient in India:** **+43 В°C DB / +27 В°C WB** (ISHRAE).
- **Total cooling load** = (1.10 to 1.20) Г— sum of individual loads.
- **Refrigeration capacity unit:** 1 TR = **3.517 kW** = 3024 kcal/h.
- **Doors:** always use insulated sliding doors with air curtains to cut infiltration.
- **Lighting:** vapour-proof LED, ~10вҖ“15 W/mВІ, counts as internal load.
- **Forklifts** inside the cold room are major internal heat sources вҖ” average over 24 h, not just running hours.
- **Sustainability trend (KTU 2024):** Ammonia (R-717) and COвӮӮ (R-744) are preferred over CFCs/HCFCs due to zero ODP / low GWP.
- **Always round up** the calculated TR to the next standard plant size вҖ” never undersize.
- **Examination trick:** if outside $T$ and inside $T$ are both given in В°C, $\Delta T$ is numerically the same in K (only the *unit symbol* changes).

> **End of Notes вҖ” Module 3: Constructional Features of Cold Storage and Basic Design Approach**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Freezers Including Plate Contact Freezer and Air Blast Freezer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Freezers Including Plate Contact Freezer and Air Blast Freezer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Freezer? (KTU Formal Definition)

In Food Engineering, a **Freezer** is a thermal processing unit that rapidly removes sensible and latent heat from a food product, lowering its temperature below its freezing point (typically below **вҲ’18 В°C** for long-term commercial storage), thereby arresting microbial growth and biochemical deterioration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Write in ESE):**
> *Freezing is a unit operation in which the temperature of a food product is reduced below its freezing point, and a portion of the water in the product is converted into ice. The process is governed by the rate of heat removal through conduction, convection, or a combination of both.*

### 1.2 Classification of Commercial Freezers

Freezers are classified broadly by **mode of heat transfer** between the refrigerant/cold medium and the food product:

| Category | Mode of Heat Transfer | Heat Transfer Coefficient *h* (W/mВІK) |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Plate Contact Freezer | Conduction | **100 вҖ“ 1000** |
| Air Blast Freezer | Forced Convection | **10 вҖ“ 50** |
| Immersion / Cryogenic Freezer | Convection (Liquid) | **500 вҖ“ 1500** |
| Scraped Surface Freezer | Convection + Scraping | **200 вҖ“ 600** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of freezing food the way you cool down hot milk. If you put the vessel in a still room, cooling is slow (still air). If you blow a fan across it, cooling speeds up (air blast). If you press the vessel against an ice slab, heat flows out almost instantly through the metal (plate contact). The freezer simply "industrializes" these three scenarios.

### 1.4 Why Different Freezers?

The **freeze-rate** dictates ice crystal size, which determines **quality**:
- **Slow freezing** вҶ’ Large extracellular ice crystals вҶ’ Cell rupture вҶ’ Texture loss
- **Rapid freezing** вҶ’ Small intracellular ice crystals вҶ’ Intact cells вҶ’ Better quality

Therefore, the choice of freezer depends on the product geometry, water content, required throughput, and quality demands.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Plate Contact Freezer (PCF) вҖ” Operational Theory

**Principle:** Heat flows from the food вҶ’ through a thin metallic plate (aluminium/steel) вҶ’ to the evaporating refrigerant on the other side of the plate.

**Mechanism:** Pure **conduction** through the plate, with the food pressed hydraulically against the plate for intimate contact.

**Key Design Points:**
- Plate surface is hollow; refrigerant (NHвӮғ or Freon) flows/evaporates inside
- Food is packed between two plates (block freezing) or placed in moulds
- Pressures of **0.5 вҖ“ 1.0 bar** are applied pneumatically/hydraulically
- Plate spacing adjustable: typically **25 вҖ“ 75 mm**

**Types of Plate Contact Freezers:**
1. **Horizontal Plate Freezer** вҖ“ product placed between horizontal stacked plates. Used for fish blocks, meat blocks, butter.
2. **Vertical Plate Freezer** вҖ“ vertical plates with product in between. Used for whole fish in bulk.

### 2.2 Air Blast Freezer (ABF) вҖ” Operational Theory

**Principle:** Cold air is circulated over unpackaged or packaged food by fans, removing heat by **forced convection**.

**Key Parameters:**
- Air velocity: **1.5 вҖ“ 6 m/s** (normal), up to **25 m/s** for rapid blast
- Air temperature: **вҲ’25 В°C to вҲ’40 В°C**
- Refrigerant coil banks located inside the chamber

**Types of Air Blast Freezers:**

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Still-Air (Batch) Freezer** | No forced circulation; product on trays in insulated room | Small scale / laboratory |
| **Tunnel / Belt Freezer** | Product on a continuous belt; cold air blown from one end | Fish fillets, meat pieces |
| **Spiral (Belt) Freezer** | Belt winds around a central drum; short footprint | Pizza, patties, ready meals |
| **Fluidized Bed Freezer** | Air blown upward through a perforated bed; particles suspended | Peas, sweet corn, diced vegetables |

### 2.3 Comparison of Plate Contact vs Air Blast Freezer

| Feature | Plate Contact Freezer | Air Blast Freezer |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Heat transfer mode | Conduction | Forced convection |
| Heat transfer coeff. *h* | **100 вҖ“ 1000 W/mВІK** | **10 вҖ“ 50 W/mВІK** |
| Freezing rate | **Fast** | Moderate to Fast |
| Air handling | None required | Fans + ducting |
| Product contact | Direct (intimate) | Indirect (air gap) |
| Energy efficiency | **High** (no fan load) | Lower (fan power loss) |
| Suitability | Packaged, regular shapes, flat products | Irregular shapes, particulate foods |
| Capital cost | High | Moderate |
| Dehydration loss | Negligible | **1 вҖ“ 5 %** (depends on RH) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` inside tables to avoid breaking Markdown pipe syntax.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat removal (pre/post freezing zone) | W or J/s |
| 2 | $Q_{latent} = m \cdot \lambda_{fusion}$ | Latent heat of fusion removal at freezing point | W |
| 3 | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Newton's Law of Cooling (convective freezing) | W |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{k}{x} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Fourier's Law (plate contact conduction) | W |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{L}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient from Nusselt number | W/mВІK |
| 6 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter for turbulent flow over plates | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (governs flow regime) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f}{\theta} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{\Delta T} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{4 \cdot k_f} \right]$ | **Plank's Equation** (modified) | seconds |
| 9 | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot D}{k_f}$ | Biot number (surface vs internal resistance) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f}{\theta} \cdot \dfrac{D}{h} \left( \dfrac{P \cdot Bi + R \cdot Bi^2}{Bi + 1} \right)$ | Plank's equation expanded (general) | seconds |

**Where:**
- $\rho$ = density of food (kg/mВі)
- $L_f$ or $\lambda$ = latent heat of fusion (вүҲ **334 kJ/kg** for water)
- $\theta$ = temperature difference between product initial & refrigerant
- $D$ = characteristic thickness (m)
- $k_f$ = thermal conductivity of frozen food (W/mВ·K)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/mВІK)
- $P$, $R$ = Plank's geometric constants (slab: P = ВҪ, R = в…ӣ; cylinder: P = Вј, R = 1/16; sphere: P = 1/6, R = 1/24)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plate Freezers** dominate the **seafood export industry** (Kerala вҶ’ EU fish blocks).
- **Air Blast Freezers** are the backbone of **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing)** plants for berries, peas, and shrimp.
- Cryogenic freezers with liquid nitrogen are used for premium "flash-frozen" gourmet products.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation (Bi вүӘ 1 Limit)

The classical **Plank's Equation** for freezing time is derived from **steady-state heat conduction** through a slab whose surface temperature equals the refrigerant temperature.

**Step 1:** Heat flux through the slab (Fourier's law):

$$q = k_f \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the temperature distribution for an infinite slab with surface at $T_s$ and centre at $T_c$:

$$q = k_f \cdot A \cdot \frac{(T_c - T_s)}{x}$$

**Step 3:** Average temperature difference across slab from centre to surface is:

$$\Delta T_{avg} = \frac{\theta}{2}$$

where $\theta = T_c - T_s$ is the difference between initial product temperature and refrigerant temperature.

**Step 4:** Heat removed per unit time (latent only):

$$q = \rho \cdot L_f \cdot A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Equate conduction and latent heat removal rates:

$$k_f \cdot A \cdot \frac{\theta}{2x} = \rho \cdot L_f \cdot A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 6:** Separate variables and integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = D/2$:

$$\int_0^{D/2} x \, dx = \frac{k_f \cdot \theta}{2 \rho L_f} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$\frac{D^2}{8} = \frac{k_f \cdot \theta}{2 \rho L_f} \cdot t_f$$

**Step 7:** Solve for freezing time (slab geometry, P = ВҪ, R = в…ӣ):

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D^2}{4 k_f \cdot \theta}$$

**Step 8:** Introduce general Plank's geometric factors:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P \cdot D}{\Delta T} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{4 k_f} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard Plank equation form expected in KTU ESE** вҖ” memorize Step 8.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Plate Contact Freezer

**Problem:** A fish block of thickness **50 mm** is frozen in a horizontal plate contact freezer. The refrigerant temperature is **вҲ’40 В°C**, initial product temperature is **10 В°C**, latent heat of fusion = **234 kJ/kg** (typical for fish), thermal conductivity of frozen fish $k_f$ = **1.5 W/mВ·K**, density $\rho$ = **1050 kg/mВі**. Compute freezing time using Plank's equation for an infinite slab.

**Given:**
- $D$ = 0.05 m
- $T_{initial}$ = 10 В°C, $T_{refrig}$ = вҲ’40 В°C
- $\theta$ = 10 вҲ’ (вҲ’40) = **50 В°C**
- $L_f$ = 234,000 J/kg
- $k_f$ = 1.5 W/mВ·K
- $\rho$ = 1050 kg/mВі
- For infinite slab: P = ВҪ, R = в…ӣ

**Solution:**

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P D}{\Delta T_{av}} + \frac{R D^2}{4 k_f} \right]$$

Average temperature driving force for slab: $\Delta T_{av} = \theta/2 = 25$ В°C.

Substituting all values:

$$t_f = \frac{1050 \times 234{,}000}{50} \left[ \frac{0.5 \times 0.05}{25} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.05)^2}{4 \times 1.5} \right]$$

$$t_f = (4{,}914{,}000) \left[ \frac{0.025}{25} + \frac{0.0003125}{6.0} \right]$$

$$t_f = (4{,}914{,}000) \left[ 0.001 + 5.208 \times 10^{-5} \right]$$

$$t_f = (4{,}914{,}000) \times (0.00105208)$$

$$t_f \approx 5170 \, \text{seconds} \approx 1.43 \, \text{hours}$$

**Answer:** Freezing time вүҲ **1.43 hours**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Air Blast Freezer

**Problem:** A bed of peas of diameter **8 mm** is fluidized in an air blast freezer. Air at **вҲ’30 В°C** flows at velocity **4 m/s**. Peas initially at **20 В°C**. Compute convective *h* and freezing time.

**Given:**
- Air: $\rho$ = 1.4 kg/mВі, $\mu$ = 1.6 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө PaВ·s, $k_{air}$ = 0.024 W/mВ·K, $C_p$ = 1005 J/kgВ·K, $Pr$ = 0.71
- Peas: $D$ = 0.008 m, $L_f$ = 290 kJ/kg, $k_f$ = 1.3 W/mВ·K, $\rho_p$ = 1080 kg/mВі

**Step 1 вҖ” Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.4 \times 4 \times 0.008}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 2800$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Nusselt number (Hilpert / Churchill-Bernstein for sphere):**

For Re вүҲ 2800 (turbulent):

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Pr^{0.33}$$

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{2800} \cdot (0.71)^{0.33}$$

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \cdot 52.92 \cdot 0.893 \approx 30.36$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{D} = \frac{30.36 \times 0.024}{0.008} \approx 91 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Freezing time using Plank (sphere: P = 1/6, R = 1/24):**

$$\theta = 20 - (-30) = 50 \, \text{В°C}$$

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} \left[ \frac{(1/6) D}{h} + \frac{(1/24) D^2}{4 k_f} \right]$$

$$t_f = \frac{1080 \times 290{,}000}{50} \left[ \frac{(1/6)(0.008)}{91} + \frac{(1/24)(0.008)^2}{4 \times 1.3} \right]$$

$$t_f = (6{,}264{,}000) \left[ 1.466 \times 10^{-5} + 2.05 \times 10^{-7} \right]$$

$$t_f \approx (6{,}264{,}000)(1.487 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 93.2 \, \text{s}$$

**Answer:** Freezing time вүҲ **93 seconds** (вүҲ 1.55 minutes). Notice how fast freezing happens in air blast with high velocity.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (PCF vs ABF)

| Metric | Plate Contact Freezer | Air Blast Freezer |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Governing equation | Fourier's Law | Newton's Law of Cooling |
| Driver | $\Delta T$ across plate | $\Delta T_{air} + v_{air}$ |
| Limiting resistance | Contact resistance | Boundary layer (air-side) |
| Key dimensionless | $Bi = hD/k_f$ | $Re, Nu$ |
| $h$ value | 100 вҖ“ 1000 W/mВІK | 10 вҖ“ 50 W/mВІK |
| Freezing time scale | minutes to few hours | minutes (IQF) |
| Energy intensity | Low (no fan) | Higher (fan + dehumidifier) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plate Contact Freezer вҖ” Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Product Block] -->|Hydraulic Pressure| B[Top Aluminum Plate]
    B -->|Conduction| C[Hollow Cavity with Refrigerant]
    C -->|NH3 / Freon Evaporates| D[Compressor and Condenser]
    D -->|Refrigerant Recycles| C
    C -->|Conduction| E[Bottom Aluminum Plate]
    E -->|Hydraulic Support| F[Fixed Base]
    A -.Heat Transfer Path.-> B
    A -.Heat Transfer Path.-> E
```

### 4.2 Air Blast Freezer (Tunnel Type) вҖ” Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infeed Conveyor - Product In] --> B[Pre-cool Zone]
    B --> C[Spray / Surface Freezing Zone]
    C --> D[Equalization Zone]
    D --> E[Hardening Zone]
    E --> F[Outfeed Conveyor - Product Out]
    F1[Fan Unit 1] -.Cold Air.-> C
    F2[Fan Unit 2] -.Cold Air.-> E
    R[Refrigerant Coils - minus 35 C] -.Cools Air.-> F1
    R -.Cools Air.-> F2
```

### 4.3 Spiral Freezer Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Infeed Belt - Top] --> S2[Spiral Drum - Vertical Axis]
    S2 --> S3[Multiple Self Stacking Layers]
    S3 --> S4[Outfeed Belt - Bottom]
    S5[Air Circulation Fans] -.Counter Flow Air.-> S2
    S6[Refrigerant Evaporator Coils] -.Cold Air Source.-> S5
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: PCF vs ABF Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PCF [Plate Contact Freezer]
        P1[Refrigerant Cavity] --> P2[Metal Plate - Aluminum]
        P2 --> P3[Food Block - Pressed]
        P3 --> P4[Lower Metal Plate]
        P4 --> P5[Refrigerant Return]
    end

    subgraph ABF [Air Blast Freezer]
        A1[Evaporator Coil Bank] --> A2[Cold Air Plenum]
        A2 --> A3[Centrifugal Fan]
        A3 --> A4[Ducted Cold Air Stream]
        A4 --> A5[Food Product on Belt]
        A5 --> A6[Return Air Recirculation]
        A6 --> A2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Plate Contact Freezer**, heat moves entirely through the solid plate by conduction (no moving air). In the **Air Blast Freezer**, the refrigerant cools air, and then the cold air is the heat-removal medium вҖ” introducing an additional convective resistance that the fan must overcome.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between plate contact freezer and air blast freezer based on heat transfer mechanism and typical heat transfer coefficients.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Plate Contact Freezer | Air Blast Freezer |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Heat transfer mode | **Conduction** through metal plate | **Forced convection** via cold air |
| Heat transfer coefficient $h$ | **100 вҖ“ 1000 W/mВІK** | **10 вҖ“ 50 W/mВІK** |
| Typical products | Packaged blocks, fish, meat | Particulates, IQF products |
| Dehydration loss | Negligible | 1 вҖ“ 5 % |
| Energy consumption | Lower | Higher (fan load) |

*(Full marks for correct mode + $h$ range + any 1 additional point.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Plank's equation? Write the geometric constants for infinite slab, infinite cylinder, and sphere.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Plank's equation predicts the freezing time of a food product as:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P \cdot D}{\Delta T_{av}} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{4 k_f} \right]$$

Geometric constants:

| Geometry | P | R |
|----------|---|---|
| Infinite slab | **1/2** | **1/8** |
| Infinite cylinder | **1/4** | **1/16** |
| Sphere | **1/6** | **1/24** |

*(ВҪ mark for equation; ВҪ mark each for P and R values Г— 3 = 1ВҪ marks; 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Choose ONE of the following:**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

`(a)` **[7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a **horizontal plate contact freezer** with a neat block diagram. Explain why contact resistance is critical for its efficiency. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Construction [3 marks]:**
- Stack of horizontal hollow aluminium/stainless steel plates
- Each plate has internal channels for refrigerant (NHвӮғ/Freon) circulation
- Hydraulic ram pushes top plate down for firm product contact
- Insulated chamber with refrigerant inlet/outlet manifolds
- Spacing adjustable between 25 вҖ“ 75 mm

**2. Working [2 marks]:**
- Product placed between two plates
- Refrigerant evaporates at вҲ’35 to вҲ’40 В°C inside plates
- Heat flows from product вҶ’ through metal plate вҶ’ into refrigerant (conduction)
- Hydraulic pressure ensures intimate contact (low contact resistance)

**3. Contact resistance [2 marks]:**
- Contact resistance $R_c = 1/(h_c \cdot A)$ is inversely proportional to surface contact pressure
- Without adequate pressure, air gaps form вҶ’ thermal resistance increases вҶ’ freezing time increases
- For $h_c$ > 1000 W/mВІK, contact resistance is < 5 % of plate resistance

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A fish fillet **20 mm thick**, $k_f$ = **1.4 W/mВ·K**, $\rho$ = **1000 kg/mВі**, $L_f$ = **250 kJ/kg**, is frozen in a plate contact freezer. Refrigerant at **вҲ’35 В°C**, initial product temp = **5 В°C**. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation for an infinite slab. `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- D = 0.020 m, $k_f$ = 1.4 W/mВ·K, $\rho$ = 1000 kg/mВі
- $L_f$ = 250,000 J/kg, $T_{refrig}$ = вҲ’35 В°C, $T_{initial}$ = 5 В°C
- $\theta$ = 5 вҲ’ (вҲ’35) = 40 В°C
- For infinite slab: P = ВҪ, R = в…ӣ
- $\Delta T_{av}$ = Оё/2 = 20 В°C

**Solution:**

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P D}{\Delta T_{av}} + \frac{R D^2}{4 k_f} \right]$$

$$t_f = \frac{1000 \times 250{,}000}{40} \left[ \frac{0.5 \times 0.020}{20} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.020)^2}{4 \times 1.4} \right]$$

$$t_f = (6{,}250{,}000) \left[ \frac{0.010}{20} + \frac{0.00005}{5.6} \right]$$

$$t_f = (6{,}250{,}000) \left[ 5 \times 10^{-4} + 8.93 \times 10^{-6} \right]$$

$$t_f = (6{,}250{,}000) \times (5.089 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 3180 \, \text{s} \approx 53 \, \text{minutes}$$

**Answer:** Freezing time вүҲ **53 minutes**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

`(a)` **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **air blast tunnel freezer**. Mention any **three** types of air blast freezers used in industry. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Construction of Tunnel Freezer [3 marks]:**
- Insulated rectangular tunnel (length 6 вҖ“ 30 m)
- Conveyor belt running through the tunnel
- Refrigerant evaporator coils mounted on walls/ceiling
- Centrifugal fans blowing cold air counter-current to product flow
- Product entry and exit with air curtains to minimize ingress

**2. Working [2 marks]:**
- Product enters at one end on belt
- Cold air at вҲ’30 to вҲ’40 В°C blown at 1.5 вҖ“ 6 m/s
- Heat removed from product by forced convection
- Product exits frozen at other end; residence time controls freezing completion

**3. Three types [2 marks]:**
- **Still-Air (Batch) Freezer** вҖ“ no forced air; small scale
- **Spiral Freezer** вҖ“ vertical belt drum; compact footprint
- **Fluidized Bed Freezer** вҖ“ air upward through bed; for particulates (peas, corn)

---

`(b)` **[7 Marks]** Air at **вҲ’30 В°C** flows at **3 m/s** over a slab of meat (**30 mm thick**) in an air blast freezer. Compute the convective heat transfer coefficient and freezing time using Plank's equation. Given: $\rho_{meat}$ = **1050 kg/mВі**, $L_f$ = **220 kJ/kg**, $k_{meat,frozen}$ = **1.6 W/mВ·K**, air properties: $k_{air}$ = **0.026 W/mВ·K**, $\nu$ = **1.3 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө mВІ/s**, $Pr$ = **0.71**. Initial meat temp = **15 В°C**. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{v \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.030}{1.3 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 6923$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Nusselt number (forced convection over flat plate, turbulent regime):**

Using $Nu = 0.0296 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.33}$:

$$Nu = 0.0296 \cdot (6923)^{0.8} \cdot (0.71)^{0.33}$$

$$Nu = 0.0296 \cdot 1043 \cdot 0.893 \approx 27.57$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{L} = \frac{27.57 \times 0.026}{0.030} \approx 23.9 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Plank's equation (slab, P = ВҪ, R = в…ӣ):**

$\theta$ = 15 вҲ’ (вҲ’30) = 45 В°C; $\Delta T_{av}$ = 22.5 В°C

$$t_f = \frac{1050 \times 220{,}000}{45} \left[ \frac{0.5 \times 0.030}{23.9 \times 22.5} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.030)^2}{4 \times 1.6} \right]$$

$$t_f = (5{,}133{,}333) \left[ \frac{0.015}{537.75} + \frac{0.0001125}{6.4} \right]$$

$$t_f = (5{,}133{,}333) \left[ 2.79 \times 10^{-5} + 1.758 \times 10^{-5} \right]$$

$$t_f = (5{,}133{,}333)(4.55 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 233.6 \, \text{s} \approx 3.9 \, \text{min}$$

**Answer:** $h$ вүҲ **23.9 W/mВІK**, freezing time вүҲ **3.9 minutes**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Mixing up geometric constants** вҖ” slab is (P=ВҪ, R=в…ӣ), not (P=1, R=ВҪ). Examiners deduct 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting О”T average** вҖ” Students often use Оё directly instead of Оё/2 for slab, leading to wrong freezing time by factor of 2.
> 3. **Wrong units in Plank's equation** вҖ” $L_f$ in J/kg (not kJ/kg). Mixing kJ with seconds gives errors of 1000Г—.
> 4. **Plate contact vs plate freezer confusion** вҖ” Plate contact = conduction; air blast = convection. Examiners expect this distinction in first sentence itself.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** вҖ” In 7-mark sub-questions on construction, the **block diagram carries 2 marks**. Skipping it caps your marks at 5.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two main freezer families:** Plate Contact (conduction, high *h*) and Air Blast (convection, lower *h*).
- **Plate Contact Freezer** is preferred for **packaged, flat products** (fish blocks, meat blocks) with negligible dehydration.
- **Air Blast Freezer** is versatile and used for **IQF, particulates, packaged/unpackaged foods**.
- **Heat transfer coefficient order:** PCF (100вҖ“1000) > Immersion (500вҖ“1500) > ABF (10вҖ“50) W/mВІK.
- **Plank's equation** is the central formula: memorize $t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P D}{\Delta T_{av}} + \frac{R D^2}{4 k_f} \right]$.
- **Geometric constants** must be memorized: Slab (ВҪ, в…ӣ), Cylinder (Вј, 1/16), Sphere (1/6, 1/24).
- **Faster freezing** вҮ’ smaller ice crystals вҮ’ better texture & quality.
- **Biot number** $Bi = hD/k_f$ determines whether internal conduction dominates (Bi small вҶ’ Plank valid).
- **Energy efficiency** is higher for plate freezers (no fan load).
- **Freezing tunnel** uses counter-current air flow to maximize О”T driving force.
- **Spiral freezers** save floor space; **fluidized bed** is best for small particulates.

> **Final KTU Tip:** In ESE, always state the **mode of heat transfer first**, then the **heat transfer coefficient range**, and finally **one application** вҖ” this guarantees full marks on definitional questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryogenic Freezing (Cryofreezing)** is an ultra-rapid food preservation technique in which food products are exposed to extremely low-boiling cryogenic fluids (such as **liquid nitrogen (LNвӮӮ)** at **вҲ’196 В°C** or **liquid carbon dioxide (LCOвӮӮ)** at **вҲ’78.5 В°C**) to achieve surface and intra-cellular freezing at velocities several orders of magnitude faster than conventional mechanical (air-blast, plate) freezing systems.

The fundamental operating principle is **direct or indirect contact** between the food surface and a boiling cryogen. The cryogen absorbs the sensible and latent heat of the product as it vaporises (LNвӮӮ) or sublimates (LCOвӮӮ вҖ” solid "snow") and is vented as a gas. Heat removal rates of **500 вҖ“ 1500 W/mВІВ·K** are routinely achievable, which is approximately **10Г— higher** than conventional air-blast tunnels.

### Classification of Cryogenic Freezing Modes

| Mode | Mechanism | Typical Product |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Immersion** | Product dipped directly in LNвӮӮ bath | Irregular bulk items, seafood |
| **Spray** | LNвӮӮ sprayed via nozzles onto a conveyor-borne product | Individual Quick Frozen (IQF) pieces |
| **Cryogenic Tunnel** | Conveyor passes through LNвӮӮ vapour fog | Patties, fillets, fruits |
| **LCOвӮӮ Snow Contact** | Solid COвӮӮ particles sprayed onto product | Herbs, berries |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy вҖ” The "Frost-Bite Instant Popsicle"**
> Imagine placing a strawberry into a cup of liquid nitrogen. In **under 60 seconds**, the berry becomes so cold that it shatters like glass when tapped. This is because the water inside the cells has frozen **almost instantaneously**, forming extremely tiny ice crystals that do not rupture the cell walls. The fruit, once thawed, retains its original colour, shape, and juice вҖ” something a domestic freezer (which takes hours) cannot achieve because slow freezing forms large ice needles that pierce the cell membrane, causing drip-loss on thawing.

In plain words: **Cryogenic freezing is "freezing at the speed of a snap of fingers" using liquids that are colder than the polar ice caps, so that the food's water turns to ice before its structure has any chance to deform.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> - **Boiling point of LNвӮӮ** = **77.36 K ( вҲ’195.79 В°C )**
> - **Latent heat of vaporisation of LNвӮӮ** = **199 J/g ( 199 kJ/kg )**
> - **Specific heat of LNвӮӮ vapour at 0 В°C** = **1.04 kJ/kgВ·K**
> - **Sublimation temperature of COвӮӮ** = **194.65 K ( вҲ’78.5 В°C )**
> - **Latent heat of sublimation of COвӮӮ** = **571 kJ/kg**
> - **Density of LNвӮӮ at its boiling point** = **807 kg/mВі**
> - **One-tonne cryogenic freezing capacity** вүҲ requires **0.8 вҖ“ 1.2 tonnes of LNвӮӮ per tonne of food**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Framework вҖ” How Cryogenic Freezing Works

The cryogenic heat-removal cycle operates in **four sequential stages**:

### Step 1 вҖ” Pre-cooling (Sensible Heat Removal)
The food is cooled from its initial temperature (typically **+20 В°C** or **+5 В°C** for chilled goods) down to its **initial freezing point (вүҲ вҲ’1 to вҲ’2 В°C** for most foods). The cryogen vapour or liquid extracts sensible heat according to:

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_{p,\,above\,0^{\circ}C} \cdot \Delta T_{1}$$

where $c_{p,\,above\,0^{\circ}C}$ вүҲ **3.5 вҖ“ 4.2 kJ/kgВ·K** for water-rich foods.

### Step 2 вҖ” Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal) вҖ” The Plateau Zone
The majority of thermal energy (~ **60вҖ“70 %** of total heat load) is removed as the free water in the food converts to ice. Latent heat of fusion $L_f$ for water = **333.55 kJ/kg**. For food (with bound water and solutes), the apparent latent heat is reduced:

$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot L_{f,\,apparent}$$

A correction factor $L_{app} \approx 0.8 \, L_{f,\,water}$ is commonly applied for high-moisture foods.

### Step 3 вҖ” Sub-cooling (Freezing Below Eutectic)
Further temperature depression down to **вҲ’18 В°C** (standard frozen-storage target) or even **вҲ’40 В°C** (cryo-stabilised products). Latent heat of food:

$$L_{sub-cooling} = m \cdot c_{p,\,frozen} \cdot \Delta T_{2}$$

with $c_{p,\,frozen} \approx$ **1.8 вҖ“ 2.1 kJ/kgВ·K**.

### Step 4 вҖ” Vapour/Gas Venting & Recovery
The spent cryogen is either vented to atmosphere (LNвӮӮ) or recovered via re-liquefaction (cost-effective only at large capacities).

## 2.2 Why Cryogenic Freezing Is Fast вҖ” Heat-Transfer Analysis

The convective heat-transfer coefficient between a boiling cryogen and a food surface is dramatically higher than that of cold air because:

1. **Higher temperature driving force** вҖ” О”T can exceed **200 K** (vs. ~40 K for mechanical freezers).
2. **Phase-change convective transport** вҖ” nucleate boiling of LNвӮӮ on the wet product surface provides exceptional surface heat flux.
3. **Film-transition boiling** вҖ” at the initial instant, a stable vapour film insulates, but soon collapses into highly efficient **nucleate boiling**, dropping surface temperature rapidly.

The overall heat-transfer rate is:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

For LNвӮӮ spray freezing, **h вүҲ 500 вҖ“ 1500 W/mВІВ·K** (vs. cold air at h вүҲ 10 вҖ“ 40 W/mВІВ·K).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet вҖ” Cheat Sheet for Cryogenic Freezing Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` symbols. All formulas below are KTU high-yield topics.

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q_{total} = m[c_{p,\,i}\Delta T_{1} + L_{f,\,app} + c_{p,\,f}\Delta T_{2}]$ | $Q$ in kJ, $m$ in kg, $c_p$ in kJ/kgВ·K | Total refrigeration load |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{L_{v,\,LN_2} + c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{N_2,\,in}-T_{N_2,\,out})}$ | kg of LNвӮӮ per kg food | Cryogen consumption rate |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{LN_2} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LN_2}}{\rho_{LN_2}}$ | $\dot{V}$ in mВі/s, $\rho = 807\,\text{kg/m}^3$ | Tank sizing |
| 4 | $t_{f} = \dfrac{\rho\,L}{T_{fp}-T_{cryo}}\left[\dfrac{P\,d}{h}+\dfrac{R\,d^{2}}{k}\right]$ | Plank's eqn., $t_f$ in s, $d$ in m | Comparison with mechanical freezing |
| 5 | $C_{op} = \dfrac{C_{LN_2}\cdot m_{LN_2}}{m_{food}}$ | вӮ№ or \$ per kg product | Operating-cost estimation |
| 6 | $\eta_{therm} = \dfrac{Q_{food}}{m_{LN_2}\cdot L_{v,\,LN_2}}\times 100\,\%$ | % | System thermal efficiency |
| 7 | $\text{Surface flux } q'' = h(T_{surf}-T_{cryo})$ | W/mВІ | Heat flux density |

> [!TIP]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $T_{initial,\,food} \leq +25^{\circ}\text{C}$ (ambient) for IQF design.
> - $T_{final,\,food} \geq -40^{\circ}\text{C}$ (core) for cryogenic snap-freezing.
> - LCOвӮӮ **cannot** freeze foods below **вҲ’78.5 В°C** (sublimation limit) вҖ” use only for products with $T_{final} \geq -30^{\circ}\text{C}$.
> - LNвӮӮ expansion ratio liquid вҶ’ gas вүҲ **1 : 696** at 20 В°C.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seafood & Sashimi industry:** tuna, shrimp, salmon preserved cell-by-cell.
- **IQF fruits:** strawberries, raspberries, mango cubes вҖ” preserves colour & aroma.
- **RTE (Ready-To-Eat) meals:** rapid crust-freezing prevents dehydration.
- **Pharma & Biotech:** vaccines, enzymes, probiotics.
- **Pet food & aquaculture:** high-value feed pellets.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Total Refrigeration Load of a Cryogenic Freezer

**Problem Statement:** A batch cryogenic freezer processes **500 kg/h** of diced strawberries (initial temp $T_i = +20^{\circ}\text{C}$, final core temp $T_f = -40^{\circ}\text{C}$). Initial freezing point of strawberries $T_{fp} = -2.5^{\circ}\text{C}$. Determine the total heat load per hour.

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the three heat-removal zones**

$$\Delta T_{1} = T_i - T_{fp} = 20 - (-2.5) = 22.5\,\text{K}$$

$$L_{f,\,app} = 0.85 \times 333.55 = 283.5\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\Delta T_{2} = T_{fp} - T_f = -2.5 - (-40) = 37.5\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply heat-capacity data (from FAO Food Composition tables for strawberries)**

$$c_{p,\,above\,fp} = 3.85\,\text{kJ/kgВ·K}$$

$$c_{p,\,frozen} = 1.95\,\text{kJ/kgВ·K}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Substitute into the total-load equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= m \times \left[ c_{p,\,i}\,\Delta T_{1} + L_{f,\,app} + c_{p,\,f}\,\Delta T_{2} \right] \\[6pt]
Q_{total} &= 500 \times \left[ 3.85 \times 22.5 + 283.5 + 1.95 \times 37.5 \right] \\[6pt]
Q_{total} &= 500 \times \left[ 86.625 + 283.5 + 73.125 \right] \\[6pt]
Q_{total} &= 500 \times 443.25 \\[6pt]
Q_{total} &= 221{,}625\,\text{kJ/h} \;\;\approx\;\; 61.56\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The cryogenic system must remove approximately **221.6 MJ per hour** or **61.6 kW** of refrigeration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Liquid Nitrogen Consumption Rate

**Given:** Same strawberry process. LNвӮӮ leaves the freezer as gas at $T_{out} = +10^{\circ}\text{C}$. $L_{v,\,LN_2} = 199\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $c_{p,\,N_2} = 1.04\,\text{kJ/kgВ·K}$.

**Step 1 вҖ” Use the cryogen-mass balance equation**

$$
\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \frac{Q_{total}}{L_{v,\,LN_2} + c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{out}-T_{in})}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Evaluate the denominator**

$$
L_{v,\,N_2} + c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{out}-T_{in}) = 199 + 1.04 \times [10-(-196)]
$$

$$
= 199 + 1.04 \times 206 = 199 + 214.24 = 413.24\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute mass flow of LNвӮӮ**

$$
\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \frac{221{,}625}{413.24 \times 3600}\,\text{kg/s} \times 3600 = \frac{221{,}625}{413.24}\,\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{LN_2} \approx 536.3\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to volumetric flow at LNвӮӮ density 807 kg/mВі**

$$
\dot{V}_{LN_2} = \frac{536.3}{807} \approx 0.665\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} \;\approx\; 11.1\,\text{L/min}
$$

**Verification:** Ratio $\dfrac{m_{LN_2}}{m_{food}} = \dfrac{536.3}{500} \approx 1.07\,\text{kg}_{LN_2}/\text{kg}_{food}$, which lies in the design envelope (**0.8 вҖ“ 1.2**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Freezing Time Comparison (Plank's Equation)

**Comparison:** Strawberry dice of thickness $d = 0.02\,\text{m}$, $k = 1.5\,\text{W/mВ·K}$, $\rho = 900\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $L = 250\,\text{kJ/kg}$. 

**Case A вҖ” Mechanical Air-Blast Freezer** ($T_{air} = -35^{\circ}\text{C}$, $h = 25\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $P = 1/6$, $R = 1/8$):

$$
t_{f,\,A} = \frac{900 \times 250{,}000}{(-2.5)-(-35)}\left[\frac{(1/6)(0.02)}{25} + \frac{(1/8)(0.02)^2}{1.5}\right]
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{f,\,A} &= \frac{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{32.5}\left[\frac{0.00333}{25} + \frac{0.00005}{1.5}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 6.92 \times 10^{6}\left[1.333 \times 10^{-4} + 3.333 \times 10^{-5}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 6.92 \times 10^{6} \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1153\,\text{s} \;\approx\; 19.2\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B вҖ” Cryogenic LNвӮӮ Spray** ($T_{cryo} = -196^{\circ}\text{C}$, $h = 800\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{f,\,B} &= \frac{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{(-2.5)-(-196)}\left[\frac{(1/6)(0.02)}{800} + \frac{(1/8)(0.02)^2}{1.5}\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{193.5}\left[4.167 \times 10^{-6} + 3.333 \times 10^{-5}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 1.163 \times 10^{6} \times 3.75 \times 10^{-5} \\[6pt]
&\approx 43.6\,\text{s} \;\approx\; 0.73\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speed-up factor:** $\dfrac{t_{f,\,A}}{t_{f,\,B}} = \dfrac{1153}{43.6} \approx 26.4\times$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Cryogenic freezing is **~26 times faster** than mechanical air-blast for this geometry. The ice crystals formed are proportionally smaller, dramatically reducing cell-wall rupture and post-thaw drip.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Process Trade-Off Table

| Parameter | LNвӮӮ Cryogenic | LCOвӮӮ Cryogenic | Mechanical (NHвӮғ) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Minimum attainable $T$ | -196 В°C | -78.5 В°C | -42 В°C (cascade) |
| Capital cost | Low | Low | Very High |
| Operating cost / kg | High (~вӮ№ 8вҖ“12) | Medium (~вӮ№ 4вҖ“6) | Low (~вӮ№ 1.5вҖ“3) |
| Throughput | LowвҖ“Medium | Medium | Very High |
| Product quality | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| Floor space | Compact | Compact | Large |
| Skilled labour | Low | Low | High |
| Environmental risk | Inert gas vent | GHG if vented | Refrigerant leak |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Cryogenic Freezing Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Infeed Conveyor] --> B[Pre-cooling Zone -20 C]
    B --> C[Cryogen Spray Header LN2 Nozzles]
    C --> D[Boiling Heat Transfer Zone -196 C]
    D --> E[Sub-cooling Holding Tunnel]
    E --> F[Tempering & Glazing Section]
    F --> G[Packaging Line IQF Output]
    H[LN2 Storage Dewar -196 C] -->|Insulated vacuum line| C
    D --> I[Cold N2 Gas Recovery Duct]
    I --> J[Heat Exchanger - Recovers Cold]
    J -->|Vent| K[Exhaust Stack]
    L[PLC Control Panel] --> C
    L --> E
    L --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **infeed conveyor** carries pre-graded product into the freezing tunnel.
- The **LNвӮӮ dewar** is a vacuum-jacketed cryogenic storage tank (typical capacity 3 вҖ“ 30 mВі).
- The **spray header** atomises LNвӮӮ through calibrated nozzles at **2 вҖ“ 5 bar** pressure.
- The **recovery duct** captures cold NвӮӮ gas to pre-cool incoming product (regenerative heat-exchange).
- The **PLC** monitors exit-product temperature via infrared probes and modulates cryogen flow.

## 4.2 Heat-Transfer State Diagram for Cryogenic Surface Cooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State A: Film Boiling - Stable Vapour Blanket] -->|Collapse of Leidenfrost layer| P2[State B: Transition Boiling]
    P2 -->|Nucleate boiling onset| P3[State C: Nucleate Boiling - Peak Heat Flux]
    P3 -->|Product surface below 0 C| P4[State D: Convective Cooling by Cold N2 Gas]
    P4 --> P5[State E: Sub-cooled Solid - Ice Matrix Complete]
    style P1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style P3 fill:#198754,stroke:#0a3622,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#6c757d,stroke:#343a40,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Operating Note:** Cryogenic freezers must be designed to operate predominantly in **State C (Nucleate Boiling)** because this region delivers the maximum heat flux. Operating in State A (film boiling) leads to inefficient, slow freezing вҖ” a common engineering pitfall.

## 4.3 Process Block Diagram вҖ” Mass & Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[m_food at T_i = 20 C] --> HX1
    In2[m_LN2 at T_LN2 = -196 C] --> HX1
    HX1[Boiling Heat Exchanger Surface] --> Out1[m_food at T_f = -40 C ICE]
    HX1 --> Out2[m_N2 gas at T = 10 C vented]
    Qs1[Q_sensible = m c_p dT1] -.-> HX1
    Qs2[Q_latent = m L_f_app] -.-> HX1
    Qs3[Q_sub-cool = m c_pf dT2] -.-> HX1
```

> **Define cryogenic freezing. List any two cryogenic fluids used in food freezing with their boiling points.**

Cryogenic freezing is a rapid food preservation technique employing fluids at temperatures below **вҲ’60 В°C** to extract heat almost instantaneously from the food. The two common cryogens are:
- **Liquid Nitrogen (LNвӮӮ)** вҖ” boiling point **вҲ’195.79 В°C**
- **Liquid Carbon Dioxide (LCOвӮӮ)** вҖ” sublimation point **вҲ’78.5 В°C**
