## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A вҖ” `[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**a) Explain with a neat sketch the principle and working of a liquid-nitrogen spray freezer used for Individual Quick Freezing (IQF) of foods. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A liquid-nitrogen spray freezer consists of a **tunnel** fitted with a vibrating or perforated-belt conveyor that carries the product through successive zones:

1. **Pre-cooling zone** вҖ” incoming food at ambient is contacted with cold NвӮӮ vapour at approximately **вҲ’40 В°C**.
2. **Spray zone** вҖ” LNвӮӮ at **вҲ’196 В°C** is atomised through **manifold-mounted nozzles** onto the product; nucleate boiling extracts the latent heat.
3. **Equilibration zone** вҖ” the product reaches uniform core temperature (~ **вҲ’40 В°C**).
4. **Discharge zone** вҖ” frozen product exits onto a packaging line.

The **PLC** modulates cryogen flow via temperature feedback from IR sensors.

**Working Principle:** Heat transfer occurs by **(i)** film evaporation of LNвӮӮ at the food surface, **(ii)** convection by cold nitrogen gas, and **(iii)** conduction through the frozen crust.

**Sketch (description):** A horizontal insulated rectangular tunnel with conveyor running through its length, LNвӮӮ dewar connected via vacuum-insulated pipe to overhead spray bars fitted with multiple nozzles, exhaust duct at the discharge end for spent NвӮӮ gas.

*(7 marks вҖ” 3 for description, 2 for principle, 2 for sketch)*

---

**b) A cryogenic freezer processes 800 kg/h of peas from +5 В°C to вҲ’18 В°C. Initial freezing point of peas is вҲ’1 В°C. Compute (i) total refrigeration load and (ii) liquid-nitrogen consumption rate in kg/h. Assume $c_{p,\,i} = 3.6\,\text{kJ/kgВ·K}$, $c_{p,\,f} = 1.9\,\text{kJ/kgВ·K}$, $L_{app} = 270\,\text{kJ/kg}$, and LNвӮӮ leaves at 5 В°C. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify О”T values**

$$\Delta T_1 = 5 - (-1) = 6\,\text{K}, \quad \Delta T_2 = -1 - (-18) = 17\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Total refrigeration load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= \dot{m} \times \left[ c_{p,\,i}\Delta T_1 + L_{app} + c_{p,\,f}\Delta T_2 \right] \\[4pt]
&= 800 \times \left[ 3.6 \times 6 + 270 + 1.9 \times 17 \right] \\[4pt]
&= 800 \times \left[ 21.6 + 270 + 32.3 \right] \\[4pt]
&= 800 \times 323.9 \\[4pt]
&= 259{,}120\,\text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Available heat per kg of LNвӮӮ**

$$
\Delta H_{LN_2} = 199 + 1.04 \times [5-(-196)] = 199 + 1.04 \times 201 = 199 + 209.04 = 408.04\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” LNвӮӮ consumption**

$$
\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \frac{259{,}120}{408.04} \approx 635.0\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Cryogen-to-product ratio**

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_{LN_2}}{\dot{m}_{peas}} = \frac{635}{800} \approx 0.794\,\text{kg}_{LN_2}/\text{kg}_{peas}
$$

*(7 marks вҖ” 2 for setup, 2 for Q, 2 for LNвӮӮ rate, 1 for ratio verification)*

---

### Question B вҖ” `[KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024, Module 3]`

**a) Compare cryogenic freezing with mechanical (air-blast) freezing on the basis of (i) freezing time, (ii) ice-crystal size, (iii) capital cost, (iv) operating cost, and (v) product quality. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Cryogenic (LNвӮӮ) | Mechanical Air-Blast |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Freezing time** | Seconds to 1вҖ“2 min | 15вҖ“90 min |
| **Ice-crystal size** | 10вҖ“50 Ојm (intra-cellular) | 100вҖ“500 Ојm (extra-cellular) |
| **Capital cost** | Low (dewar + tunnel) | Very High (compressors, evaporators) |
| **Operating cost** | High (~вӮ№ 8вҖ“12/kg) | Low (~вӮ№ 1.5вҖ“3/kg) |
| **Product quality** | Excellent вҖ” colour, texture, aroma retained | Moderate вҖ” drip loss on thawing |
| **Floor space** | Compact | Large plant room |
| **Refrigerant risk** | None (inert NвӮӮ) | Ammonia leak hazard |
| **Suitability** | Small batches, premium IQF | Large continuous throughput |

**Conclusion:** Cryogenic freezing is preferred for **high-value, low-throughput, premium-quality** foods; mechanical freezing is preferred for **bulk commodity** products.

*(7 marks вҖ” 1 each Г— 5 criteria + 2 for conclusion)*

---

**b) Derive the expression for liquid-nitrogen consumption rate of a cryogenic freezer, and comment on how it is influenced by (i) initial product temperature and (ii) food moisture content. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Energy balance on the freezer**

The total heat removed from the food per unit time equals the heat absorbed by the cryogen:

$$Q_{food} = \dot{m}_{LN_2} \cdot \Delta H_{LN_2}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Expand both sides**

$$
\dot{m}_{food}\left[c_{p,\,i}(T_i - T_{fp}) + L_{app} + c_{p,\,f}(T_{fp} - T_f)\right] = \dot{m}_{LN_2}\left[L_{v} + c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{out} - T_{in})\right]
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Rearrange to obtain LNвӮӮ rate**

$$
\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{food}\left[c_{p,\,i}(T_i - T_{fp}) + L_{app} + c_{p,\,f}(T_{fp} - T_f)\right]}{L_{v} + c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{out} - T_{in})}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Comment on influence of parameters**

**(i) Initial product temperature $T_i$:** A higher $T_i$ increases $(T_i - T_{fp})$ linearly, hence increasing the sensible-heat term. **LNвӮӮ consumption rises directly with initial temperature.** For example, food entering at **+25 В°C** instead of **+5 В°C** requires roughly **20 kg more LNвӮӮ per 100 kg food**.

**(ii) Food moisture content $X_w$:** $L_{app} = X_w \cdot L_{f,\,water}$, so the latent-heat term is **directly proportional to moisture content**. A high-moisture food like watermelon (~**92 % water**) consumes ~**1.4Г— more LNвӮӮ** than a low-moisture food like bread (~**35 % water**) per kg of product.

*(7 marks вҖ” 3 for derivation, 2 for parameter (i), 2 for parameter (ii))*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cryogenic Freezing Problems:**
> 1. **Using $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg directly** instead of the apparent latent heat $L_{app}$ (which accounts for bound water and solutes). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting the gas sensible-heat term** $c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{out} - T_{in})$ in the LNвӮӮ consumption equation вҖ” gives an answer ~50 % lower than correct. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Mixing up О”T signs** вҖ” when computing $(T_i - T_{fp})$, students often write a negative number. Use absolute magnitudes; the equation is algebraic.
> 4. **Confusing LNвӮӮ boiling point (вҲ’196 В°C)** with **LCOвӮӮ sublimation point (вҲ’78.5 В°C)** вҖ” examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Not stating units** in final answers вҖ” KTU strictly awards the last mark only when correct units are written.
> 6. **Ignoring cryogen storage losses** вҖ” for design problems, account for **2вҖ“5 % daily boil-off** in the dewar sizing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Recap вҖ” Cryogenic Freezing (Module 3)**
> - **Cryogens:** LNвӮӮ (вҲ’196 В°C), LCOвӮӮ (вҲ’78.5 В°C). LNвӮӮ = *boiling*; LCOвӮӮ = *sublimation*.
> - **Latent heats:** LNвӮӮ vaporisation = **199 kJ/kg**; LCOвӮӮ sublimation = **571 kJ/kg**.
> - **Total refrigeration load equation:**
>   $Q = m[c_{p,i}\Delta T_1 + L_{app} + c_{p,f}\Delta T_2]$
> - **Apparent latent heat:** $L_{app} \approx 0.8$ to $0.9 \times L_{f,\,water}$ for most foods.
> - **LNвӮӮ consumption formula:** denominator includes sensible-heat pickup of NвӮӮ gas.
> - **Speed-up factor over air-blast:** typically **10вҖ“30Г—** faster freezing.
> - **Ice-crystal size:** cryogenic вҶ’ **10вҖ“50 Ојm** (intra-cellular) вҶ’ minimal drip loss.
> - **Modes of contact:** immersion, spray, tunnel, LCOвӮӮ snow.
> - **Typical LNвӮӮ usage:** **0.8вҖ“1.2 kg LNвӮӮ per kg food**.
> - **LCOвӮӮ cannot go below вҲ’78.5 В°C** вҖ” not suitable for very low final-product temperatures.
> - **Boiling regimes:** film вҶ’ transition вҶ’ nucleate вҶ’ convective; design for nucleate-boiling peak flux.
> - **Plank's equation** for freezing time is still valid; only $\Delta T$ and $h$ change for cryogenic systems.
> - **Advantages:** speed, quality, low capital, compact, inert cryogen.
> - **Disadvantages:** high operating cost, cryogen storage logistics, vent losses.
> - **Applications:** seafood, IQF fruits, herbs, RTE meals, pharma.
> - **Density of LNвӮӮ at boiling point:** **807 kg/mВі**; volumetric flow calculation needed for tank sizing.
> - **Expansion ratio LNвӮӮ liquid вҶ’ NвӮӮ gas at 20 В°C** вүҲ **1 : 696** (vent design critical).
> - **KTU trick:** Always remember to **add the sensible-heat-of-the-vapour term** in cryogen consumption вҖ” most students forget this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerated Mobile Vans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refrigerated Mobile Vans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Refrigerated Mobile Van** вҖ” also called a **Reefer Van** or **Refrigerated Carrier** вҖ” is an insulated transport vehicle fitted with a mechanical (or cryogenic / eutectic) refrigeration unit, designed to maintain a pre-selected low temperature inside its enclosed cargo space so that perishable, frozen, or chilled food products retain their physico-chemical, microbiological, and sensory quality from the point of loading to the point of unloading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition:**
> A refrigerated mobile van is a *tempered, insulated, mobile cold-chain node* in which the rate of heat ingress into the payload equals (or is less than) the rate of heat extraction by the on-board refrigeration system, the latter being sized against the total instantaneous **Heat Load (W or kcal/h)**.

### Key Design Objectives (CO Mapping вҖ“ CO3, CO4)

1. Maintain the **Required Carriage Temperature (T_c)** within Вұ 1 В°C of the set-point for the entire journey.
2. Preserve the **Cold Chain Integrity** вҖ” no temperature abuse above 5 В°C for chilled foods or above вҲ’15 В°C for frozen foods.
3. Provide **Hygienic, Corrosion-Resistant, Food-Grade Interior** surfaces (SS 304 / food-grade FRP).
4. Ensure **Energy-Efficient Operation** under intermittent door-open cycles, road shocks, and ambient swings (5 В°C to 45 В°C).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” "The Cold-Box on Wheels"

Imagine you take your home refrigerator, strip away its fixed plug, mount it on a truck, give it a diesel/gasoline-driven compressor, surround it with **6вҖ“10 cm of polyurethane (PUF) foam walls**, and then drive it across Kerala in the summer afternoon heat (вүҲ 40 В°C). That is precisely what a **refrigerated mobile van** is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of the van as a *thermos flask with an active cooling engine*. The **PUF walls** are the thermos (preventing heat entry), and the **compressor + condenser + evaporator** are the active cooler (constantly removing whatever heat sneaks in plus the heat carried by the fresh food itself).

The crucial difference from a static cold store: the van must fight **moving hot air infiltration** (every time a door opens at a delivery stop), **vibrational loads** on the compressor, and **variable ambient temperatures** depending on geography (e.g., Munnar vs. Palakkad in the same trip).

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Standard Values)

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Wall U-value | **0.30 вҖ“ 0.45 W/mВІВ·K** | PUF (Poly-Urethane Foam) insulation, 80вҖ“100 mm |
| Carriage Temperature Range | **вҲ’25 В°C to +10 В°C** | Chiller (вҲ’2 to +8 В°C) / Freezer (вҲ’18 to вҲ’25 В°C) |
| Pull-Down Time | **вүӨ 6 hours** to reach 0 В°C from +30 В°C | FSSAI / IS 3573 specification |
| Air Circulation | **40 вҖ“ 60 air changes/h** | Forced evaporator fans |
| Internal Volume Utilisation | **65 вҖ“ 75 %** | Rest must be airflow channels |
| Hold-Over Time (eutectic) | **8 вҖ“ 14 hours** | Without engine operation |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 65вҖ“75 % volume utilization?** Because the remaining **25вҖ“35 %** of the cargo space acts as the **air-return plenum** вҖ” cold air must be free to circulate around every box, or you get *hot-spots* that spoil produce.

---

## 1.4 Functional Block Overview

A refrigerated mobile van integrates **five engineering subsystems**:

1. **Insulated Enclosure (Body)** вҖ” PUF sandwich panels with FRP / SS cladding.
2. **Refrigeration Unit (Vapour-Compression / Eutectic / Cryogenic)**.
3. **Air-Circulation & Distribution System** вҖ” ducted evaporator + return-air grille.
4. **Power Pack** вҖ” stand-by diesel/electric compressor drive (TRU вҖ“ Transport Refrigeration Unit) or engine-PTO.
5. **Monitoring & Control** вҖ” temperature recorder, data logger, GPS-linked telematics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to **(i)** sketch the layout, **(ii)** compute the total heat load, **(iii)** recommend insulation thickness, and **(iv)** select the appropriate refrigeration mode for a given food commodity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Refrigerated Mobile Van

### 2.1.1 Insulated Body Construction

The body is a **monocoque sandwich panel**:

- **Outer Skin:** 1.0 вҖ“ 1.2 mm GFRP (Glass-Fibre Reinforced Plastic) or **SS 304 sheet** for fish/meat vans.
- **Core:** Polyurethane Foam (PUF), closed-cell, density **32 вҖ“ 40 kg/mВі**, О» = **0.022 W/mВ·K**.
- **Inner Skin:** 1.0 mm food-grade FRP / SS 304, with coved corners for hygiene.
- **Floor:** Anti-slip aluminium tread plate over PUF, with drain plug and channels for melt-water.

The **overall heat-transfer coefficient (U)** for a composite panel is:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j} \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

where the surface film coefficients are typically:

- $h_i$ (inside, forced convection) вүҲ **10 W/mВІВ·K**
- $h_o$ (outside, still air / 30 km/h wind) вүҲ **23 W/mВІВ·K**

### 2.1.2 Refrigeration Unit Configurations

| Type | Working Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Vapour Compression (VCR)** | Compressor + Condenser + Evaporator + TXV | Long-haul, multi-temp |
| **Eutectic Plate** | Phase-change material (e.g., eutectic salt вҲ’21 В°C) re-charged at depot | Short-haul, milk & ice cream |
| **Cryogenic (LNвӮӮ / LCOвӮӮ)** | Liquid nitrogen or COвӮӮ expansion cools the space | Pharma / premium frozen |
| **Ice-Bank / Dry Ice** | Latent heat of fusion / sublimation | Pre-cooling, very short trips |

---

## 2.2 Heat Load Components вҖ” The "Five-Heads" Model

The KTU syllabus insists on splitting the total refrigeration load into **five independent heads**. This separation makes both design and troubleshooting systematic.

### Head 1 вҖ” Transmission Heat Load ($Q_{tr}$)

Heat flowing **through the insulated envelope** (walls, floor, ceiling, doors) due to the temperature difference between outside ambient and inside carriage.

$$Q_{tr} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad [\text{W}]$$

- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient of panel (W/mВІВ·K)
- $A$ = total envelope area (mВІ)
- $\Delta T$ = (T_ambient вҲ’ T_carriage) in K

> **Why it matters:** This is the *largest* contributor in tropical climates (Kerala peak ambient = 38 вҖ“ 42 В°C). Reducing $U$ (thicker PUF) or shrinking $A$ (smaller box) are the only two design levers.

### Head 2 вҖ” Product Heat Load ($Q_p$)

Heat that must be removed to bring the **food itself** from its loading temperature down to the carriage temperature.

**Sensible part** (cooling without phase change):

$$Q_{p,sens} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{load} - T_{carriage}) \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Latent part** (freezing the water inside the food):

$$Q_{p,lat} = m \cdot w \cdot h_{fg} \quad [\text{W}]$$

where:

- $m$ = mass of food (kg)
- $C_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kgВ·K) вҖ” for water-rich foods вүҲ 3.5 вҖ“ 4.0
- $w$ = fraction of water frozen (kg water/kg food)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of fusion of water = **334 kJ/kg**

> For pre-cooled loads (most common in reefer vans), only a small pull-down is needed and $Q_p$ is modest.

### Head 3 вҖ” Infiltration / Air-Exchange Heat Load ($Q_{inf}$)

Heat carried in by **warm ambient air** each time the rear/side doors are opened for loading or delivery.

$$Q_{inf} = \frac{V \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T \cdot N}{3600} \quad [\text{W}]$$

- $V$ = cargo-box volume (mВі)
- $\rho_{air}$ вүҲ **1.2 kg/mВі**
- $C_{p,air}$ вүҲ **1.005 kJ/kgВ·K**
- $\Delta T$ = ambient вҲ’ carriage temperature (K)
- $N$ = number of door openings per hour (typically 2вҖ“6)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Each door opening for ~30 s is empirically equivalent to a **complete air change** in small vans.

### Head 4 вҖ” Internal Heat Sources ($Q_{int}$)

Heat dissipated **inside** the van by:

- Personnel loading/unloading: **250 вҖ“ 350 W per person** (KTU value: **290 W**)
- Evaporator fans: **150 вҖ“ 600 W** depending on van size
- Lights (if any): **50 вҖ“ 100 W**
- Defrost heaters (cyclic): **500 вҖ“ 1500 W** during defrost cycles

$$Q_{int} = Q_{personnel} + Q_{fans} + Q_{lights} + Q_{defrost}$$

### Head 5 вҖ” Respiratory Heat of Fresh Produce ($Q_{resp}$)

Live fruits and vegetables continue to **respire after harvest**, generating heat:

$$Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{resp}$$

Typical $q_{resp}$ values (W per tonne):

| Commodity | q_resp (W/t) at 5 В°C |
|---|---|
| Bananas | 60 вҖ“ 80 |
| Mangoes | 70 вҖ“ 100 |
| Tomatoes | 40 вҖ“ 60 |
| Apples | 20 вҖ“ 30 |
| Leafy greens | 100 вҖ“ 150 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT include $Q_{resp}$** for meat, fish, milk or frozen products вҖ” these are dead tissues and have no respiration.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Panel U-value | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | W/mВІВ·K | Insulation design |
| 2 | Transmission load | $Q_{tr} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Always |
| 3 | Product sensible heat | $Q_{p,s} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Pull-down phase |
| 4 | Product latent heat | $Q_{p,l} = m \cdot w \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | When freezing water in food |
| 5 | Infiltration | $Q_{inf} = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot N}{3600}$ | W | Every door opening |
| 6 | Personnel | $Q_{pers} = 290 \cdot n_{pax}$ | W | Loading/unloading |
| 7 | Fan & misc. | $Q_{misc}$ (taken as 5 вҖ“ 10 % of $Q_{tr}$) | W | Always |
| 8 | Respiration | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{resp}$ | W | Fresh produce only |
| 9 | **Total Heat Load** | $Q_{tot} = Q_{tr} + Q_{p} + Q_{inf} + Q_{int} + Q_{resp}$ | W | Compressor sizing |
| 10 | Required TR | $\text{TR} = Q_{tot} / 3517$ | TR | Convert W вҶ’ Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR = 3517 W) |
| 11 | Insulation thickness (target U) | $x = k \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{U_{target}} - \dfrac{1}{h_i} - \dfrac{1}{h_o} \right)$ | m | Sizing PUF |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Refrigerated mobile vans are the **backbone of India's cold chain** вҖ” moving fish from Ernakulam, mangoes from Kottayam, bananas from Palakkad, and frozen meat from Kochi ports. Without correct heat-load estimation:

- **Under-sized compressors** cause product spoilage (huge economic loss).
- **Over-sized compressors** short-cycle, increasing fuel cost and compressor wear.
- **Wrong insulation thickness** causes either excessive payload loss or excessive energy use.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical 5-tonne reefer van running Kerala routes needs approximately **2.5 вҖ“ 3.5 kW** of refrigeration capacity вҖ” equivalent to a small window AC working continuously in 40 В°C heat.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Total Heat-Load Equation

Starting from **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a control volume around the cargo box (steady state, no accumulation):

$$Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 0$$

For a refrigerated van operating at steady carriage temperature, $Q_{out}$ equals the refrigeration capacity $Q_{refr}$ extracted by the evaporator coil, and $Q_{in}$ is the sum of all heat ingress paths:

$$Q_{refr} = Q_{tr} + Q_{p} + Q_{inf} + Q_{int} + Q_{resp}$$

Expanding each head (carrying forward the symbols from Section 2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{refr} &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&\quad + m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{prod} \\
&\quad + m \cdot w \cdot h_{fg} \\
&\quad + \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T \cdot N}{3600} \\
&\quad + 290 \cdot n_{pax} + Q_{fan} + Q_{light} \\
&\quad + m \cdot q_{resp}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical sizing equation** for any transport refrigeration unit.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

### Problem Statement

A **5-tonne (payload) refrigerated van** is to be designed for transporting **fresh fish** from Ernakulam to Thiruvananthapuram. Given:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal box dimensions | 3.0 m (L) Г— 1.8 m (W) Г— 1.8 m (H) |
| Wall PUF thickness | 80 mm; О»_PUF = 0.022 W/mВ·K |
| h_i (inside) | 10 W/mВІВ·K |
| h_o (outside, moving) | 23 W/mВІВ·K |
| Ambient temperature | 38 В°C |
| Carriage temperature | 0 В°C (chilled fish) |
| Load entering at | 10 В°C |
| Payload mass | 3000 kg |
| C_p of fish | 3.6 kJ/kgВ·K |
| Door openings per hour | 4 |
| Personnel during loading | 2 |
| Evaporator fan power | 400 W |
| Respiration | Negligible (dead tissue) |

**Required:** (a) Calculate the total steady-state refrigeration load. (b) Specify the compressor size in TR.

---

### (a) Step-by-Step Calculation

#### Step 1 вҖ” Compute the overall U-value of the panel

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} \\
&= \frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.080}{0.022} + \frac{1}{23} \\
&= 0.100 + 3.636 + 0.0435 \\
&= 3.7795 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$U = \frac{1}{3.7795} = 0.2646 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

> **Interpretation:** This U-value is **below the typical 0.30 вҖ“ 0.45 W/mВІВ·K range** stated in Section 1, meaning 80 mm PUF is *over-designed* вҖ” but acceptable for hot Kerala climate.

#### Step 2 вҖ” Compute the envelope area (walls + ceiling + floor, ignoring door)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 2(LW) + 2(LH) + 2(WH) \\
&= 2(3.0 \times 1.8) + 2(3.0 \times 1.8) + 2(1.8 \times 1.8) \\
&= 10.80 + 10.80 + 6.48 \\
&= 28.08 \;\; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 вҖ” Transmission Heat Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{tr} &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 0.2646 \times 28.08 \times (38 - 0) \\
&= 0.2646 \times 28.08 \times 38 \\
&= 282.4 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 вҖ” Product Sensible Heat (cooling fish from 10 В°C вҶ’ 0 В°C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{p,sens} &= \frac{m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{prod}}{t_{pull-down}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming a **pull-down time of 4 hours** (14 400 s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{p,sens} &= \frac{3000 \times 3.6 \times (10 - 0)}{4 \times 3600} \\
&= \frac{108\,000}{14\,400} \\
&= 7.50 \;\; \text{W (average)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note:* Because pull-down is gradual, this head is **divided by pull-down time**. During the *steady-state* hold phase (after pull-down), $Q_{p,sens} \to 0$.

For design safety we size the compressor to handle **peak instantaneous load** (assume the load enters warm and must be cooled in 2 h worst case):

$$
Q_{p,sens,peak} = \frac{108\,000}{7200} = 15.0 \;\; \text{W}
$$

Let's adopt a **design pull-down of 3 h** (compromise):

$$
Q_{p,sens,peak} = \frac{108\,000}{3 \times 3600} = 10.0 \;\; \text{W}
$$

#### Step 5 вҖ” Infiltration Heat Load

Volume $V = 3.0 \times 1.8 \times 1.8 = 9.72$ mВі

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{inf} &= \frac{V \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T \cdot N}{3600} \\
&= \frac{9.72 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 38 \times 4}{3600} \\
&= \frac{1776.6}{3600} \\
&= 0.493 \;\; \text{kW} \\
&= 493 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 вҖ” Internal Heat Sources

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{personnel} &= 290 \times 2 = 580 \;\; \text{W (during loading)} \\
Q_{fans} &= 400 \;\; \text{W (continuous)} \\
Q_{lights} &= 100 \;\; \text{W (assumed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

During **steady-state hold** (no loading), only the fans + lights operate:

$$Q_{int,hold} = 400 + 100 = 500 \;\; \text{W}$$

During **peak loading phase**:

$$Q_{int,peak} = 580 + 400 + 100 = 1080 \;\; \text{W}$$

#### Step 7 вҖ” Respiration

$$Q_{resp} = 0 \;\; \text{(fish is dead tissue)}$$

#### Step 8 вҖ” Total Heat Load

**Steady-state (hold) total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{tot,hold} &= Q_{tr} + Q_{p,sens} + Q_{inf} + Q_{int,hold} + Q_{resp} \\
&= 282.4 + 10.0 + 493 + 500 + 0 \\
&= 1285.4 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Peak (loading + warm fish entering) total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{tot,peak} &= 282.4 + 10.0 + 493 + 1080 + 0 \\
&= 1865.4 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply **15 % safety / design margin** (KTU standard practice):

$$Q_{design} = 1.15 \times 1865.4 = 2145.2 \;\; \text{W}$$

---

### (b) Compressor Sizing in Tonnes of Refrigeration

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TR} &= \frac{Q_{design}}{3517} \\
&= \frac{2145.2}{3517} \\
&= 0.610 \;\; \text{TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** Select a **compressor of approximately 0.75 TR (вүҲ 2.2 kW cooling)** вҖ” the next standard size up to provide margin for higher ambient (42 В°C), door abuse, and refrigerant degradation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Effect of Design Variables on Heat Load

| Design Lever | Action | Effect on $Q_{tr}$ | Effect on $Q_{inf}$ | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase PUF thickness 80 вҶ’ 100 mm | $x \uparrow$ | $U \downarrow$ вҶ’ $Q_{tr} \downarrow$ вүҲ 25 % | None | Adds weight, reduces payload |
| Reduce door openings 4 вҶ’ 2 /h | $N \downarrow$ | None | $Q_{inf} \downarrow$ 50 % | Operational discipline |
| Use strip curtains on rear door | $Q_{inf} \downarrow$ вүҲ 30 % | None | None | Low cost retrofit |
| Pre-cool load before loading | $T_{load} \downarrow$ | None | None | $Q_{p,sens} \downarrow$ drastically |
| Higher carriage temp (0 вҶ’ 4 В°C) | $\Delta T \downarrow$ | $Q_{tr} \downarrow$ вүҲ 10 % | $Q_{inf} \downarrow$ вүҲ 10 % | Limited by food safety |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Refrigerated Mobile Van)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Am1: External Heat Sources] -->|Ambient 38C + Solar| B[Am2: Insulated Van Body PUF 80mm]
    B -->|Transmission Qtr 282W| C[Am3: Cargo Compartment 0C]
    D[Am4: Warm Food Load 10C Fish 3000kg] -->|Product Sensible Qp 10W| C
    E[Am5: Door Openings 4 per hr] -->|Infiltration Qinf 493W| C
    F[Am6: Personnel 2 persons] -->|290W each 580W| C
    G[Am7: Evaporator Fan 400W] -->|Continuous| C
    C -->|Net Heat In 1285W| H[Am8: Evaporator Coil R-404A]
    H -->|Low Pressure Vapour| I[Am9: Compressor 0.75 TR]
    I -->|High Pressure Vapour| J[Am10: Condenser Ram Air Cooled]
    J -->|Liquid| K[Am11: Receiver Dryer Filter]
    K -->|High Pressure Liquid| L[Am12: TXV Expansion Valve]
    L -->|Low Pressure Mix| H
    M[Am13: Standby Diesel Engine] -.PTO.-> I
    N[Am14: Temperature Controller] -->|On Off| I
    O[Am15: Data Logger GPS] --> N
```

> The diagram above traces the **flow of heat from external sources вҶ’ into the cargo box вҶ’ extracted by the refrigeration cycle**, and shows the **support systems** (controller, data logger, standby engine).

## 4.2 Layout Schematic вҖ” Cross-Section of a Reefer Van

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SB1[Am16: Insulated Van Enclosure]
        W1[Am17: Outer Skin FRP]
        W2[Am18: PUF Core 80mm]
        W3[Am19: Inner Skin SS 304]
        W1 --- W2 --- W3
    end
    subgraph SB2[Am20: Refrigeration Unit Mounted on Roof]
        EV[Am21: Evaporator with Fans]
        CO[Am22: Compressor Diesel Drive]
        CN[Am23: Condenser]
    end
    subgraph SB3[Am24: Cargo Bay 0C]
        PA[Am25: Palletised Fish Boxes]
        DC[Am26: Drain Channel Floor]
        AP[Am27: Air Return Plenum]
    end
    EV -->|Cold Air Ducted| PA
    PA -->|Return Air| AP
    AP --> EV
    DC --> PA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Flow Reading:** The compressor (CO) compresses refrigerant вҶ’ condenser (CN) rejects heat to ambient вҶ’ TXV expands вҶ’ evaporator (EV) absorbs heat from the return air coming through plenum (AP). Cold air is ducted back over the pallets (PA). Melt-water drains via (DC).

## 4.3 Decision Tree вҖ” Selection of Refrigeration Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Am28: Select Reefer Type] --> S1{Am29: Trip Duration}
    S1 -->|Less than 4 h| S2[Am30: Eutectic Plate]
    S1 -->|4 to 12 h| S3[Am31: Vapour Compression Diesel Electric]
    S1 -->|Greater than 12 h| S4[Am32: Vapour Compression with Standby Generator]
    S0 --> S5{Am33: Commodity}
    S5 -->|Pharma Vaccines| S6[Am34: Cryogenic LN2 LCO2]
    S5 -->|Fresh Produce| S7[Am35: VCR High RH Mode]
    S5 -->|Frozen Meat Ice Cream| S8[Am36: VCR Low Temp Minus 25C]
    S5 -->|Milk Dairy| S9[Am37: Eutectic Plate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A вҖ” Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three design considerations for the insulated body of a refrigerated mobile van.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Insulation material and thickness:** Use closed-cell PUF (О» вүҲ 0.022 W/mВ·K) at 80 вҖ“ 100 mm thickness to achieve $U \le 0.40$ W/mВІВ·K. *(1 mark)*
2. **Vapour barrier on the warm side:** Prevent moisture ingress into PUF which degrades О» over time. *(1 mark)*
3. **Food-grade inner skin (SS 304 / FRP) with coved corners:** For hygiene and ease of CIP (clean-in-place) washing. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between an eutectic-plate refrigerated van and a vapour-compression refrigerated van. Mention one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Eutectic-Plate Van | Vapour-Compression Van |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling principle | Phase change of eutectic salt (latent heat) | Continuous VCR cycle |
| Compressor duty | Only at depot to re-freeze plates | Continuous during transit |
| Hold-over time | 8 вҖ“ 14 h without engine | Unlimited (engine-driven) |
| Application | Milk, ice-cream short-haul | Long-haul multi-temp transport |

*(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1 for the application row)*

---

## Part B вҖ” Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A вҖ” Eutectic Plate and Heat Load Computation

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022 Model | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **eutectic-plate refrigerated van**. State two advantages and one limitation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 2-tonne capacity insulated van is used to transport chilled meat (C_p = 3.5 kJ/kgВ·K) at 4 В°C. The meat enters at 15 В°C. The body has surface area 22 mВІ and overall U-value 0.40 W/mВІВ·K. Ambient is 35 В°C. Door openings occur 3 times/hour. Evaporator fans dissipate 300 W. Two personnel work during loading (290 W each). Compute the **total instantaneous refrigeration load** in watts. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution

**(a) Eutectic-Plate Van (7 marks)**

An **eutectic-plate van** uses a sealed plate containing a **eutectic salt solution** (e.g., sodium acetate trihydrate + additives, freezing point вүҲ вҲ’21 В°C, or a glycol-salt mix for +4 В°C milk duty).

**Construction:**

- Rectangular aluminium tubes/plates (30вҖ“50 mm thick) housed behind the inner walls and on the ceiling of the van.
- Each plate is a **sealed envelope** of eutectic fluid.
- A small compressor-condenser unit (mounted on the depot wall or on the van itself) re-chills the plates at night or at the depot between trips.

**Working Principle:**

1. At the depot, the eutectic salt is **frozen** by the refrigeration plant; the salt releases its **latent heat of fusion** to the refrigerant and becomes a solid.
2. During transit, the **fans blow air across the eutectic plates**, and as the warm return air transfers heat into the plates, the salt **melts**, absorbing latent heat ($\approx 200$ вҖ“ $260$ kJ/kg of eutectic).
3. Once the salt fully melts, the van loses its cooling capacity and must return to the depot.

**Advantages (any 2):** *(1 + 1 = 2 marks)*

- No engine/PTO required in transit вҶ’ **silent, vibration-free**, suitable for night deliveries.
- **Hold-over** of 8 вҖ“ 14 h independent of fuel вҶ’ excellent for milk and ice-cream door-to-door delivery.
- Lower capital and running cost for short routes.

**Limitation (any 1):** *(1 mark)*

- Finite thermal storage вҶ’ **trip duration is limited**; cannot handle long-haul routes.
- Slow **re-charging time** (4 вҖ“ 8 h) limits vehicle turnaround.

**Sketch / Block Diagram:** *(2 marks)* вҖ” Draw the eutectic plates, the air-circulation fan, the control thermostat, and indicate the depot re-charging station. Label the melt and freeze phases.

---

**(b) Heat-Load Calculation (7 marks вҖ” 1 mark per head + final addition)**

**Step 1 вҖ” Transmission Load:**

$$Q_{tr} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.40 \times 22 \times (35 - 4) = 0.40 \times 22 \times 31 = 272.8 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Product Sensible Heat (instantaneous, assume pull-down of 2 h):**

$$
Q_{p,sens} = \frac{m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{t} = \frac{2000 \times 3.5 \times (15 - 4)}{2 \times 3600} = \frac{77\,000}{7200} = 10.69 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Infiltration Load** (assume V = 6 mВі):

$$Q_{inf} = \frac{6 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 31 \times 3}{3600} = \frac{672.5}{3600} = 0.187 \;\; \text{kW} = 186.8 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Internal Loads:**

$$Q_{personnel} = 2 \times 290 = 580 \;\; \text{W}$$
$$Q_{fans} = 300 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Respiration:** 0 (meat is dead tissue).

**Step 6 вҖ” Total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{tot} &= 272.8 + 10.7 + 186.8 + 580 + 300 \\
&= 1350.3 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (boxed):** $Q_{tot} \approx \mathbf{1350 \;\; W}$ *(0.5 mark for correct unit and final answer)*

---

### Question B вҖ” Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2023 Model | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the construction and components of a **refrigerated mobile van** used for transporting fresh fruits and vegetables. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **pre-cooling** requirements of horticultural produce before loading into a reefer van. Why is the respiration heat load important for these commodities, and how is it estimated? Give numerical $q_{resp}$ values for any four commodities. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution (Highlights)

**(a) Construction and Components (7 marks)**

- **Chassis:** Light commercial vehicle (LCV) frame rated for ~5 вҖ“ 9 tonnes GVW. *(1 mark)*
- **Insulated body:** PUF sandwich panels 80 вҖ“ 100 mm, SS 304 / FRP skin. *(2 marks)*
- **Refrigeration unit:** Roof-mounted VCR with engine-PTO or diesel stand-by; capacity 1 вҖ“ 4 kW. *(1 mark)*
- **Air-distribution system:** Ducted evaporator, return-air plenum, T-bar floor channels. *(1 mark)*
- **Accessories:** Strip curtains, thermograph, GPS logger, multi-temperature zones (for mixed loads). *(1 mark)*
- **Neat labelled diagram (2 marks)** вҖ” show the chassis, insulated walls, evaporator, condenser, evaporator fan, return-air path, drain, and temperature sensor.

**(b) Pre-cooling & Respiration (7 marks)**

- **Pre-cooling:** Removes field heat *before* the produce enters the van. Methods: **room cooling, forced-air cooling, hydro-cooling, vacuum cooling.** Pre-cooling reduces initial respiration, slows microbial growth, and dramatically reduces $Q_p$ inside the van. *(2 marks)*
- **Why respiration matters:** Fresh fruits/vegetables are *living* вҖ” they consume $O_2$ and release $CO_2$ + heat. Heat must be removed or product temperature rises, accelerating senescence. *(2 marks)*
- **Estimation:** $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{resp}$ (W/t). *(1 mark)*
- **Numerical values (1 mark each, any four):** Bananas 60вҖ“80; Mangoes 70вҖ“100; Apples 20вҖ“30; Tomatoes 40вҖ“60; Leafy greens 100вҖ“150 W/t at 5 В°C.

---

## вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide product sensible heat by pull-down time** вҖ” they multiply instead of dividing, inflating $Q_p$ by a factor of 1000 and blowing up the TR rating. *(вҲ’3 marks typical)*
> 2. **Using В°C difference in О”T where K is required** вҖ” magnitude is the same, but the unit must be Kelvin (О”T = T_ambient вҲ’ T_carriage). *(вҲ’1 mark)*
> 3. **Including $Q_{resp}$ for non-respiring foods** (meat, fish, milk, frozen products) вҖ” examiner deducts full 2 marks for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Not applying a safety/design margin** вҖ” practical compressor selection demands a 10вҖ“20 % margin; absence of it is marked as "design oversight". *(вҲ’1 mark)*
> 5. **Confusing "transmission" with "infiltration"** вҖ” transmission is *through the walls*, infiltration is *through the door*. Mixed-up definitions lose 2 marks outright.
> 6. **Wrong conversion:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3517 \text{ W}$ вҖ” many students use 3.517 or 3.6 and lose a unit-conversion mark.

---

## рҹ“Ң Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- вң… A refrigerated mobile van is an **insulated enclosure + active refrigeration unit + air-distribution system**, all mounted on a transport vehicle.
- вң… The **five heat-load heads** are: Transmission ($Q_{tr}$), Product ($Q_p$), Infiltration ($Q_{inf}$), Internal ($Q_{int}$), Respiration ($Q_{resp}$).
- вң… **Transmission load** is dominant in tropical climates; reduce it by increasing PUF thickness or reducing surface area.
- вң… **Infiltration** is controlled by minimising door openings and using **PVC strip curtains / air curtains** at the rear.
- вң… **Product heat** is significant only during the *pull-down* phase; pre-cool the food to almost carriage temperature *before* loading.
- вң… **Respiration load applies only to fresh produce** вҖ” bananas, mangoes, leafy greens, tomatoes, apples, etc.
- вң… **Eutectic plates** are ideal for *short-haul, depot-returnable* milk/ice-cream operations; **VCR** for long-haul and mixed loads; **cryogenic (LNвӮӮ)** for ultra-low-temperature pharma/frozen goods.
- вң… Standard design values: $U \le 0.40$ W/mВІВ·K, $h_i = 10$, $h_o = 23$ W/mВІВ·K, $h_{fg,\text{water}} = 334$ kJ/kg, $1 \text{ TR} = 3517$ W.
- вң… Always apply a **10вҖ“20 % safety margin** to the calculated heat load before specifying the compressor.
- вң… Volume utilization should remain **65вҖ“75 %** to allow air-return plenums.
- вң… Wall construction: **SS 304 / food-grade FRP outer skin + PUF core + food-grade inner skin + coved corners + floor drain** вҖ” these are KTU-recommended hygiene essentials.
- вң… Vehicle-side energy source: **Standby diesel engine** (most common) or **electric stand-by plug-in** (urban night operations).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Drying and Mechanism of Moisture Transfer in Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theory of Drying and Mechanism of Moisture Transfer in Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying** is a complex, simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** operation in which **moisture** (usually water) is removed from a **solid, semi-solid, or liquid** matrix by **thermal evaporation** into a flowing gas stream (commonly air), producing a dry solid product and a wet vapor effluent.

In food engineering terminology adopted by **KTU 2024 Scheme**:

$$ \text{Food Material (Wet)} \;+\; \text{Heat Energy} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Food Material (Dry)} \;+\; \text{Moisture Vapor} $$

Drying is fundamentally **non-adiabatic**, **non-isothermal**, and **transient** вҖ” meaning temperature, moisture content, and drying rate all change with **time and position** inside the material.

### 1.1.1 Types of Moisture in Food Materials

| Term | Definition | Physical State |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Free Moisture** | Moisture in excess of the equilibrium moisture content; removable under given drying conditions | Free water in capillaries & on surface |
| **Bound Moisture** | Moisture held by physico-chemical forces (capillarity, adsorption, osmotic, chemical bonds) | Tightly held, requires more energy |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** | Moisture content at which the material neither gains nor loses moisture to the surrounding air at given T & RH | Static, no driving force |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Moisture** = Free Moisture + Bound Moisture
> The boundary between **free** and **bound** moisture shifts with **air temperature** and **relative humidity**. Higher temperature and lower RH convert some bound moisture into free moisture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Drying Laundry on a Clothesline**
>
> Imagine drying a wet shirt on a clothesline on a sunny day:
> - Initially, the surface is **glistening wet** вҖ” water evaporates at a **steady, rapid rate** (this is the **constant rate period**).
> - As the surface dries out, you feel the shirt is "damp" rather than "wet" вҖ” the drying rate **slows down** because moisture now has to migrate from **inside the fibers to the surface** (this is the **falling rate period**).
> - Eventually, the shirt feels "air-dry" but still holds some moisture deep inside вҖ” at this stage, it has reached **equilibrium with the surrounding atmosphere** (EMC).
>
> The drying rate dropped because the **mechanism of moisture transfer changed** from surface evaporation вҶ’ internal diffusion.

### 1.2.1 Why Drying Theory Matters in Food Engineering

- **Preservation:** Reducing water activity ($a_w < 0.6$) prevents microbial growth.
- **Shelf-life extension:** Dried foods last **months to years** vs. days for fresh.
- **Weight reduction:** Cuts **transportation and storage cost** by 50вҖ“80%.
- **Quality retention:** Governed by controlling **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** and **case-hardening**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metric (KTU High-Yield):**
> The **drying rate** ($N_A$) is the central engineering quantity, expressed in **kg water / (mВІВ·hr)**.

$$\boxed{N_A \;=\; -\dfrac{W_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{dt}}$$

where $W_s$ = bone-dry mass of solid (kg), $A$ = exposed area (mВІ), $X$ = moisture content (kg water / kg dry solid).

---

## 1.3 Standard Units & Constants (Bold)

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 В°C:** $\lambda_s = 2257 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(molВ·K)}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
- **Specific heat of dry air:** $c_{pa} = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/(kgВ·K)}$
- **Specific heat of water vapor:** $c_{pv} = 1.88 \; \text{kJ/(kgВ·K)}$
- **Density of dry air at STP:** $\rho_a = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

---

## 1.4 Mechanism of Moisture Transfer вҖ” Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Coupled Transport Phenomena in Drying:**
>
> **A. External Transport (Convective)** вҖ” from surface to bulk air
> Driven by **vapor pressure difference** $ (P_{A,s} - P_{A,b}) $.
>
> **B. Internal Transport (Within Material)** вҖ” from interior to surface
> Driven by one (or more) of the following mechanisms:
> 1. **Liquid diffusion** (Fickian)
> 2. **Capillary flow** (Darcy/Kelvin)
> 3. **Vapor diffusion** (in pores)
> 4. **Thermal diffusion** (Soret effect)
> 5. **Surface diffusion** (Knudsen)

Which mechanism dominates depends on **moisture content**, **temperature**, **material porosity**, and **pore structure** of the food.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Drying Curve вҖ” A Complete Theoretical Picture

### 2.1.1 Characteristic Regions

A **moisture content vs. time** plot and its derivative (**drying rate vs. moisture content**) form the **master fingerprint** of any drying operation.

The complete drying curve exhibits **four distinct regions**:

| Region | Name | Physical State | Rate-Controlling Step |
|--------|------|----------------|------------------------|
| **AB** | Initial Adjustment (Transient) | Material warms up; surface saturates with moisture | Heat transfer to material |
| **BC** | Constant Rate Period | Surface is saturated with free water | External mass transfer (convective) |
| **CD** | First Falling Rate Period | Surface partially dry; wet patches recede | Surface evaporation + internal liquid flow |
| **DE** | Second Falling Rate Period | Surface dry; moisture front recedes into interior | Internal diffusion (liquid + vapor) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$)** marks the transition **BC вҶ’ CD**.
> The **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)** is reached at point **E** (no further drying).

---

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Region

#### Step AвҶ’B: Initial Adjustment
- Material enters dryer below drying temperature.
- Surface moisture rapidly reaches saturation.
- Drying rate may **rise, dip, or hold steady** depending on initial condition.

#### Step BвҶ’C: Constant Rate Period (CRP)
- Surface is **completely saturated** with free water вҶ’ behaves as if water surface exists.
- **Driving force** = vapor pressure difference $(P_{w,sat}(T_s) - P_{w,b})$.
- **Rate is independent of food type** (depends only on external conditions).
- Surface temperature вүҲ wet-bulb temperature of air.

#### Step CвҶ’D: First Falling Rate Period (FRP-1)
- Surface becomes **unsaturated** вҖ” dry patches appear.
- Wetted area on surface decreases progressively.
- Rate falls **linearly** with moisture content (often assumed).
- Surface temperature begins to rise above wet-bulb.

#### Step DвҶ’E: Second Falling Rate Period (FRP-2)
- Surface is **completely dry**.
- Evaporation plane (drying front) **recedes into the interior**.
- Internal moisture transport becomes rate-limiting.
- Described by **Fickian diffusion** or **Luikov coupled model**.

---

## 2.2 Heat Transfer Analysis in the Constant Rate Period

> [!TIP]
> **Energy balance approach:** All heat reaching the wet surface = used for evaporation (neglecting sensible heating in CRP).

$$\boxed{R_c \;=\; \dfrac{h_c\,(T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s \, \rho_w}}$$

where $h_c$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/mВІВ·K), $T_a$ = air dry-bulb temperature, $T_s$ = surface (вүҲ wet-bulb) temperature, $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporization, $\rho_w$ = water density.

---

## 2.3 Mass Transfer Analysis in the Constant Rate Period

The same rate, expressed via mass transfer:

$$\boxed{R_c \;=\; k_y\,(Y_s - Y_b) \;=\; k_x\,(X_s - X_b)}$$

where $k_y$ = mass-transfer coefficient (kg/mВІВ·sВ·О”Y), $Y_s$ = humidity at surface (saturated), $Y_b$ = bulk air humidity.

The **Lewis relation** ties heat and mass transfer:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{h_c}{k_y \, c_s} \;=\; L_e^{2/3} \;\approx\; 1}$$

where $c_s$ = humid heat, $L_e$ = Lewis number (вүҲ 1 for air-water system).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations вҖ” they form the spine of every KTU numerical.**

| # | Equation | Name / Use | Units |
|---|----------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\;solid}}$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | kg/kg |
| 2 | $X_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}}$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | kg/kg |
| 3 | $N_A = -\dfrac{W_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Drying rate definition | kg/(mВІВ·s) |
| 4 | $R_c = \dfrac{h_c(T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s \rho_w}$ | CRP heat-transfer form | kg/(mВІВ·s) |
| 5 | $R_c = k_y(Y_s - Y_b)$ | CRP mass-transfer form | kg/(mВІВ·s) |
| 6 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{W_s(X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c}$ | Time in CRP | s |
| 7 | $\theta_f = \dfrac{W_s(X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Time in FRP (diffusion model) | s |
| 8 | $MR = \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_0 - X^*}$ | Moisture ratio (dimensionless) | вҖ” |
| 9 | $MR = \exp(-k\,t)$ | Lewis / Exponential model | вҖ” |
| 10 | $MR = \exp(-k\,t^n)$ | Page model | вҖ” |
| 11 | $MR = a\,\exp(-k\,t)$ | HendersonвҖ“Pabis model | вҖ” |
| 12 | $MR = a\,\exp(-k\,t) + b\,t + c$ | MidilliвҖ“Kucuk model | вҖ” |
| 13 | $J = -D_{eff}\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's 1st law (moisture flux) | kg/(mВІВ·s) |
| 14 | $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 X}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's 2nd law | вҖ” |
| 15 | $D_{eff} = D_0\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius-type diffusivity | mВІ/s |
| 16 | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\nabla^2 T + \dfrac{\lambda_s \,\rho_w}{c_p}\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t}$ | Luikov energy equation | вҖ” |
| 17 | $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = \nabla^2 (D_e\,X) + \delta\,\nabla^2 T$ | Luikov mass equation | вҖ” |
| 18 | $a_w = \dfrac{P_{vapor}}{P_{sat}(T)}$ | Water activity | вҖ” |
| 19 | $L_e = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{h_c}{k_y \, c_s \, \rho_a}$ | Lewis number | вҖ” |
| 20 | $Bi_m = \dfrac{k_c \, L}{D_{eff}}$ | Biot number (mass) | вҖ” |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Internal Diffusion (Fickian Slab):**
> - At $x = 0$: $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial x} = 0$ (symmetry)
> - At $x = L$: $-D_{eff}\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial x} = k_c (X_s - X_b)$ (surface convection)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Drying Theory Matters |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| Spray drying of milk powder | Avoid case-hardening вҶ’ controls falling rate design |
| Freeze drying of coffee | Governed entirely by FRP-2 (sublimation) |
| Hot-air drying of grains | Predicts safe storage moisture (~12вҖ“14% wb) |
| Solar drying of fruits | Couples psychrometrics with falling rate |
| Microwave-vacuum drying | Internal heat generation modifies Luikov model |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's rule of thumb:** For food drying, the **second falling rate period** consumes **50вҖ“80% of total drying time** but removes only **20вҖ“40% of moisture**. Designing for FRP-2 efficiency gives the biggest economic gains.

---

## 2.6 Mechanism of Moisture Transfer вҖ” Deep Theory

### 2.6.1 Liquid Diffusion (Fickian)

Applicable to **non-porous, hygroscopic foods** (starches, gels, dense polymers).

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (D_{eff}\,\nabla X)}$$

Effective diffusivity $D_{eff}$ is **concentration-dependent** and **temperature-dependent**.

---

### 2.6.2 Capillary Flow (Darcy's Law)

Applicable to **porous foods** (bread, biscuits, grains, pasta).

$$\boxed{v_{cap} \;=\; -\dfrac{K}{\mu}\,\nabla P_c}$$

where $K$ = permeability, $\mu$ = viscosity of liquid, $P_c$ = capillary pressure.

The **Kelvin equation** gives the relationship between pore radius and vapor pressure:

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_v}{P_{sat}}\right) = -\dfrac{2\sigma V_m}{r_{pore}RT}}$$

---

### 2.6.3 Vapor Diffusion in Pores

When the wet front recedes inside pores, water vapor must diffuse out through air-filled capillaries.

$$\boxed{J_v \;=\; -D_{v,eff}\,\dfrac{\partial C_v}{\partial x}}$$

where $D_{v,eff}$ depends on **porosity ($\varepsilon$), tortuosity ($\tau$), and Knudsen diffusion**:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{D_{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{D_{AB}} + \dfrac{1}{D_K}}$$

---

### 2.6.4 Thermal Diffusion (Soret Effect)

In strongly heated drying, a temperature gradient drives an **additional moisture flux** opposite to heat flow.

$$\boxed{J_T \;=\; -D_T\,C_w\,\nabla T}$$

Significant only in **high-temperature, short-time drying** (e.g., microwave, IR).

---

### 2.6.5 Combined Mechanism вҖ” Luikov Model

The most comprehensive approach treats **heat and mass transfer as coupled**:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} &= \nabla^2 (D_e\,X) + \delta\,\nabla^2 T + \nabla^2 (D_e\,\delta_s\,S) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} &= \alpha\,\nabla^2 T + \dfrac{\lambda_s \,\rho_w}{c_p}\,\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial t} &= \nabla^2 (D_s\,S)
\end{aligned}}$$

where $D_e$ = mass diffusivity, $\delta$ = thermal-gradient coefficient, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $S$ = stress-strain term (for shrinkage).

> [!NOTE]
> **For most food engineering problems (KTU level), use the simplified Fickian diffusion model** with effective diffusivity $D_{eff}$ fitted from experimental data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Drying Time in the Constant Rate Period

### Starting Principle
During CRP, the drying rate $R_c$ is **constant**. Therefore:

$$N_A = -\dfrac{W_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{dt} = R_c = \text{constant}$$

### Step 1: Rearrange the Differential Equation

$$-\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dfrac{A\,R_c}{W_s}$$

### Step 2: Separate Variables

$$-dX = \dfrac{A\,R_c}{W_s}\,dt$$

### Step 3: Integrate from $X_1$ (initial) to $X_c$ (critical) and $0$ to $\theta_c$

$$\int_{X_1}^{X_c} (-dX) \;=\; \dfrac{A\,R_c}{W_s}\,\int_{0}^{\theta_c} dt$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals

$$-(X_c - X_1) \;=\; \dfrac{A\,R_c}{W_s}\,\theta_c$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\theta_c$

$$\boxed{\theta_c \;=\; \dfrac{W_s\,(X_1 - X_c)}{A\,R_c}}$$

> **Engineering meaning:** Time in CRP is **directly proportional** to (initial вҲ’ critical) moisture content, and **inversely proportional** to drying rate. Higher air temperature and velocity raise $R_c$ вҶ’ shorter CRP.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Drying Time in the First Falling Rate Period

### Assumption: Drying rate is linear in moisture content

$$N_A = R_c \cdot \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

where $X^*$ = equilibrium moisture content.

### Step 1: Equate to the rate definition

$$-\dfrac{W_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{dt} \;=\; R_c \cdot \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

### Step 2: Separate Variables

$$-\dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} \;=\; \dfrac{A\,R_c}{W_s\,(X_c - X^*)}\,dt$$

### Step 3: Integrate from $X_c$ to $X$ over $0$ to $t$

$$\int_{X_c}^{X}\dfrac{-dX}{X - X^*} \;=\; \dfrac{A\,R_c}{W_s\,(X_c - X^*)}\,\int_{0}^{t}dt$$

### Step 4: Evaluate

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right) \;=\; -\dfrac{A\,R_c\,t}{W_s\,(X_c - X^*)}$$

### Step 5: Solve for $t$

$$\boxed{t \;=\; \dfrac{W_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,R_c}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X - X^*}\right)}$$

> **Engineering meaning:** As $X \to X^*$, $t \to \infty$. You can never reach $X^*$ in finite time вҖ” a fundamental insight of drying theory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Falling Rate Period via Fickian Diffusion (Thick Slab)

### Governing Equation

$$\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} \;=\; D_{eff}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 X}{\partial x^2}$$

### Boundary & Initial Conditions

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0:\;& X(x,0) = X_0 \quad \text{(uniform initial moisture)} \\
x = 0:\;& \dfrac{\partial X}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \text{(symmetry вҖ” center plane)} \\
x = L:\;& -D_{eff}\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial x} = k_c\,(X_s - X_b) \quad \text{(surface convection)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Define Moisture Ratio and Dimensionless Variables

$$MR = \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_0 - X^*}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{x}{L}, \quad Fo = \dfrac{D_{eff}\,t}{L^2}, \quad Bi_m = \dfrac{k_c\,L}{D_{eff}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$\dfrac{\partial MR}{\partial Fo} \;=\; \dfrac{\partial^2 MR}{\partial \eta^2}$$

### Step 3: Apply Separation of Variables вҶ’ Series Solution (Crank, 1975)

$$\boxed{MR \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\; B_n \;\exp(-\mu_n^2\,Fo)}$$

### Step 4: Coefficients $B_n$ and Eigenvalues $\mu_n$ from:

$$B_n \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sin\mu_n}{2\mu_n + \sin 2\mu_n}$$

$$\mu_n\,\tan\mu_n \;=\; Bi_m$$

### Step 5: For Long Times ($Fo > 0.2$), only the first term matters:

$$\boxed{MR \;\approx\; B_1\,\exp(-\mu_1^2\,Fo)}$$

### Step 6: Take Natural Log

$$\ln(MR) = \ln B_1 - \mu_1^2\,Fo$$

**A plot of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ yields slope $= -\mu_1^2\,D_{eff}/L^2$** вҶ’ used to **experimentally determine $D_{eff}$**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Luikov Coupled Equations (Conceptual)

### Step 1: Energy Balance on a Volume Element

Rate of energy in вҲ’ Rate of energy out + Heat from evaporation = Rate of energy storage.

$$\rho\,c_p\,\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \nabla \cdot (k\,\nabla T) + \lambda_s\,\rho_w\,\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t}$$

### Step 2: Mass Balance on a Volume Element

$$\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} \;=\; \nabla \cdot (D_e\,\nabla X) + \nabla \cdot (D_T\,\nabla T)$$

The second term (thermal-gradient mass flux) is the Luikov extension.

### Step 3: Define Luikov Moisture Potential $M$

$$M = X + \delta\,T + \delta_s\,S$$

where $\delta$ = Soret coefficient, $\delta_s$ = shrinkage coefficient.

### Step 4: Substitute into Mass Equation

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial t} \;=\; D_e\,\nabla^2 M + D_s\,\nabla^2 S}$$

This converts the **coupled non-linear system** into a **linear system** in $M$, solving which simultaneously gives $X(x,t)$ and $T(x,t)$.

> **Why it matters:** Captures the **true physics** of food drying вҖ” temperature and moisture fields are **mutually dependent**, not independent.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” KTU Style

### Problem:
A food slab with initial moisture content $X_1 = 1.2 \; \text{kg/kg dry solid}$ dries to $X_2 = 0.15 \; \text{kg/kg}$. Critical moisture $X_c = 0.6$, equilibrium $X^* = 0.05$. $W_s = 50 \; \text{kg dry solid}$, $A = 5 \; \text{m}^2$, constant rate $R_c = 1.5 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{hr)}$. Calculate **total drying time**.

### Solution:

#### Part 1 вҖ” Time in CRP ($X_1 = 1.2 \to X_c = 0.6$)

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{W_s\,(X_1 - X_c)}{A\,R_c}$$

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{50 \times (1.2 - 0.6)}{5 \times 1.5}$$

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{50 \times 0.6}{7.5}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c = 4.0 \; \text{hr}}$$

#### Part 2 вҖ” Time in FRP ($X_c = 0.6 \to X_2 = 0.15$)

$$\theta_f = \dfrac{W_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,R_c}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$\theta_f = \dfrac{50 \times (0.6 - 0.05)}{5 \times 1.5}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.6 - 0.05}{0.15 - 0.05}\right)$$

$$\theta_f = \dfrac{50 \times 0.55}{7.5}\,\ln(5.5)$$

$$\theta_f = 3.667 \times 1.705$$

$$\boxed{\theta_f \approx 6.25 \; \text{hr}}$$

#### Part 3 вҖ” Total Drying Time

$$\theta_{total} = \theta_c + \theta_f = 4.0 + 6.25$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{total} \approx 10.25 \; \text{hr}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The falling rate period (6.25 hr) consumes **61% of total time** for only **34% of moisture removal** вҖ” perfectly illustrating why FRP engineering is critical.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix вҖ” When to Use Which Model

| Model | Best Use | Equation | Parameters |
|-------|----------|----------|------------|
| Lewis (Newton) | First approximation; CRP-like | $MR = \exp(-kt)$ | $k$ |
| Page | Empirical; thin-layer drying | $MR = \exp(-kt^n)$ | $k, n$ |
| HendersonвҖ“Pabis | Single-layer foods | $MR = a\exp(-kt)$ | $a, k$ |
| MidilliвҖ“Kucuk | Best fit for many foods | $MR = a\exp(-kt) + bt + c$ | $a, k, b, c$ |
| Fickian Diffusion | Thick slabs, pellets | $MR = \sum B_n \exp(-\mu_n^2 Fo)$ | $D_{eff}, L$ |
| Luikov Coupled | Research-grade, accurate | Coupled PDE system | $D_e, \delta, \alpha$ |
