# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**
Differentiate between *cold extrusion* and *extrusion cooking* based on operating parameters and product characteristics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cold Extrusion | Extrusion Cooking |
|---|---|---|
| Barrel Temperature | < 60 В°C | 120вҖ“200 В°C |
| Moisture Content | 28вҖ“45 % w.b. | 10вҖ“30 % w.b. |
| SME Input | 20вҖ“80 Wh/kg | 100вҖ“500 Wh/kg |
| Starch State | Native (ungelatinized) | Fully gelatinized |
| Product Form | Dense, plastic | Porous, expanded (puffed) |
| Downstream Need | Drying / frying required | Often ready-to-eat |
| Examples | Pasta, snack pellets, kibbles | Puffed snacks, RTE cereal, TVP |

*(1 mark for parameters, 1 mark for product state, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**
Define **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** and state its significance in extrusion cooking.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}}$$

where $N$ = screw speed [rev/s], $T$ = torque [NВ·m], $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate [kg/s].

**Significance (the 3 crucial points for full marks):**
1. **Indicator of cooking intensity** вҖ” higher SME вҮ’ more starch gelatinization, more protein denaturation.
2. **Correlates with product texture** вҖ” expansion ratio, bulk density, and crispness all correlate strongly with SME.
3. **Engineering design lever** вҖ” by changing screw speed, screw configuration, or feed rate, the engineer controls SME independently of temperature, providing flexibility to tailor product.

*(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for significance вҖ” three crisp points)*

---

## PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the functional zones of a single-screw cooking extruder. Discuss the role of each zone in achieving starch gelatinization and product expansion. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the HagenвҖ“Poiseuille equation for a Newtonian fluid in a circular die. A cooking-extruder die of radius 5 mm and land length 20 mm extrudes a maize melt of viscosity 5000 PaВ·s at a throughput of $2 \times 10^{-7}$ mВі/s. Compute the die pressure drop. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Four functional zones** of a single-screw cooking extruder:

1. **Feed / Conveying Zone (Zone I):**
   - Deep flight depth, large pitch.
   - Receives granular feed from hopper.
   - Function: Solids conveying without significant compaction.
   - Pressure rise: negligible (~0.1 MPa).

2. **Kneading / Compression Zone (Zone II):**
   - Reduced flight depth; shorter pitch.
   - Mechanical energy input rises; particles fracture and mix.
   - Water and heat begin plasticizing the mass.
   - Viscous dissipation starts dominating.
   - Pressure rises to ~3 MPa.

3. **Cooking / Metering Zone (Zone III):**
   - Constant shallow flight depth.
   - Temperature reaches 120вҖ“200 В°C via viscous dissipation + heaters.
   - **Starch gelatinization** (60вҖ“80 В°C, with moisture) вҖ” amylose leaches, granules swell.
   - **Protein denaturation** occurs.
   - Material is now a homogeneous viscoelastic melt.
   - Pressure rises to 10вҖ“15 MPa just before die.

4. **Die / Shaping Zone (Zone IV):**
   - Melt flows through land-length die.
   - At exit, sudden pressure drop вҶ’ **flash evaporation** of superheated moisture вҶ’ **expansion / puffing**.
   - Final product: porous, crisp extrudate.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
FEED вҶ’ COMPRESSION вҶ’ METERING вҶ’ DIE вҶ’ CUTTER вҶ’ PRODUCT
        вҶ‘ kneading   вҶ‘ cooking  вҶ‘ expansion
```

*(Full marks distribution: 2 marks for zones enumeration, 3 marks for explaining gelatinization + expansion mechanism, 2 marks for labeled diagram.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 вҖ” Write down the HagenвҖ“Poiseuille equation:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Rearrange for $\Delta P$:**

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Substitute values (note unit conversions):**
- $R = 5\, \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\, \text{m}$
- $L = 20\, \text{mm} = 20 \times 10^{-3}\, \text{m}$
- $\mu = 5000\, \text{PaВ·s}$
- $Q = 2 \times 10^{-7}\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$

**Step 4 вҖ” Compute:**

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \times 5000 \times 0.02 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}$$

$$(5 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 625 \times 10^{-12} = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}\, \text{m}^{4}$$

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \times 5000 \times 0.02 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\Delta P \approx 81{,}510\, \text{Pa} \approx 81.5\, \text{kPa} \approx 0.815\, \text{bar}$$

**Interpretation:** A die pressure of ~0.08 MPa is **lower than typical extrusion cooking** (3вҖ“15 MPa) вҖ” this confirms the problem represents either a very low-viscosity melt or a small pilot extruder. For cooking extrusion, viscosity would be 50вҖ“500 PaВ·s and pressure would scale accordingly to multi-MPa range.

*(Full marks: 3 marks derivation, 2 marks rearrangement, 2 marks numerical substitution & answer.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** in extrusion cooking. An extruder operates at 400 rpm with a shaft torque of 75 NВ·m and a throughput of 250 kg/h. Calculate the SME in J/kg and Wh/kg. Comment on whether the process is in the cold or cooking regime. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the principle, construction, and working of a **twin-screw extruder** with a labeled block diagram. Compare co-rotating and counter-rotating twin-screw extruders. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1 вҖ” Definition of SME:**

SME is the mechanical energy input per unit mass of product, representing the cumulative mechanical work done on the food material inside the extruder.

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Unit conversion:**
- $N = 400\, \text{rpm} = 400/60 = 6.667\, \text{rev/s}$
- $T = 75\, \text{NВ·m}$
- $\dot{m} = 250\, \text{kg/h} = 250/3600 = 0.0694\, \text{kg/s}$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute SME in J/kg:**

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 6.667 \times 75}{0.0694}$$

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{3141.6}{0.0694} = 45{,}267\, \text{J/kg}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to Wh/kg:**

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{45{,}267}{3600} = 12.57\, \text{Wh/kg}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Regime classification:**

A SME of ~12.6 Wh/kg lies **well below the cooking-extrusion threshold of 100 Wh/kg**. Therefore, the process is operating in the **cold extrusion / forming regime**. Such a configuration would be suitable for pasta forming, snack pellet pre-forming, or pet food kibble densification вҖ” *not* for producing puffed, expanded ready-to-eat products.

*(Full marks: 2 marks definition, 2 marks numerical substitution, 1 mark computation, 2 marks interpretation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Principle:**
A twin-screw extruder uses two intermeshing screws rotating inside a figure-8-shaped barrel. The two screws provide positive conveying (especially in co-rotating, intermeshing mode), superior mixing, high shear, and self-wiping action that minimizes residence-time distribution.

**Construction (Block Diagram):**

```
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  HOPPER  вҶ’  FEEDER  вҶ’  [SCREW 1 + SCREW 2] в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                              in BARREL       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                          DIE в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                          CUT в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
        вҶ‘                          вҶ‘
   Drive motor                Barrel heaters
   + gearbox                  (segmented)
```

**Working:**
Feed enters from the hopper, is conveyed forward by the intermeshing screws, kneaded by mixing elements, cooked by combination of external heat and viscous dissipation, pressurized to 3вҖ“15 MPa, and forced through the die. At die exit, flash evaporation causes expansion.

**Comparison вҖ” Co-rotating vs Counter-rotating:**

| Feature | Co-rotating (intermeshing) | Counter-rotating (intermeshing) |
|---|---|---|
| Screw speed | High (up to 600 rpm) | Low (typically < 60 rpm) |
| Mixing intensity | Very high | Moderate |
| Positive conveying | Excellent (drag flow) | Excellent |
| Shear level | Moderate to high | Very high |
| Self-wiping | Yes | No (dead spots possible) |
| Typical use | Cereal, snack, TVP cooking | High-shear specialty products |
| Wear | Lower | Higher |
| Energy efficiency | Higher | Lower |

**Advantages of Twin-Screw over Single-Screw:**
1. Better mixing and kneading.
2. Positive conveying вҖ” independent of friction.
3. Wider operating window (moisture 5вҖ“35 %).
4. Better heat transfer; shorter residence time.
5. Easier scale-up via segmented screw elements.

*(Full marks: 2 marks principle, 2 marks construction/diagram, 3 marks comparison table.)*

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing SME with shaft power.** Power is in Watts (W); SME is energy per unit mass (J/kg or Wh/kg). Mixing these up costs 2вҖ“3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions in numericals.** Screw speed in *rpm* must be converted to *rev/s* before substituting in $2\pi N T$. The factor of 60 is a classic trap.
> 3. **Stating only "temperature" as the difference between cold and cooking extrusion.** Always triangulate with **all three** parameters: temperature, moisture, and SME. Examiners explicitly look for this.
> 4. **Neglecting viscous dissipation in the cooking zone.** Many students assume barrel heaters alone raise the temperature. The examiner wants to see the explicit statement that $\dot{q} = \tau \dot{\gamma}$ is the *dominant* heating source.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw/label diagrams.** Even a simple labeled block diagram earns easy marks in the 14-mark questions.
> 6. **HagenвҖ“Poiseuille derivation skipping steps.** The KTU key demands each integration step explicitly вҖ” do not write the final formula without showing the boundary conditions.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Extrusion** = forcing plasticized food through a die to shape it. Continuous, energy-efficient, versatile.
- **Cold extrusion** вҶ’ T < 60 В°C, moisture 28вҖ“45 %, SME 20вҖ“80 Wh/kg вҶ’ dense products (pasta, pellets).
- **Cooking extrusion** вҶ’ T 120вҖ“200 В°C, moisture 10вҖ“30 %, SME 100вҖ“500 Wh/kg вҶ’ puffed products (snacks, RTE cereals).
- **Four functional zones:** Feed вҶ’ Compression/Kneading вҶ’ Cooking/Metering вҶ’ Die (with Cutter).
- **HagenвҖ“Poiseuille** for Newtonian die flow: $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8\mu L}$.
- **Power-law generalization:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2L}{R}\, K\, \left[\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\cdot\dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}\right]^{n}$.
- **SME formula:** $\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}}$ вҖ” most important single parameter in extrusion cooking.
- **Shaft power:** $P = 2\pi N T$.
- **Expansion Ratio:** $\text{ER} = D_e^{2}/D_d^{2}$ вҖ” measures puffing.
- **Viscous dissipation** is the dominant heat source (70вҖ“90 %) in metering zone.
- **Twin-screw co-rotating intermeshing** = industry standard for cooking extrusion.
- **Constant:** Latent heat of vaporization of water = **2,260 kJ/kg** (drives expansion at die).
- **Starch gelatinization** occurs at **60вҖ“80 В°C** in presence of moisture вҖ” basis of cooking extrusion.
- **Always convert units** before substitution; rpm вҶ’ rev/s, kg/h вҶ’ kg/s.
- **Exam trick:** Numerical SME < 100 Wh/kg вҮ’ cold regime; SME > 100 Wh/kg вҮ’ cooking regime.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single and Twin-Screw Extruders - Constructional and Operational Features Including Advantages/Disadvantages with Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT721 - FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single and Twin-Screw Extruders - Constructional and Operational Features Including Advantages/Disadvantages with Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Extrusion Cooking)**
> *Extrusion* is a continuous high-temperature short-time (HTST) process in which moistened, expansible food materials (starch and/or protein based) are plasticized and cooked in a **barrel** by a combination of *heat*, *moisture*, *mechanical shear*, and *pressure*, and then forced through a **die** of defined shape to produce products with desired texture, shape, and density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE):** A processing machine in which **one** rotating screw, mounted coaxially inside a heated barrel, transports, compresses, shears, and pressurizes the food mass.
>
> **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE):** A processing machine in which **two** intermeshing (or non-intermeshing) screws, rotating either *co-rotating* or *counter-rotating*, operate inside a figure-eight (or twin circular) barrel to provide positive conveying, mixing, cooking, and forming of food materials.

---

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are squeezing toothpaste from a tube:

- **The toothpaste tube** behaves like the **barrel**.
- **Your squeezing fingers** (rolling them from the bottom) behave like a **single screw**.
- **The narrow nozzle** at the mouth behaves like the **die**.
- The pressure you build up forces out a *ribbon of paste* вҖ” this is exactly what an extruder does to food dough, except with much higher pressure, temperature, and shear.

Now imagine **two such toothpaste tubes glued side by side, with two screws working together** вҖ” that's a *twin-screw extruder*. The two screws can *cooperate* (co-rotating) or *work against each other* (counter-rotating), giving you far more *mixing* and *pumping efficiency*.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Extrusion Matters in Food Engineering (Industry Relevance):**
> Breakfast cereals (corn flakes, oats), snack pellets (kurkure, 3D pellets), pasta, texturized soy protein (TSP/TVP), pet food, breadcrumbs, modified starches, baby foods, and confectionery all depend on extrusion cooking. KTU examiners frequently frame a 14-mark question around a *case study* of one such product.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Food Extruders

Extruders for food are classified by two primary criteria:

| Criterion | Type | Key Character |
|-----------|------|---------------|
| Number of screws | Single-screw (SSE) | One screw, friction-driven conveying |
| | Twin-screw (TSE) | Two screws, positively driven conveying |
| Screw rotation | Co-rotating TSE | Both screws rotate in same direction |
| | Counter-rotating TSE | Screws rotate in opposite directions |
| Screw engagement | Intermeshing TSE | Screws interlock like meshing gears |
| | Non-intermeshing TSE | Screws do not engage |
| Thermal condition | Cold extruder | No external heating; low pressure |
| | Hot (Cook) extruder | External heating + shear cooking |

> [!NOTE]
> For **food applications**, the *co-rotating, fully intermeshing twin-screw extruder* is the *industry workhorse* because of its *self-wiping action* and *positive conveying*.

---

### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Snapshot

The single-screw extruder was the first-generation design adapted from the polymer and plastics industry (originally by *Artofex* in the 1930s). The twin-screw extruder was introduced later (1960sвҖ“70s) primarily for the pet food and breakfast cereal industry because of its superior mixing capability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "extruder = only a forming machine." The correct industrial view is that a **cooking extruder** simultaneously performs *mixing, cooking, shearing, kneading, shaping, and texturizing* in a single continuous operation. This integrated nature is a frequently tested point.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Screw Extruder вҖ” Constructional Features

A typical single-screw cooking extruder for food comprises the following components:

### 2.1.1 Major Parts of a Single-Screw Extruder

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Hopper** with volumetric or gravimetric feeder | Receives and meters raw material (powder/granules) |
| 2 | **Barrel** | Cylindrical housing, often jacketed for heating/cooling; lined with wear-resistant alloy |
| 3 | **Screw (the heart of the extruder)** | Rotates inside barrel; performs conveying, compression, shearing |
| 4 | **Heater bands / Cooling jackets** | Provide external thermal energy input (steam, electric, induction) |
| 5 | **Die plate / Die** | Final shaping; may be fitted with internal inserts/cutters |
| 6 | **Knife / Cutter assembly** | Cuts extrudate at die exit (flying or face cutter) |
| 7 | **Drive system** | Motor + gearbox to provide screw torque and RPM |
| 8 | **Thrust bearing assembly** | Resists axial thrust generated by screw |

### 2.1.2 Screw Geometry вҖ” The Heart of the Extruder

The screw consists of three functional zones along its length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} \;=\; L_{feed} \;+\; L_{compression} \;+\; L_{metering}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:

- $L_{feed}$: **Feed zone** вҖ” *deep flight*, conveys material from hopper without compression.
- $L_{compression}$: **Transition / Compression zone** вҖ” flight depth *decreases* gradually, building pressure and shear.
- $L_{metering}$: **Metering zone** вҖ” *shallow flight*, ensures uniform flow and develops die pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compression Ratio (CR)** is a defining screw parameter:
> $$\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{H_{feed}}{H_{metering}}$$
> For food extruders, typical **CR = 1.5 to 5** (starch cooking demands CR вүҲ 3).

**L/D Ratio (Length-to-Diameter):** Common food extruder screws run at **$L/D = 15:1$ to $30:1$**, while high-shear cooking extruders may reach $L/D = 40:1$.

**Flight geometry parameters:**
- Helix angle: typically $\alpha = 17.6В°$ (one full pitch per diameter).
- Pitch: $p = D \tan \alpha$.
- Flight width $W_f$ and flight land $\epsilon$.

### 2.1.3 Operational Features of Single-Screw Extruder

The operation is fundamentally governed by **drag-induced conveying**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{drag} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\alpha \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

where $D$ = barrel ID, $N$ = screw RPM, $H$ = local channel depth, $\alpha$ = helix angle.

The **net throughput** is reduced by *pressure flow* (back-leakage) and *leakage flow* (over the flights):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} \;=\; Q_{drag} \;-\; Q_{pressure} \;-\; Q_{leak}
\end{aligned}
$$

This drag-flow principle **limits mixing capability** вҖ” which is the chief weakness of SSE.

---

## 2.2 Twin-Screw Extruder вҖ” Constructional Features

### 2.2.1 Major Parts of a Twin-Screw Extruder

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Twin hopper / co-feeders | Independent feeding of solid and liquid |
| 2 | **Figure-8 (or two-circle) barrel** | Two intersecting bores; modular sectional design |
| 3 | **Two co-rotating / counter-rotating screws** | Mounted on parallel shafts |
| 4 | **Modular screw elements** | Forward conveying, reverse, kneading blocks, mixing pins |
| 5 | Steam injection ports | Add moisture along the barrel |
| 6 | Side feeders / vents | Inject solids/liquids mid-barrel; vent volatiles |
| 7 | Thrust bearings (per shaft) | Handle very high axial loads |
| 8 | Die and cutter assembly | Final shaping |

### 2.2.2 Modular Screw Elements (Unique to TSE)

The screw shaft is built from **kneading discs** and **conveying elements** locked on a splined shaft:

- **Forward conveying element (FCE):** Standard pitch, positive conveying.
- **Reverse conveying element (RCE):** Reverse pitch вҖ” creates a *pressure barrier* and *residence time*.
- **Kneading blocks (KB):** Discs offset by 30В°/45В°/60В°/90В° вҖ” provide high *distributive* and *dispersive* mixing.
- **Mixing pins:** Provide localized shear and improved heat transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-wiping action:** In co-rotating, fully intermeshing TSE, the channel of one screw is wiped clean by the flight of the other. This *prevents fouling* and *eliminates stagnant zones* вҖ” a major advantage for hygienic food processing.

### 2.2.3 Operational Features of Twin-Screw Extruder

Conveying is **positively (mechanically) induced** вҖ” the screws *enforce* transport through their geometry, not merely by friction. This means:

- Independent of material frictional properties.
- Less sensitive to feed consistency.
- Excellent for *slippery*, *sticky*, *fatty*, or *low-bulk-density* feeds.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All key formulas below are *examination-relevant*. Master the units, the assumptions, and the underlying logic.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy; $P$ = net motor power, $Q$ = throughput | $\text{kWh/t}$ |
| 2 | $\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f}{H_m}$ | Compression ratio; $H_f, H_m$ = flight depths in feed & metering | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L/D$ | Screw length-to-diameter ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\alpha \cos\alpha$ | Drag-induced flow rate (Newtonian) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure} - Q_{leak}$ | Net extruder throughput | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu \pi D N}{H}$ | Shear rate proxy in metering zone | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{H}$ | Apparent shear rate (metering) | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity (Newtonian approximation) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $\text{RTD} \to \bar{t} = \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}}$ | Mean residence time; $V$ = filled volume, $\dot{V}$ = volumetric throughput | $\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $\tau_{res} = \dfrac{V_{filled}\,\rho}{Q_m}$ | Residence time (mass form); $\rho$ = bulk density, $Q_m$ = mass flow | $\text{s}$ |
| 11 | $P_{motor} = 2\pi N\,T$ | Power requirement; $T$ = torque on screw | $\text{W}$ |
| 12 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_{die}}$ | Die pressure (developed at the die entry) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 13 | $\text{LE} = \dfrac{T_{melt} - T_{in}}{T_{melt} - T_{water,\,in}}$ | Cook index (light transmittance proxy) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{Expansion}_{product}}{\text{Expansion}_{control}}$ | Expansion index | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\eta_{spec} = \dfrac{P/\dot{m}}{(\dot{\gamma})^2}$ | Specific energy per unit shear | $\text{J/kg}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The *specific mechanical energy (SME)* is the single most-asked parameter in any extruder question because it correlates directly with starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and product expansion.

---

## 2.4 Operating Variables вҖ” The "Knobs" of an Extruder

> [!TIP]
> These are the *degrees of freedom* a food engineer manipulates:

| Variable | Effect on Product |
|----------|-------------------|
| Feed moisture (15вҖ“30%) | Lower moisture вҶ’ higher SME вҶ’ crunchier, darker, more expanded |
| Screw RPM (100вҖ“500 typical) | Higher RPM вҶ’ higher SME, shorter residence time |
| Barrel temperature (100вҖ“180 В°C) | Higher T вҶ’ faster cook, less SME, less expansion (over-cook collapses cells) |
| Feed rate | Lower feed вҶ’ higher SME per kg |
| Die geometry | Smaller die вҶ’ higher back-pressure вҶ’ more shear and expansion |
| Screw configuration | Reverse elements вҶ’ longer residence time, more cook |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **complete mathematical derivations** for the core equations an examiner may ask in a 14-mark numerical/derivation problem. *Every step is explicitly retained вҖ” no shortcuts.*

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Drag-Flow Through a Screw Channel (Newtonian Model)

### Step 1 вҖ” Geometry and Coordinate System

Consider the screw channel "unwrapped" into a flat rectangular channel:

- Width (perpendicular to flight) = $W \approx \pi D \sin\alpha - e$ (where $e$ is flight width).
- Depth = $H$ (function of axial position).
- Length (along channel) = $L_c = \dfrac{L}{\cos\alpha}$.

The barrel surface moves **relative** to the channel with velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_b \;=\; \pi D N
\end{aligned}
$$

Decomposing $v_b$ along the down-channel direction $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{b,x} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

and across the channel direction $z$ (from screw root to barrel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{b,z} \;=\; \pi D N \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Simplifying Assumptions

1. **Newtonian fluid** (constant viscosity $\mu$).
2. **Steady, isothermal, laminar flow.**
3. **No leakage over flights** (idealized).
4. **Fully developed flow** (no $x$-variation of pressure).
5. **No slip at barrel wall.**

### Step 3 вҖ” Equation of Motion (z-direction)

For fully developed down-channel flow, the NavierвҖ“Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \;=\; -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} \;+\; \mu\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} v_{x}}{\partial z^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

with boundary conditions:

$$
v_x(0) = 0 \quad \text{(no-slip at screw root)},\quad v_x(H) = \pi D N \cos\alpha \quad \text{(barrel motion)}.
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Solve the ODE

Integrating twice:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,z \;+\; C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_x(z) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,z^{2} \;+\; C_1 z \;+\; C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the boundary conditions:

- At $z = 0$: $v_x = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.
- At $z = H$: $v_x = \pi D N \cos\alpha$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi D N \cos\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,H^{2} \;+\; C_1 H
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N \cos\alpha}{H} \;-\; \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,H
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_x(z) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N \cos\alpha}{H}\,z \;-\; \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,(Hz - z^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **classic screw-channel velocity profile**: a linear (Couette) drag term *plus* a parabolic (Poiseuille) pressure term.

### Step 5 вҖ” Volumetric Flow Rate (Integration)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{H} W\,v_x(z)\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting and integrating term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{H}\dfrac{\pi D N \cos\alpha}{H}\,z\,dz \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi D N H \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{H}\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}(Hz - z^{2})\,dz \;=\; -\dfrac{H^{3}}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \;=\; W\!\left[\dfrac{\pi D N H \cos\alpha}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{H^{3}}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Convert to Down-Channel and Axial Frames

With channel width $W = \pi D \sin\alpha - e \approx \pi D \sin\alpha$ (ignoring flight width $e$), the **down-channel** flow rate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{dc} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\alpha \cos\alpha}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D H^{3} \sin\alpha}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **axial** flow rate is obtained by multiplying by $\cos\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{axial} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\alpha \cos^{2}\alpha}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D H^{3} \sin\alpha\cos\alpha}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining $\partial p/\partial z_{axial} = \cos\alpha\,\partial p/\partial x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\alpha \cos\alpha}{2}}_{\text{Drag flow } Q_{drag}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi D H^{3}\sin^{2}\alpha}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}}_{\text{Pressure flow } Q_{p}}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This is the **fundamental single-screw extruder equation**. The first term (drag flow) is *pumping*, the second (pressure flow) is *back-flow*. Net throughput = drag flow вҲ’ pressure flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)

### Step 1 вҖ” Motor Power Delivery

The net mechanical power delivered to the material:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{net} \;=\; 2\pi N\,T_{net}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $T_{net} = T_{motor} - T_{no-load}$ (corrected for bearing/gear losses).

### Step 2 вҖ” Mass Throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} \;=\; Q_{net}\cdot \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho$ is the bulk density of the dough in the metering zone.

### Step 3 вҖ” Definition of SME

By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{net}}{\dot{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N\,T_{net}}{Q_{net}\cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Typical Bounds (for KTU numericals)

For a *corn puff* extrusion at $N = 300$ rpm, $T_{net} = 80$ NВ·m, $Q = 200$ kg/h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{net} \;=\; 2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 80 \;=\; 2513\;\text{W} \;\approx\; 2.51\;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{3600} \;=\; 0.0556\;\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{2513}{0.0556} \;\approx\; 45221\;\text{J/kg} \;\approx\; 45.2\;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to common units:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{45.2}{3.6} \;\approx\; 12.6\;\text{kWh/t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Answer Range:** Starch-based food extrusion typically operates at **SME = 30 to 250 kJ/kg** (i.e., 8вҖ“70 kWh/t). Anything outside this window usually indicates either *under-cooking* or *over-shearing*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Apparent Shear Rate in the Metering Zone

For a thin-metering approximation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{v_{b}}{H} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding shear stress (Newtonian):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau \;=\; \mu\,\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu \pi D N}{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For a TSE kneading block, an analogous but higher estimate is used:
> $$\dot{\gamma}_{KB} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{e_g}$$
> where $e_g$ is the inter-meshing gap (typically 1вҖ“5 mm), giving shear rates of **hundreds to thousands of s$^{-1}$**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: SSE vs. TSE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a KTU Board favourite вҖ” learn it by heart.**

| Parameter | Single-Screw Extruder | Twin-Screw Extruder (Co-rotating, Intermeshing) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| Conveying mechanism | Friction drag (passive) | Mechanically positive (active) |
| Conveying efficiency | Moderate | Excellent |
| Mixing action | Poor to fair | Excellent (dispersive + distributive) |
| Self-wiping | No | Yes |
| Stagnant zones | Yes (flight edges) | Negligible |
| Feed flexibility | Limited; sensitive to fat/oil/sugar | Wide; tolerates sticky, oily, low-bulk feeds |
| Independent liquid injection | Difficult | Easy (along barrel) |
| Heat transfer uniformity | Poor | Excellent (large surface/volume) |
| Degree of cook control | Limited | High |
| Capital cost | Low | High (1.5вҖ“3Г—) |
| Maintenance cost | Low | Higher |
| Throughput for same screw diameter | Lower | Higher |
| Screw interchangeability | Limited | Modular вҖ” fully configurable |
| Typical SME range (kJ/kg) | 50вҖ“250 | 30вҖ“500 |
| Residence time distribution | Wide (broad) | Narrow (near plug flow) |
| Hygienic cleaning | Moderate | Excellent |

---

## 3.5 Advantages and Disadvantages вҖ” Compact Comparative List

### 3.5.1 Single-Screw Extruder

**Advantages:**
1. Simple design, fewer parts.
2. Lower capital investment.
3. Easy operation and maintenance.
4. Well-suited for *low-viscosity* doughs and *forming* applications.
5. Acceptable throughput for pasta, snack pellets, low-shear products.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Limited mixing capability.
2. Poor control of residence time.
3. Stagnant zones вҶ’ product degradation / burning.
4. Sensitive to feed composition (oils, sugars, fiber reduce friction).
5. Lower heat-transfer uniformity вҶ’ uneven cook.

### 3.5.2 Twin-Screw Extruder

**Advantages:**
1. **Positive conveying** вҶ’ independent of material friction.
2. **Superior mixing** (dispersive + distributive).
3. **Self-wiping** вҶ’ hygienic, minimal fouling.
4. **Modular screws** вҶ’ tailored residence time and shear profile.
5. **Independent liquid injection ports** along the barrel.
6. Higher throughput per unit barrel volume.
7. Narrow residence time distribution вҶ’ consistent cook.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Higher capital cost (about 2Г— SSE).
2. Higher drive power requirement.
3. Gearbox complexity and skilled maintenance.
4. Sensitive to wear at intermeshing zone.
5. Over-shearing risk on heat-sensitive ingredients.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**

**Q1. Define *specific mechanical energy (SME)* and state its typical range for starch-based food extrusion.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Specific mechanical energy is the mechanical energy input per unit mass of product, defined as
> $$\text{SME} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{\dot{m}}$$
> where $P_{net}$ is the net motor power delivered to the food mass and $\dot{m}$ is the mass throughput. For starch-based cooking extrusion, SME typically lies between **30 and 250 kJ/kg** (i.e., 8вҖ“70 kWh/t). SME governs starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and product expansion.

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between *co-rotating* and *counter-rotating* twin-screw extruders.**

> **Model Answer:**
> In a **co-rotating** TSE, both screws rotate in the same direction; this design permits fully intermeshing, self-wiping action and is most common in food extrusion. In a **counter-rotating** TSE, screws rotate in opposite directions; this design offers excellent positive conveying but has limited intermeshing (often tangential). Counter-rotating TSEs are typical in *PVC and polymer* processing but see niche food use (e.g., some pasta lines). Co-rotating TSE dominates food due to better mixing and modular screw flexibility.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B, and answer BOTH sub-parts.**

---

### Question A вҖ” 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **constructional features** of a **single-screw cooking extruder**. List the functions of its three functional zones along the screw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-screw extruder has a screw diameter of 60 mm, helix angle 17.6В°, screw RPM 280, and metering zone flight depth of 5 mm. Barrel temperature = 150 В°C. Assuming Newtonian flow with viscosity of dough вүҲ 2500 PaВ·s, determine:
  (i) the drag flow rate,
  (ii) the apparent shear rate in the metering zone,
  (iii) the SME if torque is 90 NВ·m and throughput is 250 kg/h. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Constructional Features (7 marks):**

A single-screw cooking extruder consists of the following major components, each with a specific function:

1. **Hopper and feeder:** Receives premixed feed (typically flour + water + additives), meters it at a controlled rate.
2. **Barrel:** A heavy cylindrical steel housing (often Lined with high-chrome alloy or bimetallic sleeve); externally jacketed for steam/electric heating and cooling.
3. **Screw:** The rotating heart; threaded steel shaft with flights. Has three functional zones:
   - *Feed zone* (deep flight, ~50% of screw length): transports solid feed from hopper; gentle, low shear.
   - *Compression / Transition zone* (~25%): flight depth decreases, compressing material and building pressure; cooking begins.
   - *Metering zone* (~25%, shallow flight): final pressure development, uniform pumping, maximum shear вҖ” fully cooks the dough.
4. **Heating system:** External band heaters or steam jackets along the barrel.
5. **Die plate:** At the discharge end; shapes the product (ribbon, tube, pellet).
6. **Cutter / knife:** Face or flying cutter at the die face for cutting extrudate into defined lengths.
7. **Drive system:** Motor (typically 50вҖ“500 kW) + reduction gearbox with variable speed control.
8. **Thrust bearing:** Resists the substantial axial load (often tens of tonnes).

**Functional zones of the screw (summary table):**

| Zone | Flight Depth | Main Function |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| Feed | Deep (вүҲ 10вҖ“15 mm) | Conveying solids |
| Compression | Decreasing (вүҲ 5вҖ“10 mm) | Compression + initial cook |
| Metering | Shallow (вүҲ 2вҖ“5 mm) | Pressure build + final cook |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.06\,\text{m}$, $\alpha = 17.6В°$, $N = 280\,\text{rpm} = 4.667\,\text{r/s}$, $H = 5\,\text{mm} = 0.005\,\text{m}$, $\mu = 2500\,\text{PaВ·s}$, $T = 90\,\text{NВ·m}$, $\dot{m} = 250\,\text{kg/h}$.

**(i) Drag flow rate (assume zero pressure flow for maximum drag estimate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{drag} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,H\,\sin\alpha\cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin 17.6В° &\approx 0.3024, \quad \cos 17.6В° \approx 0.9532
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{drag} &\approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,(0.06)^{2}\,(4.667)\,(0.005)\,(0.3024)\,(0.9532)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,(9.8696)\,(0.0036)\,(4.667)\,(0.005)\,(0.2883)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\approx 1.197\times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \;\approx\; 7.18\;\text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Apparent shear rate in metering zone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{H} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,(0.06)\,(4.667)}{0.005} \;\approx\; 175.9\;\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Specific mechanical energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{net} &\;=\; 2\pi N\,T \;=\; 2\pi\,(4.667)\,(90) \;\approx\; 2638\;\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &\;=\; \dfrac{250}{3600} \;\approx\; 0.0694\;\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SME} &\;=\; \dfrac{2638}{0.0694} \;\approx\; 38\,008\;\text{J/kg} \;\approx\; 38.0\;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to common food-industry units:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SME} &\;=\; \dfrac{38.0}{3.6} \;\approx\; 10.6\;\text{kWh/t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The extruder operates in the *low-SME regime*, suitable for *gentle cooking* (e.g., pre-gelatinized starch or baby-food cereal).

---

### Question B вҖ” 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **constructional and operational features** of single-screw and twin-screw extruders with a specific emphasis on conveying mechanism, mixing, residence time distribution, and self-wiping action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **two industrial case studies** where twin-screw extrusion cooking is preferred over single-screw extrusion, justifying the choice in terms of SME, screw configuration, and product quality. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparative analysis (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Screw Extruder | Twin-Screw Extruder |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Conveying mechanism | Friction drag (passive, depends on material-wall friction) | Mechanically positive (active, geometry-enforced) |
| Mixing capability | Limited; relies on back-mixing only | Excellent; dispersive + distributive mixing via kneading blocks |
| Residence time distribution (RTD) | Wide (broad peak) | Narrow (near plug flow) |
| Self-wiping | Absent (fouling on flight edges) | Present in co-rotating intermeshing TSE |
| Sensitivity to feed composition | High (oils, sugars, fiber reduce friction вҶ’ stalling) | Low (positive conveying) |
| Modular screw design | Limited | Fully modular (any screw profile) |
| Capital cost | Low (1Г— baseline) | High (1.5вҖ“3Г— baseline) |
| Throughput | Moderate for same diameter | Higher for same diameter |

**Operational implications:**
- For *low-viscosity, low-fat, simple* formulations (e.g., pasta), SSE is sufficient and economical.
- For *complex, multi-component, high-moisture, or high-fat* formulations, TSE delivers uniform cook, controlled SME, and superior product consistency.

**(b) Industrial Case Studies (7 marks):**

**Case Study 1 вҖ” Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP):**
- *Feed:* Defatted soy flour (~22% protein) at 25вҖ“35% moisture.
- *Why TSE:* TVP requires *high SME* (вүҲ 100вҖ“200 kJ/kg) and *high barrel temperature* (140вҖ“180 В°C) to denature soy protein and form a *fibrous, meat-like texture*. The TSE's *kneading blocks and reverse elements* generate the necessary shear profile and pressure barrier; an SSE cannot provide this controlled high-shear cooking.
- *Screw configuration:* Feed вҶ’ kneading block (60В°) вҶ’ reverse element вҶ’ final metering вҶ’ die.
- *Product quality:* Fibrous, chewy, rehydratable chunks that mimic chicken or beef.

**Case Study 2 вҖ” Direct-Expanded Corn Puff Snacks:**
- *Feed:* Degermed corn grits at 12вҖ“15% moisture.
- *Why TSE:* A TSE allows *independent moisture and feed control*, *rapid pressure development* at the die, and *narrow RTD* вҖ” all critical for *uniform puffing* and *crunchy texture*. The self-wiping action prevents burnt-on residue from the high-sugar coating often used.
- *SME:* вүҲ 80вҖ“150 kJ/kg.
- *Screw configuration:* Co-rotating TSE with a high-shear kneading section, followed by a final cooling section (to set the melt viscosity before expansion at the die).
- *Product quality:* Light, crispy, well-expanded snack with smooth surface and uniform cell structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (commonly losing marks):**
> 1. *Do not* describe an extruder as merely a "forming machine" вҖ” it is a *cooking, mixing, shearing, and texturizing* unit simultaneously.
> 2. *Always* mention **SME** explicitly when discussing extrusion вҖ” examiners specifically scan for it.
> 3. *Do not* confuse *co-rotating* with *counter-rotating* TSE вҖ” only *co-rotating intermeshing* is dominant in food.
> 4. *Avoid* generic advantages like "easy to use" вҖ” be *quantitative* (e.g., "SME of 100 kJ/kg," "L/D = 25," "CR = 3").
> 5. *Never* claim that a TSE is always better than an SSE вҖ” there are valid applications (e.g., pasta, basic forming) where SSE is preferred.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Single & Twin-Screw Extruders**

- **Definition:** Extrusion = HTST cooking + shaping in a single continuous operation.
- **SSE:** Single screw; drag-flow conveying; simple, cheap, limited mixing.
- **TSE:** Twin screws; positive conveying; modular; high mixing; self-wiping.
- **Co-rotating, fully intermeshing TSE** is the **food-industry standard**.
- **Three zones of a screw:** Feed вҶ’ Compression вҶ’ Metering.
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** = $H_{feed}/H_{metering}$ вҖ” typically **1.5 to 5** for food.
- **L/D ratio** = 15:1 to 30:1 (cooking) or up to 40:1 (high-shear).
- **Helix angle:** typically **17.6В°** (one pitch = one diameter).
- **Key equation:** $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure} - Q_{leak}$.
- **Drag flow:** $Q_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}NH\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$.
- **Apparent shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / H$.
- **SME:** $P_{net}/\dot{m}$, typically **30вҖ“250 kJ/kg** for starch foods.
- **Power:** $P = 2\pi N T$.
- **Residence time:** $\tau_{res} = V_{filled}\rho/\dot{m}$; **TSE has narrower RTD** than SSE.
- **Self-wiping** вҶ’ hygienic, no stagnant zones вҶ’ TSE advantage.
- **Modular screw elements:** Forward conveying, reverse, kneading blocks (30В°/45В°/60В°/90В°).
- **Liquid injection** mid-barrel вҶ’ easy with TSE, difficult with SSE.
- **SSE preferred for:** Pasta, simple forming, low-shear snack pellets, cold forming.
- **TSE preferred for:** Breakfast cereals, puffed snacks, TVP, pet food, modified starch, baby food, co-extruded products.
- **Case studies to remember:**
  1. **Corn puff snacks** (high SME, TSE).
  2. **TVP/TSP** (very high SME + high T, TSE with kneading blocks).
  3. **Pasta** (cold SSE, low shear).
  4. **Pet food kibble** (medium SME TSE).
  5. **Pre-gelatinized starch** (co-rotating TSE).
- **Always remember:** SME = $\dfrac{2\pi N\,T_{net}}{\dot{m}}$.
- **Die pressure** governs expansion ratio at the die exit.
- **Barrel temp + shear + moisture** = the three pillars of extrusion cook.
- **Twin-screw extruders** cost 1.5вҖ“3Г— single-screw but offer greater flexibility and control.
